Otherwise, the Croisière (Cruise in French) is notable for being the only Yema Yachtingraf with three sub-registers, thanks to the Valjoux 72 movement for the earlier version (which has a white sub-register for the running seconds ), and the Valjoux 7736 for the later one. The present watch shows the second execution case, with its characteristic thinner lugs and aluminum bezel, and the serial number engraved on the caseback matches the known production batches. The manual-wound chronograph movement is described as working properly despite the obvious replacement pushers. The colorful regatta counter does not show excessive aging, as is also the case for the unique looking hour sub-register with its red-cross to indicate any duration over 12 consecutive hours, honoring the Croisière name of this model (after all a cruise lasts longer than a regatta).

This Yema Yachtingraf Croisière is offered at a Buy-It-Now of $7,995; lower offers are also considered.

This later Yema Yachtingraf is often nicknamed the "Jumbo" after its oversized case that substantially grew from the 38-39mm of its predecessor to reach close to 43mm in the example here, with its distinctive C-shaped regatta countdown subdial. It is also identifiable by the blue ship printed above the patent pending mention, while earlier examples got a white ship. A couple of different Yema signatures were used during production, the present one being one of the most common. A big feature of this Yachtingraf comes from its unique countdown counter, obtained through a modification of the common Valjoux 7733, now denoted 7733S (you can find a live countdown on this French forum ). The watch is described as functioning perfectly, and comes with what the seller says is its original strap in NOS condition.

This Yema Yachtingraf "Jumbo" is advertised on Ebay France, with current bidding just above $5,000.

While Basmich does not have the same brand recognition as Yema, this chronograph shares the regatta theme, thanks to the 5-minute sectors of its minute sub-register. It also displays a case-shape typical of the 1970s, with an interesting 12-hour bezel, and relies on the workhorse chronograph caliber Valjoux 7730. Very few other examples have been found, assuming this example is of the same production as the one listed here , while another one (if not the same) was also auctioned back in 2015. The stainless steel bracelet is unsigned, and is likely not original, although it looks great with this regatta chrono. The dial is very well preserved; its multiples scales remain clearly legible. The different patina of the lume may possibly indicate a relume of the handset at some point.

This Basmich Precision chronograph is currently on Ebay, with bidding just above $500 and two more days to go.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Universal Genève 'Nina Rindt' And A Selection Of Other Chronographs Available On Ebay

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week will be about very special chronographs, each with their own distinctive features. The triple calendar Heuer and the Doxa Sub 200 T. Graph Sharkhunter both boast an indisputable rarity, in addition to their great aesthetics, and were obvious candidates from Ebay. When it came to the Zenith A271 and the Bulova reference 31000, the unusual color of their dials really stood out and made them prime candidates for a mention in Bring A Loupe.

In terms of vintage Heuer, Carreras and Autavias garner most of the attention, but pieces like this one show that the brand's mastery of chronograph wristwatches existed well before the 1960s. This triple calendar chronograph impresses with excellent legibility, and the respectable 38mm size of its case. These watches are also exceedingly rare, with very few remaining examples spotted by collectors . There is also some controversy on the exact serial number of this watch – the prevailing opinion is that the 2923 engraved on the inner side of the caseback is a parts number , with the actual reference number being either 2547 or 2543, depending whether the case is waterproof or not. The present watch does exhibit a screw back caseback, and was therefore identified on Heuer-dedicated website OnTheDash as an example of the elusive reference 2547. As its layout hints, this watch relies on the Valjoux 72C, C standing for the calendar added to the chronograph complication of a standard Valjoux 72. The case looks unpolished, with sharp edges to its lugs, while the dial only presents minor aging marks.

This rare Heuer Triple Calendar can be found on Ebay, with current bidding close to $7,900.

The Doxa Sub 200 T. Graph is something of a legend in diving circles, and not only for its excellent credentials (diving chronographs being an unusual proposition to begin with). The T. Graph was indeed the watch that astronaut Gene Cernan famously owned , and the advertising for the 200 T. Graph promised nothing less than " perfect timing " to any sportsmen. You might also remember Jason Heaton's article on the Doxa Sub 200 T. Graph Searambler , in which he highlighted the coolness and rarity of these chronographs. The difference between the Searambler and the Sharkhunter (bonus point for those awesome names) lies in the dial color: silver for the former, black for the later . Note that both come with amazing sub-register styling, and that the third version, the Professional, unsurprisingly sports an orange dial, a signature color for Doxa given its outstanding underwater legibility. The manual winding chronograph caliber is correctly described in the listing as a caliber 287 – it's a rebranded version of the reputable Eberhard caliber 310-82, with column-wheel construction and date indicator at 6 o'clock.

This Doxa Sub 200 T. Graph is listed on Ebay for $17,850.

It is hard to not love the 1960s Zenith reference A271 ; its bi-compax dial is beautifully balanced, and it has wonderfully refined applied indexes and a tastefully discreet logo. This chronograph was offered with several different dials, the grey one being a personal favorite of mine. At 38mm, the size is just perfect, and the no-lume configuration allows the wearer to focus on what matters most here: the chronograph complication. The dial of the present example seems well preserved, although some light blemishes can be seen in the hour counter. The crown bears the Zenith logo but this logo is very likely from after the production date of the watch; the star symbol would be expected there given the time period in which the watch was made, so there is little doubt that the current crown is a replacement part. Its chronograph movement, the caliber 146D , stems from a Martel ebauche that Universal Genève also favored; it is said by the seller to have been recently serviced, and comes with a 12-month guarantee.

An Italian dealer listed this Zenith A271 for $3,767.

This Bulova sums up pretty well what the late 1960s and 1970s brought to watchmaking: some very unusual case shapes (some would go so far as to call them funky) but also some beautiful blue dials as evidenced by many Patek Philippes of that time. The blue sunburst dial of this Bulova is undoubtedly its most catchy feature, especially in this reverse panda configuration. As with many vintage Bulova, the date code engraved on the caseback (N0) allow us date the production of this watch to 1970, while the M9 engraving on the 14EB movement bridge points to an assembly in 1969 for the movement. The blue dial is described as near-perfect, and this is confirmed by many macro photos in the listing. The watch comes mounted on a generic Jubilee-style bracelet that looks almost as good as the Kreisler Stelux original piece .

The dealer Watchsteez offers this Bulova 31000 for $900.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Doxa Sub 200 T. Graph Sharkhunter, A Triple Calendar Heuer, A Chronograph Zenith A271, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, we feature three watches that we have already covered at length on HODINKEE, simply because we love them so much. Let's start with the Patek Philippe reference 2526, one of the first automatic Calatravas – and here its enamel dial bears the coveted Tiffany & Co. signature. There is also an early Rolex Milgauss reference 1019, one of the first antimagnetic wristwatches made by Rolex in the 1960s. You will also find a new-old-stock Lip chronograph with blue "Paul Newman" dial. Finally, a stunning coin watch from Piaget rounds out the selection.

After Arthur's review of a modern Milgauss , we had to go back to the classic reference 1019. Introduced in 1963, it was produced until the late 1980s despite relative unpopularity, as its antimagnetic properties proved appealing to a niche (but highly technical) audience. Of those, the CERN ( Conseil Européen pour la Recherche Nucléaire , or European Organization for Nuclear Research) based in Geneva is reported to have specifically asked Rolex to develop the Milgauss for its scientists in the 1950s, resulting initially in the references 6541 and 6543.

The present example shows one of the rare CERN dials, without lume, so as not to influence any radiation measurements that a scientist could undertake with his watch on the wrist. It is also one of the earliest examples made, as evidenced by its Mark 0 dial (with the 1/5th-second tick-marks), an early serial number engraved between the lugs, and the 1/64 date inscribed on the inner side of the reinforced caseback (as it was part of the anti-magnetic inner case that surrounded the automatic caliber 1580). The seller describes the 38mm case as "untouched"; indeed, it presents thick lugs and the expected superficial scratches from regular use.

The Italian dealer Andrea Foffi offers this early Rolex Milgauss reference 1019 for 46,500 Euros (or around $52,000).

You might remember Ben's love letter to the reference 2526 . If not, the watch above will easily show you why he called it "one of the best buys in vintage Patek right now." The 2526 is indeed one of the first automatic wristwatches from Patek, relying on the excellent caliber 12-600AT, which features an incredibly beautiful rotor in 18k gold. No expense was spared on the elegant case, and the dial is grand feu enamel, a delicate process that has a high rate of material loss. Here, this yellow gold example also bears the coveted Tiffany & Co. signature on the dial, which makes it rarer (but of course, adds a sizable premium to its price). The case still shows the gold hallmarks and nice lugs, while the crown correctly bears the characteristic "double P." The dauphine handset is sharp, although it seems to show some light oxidation on its facets. On the wrist, the 35mm diameter wears larger than you would expect.

The dealer The Keystone has this Patek Philippe reference 2526 listed for $75,000.

Recently, I went very deep on this attractive Lip , reaching the conclusion that out of the different configurations found, the one with orange chronograph hands seemed the most correct. Since then I have kept digging and two interesting facts confirmed my findings. First, the reference book on Lip written by Marie-Pia Coustans was updated, and now features a blue chronograph with orange hands (and the original black strap with rally blue checkers). And then this chronograph with black hands just appeared on Ebay, with the seller confirming that it came from an old stock. What is NOS here is not the whole watch, but each of the parts; the French watchmaker who is offering this chronograph purchased the Lip inventory when the company went bankrupt in the 1970s. This explains the outstanding condition of the dial and case, and to me the fact that black chronograph hands are mounted, as those were present in many other chronographs from Lip. And inside you will find a manual-wound Valjoux 7733 that you can expect to be in mint condition as well.

A French watchmaker is offering this blue Lip chronograph on Ebay, with bidding currently above $2,250.

Piaget took advantage of the slim profile of its excellent caliber 9P in many ways: for thin pocket watches, jeweled manchettes, dress watches, and also coin watches. Coin watches were a way for manufacturers to show off their watchmaking and casemaking skills, and were offered by Rolex , Corum, Vacheron Constantin and Piaget, among others. The present watch shows a double case, with hinged Piaget clock inside the carved 1904 $20 coin, the same type that housed the historical Cartier that we had highlighted two years ago . The coin is a respectable 35mm diameter, with a thickness below 4mm – quite an accomplishment, as the 9P movement itself is 2mm thick. The watch is reported to have been manufactured in the 1970s, which seems consistent with other examples auctioned before .

This Piaget coin watch is listed on Ebay at a Buy-It-Now of $5,500, and the seller will also consider lower offers.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Tiffany-Signed Patek Philippe Calatrava Ref. 2526, An Early Rolex Milgauss Ref. 1019, A Blue Lip Chronograph 'Paul Newman,' And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
The watches you see today in Bring A Loupe don't joke around. You can expect to find the aptly named Chronometre Royal from Vacheron Constantin with all its original chronometer certificates. No less precise is the Zenith 40T, a testimony of this manufacture's ability to reach extreme precision. We also bring you a full set Daytona, and a far-from-perfect Universal Geneve Uni-Compax. This is your Bring A Loupe for April 1, 2016.

The dial of this Zenith reveals so much about this chronometer, even quoting the name of its caliber 40T in a very interesting script above the seconds sub-register. Zenith boasts incredible credentials when it comes to chronometer movements, its caliber 135 notably winning the chronometer competition from the Neuchatel Observatory for nothing less than 5 years in a row, from 1950 to 1954. The 40T is of smaller dimensions but displays the same level of quality and precision, through a simple yet extremely efficient construction. The 40T might come in a 33 mm case, but you are looking at a glorious time-only watch of the highest level, as ground-breaking today as it was upon its release in the 1950s.

This small but beautiful chronometer from Zenith was listed for $2,000 on OmegaForums here .

The name "Chronometre Royal" is pretty self-explanatory – Vacheron Constantin aimed at producing a highly precise and legible timepiece, starting with pocket watches in the beginning of the 20th Century, and moving to wristwatches from 1953 onwards. All those timepieces received countless first prizes from the Geneva Observatory and the Neuchatel Observatory, making it sound easy – but bear in mind, usually a master watchmaker would adjust the competing watch for a whole year before submitting it. Here, the reference 4838 with its legendary straight lugs could have a dial in better condition – its indexes are questioned on the Vacheron dedicated forum here – but it offers something truly rare: the original chronometer certificate, describing its "especially good results" at the test through 5 positions. It is not pictured here, but you should also expect to find the Chonometre Royal name proudly engraved on the case back. This complete Chronometre Royal is offered for 18,500 Euros or around $21,000 here , probably accounting for the rarity of the accompanying certificates.

The whole timeline of this Daytona reference 6263 underlines the destiny of this family, from the shadows in a retailer display to a place in the spotlight of the entire watch world. Initially, the Daytona chronographs were slow sellers for Rolex, as clients preferred dressy watches or true divers. Those chronographs had only a very limited following, until Paul Newman strapped one on, and made it cool. Those watches often remained unsold for years, this one for instance was produced around 1980 but wasn't purchased at a retailer until some three years later, something you would never see happening with current models. And this reference 6263 is special on many accounts, the first one being that it comes full set, with the seldom seen original box and papers. It is also nicknamed "Big Red" after the size and color of the Daytona font, here in outstanding condition.

The Key Stone listed this full set "Big Red" Daytona for $55,000 here .

This Uni-Compax is another example of the stunning brushed dials that Universal Geneve was offering to its different lines of chronographs. We had featured the three-register version on several occasions, no later than last week's Bring A Loupe for instance, but this 36 mm two-register is definitely worthy of a mention. Actually, it was recently auctioned for close to $4,500 here so it will be interesting to see where it ends on eBay. Note that the seller indicates the need for a service, the lume dot at 4 o'clock is gone and I am not sure the signed crown is original to the watch. Furthermore, I have strong doubts about its handset, as I would have expected thinner hands for the sub-registers, as displayed in an original ad that I was able to find; that said, the dauphine hands for the hours and minutes are fine. UPDATE: It seems that the metal of the case is incorrectly described in the listing as of "stainless steel" while it is chrome plated, which would even more raise the suspicions on this problematic Universal Geneve, probably coming with a non factory case. The list of issues starts to be really long here...

The eBay listing of this Uni-Compax can be found here ; at the time of publishing bidding was around $3,100.

The Top Time from Breitling remains one of the underdogs in a current market quite infatuated with chronographs. Launched in the 1960s this line was aimed at the youth, trying to appeal to the same notion of speed and adventure that the Heuer Carrera had captured. While the case of the Breitling is more simple than the fantastic Carrera case and its inner faceted lugs, the dials are no less striking, most displaying the catchy panda configuration, with plenty of reverse panda in the design of James Bond's ingenious Geiger counter in Thunderball . Here, the watch offered is a later version of the Top Time as you can see from the straight hands, while the earlier version came with baton hands, and its 35mm case is in stainless steel, more coveted than the many gold-plated ones. Interestingly, instead of the most common tachymeter ring, this watch offers a base 100 scale; this part is considered genuine by hardcore Breitling collectors, it seems it could be ordered at factory prior to the delivery of the watch but it is extremely rare to come across others. I found another example here . This Top Time is offered for 2,499 Euros or around $2,800 on a German forum here .

The beauty of the reference 1578 immediately shines through, a stunning case design with the so-called 'Spider lugs' also found on the illustrious chronograph reference 1579; we covered an extremely rare platinum version here . Let's now focus on the 1578: introduced in 1943, this model proved popular and remained in Patek's catalog until the mid-1960s. It came with a sub register or a central second, and was only cased in yellow gold and rose gold. The watch here is what I consider the most appealing version, with a rose gold case and the center second, less dated than the other combination. Even better, the dial features Breguet numerals, a wonderful touch of fantasy in this killer dress watch. The Italian dealer Casowatches is offering this amazing Patek 1578 here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Chronometers And Chronographs From Rolex, Vacheron Constantin, Universal Geneve, And Many Others

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and happy Friday! After a week's hiatus, allowing the newest novelties from Watches and Wonders to take center stage, your favorite round-up of older novelties from around the web is back and better than ever. You don't have to comment, telling me how much you missed Bring A Loupe; I'll assume every reader wants to, and we don't want to flood the comments!

As for results, the picks from the last edition proved to fall on deaf ears, to be honest. Gus Grissom's Omega Speedmaster remains available at $415,000 , while Neil Armstrong's is up to $1,366,694 at RR Auctions. The Eterna Super Kontiki has been relisted on eBay for $3,900 , but our most accessible pick, the Titus Calypsomatic, sold for a best offer under $500 .

Let's move on, here are this week's picks!

In what I believe is a first for our humble Hodinkee Bring A Loupe column, today we have an exclusive announcement. Rolex Deep Sea Special number 35 has surfaced for sale in London, and you're hearing it here first.

The Rolex Deep Sea Special is a name used for what was essentially the first prototype or experimental deep sea dive watch from Rolex. The Sea-Dweller and Deepsea all start right here with the Deep Sea Special project. Of course, the watch leans heavily on the Oyster case, but it should be noted straight away that this is an experimental watch made to be attached to the outside of a submersible. It is huge and best viewed as a piece of Rolex history rather than something to fill a slot in your watch box. Cue the Indiana Jones, "this belongs in a museum" thoughts. But have no fear—numerous examples of this watch are found in museums such as the Smithsonian and the Beyer Museum in Zurich.

Rolex produced a few versions of the Deep Sea Special throughout the 1950s and 1960s. Ultimately, this project's success came on January 23, 1960, when a Deep Sea Special reached the deepest point in Earth's seabed, the bottom of Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench (10,916 meters), while attached to the outside of the Trieste —a bathyscaphe manned by Swiss oceanographer Jacques Piccard and US Navy lieutenant Don Walsh.

This record-setting 1960 dive was preceded by two Trieste "trial" runs in 1953 and 1956. A Deep Sea Special was also attached to the vessel on those dives. Rolex made slight changes to the design after each dive; ultimately, three Deep Sea Special versions were created, culminating in that 1960 dive.

After the successes of Trieste and the actual Deep Sea Specials, Rolex wanted to get the word out. A run of commemorative Deep Sea Specials was made to the exact same specs as the Mariana Trench watch. The total number of examples produced is not known, as it has never been confirmed by Rolex, but we know of a number 47 owned by Rolex. Whatever the exact number may be, a two-digit production run is extremely, extremely low for the Crown.

Not technically available for retail purchase, the commemorative Deep Sea Specials are believed to have been presented to museums and top Rolex retailers around the world in the mid to late 1960s.

The watch available for sale today is a known and accepted example. Previously owned by Wuppertaler Uhrenmuseum, in, of course, Wuppertal, Germany, Number 35 has been offered at public auction twice before, most recently via Phillips in 2021, where it sold for CHF 1,058,500 . While it's a beast on the wrist, this is a piece of Rolex history. If the three Deep Sea Specials that were actually strapped to the Trieste are similar to Apollo-flown Speedmasters, these commemorative examples are akin to the gold Speedies we looked at from Gus Grissom two weeks ago.

The seller, Adrian, Oscar, and the team at Maunder Watches plan to list this historically important Rolex in the coming days. It will be "Price Upon Request.” Head over to the Maunder website for more details.

If I were to create a list of my personal Patek Philippe "grails," you might find a stainless steel ref. 1518 and other extremely out-of-this-world choices. But possibly the most realistic Patek "grail" of mine is a doré-dialed ref. 3940J, specifically this one, "No. 10" made for Beyer , but just about any example would suffice, I suppose.

Surfacing for sale via a smaller, estate-focused auction house in Detroit, Michigan, this ref. 3940 features that all-important champagne dial tone. "Doré" dials, as they are known on original Patek certificates of the time, are a rarer dial option than the more standard silvered or "white" dials that are typically seen on these yellow gold Patek QPs. As 3940s go, the earlier the production, the better, and it just so happens that these champagne-flavored dials are concentrated in first and second-series examples, only adding to their collectibility. While the dials are typically found in the most collectible production runs and on those earliest Beyer-retailed examples, the flat-out truth is that the doré tone suits the 3940J best. I am typically not a fan of gold-tone dials, but this reference is a notable exception.

This particular watch comes to auction from what we can presume to be either the original owner or a long-time owner. Fresh from a private collection in Bloomfield Hills, Michigan, the watch appears to be consigned by the same owner as an early Aquanaut ref. 5066 — quite the two-watch collection. Per the auction listing, this example is a full set with its original box (peeling on the inside, as is typical) and papers. Important to note are the two hallmarks on the nine o'clock side case flank. These hallmarks are unique to early examples and often, with polishing, completely gone. A similar example with box and papers but polished hallmarks sold for £105,000 in November of 2022 via A Collected Man.

This Patek ref. 3940J is lot 1 of DuMouchelle's April 2025: Day One sale on Thursday, April 17th, at 11:00 PM ET. The estimate is $20,000 to $30,000. Check out the auction listing right here .

​Here is a longtime Hodinkee favorite. In the early 1990s, IWC introduced the Doppelchronograph ref. 3711, a pioneering timepiece that redefined the split-seconds chronograph. With Günter Blümlein at the helm and the technical prowess of Richard Habring, IWC developed a robust and accessible rattrapante mechanism by modifying the Valjoux 7750. This innovation utilized a cam system for both the chronograph and split-seconds functions, departing from the traditional column wheel design. The result was a durable, mass-producible complication that maintained precision while being more affordable than its high-end counterparts. The good news for you (and me) is that while this watch has become a collectible with time, it remains extremely accessible in the pre-owned market.

The ref. 3711 design draws inspiration from IWC's Mark XI pilot's watch, which has a 42mm stainless steel case, domed sapphire crystal, and a soft-iron inner case for magnetic resistance. While we now know IWC's Flieger-style aesthetic well, the 3711 and its commercial success were instrumental in establishing this iconic visual language. It's practical and wearable for a split-second chronograph, making it suitable for everyday wear, while the innovative movement appeals to the nerdiest of enthusiasts.

The seller, Tim, and the team at Subdial in London have listed this IWC 3711 on its website at an asking price of £5,450. You can view the listing in full right here .

I think I have a problem with anonymous vintage watches. When I see a great one in shopping for Bring A Loupe, I can't help but include it. This does cause issues as I like to be quite verbose in my descriptions of each watch, while the reality is we don't know much about these old watches. If a brand didn't put a name on the dial, case, or movement, there just isn't much to dig into. What I can say is, in this period, your standard, middle to low market watch was so well made, with old world techniques, that I don't need to see a brand name to fall in love or understand that what I am looking at is a nice watch.

Here we have a relatively simple, rectangular, "tank" watch with hooded lugs and a case of solid stainless steel — a feature given away by what amounts to our only clue, "edelstahl," German for stainless steel, on the caseback. I'm sure the movement is sufficient and will run within reason for just about any other wristwatch from the 1930s, but the real reason I picked this one is the dial.

If you're a regular reader of this column, you understand my love for glossy gilt dials of the pre-1960s watch world. I recently met with an independent maker who went through the effort of recreating something similar to this technique today. He sent his dials from Switzerland to Japan and back to Switzerland again to have all the necessary steps done to recreate a finish that could even be considered similar to a true vintage gilt dial. It's almost impossible to create today; I'm a fan when a watch is in solid condition and boasts the real deal vintage gloss.

The seller, Mr. A, and the team at Avocado Vintage Watches in Italy have listed this anonymous 1930s tank on its Instagram with an asking price of €1,250. Get all of the details right here .

While catching some steam amongst collectors and dealers alike, Vintage Movado continues to be one of the best buys in all vintage watches. Here, we have a double factor of value as this Movado-made piece is not signed by Movado but rather by E. Gubelin. As one of the largest retailers in Switzerland, Gubelin offered many watches in the vintage period made by other brand names yet signed only with the retailer on the dial, case, and movement. The market for these watches is tough to peg because, yes, this is a Movado, and this is an Audemars Piguet , but many buyers have a hard time understanding that, suppressing prices.

Obviously, a Movado by the dial style and scalloped bridges of the movement, this is a nice little watch in good to great condition for its age. For square-shaped cases, I always look for 25mm in width as a magic number. If over this measurement, it should wear quite nicely on my 7-inch wrist in 2025 without appearing all too "vintage" to those who have no idea what a vintage watch is. By that, I mean I don't want to wear a square watch that is absolutely swimming on my wrist and gives off the impression that I am trying too hard or really going for a certain "look." The reality is I just like watches and don't want people to talk about me behind my back.

An eBay seller in Antibes, France, has listed this Movado-made Gubelin for a Buy It Now or best-offer price of €750. Check it out in full right here .

Slotted here at the bottom of an already long edition of your favorite column, if I went too deep into what makes this Breguet pocket watch important, my editor would have a stern word with me. To put it simply, this Breguet watch was produced while the master, the greatest watchmaker of all time, Abraham-Louis Breguet,, was still at his bench. It features a dial layout that is now immediately recognizable, serving as the direct inspiration for many contemporary Breguet wristwatches. Got it?

This year is Breguet's 250th anniversary. With that, you may see more Breguet watches around the web than is typical. You may not want to bid on this watch right now, but remember it. Remember how many Breguet pocket watches you see in the coming months that are not old enough to have been touched by the master Abraham-Louis, who left the watch world in 1823. Slowly, you might understand just how rare of an opportunity it is to see a real-deal vintage Breuget like this one.

This Breguet is lot 4280 of Cortrie's 267th Auction: Watches sale on Saturday, April 12th, at 10:30 AM ET. The estimate is €40,000 to €60,000. Get all of the details right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Historically Important Rolex Deep Sea, A Doré Dial Patek QP, And A 19th Century Eccentric Breguet

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's Friday once again, which means it's time for your weekly dose of the Internet's vintage watch selection. Since many seem to get a kick out of the less conventional options up for grabs in last week's roundup , I thought I'd try my hand at a sequel of sorts, with a group of hard hitting timepieces that ought to impress. Should sporty be your thing, I've got you covered with a rare Favre Leuba, complete with mountaineering provenance, an under appreciated diver from Vulcain, and a Wakkman triple date chronograph which I believe to be one of the better buys in accessible vintage watch collecting. At the smarter end of the spectrum, there's a rare WOSTEP watch, and the nicest Longines tank I've come across in a while. Buckle up!

Early tank-style watches will forever be held in high regard by yours truly. I've owned a few such small-cased beauties, all of which have been fitted with interesting dials. I find them a true joy to wear. Head turners they are not – in fact, they go completely unnoticed in most instances thanks to their small and discrete case sizes, but if dropped jaws and the inducement of drooling amongst passersby are what you're out for, look elsewhere. This is the kind of watch that's for a select few who will appreciate it immensely. It also ought to bring a smile to your face upon glancing down at it every now and then. I speak from experience, after having been called out as "that guy" at that the table who's curiously smiling down at something glinting on my wrist.

This piece measures 20mm across, and 37mm from top to bottom, but despite the smaller case size, it ought to wear rather well on the wrist. Having addressed that, let’s move onto the main attraction with this one: the stunning two-tone dial with a salmon colored outer portion and an inner section in beige. Combined with gold Roman numerals and a blued steel handset, this is a supremely handsome piece fit for the wrist of a discerning gentleman or lady collector.

While the watch itself is quite attractive to say the least, allow me to direct your attention toward the original box that the seller is including in the sale. It's finished in brilliant green bakelite, with intricate detailing and multiple Longines signatures, sliding open to reveal the watch within. I've seen other bakelite boxes from this era of Longines production, though I can't say I've seen one quite like this before, making it all the more exciting. I used to joke around with a former colleague of mine that I found the boxes, paperwork and vintage watch-associated ephemera more captivating than the watches themselves, and this box might be pushing me over into that camp once again.

An Italian collector named Sandra has listed this piece for sale on the Chronotrader Forum with an asking price of €2,200. Click here for the full scoop.

Everyone likes a watch with a story, and I’m no exception. The story that accompanies this piece, however, is perhaps one of the most badass stories I've had the pleasure of featuring in this column to date. As the heading would indicate, you're looking at a Favre-Leuba Bivouac – a supremely cool watch to begin with, as a result of the incorporated altimeter and barometer complications – with some seriously awesome provenance attached. This watch was once owned by a man named Charles Malfetti, who in 1969 used it to summit the Matterhorn.

On July 28, 1969, Malfetti began climbing the Matterhorn with this very watch on his wrist. Exactly one month later, on August 28, Malfetti successfully summited the mountain range. This provenance has been confirmed by the auction house offering the watch for sale, and along with the watch they will be including a framed set of documents including a Western Union telegram sent after Malfetti achieve his summit, along with a signed and dated ascension card and various photographs taken throughout the rigorous journey.

What's more, is the watch itself still remains in stunning shape, with a dial that appears to be flawless and a sharp-looking case. The condition of the bezel is also quite good. Bivouacs are often seen with heavily scuffed bezels, making this one a rarity. One last thing to note is that the Bivouac was the very first mechanical wristwatch offered with a built-in aneroid barometer upon its release in 1962, making it somewhat of a significant piece within the world of obscure sporting complications. You'd be hard pressed to find a more compelling example of this Favre-Leuba.

Skinner Auctioneers of Marlborough, Massachusetts, is offering this Bivouac for sale in their auction of clocks, watches, and scientific instruments taking place today (April 12, 2019), with an estimate of $2,000 to $3,000. While this is admittedly short notice, the watch will come up later in the sale, so there's still time to bid. Click here to see more.

This next watch comes from the same sale as the Favre-Leuba above, though it's a radically different piece. In fact, this watch was at no point ever offered for sale as a commercial product. Its dial is signed WOSTEP, indicating that it can be traced back to the same Neuchâtel school from which modern titans of watchmaking royalty graduated, including Stephen Forsey, Kari Voutilainen, and brothers Bart and Tim Grönefeld. The list goes on and on.

For those not familiar with the school, here's a primer. WOSTEP stands for the Watchmakers of Switzerland Training and Educational Program, and it was founded in 1966 with the goal of training Americans in the art fine of Swiss watchmaking. At the time, the United States's title as the top producer of watches in the world was being endangered by the Soviet Union's efforts in the field, thus motivating government officials to collaborate with the Swiss in a nationalistic attempt to further educate and create the generation of great watchmaking minds. Since its founding, the school is less focused on the goal of revitalizing the American watchmaking industry, and is now simply one of the finest institutions at which one can learn this age-old craft.

My bet is that this piece likely would've been produced by a student of the Swiss watchmaking school in an effort to demonstrate their abilities towards the end of their training. I'd also add that whoever is behind this piece did an excellent job. Nice going, dude! Although the complicated timepiece sits in a gold plated case, it’s still an interesting watch to say the least, given its connection to an institution dedicated to furthering the future of watchmaking.

The estimate on this triple date moonphase has been set conservatively at $500 to $700, so maybe there's a good deal to be had here. Find the full listing and the rest of the catalog here.

Having made mention of the uniquely complicated Bivuoac, I thought we might as well keep the weird-complication train rolling with the inclusion of another similarly idiosyncratic piece. In its more traditional forms, the Vulcain Cricket can represent one of the better values on the market, in my opinion. These watches are equipped with alarm functions, have a documented Presidential past that ought to excite any history buff, and are simply great looking. Plus, Captain Kangaroo himself wore one, so there's that. Despite all this, they can still be had for relative deals. With that said, this is no traditional Vulcain Cricket – instead, it's what many regard to be the alarm watch in its most impressive form.

This is a Vulcan Cricket Nautical, which is without question one of my all-time favorite sports watches of the 1960s. Like a standard Cricket, the Cricket Nautical is equipped with the same chiming alarm function, but on this piece it serves a different purpose. Back in 1960, this wasn’t the watch you’d use to keep track of your parking meter or an important upcoming meeting, but instead when you'd need to ascend to the surface of a body of water, as the name of the watch would suggest, without getting the bends . Aiding in this process are the decompression charts found on the surface of the dial and just below (the latter are revealed as the central portion rotates). I personally believe this to be one of the most fascinating dial designs of the mid-century, and the added functionality only makes it more irresistible.

The present example is being offered in excellent condition, with evenly aged luminous compound in its hands and on the surface of the dial. On all too many Cricket Nauticals, the green outer scale will fade to an indistinguishable tone, but on this one it's bright and clearly visible, which is certainly appreciated. You just don’t see many watches with green details on them, making this already rare watch even more appealing. Should you be in search of a storied sports watch that’s a bit off the beaten path, I can't think of a better option.

The Miami-based dealer Matthew Bain has this example in his inventory, and is offering it with an asking price of $20,000. I'd argue that it's a far more exotic watch than a lot of other sports watches in its price bracket, and one that offers a lot of enjoyment pound-for-pound. Check out the watch here.

To wrap things up for the week, we've got a watch I’ll often use to gauge exactly where the market for obscure vintage chronographs is at. It's a fairly common triple date chronograph manufactured by Wakmann, but not all examples of this watch were created equal by any means. Certain case and dial variants are much more sought after than others. I can remember the days when these could be had at G-Shock–like prices, but sadly those days are long gone. Still, they represent a decent value and indicate that the vintage chronograph market is strong and steady.

On some of these triple date Wakmann chronographs of the 1960s, you'll find a case that's a bit uninspiring and flat to the eye, but that's nowhere to be found on this example. Instead, this one features the more curvaceous lug shape that has come to define the more desirable class of these Wakmann chronographs. Factor in the "reverse-panda" dial, contrasting red chronograph hand, and moon-shaped date indication, and you’ve got a recipe for wrist-mounted success.

You'll also notice that this watch is powered by the Valjoux 72C, which as you might now is based upon the same caliber found in iconic chronographs like the Rolex Daytona and Heuer Autavia, among others. The C that follows the 72 indicates its additional degree of complication, which in this case is a day, month, and date indication. All of this is contained within an unpolished case, complete with the original crown. For the money, it's definitely a lot of watch.

An eBay seller based in Chandler, Arizona, that goes by the monicker "bobothealligatorboy" (alrighty then!) has this piece listed with an asking price of $4,300. You also have the option to make an offer, so you might as well. See the watch here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A 1960 Vulcain Cricket Nautical, A Longines Tank With A Bakelite Box, And A WOSTEP-Made Triple Date Moonphase

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week we're focusing on some memorable watches from the 1970s, starting with the iconic stainless steel Royal Oak from Audemars Piguet – a watch which, with an original price tag higher than that of contemporary gold dress watches, truly created the sport-luxury category. We've also found a Beta 21, the very first Swiss quartz caliber, here housed in a Piaget reference 14101 with a striking tiger's eye dial. The Zenith A3637 shows how it earned its "Big Lemon" nickname, while the Favre-Leuba Deep-Blue proves again that bright colors can really work in a diving watch. This is your Bring A Loupe for April 14, 2017.

Strictly speaking, the Favre-Leuba Deep-Blue is not a watch from the 1970s since it was launched in 1966, but its aesthetics announce the next decade in many ways. The bright orange color scheme might be one such indication, but it is truly the squarish 43mm case that's the biggest hint of the '70s. The use of orange is common in many divers as it is a very legible color underwater, and the 500-meter water resistance also explains the Aqualung logo on the dial. The Deep-Blue relies on the high-beat automatic caliber FL1164, which offers the day-date complication highlighted by the black date discs that keep to the color scheme of the minute track. The black hands maintain the same legibility against the orange dial, and their distinctive shape is designed to avoid any confusion in reading the elapsed time on the rotating bezel during a dive. Originally, the watch came on a bracelet, which is not included here.

You can find this this Favre-Leuba Deep-Blue offered on Instagram for $4,000 or best offer.

Launched in 1972, the Royal Oak remains one the most emblematic watches of the decade, if not of the century. This lasting legacy testifies to the talent of its designer, Gerald Genta, who was hired to come up with a watch that would bring new attention to Audemars Piguet at a time when quartz was the cool newcomer. And the Royal Oak definitely succeeded; the watch has had great success, right down to today. It also introduced the idea of a luxury (and very expensive) sports watch in steel, a category that Patek Philippe would join in 1976 with the Nautilus, which was born from the very same designer. As can be seen on the caseback, the watch here belongs to the A-series, which is the first batch of 2,000 watches produced, and the most coveted by collectors. These can also be recognized by the AP logo placed at six o'clock; the logo would eventually be moved to 12 o'clock in the later C-series. The crown is correctly unsigned; an AP logo there would have indicated a service part. The case and dial show a bit of aging but this is to be expected, and wear is even throughout. Similarly, you should expect some stretch from the original integrated bracelet. It is photographed in the listing in an Audemars Piguet box, but this is not the original box.

The Keystone just listed this A-Series Royal Oak for $39,500.

You might remember the history of the Beta 21 movement from our previous Quartz Week . It was the first commercial quartz caliber made in Switzerland, developed and adopted by all the big names in watchmaking, from Rolex to Audemars Piguet to IWC. Piaget was of course included, as the company was involved extremely early on with the CEH (Centre Electronique Horloger), which was in charge of the research. Beta 21 movements offered great accuracy but they were definitely on the chubby side, which explains the big profile of the cases housing them, as can be seen on this Piaget – far from the thin dress watches that the company produced in the previous decade. The reference 14101 is undoubtedly the most common Piaget to feature the Beta 21 (it was the one presented in Basel in 1970) but other shapes existed, as a watch formerly owned by Andy Warhol testifies. The multi-stepped case aims at giving a more dressy feel to the massive case (measuring 41mm x 33mm) in 18kt gold as you would expect from Piaget (the brand had sworn to only use precious materials for its cases since the mid-1950s). The tiger eye dial is very much in line with the vibrant designs of the time, and seems free of any cracks or fractures. Note that the quartz caliber was recently serviced, and is said to work flawlessly (here it is often called the XP4 following Piaget denomination but it is indeed a Beta 21). Last but not least, the box and papers accompanying the watch are described as original, which would make for a nice full set.

A German dealer presents this this Beta 21 Piaget for €6,995 (approximately $7,430 at time of publishing) on Ebay; he will also consider lower offers.

This MultiChron is everything we love in Gallet chronographs, and more. It has the stylish simplicity that means great legibility, even considering the addition of a triple date complication. This watch is powered by the caliber Valjoux 72C, the C standing here for calendrier (calendar in English) indicated in the two small apertures, and on the periphery of the dial. Initially introduced in 1946 , the MultiChron Calendar was produced through the 1970s, the current piece belonging to the early production from the 1950s. True to its elegant style, the case is made from solid yellow gold, and seems extremely well preserved (the lugs are still thick and sharp, and the hallmarks on the caseback deep). The dial is equally nice, without any noticeable imperfection, and a very bright date track. The provenance is also great, as it is said to have come from a former executive at Gallet USA, who definitely knew how to take care of his watches.

Vesper & Co has this beautiful Gallet MultiChron Calendar for $7,950.

The bright yellow of the Zenith reference A3637 puts the orange of many other divers to shame, including all the other configurations that Zenith came up with for this reference. Its bold looks easily earned it the catchy "Big Lemon" nickname, another reason to love this watch. However, this 43mm piece is not only about looks; it was guaranteed to be water-resistant up t0 1,000 meters . It relies on the automatic caliber 2542PC, which is known for its reliability. The date was placed between four and five o'clock, a characteristic of the Zenith watches of that time, as is the triangular minute hand, which is found in many other contemporary sport Zeniths. It comes with the original Gay Frères bracelet, which shows a production date sometime in 1970. It seems this diver always came with a GF bracelet – either the ladder one found on the El Primero or the fully folded one included here.

The dealers Davidoff Brothers posted this this Zenith Diver A3637 on their website for 4,800 CHF (approximately 4,770).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Some Funky 1970s Vibes With An Audemars Piguet Royal Oak Reference 5402 'A-Series,' A Piaget Beta 21, A Zenith 'Big Lemon,' And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, we'll look at a wide range of chronographs, from an exceptional Rolex Paul Newman Daytona to a (alas) very fake Breitling Top Time. You will also see a very seductive Heuer Autavia, a tough Movado Sub Sea, and an unusual Omega De Ville. And because there is a world outside of chronographs, you will discover a Grand Seiko dress watch with a sumptuous blued handset. This is your Bring A Loupe for April 15, 2016.

Early Autavias are among the most coveted Heuers and you can see an outstanding example from 1964 here, offering the early screw-back case instead of the later and bulkier compressor case. The Autavia reference 2446 is particularly famous for being worn by the Formula 1 driver Jochen Rindt – his wife, the Finnish model Nina Rindt, also notoriously sported a Universal Geneve Compax . The Autavia reference 2446 here came earlier than Mr. Rindt's version; both present a second execution dial with smaller sub-register than the first execution, but the handset retains a dauphine shape, while it would later feature batons. In short, this is truly an enchanting chronograph where the whole is magically greater than the sum of the parts, including the ubiquitous Valjoux 72 that takes care of the chronograph function.

You can find this stunning Autavia offered here , and the seller indicated that he is considering offers above $30,000.

The qualifier "rare" may be thrown around a bit loosely when it comes to vintage Rolex, but for this Paul Newman this could not be more accurate. On its own, the reference 6264 is not common; it was only produced for a short while in the early 1970s, but add to that a 18k yellow-gold case AND a Paul Newman dial, and you get the ultimate grail watch for most Rolex collectors. There is something superb about the pump pushers of this reference, dressier than the more imposing screw-down pushers that followed. As for the dial itself, it features the seldom-seen white font in the sub-registers, affording a higher legibility than the more common gilt script. This is one hell of a Rolex, the type that does not stay available for long.

Matthew Bain is offering this exceptional Daytona PN here .

This Movado belongs to the tough and cool crowd, with its 41 mm Compressor-style case and the very attractive bezel. The Super Sub Sea was offered both by Zenith and Movado, a sign of the strong partnership between those two brands. At first, they used the Zenith movement M95, but then turned to the 146-HP, the last manual-wound chronograph movement that Zenith released before the launch of the automatic El Primero. You can actually guess which movement powers the chronograph from the sub-register layout – those with the 146-HP being further apart (as it can be seen here). A vast combination of handsets have been found over the years and this Zenith-Cairelli style second hand is now accepted as correct for this reference; I would just have expected the chronograph hour hand to be blued, but the pictures make it hard to tell if this is the case.

While eBay listings from Thailand offer their fair share of questionable pieces, this Movado looks lovely and can be found here . At the time of publishing bidding was just above $3,500.

Integrating a date window into a chronograph is no small challenge, as it often throws off the whole balance of the dial. Heuer offered some interesting solutions with the Carrera "Dato" reference 3147, first offering a date at 12 o'clock, and later at 9 o'clock. Their later iteration took out the left register to make a clean spot for the date window, while this Omega combined both – quite successfully I would argue. This Omega reference 146.017 houses the caliber 930, also used in the visually striking Bullhead that Omega still offers to this day – we reviewed a limited edition here . This vintage De Ville comes with a a lovely Omega jubilee bracelet, something you see very rarely with those chronos, and it actually does not seem that stretched. One minor detracting point, the dial shows its age with a bit of spotting, some faded parts on the scale, and missing tritium dots.

This uncommon Omega is listed for 3,300 Euros or around $3,700 here .

This Grand Seiko might be less of a crowd favorite than the rest of our selections, but to me it is nonetheless a great head turner, thanks to its incredible handset. Those are among the nicest blued hands I ever came across, but this is not all this watch offers. The dial also presents quite a show with the contrasting applied golden numerals with matching script and an expansive dotted minute track that you usually find in the most coveted vintage Patek. The 36 mm case offers a nice balance, more traditional and subdued than some of the more angular design that made GS famous. The finishing of the hand-wound movement remains fairly austere, as Grand Seiko usually puts the reliability of its calibers over any decoration.

This rare Grand Seiko is offered for $6,000 or your best offer on a forum here .

This Top Time is so bad, it is almost funny. This is the type of watch where it takes longer to look for the correct parts than listing the obvious issues. Here, the dial is horrendous: the sub-registers are incorrect, Top Time is incorrectly spelled (it should not come with a dash), and so is the lume mention without the T after Swiss Made. And the list could go on, knowing that the chronograph hands, the inner track, and the crown are fantastically wrong too. The only correct thing here is the case, and it is not even in great condition. This phenomenally franken Breitling can be found on eBay here , with bidding already above $500. Don't have the HODINKEE App yet? Get years of amazing watch content plus new stories, breaking news, and access to great new features like HODINKEE Live, free on iOS .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Gold Rolex Paul Newman Daytona, A Gorgeous Heuer Autavia, A Very Fake Breitling, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! After highlighting a "Price On Request" Rolex Deep Sea Special last week, I've done all I can to focus this week's picks around value. That's not to say there are no "expensive" watches here—of course there are—but at each asking price, I feel a lot of watches are being offered. We have everything from neo-vintage classics to the first Patek Calatrava, with 1950s Rolex and Movado along the way.

But first, results! While good news these days is hard to come by, I'm happy to report some more than solid showings from last edition's picks. In Detroit, the estate-fresh and doré-dialed Patek ref. 3940 sold for $156,250 — by my count, a public auction record for a second series doré 3940. The Abraham-Louis Breguet-era "eccentric" Breguet pocket watch, a real piece of watchmaking history, hammered for €85,910 . And our humble Movado-made Gübelin found a new home on eBay for a best offer below €750 — congrats to the new owner!

Onward! Here are this week's picks.

Chopard's watchmaking reinvention and successes of the 1990s deserve a lot more attention. Karl-Friedrich Scheufele's repositioning of Chopard with the caliber 1.96 in 1996 and other offerings like this Luna D'Oro was remarkable. And the brand's crafting of the L.U.C collection as a premium option within an already premium brand was groundbreaking. So groundbreaking, in fact, that now, decades later, larger brands are trying to copy Chopard's model (but that's a story for another time).

The Luna D'Oro is a 36mm in diameter automatic perpetual calendar option with an interesting retrograde date at 12 o'clock. When Chopard made this watch, the competition was quite thick. Patek Philippe, Audemars Piguet, Blancpain, Breguet, and Vacheron Constantin all made an automatic perpetual calendar. But that's what this time period was all about for Chopard—making watches that were on the level of that list of brands, associating Chopard with that list, and not being afraid of that competition.

As a watch rather than a brand-building tool, the Luna D'Oro is gorgeous, especially in platinum like this one. The case has shades of Laurent Ferrier in its shape, but has few details like the crown guards and crown-shaped calendar correctors that lean more sporty than classy elegance. It's a balance that is well struck. The movement is cool too, Svend Andersen, of Andersen Genève, developed the calendar module, and the base is a LeCoultre cal. 888.

The seller, Ben at Watch Brothers London, is asking £19,450. See his great photos and contact him here .

Considering what you would pay for one of those competitors' automatic perpetual calendars in platinum, the sub-20k pound asking price here is beyond reasonable.

Early Parmigiani Fleurier watches are among the best made from the neo-vintage period. In the 1990s, with this whole movement happening in Switzerland, reviving traditional, ultra-well-made watchmaking, Michel Parmigiani set up a brand, made some fantastic watches, and changed an entire small Swiss town. As traditional watchmaking fell out of favor decades ago, Fleurier, a town built on horology, felt the pain. Parmigiani was established, and Mr. Parmigiani successfully convinced Chopard to move to the town, which revived the area a few years later. It's an amazing story , and the watches produced in these first few years are so good.

The brand was established in 1996, and the first model was the Memory Time, a GMT, but by 1998, the Toric Chronograph was added to the catalog. It utilizes a Zenith cal. 400z El Primero ébauche modified and finished in the Parmigiani workshops, features a gorgeous case with hand knurling, and has an engine-turned "barleycorn" guilloché dial, and was good enough for the King of the United Kingdom . In platinum with a black dial, this is the ultimate spec for this model. It's in the Bonham's catalog at an extremely attractive estimate, too — have at it.

This Parmigiani Fleurier is lot 22 of Bonhams New York Watches online sale ending on Thursday, April 24th, at 12:00 PM ET. The estimate is $5,000 - $7,000. Check it out in full right here .

An entirely different waffle-textured dial than the new Land-Dweller, this Oyster Perpetual, made in either 1952 or 1953, is special in a few ways. Firstly, the reference. If you fire up Google and punch in "Rolex ref. 6098" you're going to find some truly spectacular watches. I'm talking about half a million dollar star or "Galaxy" dials and "Pre-Explorer" examples worn by Sir Edmund Hillary and other early Everest explorers . In the 1950s, this was Rolex's do everything, be anything 36mm Oyster Perpetual—the brand used it for everything, both common and special.

While the one we have here probably sits more towards the common end of the 6098 spectrum, it is still special and far more accessible. The honeycomb or waffle-textured dial, which collectors tend to use the terms somewhat interchangeably, is clean and devoid of any luminous material. Which, in this case, is intentional rather than a condition flaw. Without lume in the hands, the dial has not been exposed to the radiation that would come with it, resulting in a very even patina throughout that matches the yellow gold case quite well.

Interestingly, the serial number of this watch, 917,XXX, sits within the same range as those incredibly rare and uber-expensive Galaxy dials I previously mentioned and linked out to. For under $20,000, you can own a watch that is not only great on its own but was made alongside one of the most collectible vintage Rolex oddities.

The seller, Michael Morgan of Iconic Watch Company in California, has listed this Rolex on his website with a price of $19,500. Get all of the details right here .

Patek's original Calatrava—before it was known as the Calatrava—is the reference 96, which was produced for over 40 years, from 1932 to 1973. As with any area of vintage watch collecting, typically the earlier the production, the better. But with the 96, outside of special dials or case metals, condition really does top everything else. This is a small, 31.5mm in diameter, simple watch. With that, the general tendency is to focus on the condition. And while I can sometimes disagree with the overall hyper-fixation on perfect, perfect condition, for the 96, I wholeheartedly support collectors waiting for a really nice one.

This example is just that—really nice. It's not the oldest version of this reference, as it was produced in the 1960s, and no, it's not a special variant either. In fact, it's probably the most common. But the condition is very, very nice. The "short signature" dial looks, from the photos at least, to be excellent with no major spots or flaws. Patek signatures are hard to examine from afar, especially when a small auction house takes sub-par photos, but I would take the gamble here as it looks clean. Notably, the case condition is super strong. Just look at that hallmark on the case flank and the general shape of the lugs. As a smaller watch, you want to find an example with nice, full lugs to get the most out of the original design.

I'm not a huge fan of this vintage 14k yellow gold bracelet, but it is still a positive. If you're like me and hate the look, you can take it off, fit your favorite strap, and sell the bracelet to reduce your cost. Boom, you just bought a vintage Patek for a discount.

This Patek ref. 96 is lot 67 of Ahlers & Ogletree Inc.'s Fine Jewelry, Watches & Luxury Accessories sale on Thursday, April 24th, at 10:00 AM ET. The estimate is $10,000 to $14,000. Check out the auction listing right here .

A vintage Movado on Bring A Loupe, what a surprise, right? Yet, this is a special one. Last October , I featured a similar Tiffany & Co.-stamped Movado on offer via the estate auction website Everything But The House, and it's haunted me ever since. It was the nicest Movado for Tiffany I had ever come across. I decided not to bid as I was a bit overindexed on Movado at the time, and, ultimately, a friend of mine here in New York won the auction. However, it must be said that the watch is so good, annoyingly good, really.

Anyway, chasing that dragon, here is a very similar watch on eBay with some notable differences. First off, we have the same Movado cal. 431 full-rotor automatic movement, one of Movado's best time-only movements, and the culmination of the brand's early automatic winding development. However, the dial here is one I have come across on other Kingmatics of the time. It is simple yet elegant, with nice pearling on the minute track. Compared to the watch sold in October, I believe this was made a couple of years later, probably closer to 1960, as the dial design is more 60s than 50s. The main difference with this watch is the case.

This 14k yellow gold case is stamped inside with a Movado USA logo, meaning it was made here in the States, a common practice at the time. The movement and dial were shipped to the States for casing as a tax workaround. Sometimes, the US-made cases are of poor quality (particularly with Rolex, to be honest), but this specific manufacturer, Lapwell Watch Case Company of Greenvale, NY, did excellent work. Lapwell designed and manufactured cases for Longines, Omega, LeCoultre, and others. If you run into a vintage US-made case, check for the circle L hallmark.

An eBay seller out of Dumont, NJ has listed this Movado for a buy it now price of $2,625 or best offer (make an offer, I think $2k would buy it). The full listing is right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Neo-Vintage Genius From Chopard And Parmigiani, A Waffle-Dialed Rolex, And A Patek 96

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's Friday, and a good one at that. This week, we've got a bit of a heavy-hitter, sport watch theme going, with a retailer-signed ref. 3700 Nautilus, a CK2915-3 Speedmaster being offered for sale on eBay, and the nicest Navitimer you'll see in a good long while. To keep things interesting, there's an Oyster Perpetual from the late 1950s with a bit of a rare twist, along with an equally rare LeCoultre for those who appreciate a smaller timepiece. I wouldn't blame you for glossing past this intro and jumping straight into this week's roundup, but if you were waiting for my word – commence reading!

On the surface, this looks like your standard fare, run-of-the-mill, early Oyster. It's cased in stainless steel, and measures 34mm across, affording it a more subtle edge on the wrist over larger Oysters. Though while most prefer the 36mm variants of the bivalve mollusk-inspired timepieces – myself included– I'd recommend taking a closer look into the this chapter of Oyster production, as the offered dial and hand combinations are more often than not far more interesting than their ever so slightly larger cousins. This example surely supports this notion, as evidenced by the presence of luminous dauphine style hands, along with the unique arrowhead indices found at three, six, and nine o'clock. But take an even closer look, and you'll find that this Ref. 6564 is far from run-of-the-mill.

You might've already taken note of this example's special sauce, so to speak, but if you're waiting for me to clue you in, allow me to do just that. On most other examples of this reference, you'll find the words "OFFICIALLY CERTIFIED CHRONOMETER" printed neatly above the six o'clock marker. On this example, there's a bit of a Deutschland twist, in that instead of the traditional declaration of certification, we see those same words printed in German, reading "AMTLICH GEPRÜFTER CHRONOMETER." Before you say it, I know what you’re thinking. Big whoop! It’s got some German instead of the usual English fare. While this is plainly true, it's also a serious rarity.

Given the vast number of nations in which Rolex retailed their watches, day and date wheels in foreign languages are seen rather often. However, dials printed with foreign languages are an altogether different story. Knowing the mass-produced nature of Rolex watches, it made little sense for the brand to print dials with market specific text, but it did still occur every now and then. This watch dating back to 1957 is proof, and a fine example of such to boot. I'm not usually one for heavily patinated dials, but the even, pseudo-salmon tone on this one is rather pleasing.

San Francisco's vintage Rolex specialist H.Q. Milton is offering this Oyster for $4,550. Find the full listing here.

As far as I'm concerned, there's only one Nautilus reference worth putting on your wrist. That is of course is the OG Ref. 3700. While the 5711 is nice and all, the 3700 is just an infinitely better looking and feeling watch. What's more, is its place in the brand's history is far more historically significant being the first Nautilus, as opposed to the Yeezy-esque reputation of hype driven flip potential that the 5711 enjoys in today's market. Not convinced? Just try one on. They really are that good, and Patek really did get it right the first time.

During a quick browse of upcoming auction catalogues, I was delighted to come across an honest looking example that's not only coming direct from the original owner, but also includes the coveted cork box. If that wasn't enough to pique your interest, focus your attention on the dial. Though it does show signs of its age, consistent with other examples on the market, you’ll find a Gübelin signature, indicating it was originally sold by the Swiss retailer of jewelry and watches. The disassembled photos of the watch are also an interesting detail worth reviewing should you not be familiar with how the original Nautilus is constructed.

I also have a bit of a personal connection to this exact Nautilus variant, that left a bit of a bad taste in my mouth after I once tried to purchase one. Key word – tried. While purchasing a Ref. 1675 GMT Master from a local jeweler, I found a Gübelin signed ref. 3700 also in their inventory at a very attractive price, that was by no means in line with its market value at the time. Being my opportunistic self, I quickly made it known to the jeweler that I'd take it as well, which must’ve spooked them as I was told I'd have to come back the next week. Sure enough, the weekend passed, and upon calling them up, I was informed the price had jumped up by some $20,000. Moral of the story – keep a comical amount of deal-closing cash on you at all times. Actually you know what, don't. That could go really wrong.

Cortrie Spezial Auktionen of Hamburg will offer this example of the ref. 3700 on May 4 with an estimate of €40,000 to €80,000. Click here for the full scoop .

There are certain watches you fully expect to come across during a routine eBay hunt. Submariners, sure. Funky, obscure chronographs, you betcha. The occasional decent Daytona, why not. But a ref. 2915 Speedmaster? Not exactly. Late last week I was doing my usual daily scour of the online auction marketplace, and to say this watched jumped out and caught my eye would be an understatement. From the watch, to the listing, to the seller’s reputation, everything checks out and then some. It's just not the caliber of watch you see everyday on eBay.

As its reference number would suggest, this is the third iteration of the original Speedmaster reference. Within the lineage, the 2915-3 is a transitional reference of sorts, seeing as it ushered in the use of black anodized aluminum bezels that would go on to become an iconic, defining trait of all Speedmaster references to follow. While the "-3" is regarded as the most common of all 2915 references, it's worth acknowledging that production numbers across the all reference's variants were notably limited to begin with. This contrasts most other references that followed, as the Speedmaster grew increasingly popular.

Condition wise, it's a decent example. The dial looks to be in reasonable shape, with evenly aged applications of luminous compound, and only minor spotting. Though the original bezel is a bit worn, and the broad arrow hands have been re-lumed at some point, this has been fully disclosed in the listing by the seller. Transparency is the name of the game.

This Speedmaster is currently listed on eBay with no reserve by a seller based out of Los Angeles. At the time of publishing, the highest stands just over $50,000. Get in on the action right here.

As of late, I've been on the hunt for a 33 mm, time-only watch for a good friend of mine with particularly small wrists. He knows who he is. In the process, I was reminded of a host of watches I had always been aware of, but hadn't given the degree of consideration they so deserve. Should you be open to the idea of wearing something that isn't sized like something you’d buy new from a retailer today, there's a whole world of horological awesomeness out there, I assure you. With this in mind, I thought I'd highlight one of my favorite sports timepieces that measures on the smaller side. Readers, LeCoultre Quartermaster. LeCoultre Quartermaster, readers. Now that you’re acquainted let's dive a little deeper.

With a dial that displays the time using a 24 hour system, the Quartermaster was originally intended as a military style timepiece upon its release in the late 1950s. To the best of my knowledge, examples were never officially issued by any military outfit. Regardless, it remains as one of the more captivating dial designs in the history of Jaeger-LeCoultre watches delivered to the US market, and an especially rare one, too. The general consensus is that somewhere in the range of 2,500 examples of the Quartermaster were ever produced across the various range of references it was existed as, each differentiated by unique case designs.

While I'm not absolutely certain, I'd hazard a guess that the white dial variants were produced in fewer numbers than the examples fitted with gilt dials. I say this because in my years of hunting down vintage watches, I've simply seen far more gilt dial Quartermasters than those with white dials. Each has its own appeal, but in the case style seen on the ref. 2405, I tend to prefer it with the white dial. This example looks to be a nice one, with a sharp case, and a clean dial that only has a bit of radium burn, likely caused by an extended period of storage. If your wrists are up to the challenge of pulling it off, there's a lot to like with this one.

An eBay seller based out of San Clemente, California has this watch listed in an auction that ends on Saturday evening. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at $818. Additional photos and details can be found here .

I've said it once, I've said it again, and I'll say it once more. Come to think of it, I'm pretty sure I've used that line before, too. The original Breitling Navitimer is an impossibly cool chronograph, and if you're not already hip to the ref. 806, I'd highly recommend changing that. Between their oversized without going overboard dimensions, detail-rich, slide rule equipped dials, and high contrast facets, it's easy to get lost in this one. Just be sure to show those around you what it is you're staring down at for extended periods of time, so as to not be deemed an impatient, clock-watching nitwit.

There's an Instagram-based dealer by the name of Rob Cooper, and I’m not sure how he does it, but the man seems to get more top-tier, early Navitimer examples than anyone else. If Navitimers are your thing, I'd say a follow is in order. With that said, he seems to have done it again with the discovery of what might be one of the best ref. 806s I've seen to date. This example features the "small bead" bezel, with 98 beads to be exact. Fun fact for you, that would likely be interpreted as the nerdiest thing ever by any non-reader of the site – Navitimers can be dated by the numbers of beads that make up their rotating bezel. The count of 98 would confirm that this piece dates back to 1957.

It's been described as being in "time capsule" condition, which I'd agree with wholeheartedly. I mean, just look at it. The case is undoubtedly unpolished, with sharp lines and well-defined edges, plus the dial looks to be flawless. The really don't get much better than this, unless you have access to a time travel machine, in which case call me!

This example is being offered for $16,000. Click here for more information.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A 1959 Omega Speedmaster Ref. CK2915-3, A 1957 Breitling Navitimer AOPA Ref. 806, And A 1979 Patek Philippe Nautilus Ref. 3700/1 Gübelin

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Let's start this week with a big name: the Rolex 'Paul Newman' Daytona, easily one of the most coveted in vintage watches. The one we have is not only in nice condition, but comes full set directly from the original owner, according to the auction catalog. So we can expect some heated action in a Chicago auction room next week. We have also rounded up a great Eberhard Scientigraf, and a blue Universal Genève Compax, among other picks. This is your Bring A Loupe for April 21, 2017.

The Scientigraf got more than an honorable mention in Phil's love letter to Eberhard , and for good reason. It is a true tool watch, with winning looks thanks to its smooth bezel and Explorer-type dial. Launched in the early 1960s, the Scientigraf was a real competitor to the Rolex Milgauss, the IWC Ingenieur, and the Omega Railmaster when it came to anti-magnetic properties. This ability was proudly engraved on the caseback (antimagnetic resistance was stated by Eberhard as 900-1000 gauss) as was the fact that it uses an automatic movement.

This 38mm watch comes here with its original extendable bracelet, signed Eberhard on the clasp. This handset is absolutely correct for the reference (including the lollipop second hand), while the signed crown seems consistent with the shape shown in the original catalog. One caveat, it seems very likely that its dial underwent an expert restoration , not disclosed in the listing. The watch comes with its original guarantee, which is probably how the seller dates it back to 1964.

An Italian dealer offers this Eberhard Scientigraf for €25,000 (approximately $26,815 at time of publishing).

The "Paul Newman" dial is such an iconic feature that it has its very own Reference Points article. There you can find some info on the transitional reference 6262 – with pump pushers, it very much looks like the reference 6239, except that it relies on a more recent evolution of the Valjoux 72, the caliber Valjoux 727. It was only produced in 1970-1971, which is consistent with the 2.5M serial number of this example (the listing dates it to 1968, which is tad too early for such a serial number). However, there is much more to this chronograph than Rolex geek points: as mentioned in this article from Watchpatina (where the photos also originated) it comes with a fascinating provenance and accessories. It is described as "full set," meaning that the original box and papers are included, although the guarantee was almost left blank , without any matching serial number and only the 6239 reference, which is an issue in establishing full correctness of such a set.
