It's one hell of a watch, chock full of iconic style and rugged reliability, but as previously mentioned, it's an absolute deal considering the prices they currently trade at. Having said that, the true "better deal" is perhaps found by going just a few years and pages prior in the back catalogue. This brings us to our next pick of the week, which as you can probably already tell is a terrific example of the Ref. 1003 Oyster Perpetual. Curb your drooling for one second while we break down what makes this one special.

As you might've already caught wind of in the heading, this example dates back to 1960, during an ineffably tasteful period of Rolex dial production. Despite having rather bold indices, and dauphine-shaped luminous hands, the watch retains a quiet elegance that I'd ultimately attribute to the fact that its dial is silver. Speaking of its dial, the aesthetic-making role of the dial's text shouldn’t go unmentioned. The slightly less stylized nature of the dial text on Oyster Perpetuals and Datejusts of this era really allowed the rest of the watch to shine, directing your attention towards this piece's laundry list of unique facets instead.

This really represents one of the most tastefully configured Oyster Perpetuals one could walk into a store and buy way back when, and that with not only an engine turned bezel, but a blued steel seconds hands which traces the dial at every waking moment of the day. If you weren't a hardcore collector, and simply wanted one nice vintage watch, this is undoubtedly the direction I'd point you in. With styling as versatile and timeless as such, you could seriously make this a one watch collection, and one to be proud of at that.

A collector named Kevin O’Dell has this piece listed on Instagram , where he’s trying to get $2,650 for it. How this hasn’t already sold I’m not sure, but I have a feeling it’ll be sold very soon.

Maybe I'm an exception to the rule, but I'd argue that Canadians don't have the same nationalistic pride as our neighbors to the south. Despite this, I always get a kick out of finding a Canadian watch, as with a history of so few luxury retailers in the country, its usually pretty easy to figure out where a watch was purchased, and where it's been over the years. While browsing the inventory of a notable dealer and friend, I was delighted to come across a timepiece of northern provenance, that warrants a little history lesson if I do say so myself.

Back in the 60s, a number of watch manufacturers were tasked with producing single button chronographs for the Royal Canadian Air Force (RCAF). That number was five, and those five were Breitling, Lemania, Omega, Rodania, and Birks. Though there's something undeniably cool about a military issued Breitling or Omega, I’d make a case that the Birks variant is the most interesting of the bunch. For those who don’t have the distinct pleasure of suffering through our winters, Birks is a long-established luxury retailer here in Canada, and to have a RCAF watch made under the watchful eye of a Canadian firm is pretty cool if you ask me.

While it is indeed a military watch, you'll notice that the caseback is free of any inscriptions. Though there's no concrete, definite consensus as to why all remaining examples have these sterile casebacks, many believe the watches were "civilianized" after military service by way of polishing and re-brushing after decommissioning, after which they'd be resold by Birks. The idea of walking into a store and buying a "brand new," military issued watch is equal parts nonsensical and beautiful, which I think you'll have to agree with.

Trusted dealer and friend of the site Eric Wind has this watch listed for sale for $4,500, which is more than reasonable considering past sales of RCAF chronographs. Additional photos and details can be found on Eric's site.

To cap things off this week, we've got yet another timepiece that's as clean as it is rare, but unlike all of the aforementioned watches, this piece has been cased in 18k pink gold. No, I'm not about to hit you with another iced-out showstopper, but instead a tasteful and refined piece that'll go largely unnoticed by the masses. We're talking about a Universal Geneve, and while it's not exactly one of the brand's flagship references, there's a whole lot to like here.

Although Universal Geneve's Monodatic is arguably one of the less sought after models in UG collecting circles, the present condition of this example ought to inspire further consideration. First off, we’ve got the dial, which is clean as the day is long. Then there's the case, which has fancy style lugs, and although polished, it remains thick. If not convinced, note the hallmark on the side of the case, which is still visible. There's only one single part that I have doubts concerning originality, and that’s the crown which might’ve been replaced, though that's no reason to pass up a piece in such otherwise great condition. I'd personally try and not let that bother you, though period correct crowns can be tracked down if desired.

All in all, I think this piece would make just as excellent an addition to any serious Universal Geneve collection as it would to that of a budding collector. This is just one of those watches you don't see everyday, and especially not in this condition, definitely making it an enticing proposition. I'll definitely be watching to see how this auction unfolds over the next week.

An eBay seller based out of the impossibly vibrant Mexico City has this piece listed in an auction that’ll come to a close in just under a week’s time. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at $1125.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Royal Canadian Air Force Chronograph, A Rolex 1960 Rolex Oyster Perpetual, And A Universal Geneve Monodatic

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! We saw quite the response to last week's edition, comments are always welcome and even encouraged! There was some talk about the "T SWISS T" designation on a "no-lume" Zephyr featured. As I attempted to clear up in the comment section, this is something we see in the world of vintage Rolex, with shared dials between lumed and non-lumed variants for ease of production. By the way, it looks like our lead pick from last go around, the Patek Phillipe ref. 2545 in rose gold is still available!

As for what did sell, the gorgeous Movado triple calendar in yellow gold with Breguet numerals has been marked as "Sold" — the asking price was $6,295 . On eBay, the aforementioned Zephyr found a discerning buyer for a best offer price below the ask of $5,990 . Both are great watches in that "mid-semi-accessible" vintage watch price band that I am sure the next owners are going to love.

And now, this week's picks!

Ah, the "Evil Clapton" Tri-Compax, there really is nothing quite like it. When vintage watches first came into vogue, around ten years ago or so, this is one of those models that rose to the level of "grail." At the time, in the world of mid-century sport chronographs, collectors dialed in to specific watches like the Panda Paul Newman Oyster Daytonas, early Heuer Carreras, and, of course, the Omega Speedmaster ref. 2915. Interest has since spread out a bit, and, in turn, the ravenous chase for these so-called "grail" chronographs has slowed down a bit. That said, the reasons why collectors saw these watches as the mountain top are still present and always will be.

Produced in both white and black dials, these Tri-Compax references gained their nickname thanks to Eric Clapton wearing one. Not when Clapton was known as a watch collector in the 1990s, but back in the 1960s, such as in this photo from 1967 with Jimi Hendrix ! Clearly a man of discerning watch taste, Clapton likely recognized that the Tri-Compax was superlative in its day. This is essentially a Daytona with added triple calendar and moonphase functionality. The 36mm stainless steel case features twisted lyre lugs a la the Speedmaster Professional, and the reverse-panda layout is simply gorgeous. In my mind, the Clapton Tri-Compaxes represent the ultimate mid-century chronograph design.

The Clapton's dial is not cluttered; the added calendar and moonphase complications are gracefully integrated into the design. The bright radial sub-dials with sharp serifed fonts catch your eye at a glance, while the day and date wheels are snuck in between, there only when you need them. Large luminous plots at each hour marker and broad sword hands go a long way towards making the watch readable as well.

The dial design is what brings us in, but the in-house UG caliber 281 inside is nothing to scoff at. It's manually wound, designed, and produced all by UG, which is something to note at this time, where most brands leaned on outside suppliers like Valjoux — namely, Rolex for the Daytona.

Condition here is exactly what I'm looking for in a Clapton. The dial is clean, free of any major blemishes to my eye, outside of the slight patina to the sub-dials, which I find pleasing. The luminous material in the hands and on the dial has aged to a creamy tone that matches throughout, adding to the vintage look. And the case is well-defined, the seller remarks that it is unpolished, but at the very least, the twisted edges on the lyre lugs are clear.

The seller, Maximiliano of Relojes Vintage Mexico in, well, Mexico, is offering this Evil Clapton for $20,999. Click here to get all of the details.

In the world of vintage Heuer, few variations spark instant recognition like the "Chronomatic" dials. Produced for a very, very short run of dials, right at the dawn of the Caliber 11 era starting in 1969, the word "Chronomatic" sits proudly above the Heuer logo, and the model name, "Carrera," has been relegated to the bottom. This was a departure from the dial branding of the previous manually-wound Carrera generation and speaks to how proud the brand was to offer an automatic chronograph movement. Heuer quickly reverted to a "normal" layout, basically only offering Chronomatic dials for a few short months. As one can imagine, these dials have become the ultimate for Heuer collectors, serving as almost a secret handshake between those who really know.

The reasoning for the switch from using Chronomatic to not stems from Heuer's collaboration within the Project 99 consortium, where Hamilton-Büren, Dubois-Dépraz, Breitling, and Heuer co-created one of the first automatic chronograph movements. The details of the movement are quirky as it features a micro-rotor and a left-handed crown. While Heuer stopped using the Chronomatic branding, you can find watches with this same movement branded as such from Breitling and Hamilton. It's possible Heuer moved away from the word to differentiate its offerings, but many collectors have also claimed the market didn't quite understand what it meant. "Automatic Chronograph," the branding that replaced it, was much easier to understand.

This small dial detail can be found on Carreras, Autavias, and Monacos, but among the group, I do prefer the ref. 1153N. It's simple, but the blue dial really shines, and simplicity is what makes the Carrera, in any form, so great.

The sellers, Andee and the team from rarebirds.de in Germany, are offering this Chronomatic Carrera for €17,900. Click here for all of the photos and information.

Editor's Note: After publishing this article, the seller updated the listing to include more information. Specifically, it has come to light that this Chronomatic dial was not originally fitted within this specific case. All the parts here are authentic and period correct but likely, were not born together, please read more via the link above.

Here's a quick one in the world of vintage Rolex. There's not a ton I need to say as it's a great watch from one of the most respected vintage Rolex sellers in the world, but I'll do my best!

The Rolex Explorer II ref. 1655 targeted a shockingly niche market: cave explorers. This explains the bold and bright 24-hour hand (notably not a GMT hand since the external 24-hour bezel is fixed and the hand cannot be independently set). The purpose of this orange hand was to differentiate between day and night underground. The watch was never a huge success over its 14-year production from 1971 to roughly 1985, but the lower sales and its distinctive look compared to other Rolex sport models eventually caught the interest of Rolex collectors. It's known by the nicknames "Freccione," arrow, in Italian, and "Steve McQueen," although there is no evidence that the actor ever owned one.

The earliest examples of the reference featured a straight seconds hand rather than the more Rolex-standard shape we see here. Ref. 1655 dials are commonly categorized into 7 variants or "marks," this one being a Mark 4. The watch has a great look overall with excellent patina, as well as the ever-desirable accessories of an original box, papers, and product literature.

The seller, Andrew Shear of Sheartime in New York City, is offering this Rolex Explorer II "Freccione" for $30,000. Check it out in full right here .

There's something special about Gübelin-branded wristwatches of the mid-century. As one of the largest watch and jewelry retailers in the world at the time, you just know that these white label products (produced for Gübelin by watch brands) are of the highest quality possible. Gübelin wouldn't dare to offer their clients anything less. Don't believe me? Take a look at the 1920s triple calendar watches produced by Audemars Piguet for the retailer, only branded as Gübelin, just like this watch.

From the movements to the dials and cases, even when not produced by Audemars Piguet, these watches are incredibly well made and insane value because, put simply, people just don't know. The movement here is a Cyma 12 ligne caliber; you can find this family of movements under Cyma caliber numbers 214 and 216. Introduced in 1930, this caliber could be regulated to the level of an observatory chronometer and features gorgeous architecture. Just look at the curved three-finger bridges of the going train; it doesn't get much better than that in a vintage time-only wristwatch caliber. Oh, and the case is 35.5mm in diameter, a great size.

The seller, Craft and Tailored in Los Angeles, is offering the Gübelin for the incredible price of $1,850. Grab it right here .

This may be the only fake watch I have ever found to be interesting. If you are familiar with the history of Breguet, the brand, and Abraham-Louis Breguet, the man, you'll remember his 1795 invention of the secret signature. At this time, Breguet was so well respected in his field that, likely for the first time ever, other watchmakers began to produce pocket watches (by hand, of course) and sign them with Breguet's name. Essentially, this is the birthplace of fake watches. Breguet, the man, thus began to combat these fakes by signing every authentic Breguet dial with a pantograph, an engraving machine capable of precise miniature lettering that was almost invisible.

The watch we have here, listed on eBay, likely dates to the early 1800s and is an example of a well-known group of Breguet fakes, which are all signed "Breguet A Paris" on the dial and movement. Breguet signed watches in different ways throughout its history, including "Breguet," "Breguet et Fils," and "Breguet et Fils, Horloger de la Marine Royale," but never "Breguet A Paris" or "Breguet et Fils A Paris." This group of fakes is such a problem that, for a time, the Breguet website featured a warning before submitting for an extract from the archives stating that if your antique pocket watch says "Breguet A Paris" on the dial, it's probably not even worth asking us if it's real.

So yes, this is a somewhat interesting piece of horological history, but that doesn't make it any less of a fake. Feel free to check it out right here and proceed at your own risk now that you know what it is!
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An 'Evil Clapton' From Universal Genève, A Chronomatic Heuer Carrera, And A Very Early Fake Watch

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
The selection for this week's Bring A Loupe doesn't have any particular theme – its aim was simply to find some of the coolest watches available right now. You will find one of the greatest Lange watches ever made; a Rolex Explorer with a very special dial; and an electric blue Memovox from Jaeger-LeCoultre. There will also be one Universal Geneve that you should not be willing to buy under any circumstances. And you will discover why a Patek was recently sold for 10 times its initial estimate. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 11, 2015.

In Walter Lange's own words, the Tourbillon Pour le Mérite was the "technical masterpiece" of Lange re-birth in 1994. It was indeed the first time that a wristwatch featured a fusee-and-chain transmission, coupled with a tourbillon. And this superlative model is fitted in a case only 39 mm in diameter. The market was awestruck, and orders for the four watches presented piled up – initial retailers actually entered a lottery to carry the first watches. Huber, a watch retailer based in Munich, was among the very first and sold this watch in January 1995, only three months after the 1994 launch on October 25th – a bit of trivia here, you will notice that all the promotional shots of Lange with calendar always display this date. It's hard to say which side of the watch is more beautiful; the wonderful dial side, with the open tourbillon, or the lavishly finished back with the traditional three-quarter German plate for the movement. Watches of this caliber usually never appear on the market if not featured in auctions; this time a very respected U.S. collector is selling one for $260,000 on Timezone here ; it comes full set, and, even better, unpolished as evidenced by the patina on the yellow-gold case.

The Explorer is emblematic of Rolex's emphasis on producing sturdy and highly reliable watches: this watch can follow you everywhere, every day. The reference 14270 was the modern incarnation of that vision, featuring the same 36 mm Oyster case as its predecessor, the glorious reference 1016, which made it into our list of the Five Best Buys In Vintage Rolex Under $8,000 . As often happens, a small detail makes this Explorer highly desirable: only one line on the dial, underlining its special provenance, and, therefore, its rarity in comparison to the "standard" production. This watch comes with its original papers, also stamped by Tiffany & Co. This is a perfect watch for every day wear, but it also has a purity of design that appeals to real connoisseurs as well. The Keystone just listed this rare Explorer for $16,500 here .

The Air-King line was recently phased out of Rolex production, but for the past 70 years they represented a very attractive offering: Rolex quality at a lower price point, if you will. Again, this watch stands out for two reasons: a larger case, and a very complete set. In the 1950s, most of the automatic Rolex models without date were housed in 34 mm cases, but I can think of three exceptions that had a bigger 36mm Oyster case in stainless steel: the Explorer, the Oyster Perpetual reference 1018, and the rare Air-King reference 5504. This watch also comes with its original box and papers, tracing back to the decade it spent at a retailer in the Bahamas before being purchased at the end of the 1960s. It is currently listed for $5,768 on eBay here , which seems high given the absence of the original bracelet.

The Memovox is a mythical model from Jaeger-LeCoultre, offering a very useful alarm complication in an elegant wristwatch. Many versions were released, and the one with this striking blue lapis lazuli dial might actually be the rarest. Only a handful of examples are presently known: one will be auctioned by Christie's next week with an estimate between $8,000 and 12,000, and this one, by one of Instagram's darlings. Its unique look does not need much description; this watch is even more dashing when it comes with its original Gay Fr è res bracelet, as it is the case here. This very rare Memovox was listed by a watch journalist on his personal Instagram account here , and it will likely be sold for around $13,000.

The Space-Compax is a regular guest in our the Bidder Beware section, but the number of examples to avoid is apparently inexhaustible. The original chronograph represents one of Universal Geneve's greatest designs, and its imitations are a solid candidate for the pantheon of fake watches. The most obvious telltale when it comes to this incorrect Space-Compax is related to the case back: Universal Geneve never engraved a diver there – Longines legitimately did, but that is another story. To add insult to injury, here the hands are completely wrong – they did not come from Universal Geneve; I would actually bet the orange second hand is from a Minerva. Lastly, the dial, the indexes and the case are not proper, but you probably did not need more convincing. If you are curious, a private seller is offering this flagrantly fake chronograph on Chrono24 here .

This Omega Seamaster 200 model is often overlooked despite an attractive case and very cool sword hands. It was manufactured in the early 1970s after an order from a French diving body, the Service Hydrographique et Océanographique de la Marine, which was mapping the French coast. It was also selected for French Army divers, so some would even sport a M.N 79 engraving. Unfortunately, most of the examples you will find are comparable to this one: they were later put together with spare parts from various Omega references. In a way, it is kind of an "official" franken, as Watchco, the main source for the later variation, even signed the case back instead of the diving helmet symbol from the initial maker, Piquerez. How to tell one apart from the other? The original displays more patina on the tritium indexes and hands; in addition, they have an acrylic insert bezel rather than an aluminum one. At the time of publishing, bidding for this franken Seamaster 200 was at $768 on eBay here .

This week, Bonhams sold an early Patek Amagnetic for the record breaking sum of $115,000, including premium. I previously covered this watch , and expected strong results; nonetheless we suddenly witnessed the highest price for a reference 3417 ever reached at auction – to my knowledge. Let's now see what could explain this staggering performance, some 10 times over the initial estimate. First and foremost, the $12,000 to $18,000 estimate provided was completely off base. This valuation might have worked in the early 2000s; since that time prices have risen significantly as the market noticed the attraction of this atypical model in Patek Philippe's production. Currently, you should expect a decent Patek reference 3417 to fetch around $50,000 at auctions, if it features the Amagnetic script on the dial. Still, the final price this week was more than double this figure because the lot 194 was not a random Amagnetic. Its Beyer-signed dial obviously added a premium; like any double-signed dial it distinguishes this watch from the regular production of steel Amagnetics, reaching around 600 pieces through the 1960s.

Moreover, the original box and papers added a very special dimension to this lot: when it comes to vintage collecting those are way more than just extras – they are a testimony to the good care that the given by owners over many years. Lastly, this Patek ignited an intense battle between two very determined collectors when the $70,000 mark was broken. This literal arms race obviously drove the final bids above and beyond the market price, but this unpredictability is truly what makes auctions so interesting, and – let's face it – fun to watch. As a side note, I can strongly recommend reading a terrific guide to the Amagnetic here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Original Lange Tourbillon, A Tiffany-Signed Explorer, A Very Fake Universal Geneve, And Some Other Interesting Watches

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Auction season has officially come to a close, but vintage watch finds just keep on coming. This week we’ve got a balanced mix of everything from an Eberhard Extra Fort, to an IWC Mark XI with airline provenance, to a sought after configuration of the pioneering Angelus Chronodato. For the Rolex heads out there, fear not, we’ve got you covered with the best Ref. 1675 you’ll see all week, and a set of books released by the brand back in 1946.

Tool watches will always be my favorite subcategory of the watchmaking world, for the simple reason that they were built to last. Don’t get me wrong, I get a kick out of early Patek’s and modern executions of haute horology as much as the next guy. But their crux has always been and always will be their fragility. This boils down to not liking to worry about my watch, or have something I own end up owning me. Therefore, hard-wearing timepieces are the logical choice. Throughout history, few have consistently delivered in this department as masterfully as Schaffhausen’s IWC, which continues to innovate in the field. While there are a few modern offerings of note, let’s take a look at what is arguably the brand’s purest expression of tool watch production.

IWC’s Mark XI succeeded the British Ministry of Defence commissioned Mark X, which along with 11 other purpose-built watches, pioneered the class as a whole during World War II. While other manufacturers fulfilled their orders and proceeded to carry on with more conventional timepieces, IWC recognized the value of such watches and kept at it. The Mark XI in 1949 added an antimagnetic shield, hacking movement, and a reinforced crystal capable of withstanding changes in pressure. This naturally made the pilot’s watch an attractive option for civilian and military personnel alike, earning it a place on the wrists of Royal Air Force and Royal Australian Air Force pilots. These two applications receive most of the fanfare, leaving more obscure Mark XI variants in the shadows.

If you thought I was about to dive into one such variant, you’d be correct. On the dial side, this piece appears to be a standard Mark XI, but upon flipping it over, text reading “B.O.A.C. PROPERTY” can be found on its caseback. This indicates the watch was worn by a pilot of the British Overseas Airways Corporation, the state-owned airline which would merge with three others in 1974 to form British Airways. I’ve always appreciated this variant of the Mark XI in that it represents a unique in-between, that’s neither military nor civilian, but still saw cockpit use. The infrequency of examples surfacing is always a bonus, too.

An eBay seller in East Sussex has this piece listed in an auction that’ll come to a close on Sunday here. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at £2,362 GBP.

Typically, I don’t like to rehash what you’ve already seen, and gravitate towards references we’ve yet to explore. But every now and then, something so good surfaces that I can’t help but toss it into the roundup. That’s exactly what went down earlier this week, when a routine scouring of the bay revealed an outstanding example of a seldom seen chronograph. Eberhard and eBay are two words which are rarely found in the same sentence. For some inexplicable reason, very few noteworthy examples of the brand find themselves listed there. Nonetheless, we’ve defied the odds this week with the discovery of an extra clean Extra Fort. Couldn’t resist that one!

You’re looking at the Ref. 31003, which is one of the best looking dual register chronographs of this era. With its sunburst silver dial, polished silver details, and conservation applications of red accenting, it’s got a far more sophisticated vibe than other sports chronographs in its price bracket. Moreover, it’s powered by the Cal. 310-8, which was produced exclusively for Eberhard by Dubois-Depraz, the famed watchmaker to the watchmakers.

In comparison to the last one I came across, which was the best I’d ever seen, this one certainly stands out as the more impressive example. That’s because its dial is quite literally immaculate and free of any flaws. On a watch with a sunburst dial, this really counts, in that the slightest mark will really stand out like a sore thumb. My only gripe would be that the case looks to have been polished, though its defining lines are still visible, so it’s ultimately not a deal breaker.

This piece can be found listed for sale on eBay by a seller in Beverly Hills. They’re asking $4,300, but you’ve got the option to make an offer. Click here should you wish to do just that.

If you’ve been keeping up with the watch world, you’ll know that Marlon Brando’s GMT Master was auctioned off earlier this week in New York, raking in just under $2 million at Phillips. It was an interesting sale, to say the least, which climbed upwards rather slowly in strangely small bid increments. It’s always an interesting statement on the standards of this scene when bids just ten thousand dollars higher than the last inspire confusion. I’d argue it’s a sign of the market’s more sober state compared to years past.

Having said that, a number of dealer friends informed me that upon the end of the sale, clients began getting in touch in search of 1675s, suggesting that vintage Rolex is and always will be a force to be reckoned with. Should you be one such collector in search of a GMT Master, allow me to direct your attention towards a complete-as-can-be-example. It’s not only fitted with a fuchsia Pepsi bezel, but includes the original box, punched warranty papers, and a punched chronometer certificate. These documents in their current state are referred to as “double-punched” papers, and add to the already considerable desirability of this top notch example.

As for the watch itself, it’s a stunner. This piece, dating back to 1966, is fitted with a Mark 1 “Long E” dial, which is, of course, the earliest variant Rolex produced of the matte dial Ref. 1675. Another indication of this is the small GMT hand, which Rolex briefly carried over from earlier gilt-dial examples. Essentially, this makes this 1.6 million serial range Ref. 1675 a transitional sub-reference within a reference, of sorts, and an especially clean one at that. Between the unpolished case, truly flawless dial, and fascinating evolution of the once red and blue bezel insert, there’s a whole lot to love with this one.

The Miami dealer Menta Watches has this early example of the revered Rolex sports reference listed with an asking price of $29,000. More details can be found on their site , where you can make an offer.

Complications are undeniably cool, and anyone who says they don’t care for them is likely hiding their love in favor of a borderline hipsterish minimalism trip. Problem is, complications are expensive, especially when the word perpetual factors into the equation. This is why many people turn to full calendar timepieces, which afford all the indicating apertures your heart desires. Any conversation of full calendar watches wouldn’t be complete without the one that took things to the next level, through the incorporation of a chronograph function, which is why we now take a look at one.

Angelus introduced the Chronodato in 1942, and sent shockwaves through the industry. This was the very first conventional chronograph to feature day, date, and month indications. It was also distinguished by its large size, at a bold 38 mm across; admittedly it feels larger than that on the wrist. In the following decade, the watch was replaced by the Chrono Datoluxe, which would trade the month display for a moonphase function. Though a moonphase complication is perhaps more aesthetically elaborate, the earlier Chronodato will always be the more enticing proposition in the eyes of collectors.

This particular example is appealing for a number of reasons. First, its two-tone dial is finished with a deep black outer ring contrasted by a grey inner portion. Account for the sparingly applied red accents, and you’ve got one stunning timepiece. Also of note is the fact that, unlike other similarly configured Chronodato’s, this piece features a non-luminous dial. While not as sporty as the lumed variant, such examples age far more gracefully due to the impossibility of radium burning the dial’s surface. If I was after such an Angelus, this would be the watch I’d opt for.

Interested collectors should make their way over to the Omega Forum, where this piece is being offered by an individual by the name of Andre for $11,000. Get in touch by clicking here .

As I’ve said before, I get a bigger kick out of studying the history behind my favorite watches, and learning how they were marketed, than owning the watches themselves. This stems from a a beyond knowledgeable group of friends, who’ve helped shape my own collection. Thus, early marketing materials and in-depth reference guides are always of interest to me, especially those which date back to watchmaking’s golden era. A set of books that fits this description to a tee popped up on eBay not too long ago, and to not make mention of it would be to do you dear readers a disservice.

This is known as the Jubilee Vade Mecum set, which is comprised of four small books titled Step By Step; The Evolution Of The Wrist-Watch Chronometer; How The Waterproof Watch Came Into Being; and The Story Of The Self-Winding Watch. Rolex released the set back in 1946 in a numbered limited edition of 1,000. They were intended to serve as a reference guide for collectors. Like their now iconic designs, the idea to publish a set of collector’s handbooks was ahead of its time.

Rolex obsessives and enthusiasts of graphic design alike will enjoy this set, which documents the brand’s progression in style. The four books are bound with gilt coronet-clad covers in purple, orange, green, and blue. All of this is then neatly contained within a darker blue sleeve, which features the same coronet and diamond grid motif. This particular set is complete with four books and the sleeve, all of which look to be in good shape. While not exactly cheap, the set is less expensive than you’d expect something of its age and rarity to be, and would make an excellent addition to any horological library.

You’ll find this set for sale on eBay, where someone in Miami has listed it with an asking price of $699, though offers are being accepted. Claim it as yours here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rolex GMT Master, An Eberhard Extra Fort, And An Angelus Chronodato

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! Rewinding two weeks here to Thanksgiving week here in the States, our last standard edition of this column included some devastating personal news – losing access to my eBay account. The good news is that I'm back in after a few calls and a random address in Brooklyn, NY, receiving two iPhones and an iPad. I dug up a nice little vintage Omega eBay pick to celebrate all things being right in the world.

After the Longines "school watch" tourbillon pocket watch passed at Iconeek without a bid, we have just one result to recap. The Heuer Carrera ref. 2447 NST up for auction at Adam Partridge Auctioneers & Valuers in Cheshire, England, ended up selling for 8,800 GBP before fees. For a bit of a project watch in need of a crystal tension ring and crown, that number feels spot-on.

And here are this week's picks!

I'll go out on a limb to say that it's a great time to buy a vintage Nautilus or Royal Oak. And when I say vintage, I mean true vintage, specifically the ref. 3700/1 and ref. 5402 respectively. These watches saw a somewhat dramatic increase in value over the last three to five years but have since retracted. With that pullback, so many collectors are just staying away from these great watches completely. At some level, the price decrease has prevented a lot of new interest from flowing in. That all said, it is nearly inarguable that these two Gérald Genta designs changed watches forever.

Introduced in 1976, the "Jumbo" Nautilus ref. 3700/1 epitomized the "luxury sports watch" and was famously advertised with the tagline: "One of the world's costliest watches is made of steel." The first Nautilus and this marketing did a lot to change the perceptions of watch buyers. No longer did a luxury watch have to be complicated and in precious metal. While onstage at our UBS House of Craft, Jean-Claude Biver told a great story about how Genta downplayed the similarities between the Royal Oak and the Nautilus by proclaiming the Nautilus was as if you put a Royal Oak underwater for years and years, a story I will never forget.

In really solid condition with box and papers, this particular 3700/1 is co-signed by Gübelin at six o'clock on the dial, making it all the more special. If you subscribe to my theory that a vintage Nautilus is a great buy right now, you might as well pony up for one that is truly special and made rare by a retailer stamp. Gübelin is among my favorite Patek stamps; it's one of the most prestigious jewelers and watch retailers in the world. Plus, I've always loved the tiny detail of the umlaut over the "U," tiny but really cool.

The seller, Adam Golden of Menta Watches in Miami, has listed this vintage Nautilus on his website for just today at $165,000. You can view the listing in full right here .

If a $165,000 Nautilus is not your thing, I love this 1930s Audemars Piguet listed by Menta yesterday as well. It has to be ridiculously rare.

The Santos Dumont is quickly rising up my rankings of Cartier models. The modern iteration is the best "standard" catalog men's watch Cartier makes, and these neo-vintage executions are one of the more accessible ways to add a truly heritage-inspired and not super upsized Cartier to your collection. The reference 1575 is essentially the same watch as the ref. 96054, which came before it in the 1980s, was a lightly updated version of the ref. ​​78097, a part of the 1973 Louis Cartier Collection. I say all this not only to bore my audience with reference numbers but also to point out that this model has a direct lineage to true "vintage" Cartier. The Louis Cartier Collection was the first fully fleshed-out watch catalog at Cartier and was inspired by all of the best watches made up until that point. Long story short, the throughline from the first Santos in 1904 to the 1575 we see here is fairly direct.

This is where neo-vintage watches shine when the 1980s and 1990s technology and build quality are applied to great designs from decades previous, where the idea of everyday wear is not quite as realistic in today's world. I would love to daily a 1910s Cartier Santos, but that's not really smart to do. The 1575 offers much of the same look with stout build quality.

Finding this watch in platinum is no easy feat. You can dig up a few via listing sites like Chrono24, but typically, the asking prices are a bit inflated, up into the low to mid-20s. When most dealers get this watch in, their eyes light up because it is gorgeous and quite rare, but I find anything above 20k for this watch to be a bit rich. It's great, don't get me wrong, but ultimately, it is on the small side and, therefore, has somewhat of a thin market.

As a "Pre-CPCP" variant, this particular one dates prior to 1998 when the Collection Privée Cartier Paris officially started. A quick and easy tell is the lack of "Paris" on the dial; this one is simply signed with "Cartier" at 12 o'clock. The watch is otherwise identical to the CPCP variant that came later, both powered by the high-end-for-Cartier, F. Piguet base, caliber 21.

The seller, Mike Nouveau, has listed this Cartier on his new website/app, Pushers.io, and is asking a reasonable $17,000. Check it out right here .

A while back now, on Bring A Loupe, I included a Zenith Chronometer caliber 135 alongside a Philippe Dufour Duality, and nothing looked out of place. I am fond of these vintage chronometer-rated time-only watches as they were the top quality, simple watches made in their era – not too dissimilar from Dufour's aim today. Cyma is not a name many people know in 2024, but it was a really solid brand in the vintage period. It dates back to 1862, and the most notable part of the brand's history is its partnership with Tavannes in 1903. In the ensuing decades, Cyma and Tavannes collaborated to create some great watches with excellent movements. Cyma is one of those vintage brands that modern watchmakers call out for having genius movement architecture; I'll never forget Raúl Pagès calling out a Cyma movement as inspiration for his first watch, the Soberly Onyx.

This watch is powered by a Cyma caliber 216, which, while not the inspiration for a modern Raúl Pagès movement, is chronometer-rated and finely finished with côtes de Genève and seemingly solid anglage based on what I can make out from the seller's photo. The dial side grabbed my attention as well with crosshair detailing, rose-gold tone hour markers, and syringe-esque hands that I like to call "skyscraper" hands, but no one else really uses that term.

The seller, Arrow Of Time, is located in Paris and has listed this Cyma for a very reasonable €2,750. Get all of the details right here .

I promised an eBay pick to celebrate my return to the platform, and here it is. This 1970s Omega Constellation is, like the Cyma, also chronometer rated, but that's less important here as by the 1970s, Omega was sending most, if not all, of their many Constellations to be certified. In this era, Omega made a lot of watches and, in particular, a lot of different variants of the Constellation. Building on the brand cache of its first Constellation in 1952, Omega aggressively built out the model's offering over the next few decades. By the 1970s, the Constellation was less a specific model with a gold case and "pie-pan" dial but more a signifier that the watch you are looking at was a premium time-only Omega offering.

So, while this one is in a stainless steel case, you get a little bit of extra sauce with an eggshell white dial and Breguet numerals. This style of numerals is not something Omega did a ton of back in the day, and they're quite hard to find on Constellations. Thinking back to my days on the Hodinkee Shop Vintage Team, I remember a few Breguet-numeraled vintage Omegas crossing my desk, but typically, they were lower-quality, more "workhorse" Omega models like this one .

An eBay seller out of Homewood, Illinois (a town over from my hometown, by the way), has put this Omega up for auction, ending Sunday, December 15, at 1:04 PM ET. At the time of publishing, there are no bids at the opening price of $500. Check it out here .

And a bonus eBay pick! This Universal Genéve Compur 30 falls into the category of vintage UG that is getting harder and harder to find ahead of the brand's relaunch. My observation has been a lack of quality Universal examples hitting the market lately as collectors wait to see what the relaunch by Breitling means for the "value" of their old watches. Regardless, this is a great one. It is 36mm in diameter, 18k yellow gold, and has a beautiful-although-slightly-aged, two-tone dial. I find the dial aging to be quite charming actually. For reference, to find a dial with this true vintage two-tone look from a brand like Patek, you're going to be spending over $50,000 if not $100k. This is where UG excels, offering the look of this golden era of vintage watches at a fraction of the price compared to the "big boys."

The case was made by Jung & Fils, UG's go-to source for solid gold chronograph cases, who also did work for JLC and a few others. It features nice large square pushers and these very interesting downturned and somewhat undersized lugs. This is a tip to how a 36mm watch was designed in the 1930s, the lugs do everything they can to "downsize" the look and feel of this "oversized" watch on the wrist. I love the small dial detail of the extended markers at three, six, and nine minutes in the sub-register at three o'clock – emphasized as calls were billed per three-minute increment back in the day. The serial number on the caseback dates this watch between 1938 and 1941.

An eBay seller right here in New York has put this Universal up for auction, ending Thursday, December 19, at 11:05 PM ET. At the time of publishing, bidding had reached $365. Get all of the details and photos right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Nautilus Retailed by Gübelin, A Platinum Santos Dumont, And A Chronometer-rated Cyma

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
We took a bit of a break with the happenings of New York auction season and the excitement surrounding H10, though Bring A Loupe is back with all the vintage watch goodness you could ask for and then some. There’s a little something fit for every collector’s tastes in this week’s roundup, including an ultra-thin from AP, one of the sharpest gilt dial chronographs we’ve seen in a while, and a little something from Omega for your inner gentleman racer. Also included is a less common Tudor that might catch you off guard. Let’s jump right in.

Upon initially getting into watches, one of the first pieces I ever had eyes for was Omega’s uniquely complicated Chronostop. Though the mechanism found in the movement was intriguing, I think it was more so the accessible price point at which these were trading at which surprised me, as it seemed to be a lot of watch for the money. They can still be had for reasonable prices, and continue to offer a lot in the value department, though not all are created equally — allow me to explain.

In that the Chronostop only tracks elapsed seconds, and won’t tally up minutes and hours as they pass, the watch originally saw a great deal of use in sporting events in which short periods of time were of interest, including motorsport. With this in mind, Omega created the “Driver” variant of the Chronostop, with a dial rotated 90 degrees clockwise for increased legibility when behind the wheel, and looking to shave that extra few seconds off your lap time.

While scrolling through page after page on eBay, I came across a grey dial example of the aforementioned racing timepiece that looks to be in top condition. All luminous plots remain intact, the case is seemingly unpolished, and the original Omega No. 27 clasp is present, which is one of the cooler deployant strap clasps if I do say so myself. For the petrolhead, this would make quite a solid addition to the collection.

An eBay seller out of Brazil has this piece listed for $1,900, though you do have the option to make an offer. Click here for the full listing.

The gilt dial chronograph is something every astute collector of vintage watches aspires to one day own, and for good reason. In simple terms, they’re some of the most attractive chronographs ever made — thanks to the pops of gilty goodness against an abyss-like gloss backdrop — and collectors are certainly hip to this. With all this said, note that not all gilt dial chronographs are created equally, with case shape, powering caliber, and dial scale design having a say in the determination of overall value.

Luckily for you, today we’re looking at one that could be described as a cut above the rest, and upon seeing it you’ll get the picture. This is an oversized example of a Cal. 33.3 powered Omega chronograph, which dates back to 1941, and features the gilded accents we oh so love. Under a loupe, you’ll see that there are actually two tones of gilt detailing, one slightly more copper-like than the other, that work together to create one of the most stunning sights you could ask to see regularly on your wrist.

Its lugs look have to have been polished at some point, though not terribly much, as their current thick state would indicate. The good news continues, as the watch has just been serviced, and is being offered with an Omega archive extract, guaranteeing authenticity. In these cold winter months, I wouldn’t mind a little heat on my wrist like such.

Shuck The Oyster out of Berlin is offering this gilt dial Omega chronograph for €48,500, though as with most dealers reasonable offers are considered. Find more info here.

Hunting down unconventional offerings of the Wilsdorf empire is somewhat of an horological passion, given the ubiquity of so many of the private conglomerate’s timepieces. I’m not talking about a rare dial variant, or a coveted bezel insert configuration, but an entire model variant that has gone largely overlooked — tougher find, right? Given the amount of interest in Rolex, it’s more likely that a watch like such would come from Tudor, and the last piece of the day is no exception.

With a shield on its dial and a tracing red hand, you’d be correct to guess this is a Tudor Advisor, the Rolex sister brand’s alarm watch. Though the Advisor is relatively known, and was even once reissued, this particular variant catches some off guard, given the lack of an Oyster case and signed crowns. It’s known as the Ref. 10050, and as I’m sure you’ll agree, it’s quite a looker.

What appeals to me most about this piece is just how un-Rolex/Tudor it feels. The case is more along the lines of something you’d see from a smaller brand, as are the unsigned crowns. These both come as puzzling inclusions, though it’s all correct and worth knowing, should you happen to come across an example in the wild.

Los Angeles’s Craft & Tailored has this Advisor listed accessibly at $2,650. Click here for the full scoop.

If there's one thing to be learned from the past year of notable auctions, is that despite the strength of more mainstream pieces in the current market, tastes are continually branching out in search of the lesser-known and perhaps overlooked. This is why collectors in years past have gravitated towards the expertly crafted, early offerings of Audemars Piguet, many of which were kitted out to the nines with chimes among other welcome complications. For today, let's focus on a more mechanically subdued piece that still manages to amaze with out-of-this-world style.

You guessed it – it's a so-called "Disco Volante," which of course translates to flying disc, or flying saucer, thanks to the wide bezel and extremely thin case profile. This is one of the few cases in which I don't mind the presence of what's technically a hobnail bezel, in that the finish has been applied in the form a pattern, that's actually quite nice. It contrasts the admittedly barebones, crosshair style dial well, and adds a little bit to the overall aesthetic. You really don't see many designs like this from the 1950s – especially those executed in a white metal.

Aside from a little discoloration towards the edges of the dial, this is pretty much what a flawless piece looks like. I personally wouldn't bother holding out for another, better example, in that you're more likely to find a needle in a haystack.

The Keystone has this Audemars Piguet listed for $8,000.00 USD, which doesn't sound unreasonable. Find more info on their site.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Two Omega Chronographs, A Tudor Advisor, And An Audemars Piguet Disco Volante

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, we look at some highly unusual vintage watches, two of which have real mysteries surrounding their origin. Specifically, we're looking at a Rodania that is an almost exact duplicate of a well-known military Lemania, and a Zenith El Primero with a formerly unknown yellow gold case. The rare Angelus Chrono-Datoluxe, and the soccer-dedicated Breitling are much better documented, but no less interesting in their inventive variations on the chronograph complication. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 16, 2016.

They are plenty of vintage chronographs aimed at a very specific audience, and this unusual Breitling is a great example. The 45-minute track in red has a definite purpose: tracking the halftime of a soccer match with the square-tipped hand. It actually marks the elapsed minutes, while the sub-dial at 9 o'clock is the running seconds indication. It's very similar to the "slow counter" principle of an earlier diving chronograph, also from Breitling – the reference 2005 , and the present reference 34-31 also has the "activity indicator" of the ref. 2005 as well, at 6:00 on the dial.

The indicator shows black when the chronograph is not running, yellow when it is, and a mix of both colors when it is stopped but not reset, as is the case in the image above – and a very logical way of taking the guesswork out of using the chronograph it is, too. This piece, made in 1973, goes even further as an oddity: it has a detachable inner case, making it a hybrid between a regular stopwatch and a chronograph wristwatch.

A reputable vintage Breitling collector is offering this rare chronograph for €3,650 (or around $3,800) on his website . He notes the mint condition of the piece, and the factory sticker that remains on the caseback.

Angelus was the first manufacture to ever put a date with split numbers in a wristwatch, and they went even further with the Chrono-Datoluxe. The earlier, simpler Chronodato model was launched in 1942, and offered dedicated counters for the day and the month, but the date was still read on the periphery of the dial with a long hand, like many other watches of that period. Yet, only six years later, in 1948, the Chrono-Datoluxe not only brought the technically challenging split date, but also boasted a nice moonphase indicator (while still keeping the chronograph complication, of course). Technically, it is impressive, but just as important for the success of this design is the beautifully legible dial, which is here virtually perfectly preserved. Furthermore, this yellow gold example also reveals a very interesting case shape, with sophisticated, beautifully curved lugs. And the beautiful movement is in a similarly near-flawless state of preservation.

The dealer Casowatches listed this great Angelus for €5,500 (or around $5,730) on its website .

At first glance, you could easily mistake this Rodania chronograph for the military Lemania used by the Swedish Air Force, known as the "Viggen" and, if you follow military watch discussions online, you may have read the thread where it was introduced to the world by its owner. The mystery of this watch is that while it exhibits an uncanny resemblance to the Lemania Viggen (though of course, it says "Rodania" on the dial) it is thus far the only known example of a Rodania Viggen clone. Rodania is actually not a totally unknown brand when it comes to military chronographs; it provided some great watches to the Canadian Army. However as we mentioned, the caseback of this Rodania shows none of the marks you'd expect on an issued military service watch – the only engraving is the serial number. The idea there might be some connection with Lemania Viggen production is supported by the presence inside, of the Lemania chronograph caliber 1872, and the same 817 engraving inside the caseback found in the Viggen. It seems impossible to find another similar Rodania, so the reasons behind its existence remain uncertain, and neither can we explain the yellow hands for the chronograph function, which are found neither on the Lemania Viggen or its other clone, the Tissot Navigator .

You can find this interesting Rodania for $7,989 on Ebay , and the French seller will consider lower offers as well.

This Heuer Carrera reference 3647 is a very nice example of something that is becoming rarer and rarer: the great eBay find, especially given the condition of its silver dial (described by the seller as pristine). The pictures do look promising. At first glance, it looks like there might be some damage to the Heuer logo but that's just due to a scratch on the crystal. The case shows some marks but overall, it's still in a state of good preservation, and the lume plots appear all present and whole, while the hands are in very decent condition. The strap obviously needs to be replaced, but when that's the only major requirement of a vintage watch, you know you've found a nice one. Lastly, the seller seems to be a longstanding member of Ebay, but without a previous sale or purchase in the past year, so any interested party would be wise to get in touch with him to verify the current ownership of the watch (by asking for an additional picture, for example). This Carrera is described in this eBay listing , and the current bidding is at $2,600 with two more days to go.

As with the Rodania Viggen, the controversy around this watch can be read in a thread on a watch discussion forum . While this El Primero looks like the reference A3817, it does not have a formal reference number as heretofore this case shape was unknown in yellow gold (only stainless steel examples can be found in the Zenith catalog, while the other case shape from the early El Primero series actually did exist in both in stainless steel and yellow gold). Yet, as it is noted in the thread, creating a fake yellow gold case seems to be irrationally painful and expensive, lending credence to the authenticity of this golden El Primero. Furthermore, three other yellow gold cases have since been found, although those watches show discrepancies in their dial configuration. To this day, it remains unclear whether the present watch was made as a prototype, or in a very limited production run, or as a special order, but it seems more and more clear that it was not born out of a clever Victor Frankenstein. One thing that is indisputable: it looks very attractive (much of which is thanks to a very careful restoration of what had been a badly over-polished case).

A German collector has this enigmatic Zenith for sale on his Instagram profile , asking €15,000 (or around $15,650).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Stunning Angelus Chrono-Datoluxe, An Intriguing Rodania 'Viggen,' A Breitling Reference 34-31 Made For Soccer Referees, And Other Unusual Finds

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This Bring A Loupe focuses on vintage watches that fly under the radar for a lot of collectors, but that are really worth looking at. This week you will not find any Universal Geneve, Heuer, Rolex or Patek; but keep reading. You will indeed discover a rarely seen Girard-Perregaux Olimpico, a couple of Enicars, and maybe the most Seventies chronograph ever made, from Revue. The purpose of this unusual selection is twofold: to introduce you to little-known yet really attractive vintage watches, and also provide you with some Christmas suggestions at interesting price points. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 18, 2015.

For me, this was the most exciting find of the week. I had previously seen the Girard-Perregaux Olimpico on the wrists of collectors, but I was not expecting to see one example suddenly pop up on eBay – especially not in such a good condition, with the original papers! Girard-Perregaux launched the Olimpico line to commemorate the Olympic Games in the 1950s, and for the next 30 years they released a different model every four years. In my book, the present one – dedicated to the 1968 Games in Mexico – is by far the most attractive. Its out-there, yet balanced dial probably explains my crush: the combination of beautifully preserved convex lume dots, bi-color scales and brightly printed sub registers could have gone really wrong; here it magically works. The 40 mm case has thick lugs, with nice bevels; it houses a caliber 40, from Excelsior Park, which is an excellent column wheel movement that you can also find in Gallet chronographs from the same era. At the time of publishing, bidding on this beautiful Girard-Perregaux was still under $3,000 on eBay here . And frankly, this is my dream Christmas gift.

This alarm watch produced in the 1960s has been a favorite at HODINKEE for quite some time, and for good reason. Girard Perregaux managed to preserve the line of an elegant 34 mm dress watch, with a smart display of the alarm complication. Its movement is based on the famed caliber 1475 caliber from Adolph Schild, further decorated and adjusted by Girard Perregaux. The AS 1475 was an alternative to Jaeger-Lecoultre's Memovox, and many brands used it to generate a vibrating alarm on the wrist. To be clear, this function – sometimes called a "cricket" alarm, after the Vulcain Cricket, the first to use this type of alarm in 1947 (though with a different movement) – might not wake you up from a deep sleep, but could serve as a convenient reminder of an important appointment. (These alarms can be pretty loud, though, so if you are a light sleeper this one just might do the trick.) The case from the example here should undergo a thorough cleaning and the plexiglass crystal should be replaced; importantly, the watch still features the original top crown engraved with "A," in order to properly differentiate the setting of the time and the alarm. This watch can be found for less than $200 on eBay here .

Here I have to confess, I know nothing about this chronograph from Revue, but I remember seeing a similar design from the brand Richard which disappeared in the Quartz Crisis -– virtually identical and also clearly a 1970s design. The tonneau case and the large indexes are clues to the era of manufacture but of course the real giveaways are the orange hands and the rounded trapezoid registers, each displaying a different style of graduation. Somehow, the over-the-top design sings to me: this is exactly the kind of colorful and casual watch that it's fun to wear on weekends. You probably recognized the layout of the manual-wound movement Valjoux 7734, with the date at 6 o'clock; for instance, you can find this caliber in the early – and very collectible – Tudor chronographs reference 7031/7032. You can find this unusual Revue chronograph on eBay for under $300 at the time of publication, here . Side note: anyone who puts "rad" on his eBay listing deserves some attention.

The association between Breitling and the AOPA – Aircraft Owners and Pilots Association – is well known: it actually spurred the launch of the Navitimer model in 1952. At that time, in order to avoid some heavy tariff fees, Breitling had set up a collaboration with a U.S. company for the local distribution, Wakmann. And some of the cases were manufactured by a supplier called Charles Gigandet. All those facts explain the present watch, an AOPA-signed chronograph from Wakmann/Gigandet, strongly reminiscent of similar Breitling models. All the Wakmann AOPA I had previously seen were triple-date chronographs, akin to the Datora from Breitling; this "simple" chronograph is obviously intriguing, and I found another – not the same watch of course – undergoing servicing here . Bidding on this Wakmann/Gigandet just reached $925 on eBay here .

Another chronograph equipped with the caliber Valjoux 72, the Sherpa Graph is a highly coveted watch, the Enicar brand recently enjoying a lot of attention from vintage collectors. The example here belongs to the second generation of Sherpa Graphs from the late 1960s, as evidenced by the straight hands that followed the original syringe-shaped hands. The case is in great condition, with few scratches on the sides, thick lugs and the correct crown. In addition, the dial is well preserved, emphasizing the striking orange color of the chronograph hands. I simply regret that this 40 mm chronograph does not come with the original bracelet, although the white tropic strap included does not lack style. A German seller is offering this great example on eBay; bidding just reached $2,750 here .

The badly scratched plexiglass crystal could give you the wrong impression: this Aqua Graph has been worn but it is still in a very decent condition for a 50-year-old watch. The dial is well preserved and the lug edges are still nice and crisp, ticking two of the most important boxes. In many ways, the Aqua Graph is similar to the Sherpa Graph featured above: same 40 mm case, same Valjoux 72 movement, and same handset. The big difference comes from its external bezel, supplemented by a rotating red ring, which is unfortunately often missing when you find these for sale. Two downsides: this one lacks the original signed crown, and the chronograph movement would need a full service, the cost of which of course you'll want to factor into your decision to bid. At the time of publishing, bidding was at $3,750 on eBay here .

The French manufacture Yema is little remembered for its innovative chronographs from the 1960s, produced for car and boat racing – the latter represented by the Yachtingraf shown here. This watch offers a very nice layout with a very interesting sub register on the right part, dedicated to timing sailing competitions. It still bears the mention "patent pending" as it was produced shortly after their 1967 Patent application . It comes in a great 38 mm case with long lugs, reminiscent of Heuer Autavia, and it is powered by the caliber Valjoux 7733, a workhorse movement easy to service. On the flip side with this example, the base of the lume on the minute hand seems tarnished. Also, the seller is offering an extra bezel which may not be original to this model; the original bezel which is included, is missing the lume triangle at 12:00. A Belgium seller is offering this Yema on Chronotrader here , though with no specific asking price.

Finally, this Tudor here is a good example of how tricky eBay can be. It looks good at first glance –it comes with the proper bracelet, and a Tudor branded box, from a seller with 50+ previous transactions over the past two years. Unfortunately here, the dial constitutes a major issue as it was badly tampered with. The index shape isn't the problem; they are unusual but correct, but the misalignment of the lume dots raises the first question, as those are usually placed on the central dotted circle. Then, the printing looks too fresh for a 50-year-old watch, the black is just too strong for something of this age. The font on "Tudor" is slightly off too; the letters are too fat and crude. Last, the "Shockresist" line is just comical – let's call it a modern interpretation of the longer "Shock resisting" on the original dial. Oh, and it also seems the "Swiss" mention is missing under the 6 o'clock index, at the bottom of the dial. As the dial is often the most important part of a vintage watch, and it can represent by far the highest part of its value, I would not recommend getting this botched one on eBay here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Girard-Perregaux Olimpico With Full Set, Some Killer Enicar Chronographs, And A Few More Great Under The Radar Finds

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Though the holidays are nearly in full swing, it’s still not too late to find a watch for yourself or a special someone. In this week’s roundup, you’ll find an even mix of conservative and unconventional picks from brands both widely known and otherwise. These include a rare bezel-fitted Vacheron Constantin, and an early Breitling Chronomat. At the more eccentric end of the spectrum, there’s a chronograph intended for medical professionals, along with a compass-bearing IWC, and a Le Phare Datocompax.

Last week I professed my love for tool watches. I thought we’d keep things going with a tool watch of a less rugged sort that ought to excite any medical professionals that frequent the column. What this doctor’s timepiece lacks in widespread recognition is made up for and then some by its unconventional defining traits, many of which I’ve never seen on another watch before. But don’t worry, it’s not too out there, as we’re ultimately talking about a 36 mm stainless steel chronograph, which is often a recipe for success.

This is one of those instances in which the brand name isn’t what’ll sell you on the watch, as there’s little to be said or known about this Paul Portinoux character. In fact, it’s likely that Portinoux is a fictional individual, as my research yielded two conflicting stories regarding the brand’s origins, both of which involve no one by this name. The first described the brand as a subsidiary of a New York-based watchmaker / distributor by the name of Adolf Schwarcz & Son, and the second purports Portinoux to be a brand formed by “the four Pauls” – Buhre, Garnier, Maillardet, and Picot. Neither story warrants lore-like historical significance, meaning that the main attraction here is the piece itself, with its many unique quirks and features.

For starters, its Valjoux 22 movement has been configured to operate as a monopusher chronograph, engaged using the protruding button at two o’clock. If that wasn’t enough to get you going, note how in addition to the pulsations scale, this medical timepiece has a respiration scale as well. I especially like the curved arrow which highlights it, as I don’t think I’ve ever seen one on a watch before. Speaking of curves, the last and most unusual trait found on this watch is the curved cyclops which stretches from 12 to 3, magnifying the pulsations scale. All of this certainly distinguishes this piece from the rest, making it an enticing proposition for someone who appreciates an oddity.

Private Eyes of Japan has recently lowered the price of this piece to ¥480,000, which equates roughly to $4,380. More details can be found on their site .

Part of the fun and, at times frustration, of writing this column is the constant search for not only new watches, but new dealers and auction houses we’ve yet to shed light on. Earlier this week, the hunt opened my eyes to a Barcelona-based outfit currently selling a Le Phare Dato Compax in 18k yellow gold.

Like the manufacturer of the previous piece, Le Phare is by no means a universally recognized name, but that’s not to say they didn’t make some great watches. I’d argue the Ref. 3114 is one of those watches, thanks to its chronograph movement that also indicates the day, date, and month. Pair that with a set of blued steel hands, smart looking applied numerals, and an ornate case shape, and you’ve got a real looker on your hands. It’s also likely that this watch was originally sold in the Spanish market, as its day wheel is set to Viernes in the provided photos.

While I might be smitten by the complicated movement and elegant indices found on this Le Phare, neither of these details are what first caught my attention. That honor belongs to the bracelet, which might very well be too nice for this watch. Don’t get me wrong, I love the watch, but those bold geometric links have far more of a wow factor going on. Additionally, the way in which the end links are secured to the rest of bracelet with a screw is rather unusual, furthering my fascination with its design. Purchasing a watch solely for its bracelet might sound ridiculous to some, but to others in possession of an outstanding yellow-gold piece, with a killer bracelet box that’s demanding to be checked, this is without question the move.

Corello Watch Specialists has this piece listed on their site with an asking price of €11,500. Find the full scoop here .

Over the last decade, we’ve seen collaborations dominate the world of fashion and design. With a new co-designed product seemingly dropping every other day, I’m getting to a point where I’m no longer excited by the lightning response times of big brands to the latest trends. That’s why I’ve started to look backwards at the earlier days of such collaborative efforts, in search of the goods that paved the way for the now go-to trick. In the horological field, one of the first to give it a shot was IWC, who navigated a difficult patch of watchmaking history with their Porsche Design partnership.

Quartz was all the rage back in 1978 when the two brands joined forces to introduce the mechanical Ref. 3510, which was produced exclusively in PVD-coated aluminum. Today, that might not sound so revolutionary, but four decades ago it was exactly that. Further fascinating the market was the lightweight case’s execution in black, and a camouflage green color, affording the reference a decidedly military-esque vibe. While neither variant of the watch is necessarily “easy” to track down in today’s market, black examples are more common, making the discovery of a green-cased piece no insignificant occurrence.

My favorite details on this watch are, of course, the compass and mirror which are revealed upon lifting the top tier of the case. Though that’s not to say they’re the only nifty features F.A. Porsche worked into the design. Its lugs have always fascinated me, with their asymmetrical indentations on the left side, along with the push-buttons on the bottom, which are used to reveal the compass. In the case of this specific watch, every last facet has been preserved extremely well, making it the best looking example I’ve encountered to date. If you’re a fan of IWC or a Porsche-enamoured enthusiast like myself, definitely give this one some closer consideration.

Gai Gohari of Classic Watch is offering this Kompassuhr for $4,950. For a perfect marriage of form and function, click here.

Many mistakenly deem several different timepieces as the first to have been equipped with a slide rule, but there’s no denying that Breitling did it in the most iconic manner. Whether they knew it or not, applying for the patent 217012 in 1940 would change the brand’s trajectory, and define much of their pilot chronograph production for generations to come. Beginning with the Chronomat, the watchmaker extended the wrist-mounted chronograph’s functions beyond timing, allowing for complex calculations for the sky-faring flock.

With a largely flawless dial and an unpolished case, there’s a lot to love about this watch, but its appeal isn’t limited to condition. Not only is this a clean Chronomat, but an early Chonomat, at that. This is a first execution example, characterized by the absence of an applied logo, along with the presence of blued steel syringe hands, and script reading “Breitling,” as opposed to the later executions with block text in all caps. What’s more, is it’s also quite complete, with its original box and hang tag still in the fold.

Typically, chromed cases are of lesser interest to collectors for the simple reason that their stainless-steel counterparts are more durable. But the chromed Chonomat (say that 10 times fast) represents an exception out of left field. Ref. 769 was manufactured with stainless steel, pink gold, gold plated, and chromed cases. Production of chromed cases can be traced from the reference’s launch in 1942 until only 1946, making it the most rare case variant of the bunch. Naturally, this makes examples like this one extremely tempting to Breitling-focused collectors, as opportunities to own one don’t arise all that often. I’ll definitely be watching closely to see what this piece achieves.

An individual based out of Frankfort, Kentucky has this piece listed on eBay in an auction that’ll end Sunday afternoon. At the time of publishing, it’s current bid stands at $2,880, and where it ends up is anyone’s guess. Check it out here .

If you’ve ever had the pleasure of finding yourself in Southeast Asia, you’ve likely heard the phrase “same same but different.” No surprises here, it means just about what you’d expect it to, and just so happens to be a both worldly and fitting introduction to my next pick of the week. Many mistake this watch to be an early Rolex Turn-O-Graph at first glance, but upon closer inspection, it’s Vacheron Constantin branding sets the uninitiated straight. In that rarity and designs reminiscent of others are always interesting niches of watchmaking worth delving into, that’s exactly what we’re going to do now.

You’re looking at the Ref. 6782, which is a bit of a curious chapter in the brand’s story. Vacheron Constantin introduced the reference back in 1962, which as Rolex heads will know, is long after the coronet brand began fitting the Ref. 6309 “Thunderbird” with an engine turned bezel. This isn’t to say the former reference was a malicious copy of a Rolex, but a little inspiration surely factored into the design process. Furthermore, it wasn’t as if Vacheron mass produced these watches so as to cash in on some Rolex cachet, as it’s believed that less than 100 examples were manufactured in total. Which is music to the ears of serious collectors.

Rarity aside, the reference as a whole, and this example, are compelling in their own right for a few reasons. First, it’s powered by the Cal. K1072/1 – the brand’s first movement to receive the Poinçon de Geneve seal. Second, this is effectively the first sports watch from Vacheron Constantin, in that it predates the 222. To wrap things up with a bang, shift your focus towards the dial on this example, which is white with black lacquered indices. Of all the Ref. 6782 dials, this variant and the gold tapisserie are said to be the rarest to have been fitted within yellow-gold cases. This means we’re talking about a rarity within a rarity, like Beethoven’s 5th, or a ’72 Dark Star.

Los Angeles’s Craft & Tailored has this piece listed on its site with an asking price of $12,950. Additional photos of the watch can be accessed by clicking here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An IWC X Porsche Design Kompassuhr, A Vacheron Constantin Ref. 6782, And A Breitling Chronomat Ref. 769

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and happy Friday! Our last Friday and BAL edition before the end-of-year holidays kick off. Are you in need of a last-minute, very nice gift for someone on your list or even yourself? I hope I can help. As this is a weekly feature, a bunch of great watches tend to sell in between. This 1957 Tudor Submariner ref. 7922 that sold on eBay a few days ago for $25,720.12 frustrated me, it would have been a perfect BAL watch. It is a project and needs TLC, but the bones are there for an excellent example of Tudor's first Submariner.

Our highlight watch from last week, the Gübelin-signed Patek Nautilus ref. 3700/1 with box and papers found a home – congrats to the new owner, that is a great, great watch. Our little Cyma Chronomètre has been marked as sold too. On the auction front, the Omega Constellation with Breguet numerals ended up at $820 , and the charming Universal Chronograph sold for $1,600 .

Let's get into this week's picks!

By now, it's fairly easy to figure out that my love for vintage Cartier knows almost no bounds. Proof positive is this 1930s coin watch. Let's start with the coin, it is an 1887 Great Britain five pound coin in 22-carat yellow gold. These coins were minted to celebrate Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee, go figure. It is a part of a set, this being the largest of the bunch.
