Obviously, Queen Victoria is depicted on one side while on the reverse is St. George on horseback slaying the dragon, a centuries-old legend. My coin collecting never moved beyond filling out American wheat penny books, so I am no expert on the matter, but I will say, among coin watches, this is one of the more stunning that I have come across.

For coin watches in general and specifically for Cartier coin watches, this example appears to be noticeably earlier than one would expect. Firmly dating to the 1930s, we see the proliferation of coin watches more towards the 1950s from brands like Patek, Rolex, Vacheron, Piaget, and more. A hint that this Cartier is slightly earlier is the opening mechanism in which a small section of the coin edge functions like a clasp. Later, opening button mechanisms were built into the edge.

A slightly later version of this same Cartier coin watch was gifted to President Franklin D. Roosevelt for Christmas in 1939 . FDR's coin dates to 1902 and depicts King Edward VII instead of Queen Victoria. The watch part of FDR's piece features that later style of opening with a button mechanism built into the now external crown.

The seller, Justin Gruenberg at The Keystone, has listed this Cartier coin watch for $25,000. Get all of the details right here .

A bonus pick from The Keystone is this probably-Genta-designed 1960s Audemars Piguet ref. 5205 in white gold retailed by Türler .

Among the many variants of Heuer's Carrera produced in the 1960s, the "Dato 45" second execution is one of the most sought-after. The result of Heuer's search for the optimal place to put a date window on the chronograph-equipped Carrera, the "winking" motif of the dial, has long attracted collectors. Put simply, the quirkiness of the dial layout here is what excites Heuer fans. While most people might be drawn to the black dial variant of this reference, the one we riffed on for our TAG Heuer Carrera Dato Limited Edition for Hodinkee in 2021, I quite like the subtlety of the silver variant as I am a proud owner of a ref. 3147S first execution.

This one comes to us via an eBay seller in France and is seemingly very fresh. By that, I mean the photos aren't the best, but the watch does not seem to have been messed with at all. The case appears to be unpolished, and the dial shows a bit of patina but is generally clean. The seller notes that the dial is scratched but as far as I can tell from the photos, that scratch is without a doubt on the crystal – simple fix with some PolyWatch. This is how I like to find them.

Adding rarity but not a ton of desirability is the added line of text on the dial reading "FAB. SUISSE." This indicates, along with the hand-engraved number on the caseback, that the watch was initially sold in the French market.

An eBay seller in Vannes, France, has put this Heuer up for auction, ending Sunday, December 22, at 3:36 PM ET. At the time of publishing, bidding had reached $1,141. Get all of the details and photos right here .

One of the first "real" dive watches, the Blancpain Fifty Fathoms hit the market in 1953 after, supposedly, the brand's then-CEO Jean-Jacques Fiechter had a near-death experience while diving. While we're not so sure about the origin story, there is no denying that Blancpain's initial attempt at a diver is among the most legendary watches of the category, ever. That said, the market for great FFs is a bit slow right now; it's been this way steadily for the past few years. We can look at that two ways: sound the alarm, no one is going to care about these watches anymore ever again, or we can say, more level-headedly, it's a great time to buy.

When this watch was released, it was a serious professional watch for serious professional divers. Therefore, the distribution process was a bit different than we might imagine it today. They were sold via specialized retailers like Aqua Lung, a manufacturer and distributor of diving equipment founded by Jacques Cousteau's invention of the eponymous breathing device. So, Aqua Lung is not a model name here but rather a retailer stamp taking the place of the Fifty Fathoms model name on the dial. Think of Aqua Lung like the Tiffany & Co. or Gübelin of the serious diving world.

This particular watch has a great, full case and a nice, even "tropical" patina on the dial. The spotting seen on the dial here can happen via UV exposure, light moisture ingress, a combination of the two, or some other unknown factor, all happening little by little over the course of decades. One man's patina may be another man's dial damage, but what I tend to look for in a good "tropical" dial is even aging, which I personally find attractive. If you don't, that's your prerogative. Go find a dial that your eye likes, I won't be upset, I promise.

The seller, Kirill, over at Lunar Oyster, is asking $26,000 (with a 3% discount available should you choose to pay via bank wire). Find it right here .

Here's a quirky one I've never seen before. Put simply, Mido was a lot bigger deal back in the 1920s through the 1960s than we may think as watch collectors today. As someone who has been trolling eBay for years now, you can see how successful Mido was by the sheer amount of watches available for sale today dating to this era. Fire up eBay at any one time and you'll be greeted with hundreds and hundreds of nice vintage Midos for sale. Some of their cases were made by the great François Borgel, they made most of their own movements and were early to the automatic winding game with the Multifort Powerwind series.

1939 was a big year for Mido. The brand released the Multifort, combining automatic winding, waterproofing, shockproofing, and anti-magnetism, all in one watch. There is some debate as to whether they were the first to do this, as Gylcine, Eterna, and Wyler had competing products the same year, but it was still a big deal. You can go back and read accounts from the 1939 World's Fair in New York. Mido, along with some of the other brands listed, were set up in the Swiss hall, showing what these mass-market everyday watches could do. Visitors were greeted by an elaborate display where a Mido Multifort was first held to a magnet, then dropped on a metal plate, and dipped into water before being attached to a moving hand, still running to show off the automatic winding feature.

In another area of the hall, Patek Philippe showed off a watch with a minute repeater, split-second chronograph, perpetual calendar with moon phases, sidereal time, time of sunrise and sunset, and a map of the constellations. Just to give you the vibe of the room.

Anyway, the watch we see here is a commemorative piece made by Mido for the 1939 World's Fair in New York. Honestly, the watch itself is nothing all too special with a square 27mm stainless steel case and a manually-wound movement (obviously, this is not a Multifort), but the dial is amazing. Depicted on the dial are the Trylon and Perisphere , the central symbols of the fair.

The seller, Fabsuisse Vintage Watch, is located in Texas and is asking $5,465 for this vintage Mido. Check it out right here .

In an entirely different part of the market to Mido, Boucheron was making some of the best jewelry-inspired watches and clocks in the same era of the 1920s through the 1960s. Here is an interesting example of that which I found at a smaller auction house in the UK. This is a 1930s or 1940s alarm clock made of sterling silver and yellow gold with a manually-wound movement. As not technically a watchmaker, Boucheron's expertise was not in the internal mechanics aspect of watches and clocks. The brand sourced movements from suppliers in France and Switzerland. I have seen these alarm clocks with Lemania movements in the past and assume this example is the same.

Rather than the movement, Boucheron really focused on case construction. We see it in their various patented case and clasp constructions on wristwatches. Check out some of that in an article I wrote last year . But here, in a seemingly innocuous alarm clock, we see Boucheron's genius at work again.

While the auction house does not show it here, this clock's case was patented by Boucheron under patent number "BT 679614." If you can envision it, the caseback hinges out from the top, which also acts as a bit of a kickstand if you would like. From there, the clock itself sits within the "mid-case," spinning on a pivot on both sides (at three and nine o'clock). You can spin the clock out halfway to reveal the "crown," a small tab at six o'clock that can be flipped out to wind the movement, change the time, set the alarm, and wind the alarm.

This Boucheron travel alarm clock is lot 2134 of Tennants Auctioneers's Jewellery, Watches & Silver sale on January 11th, 2025. Click here to view the lot (I recommend setting a calendar reminder if you are interested in bidding!).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Cartier Coin Watch, An Aqua Lung Diver, And A Boucheron Alarm Clock

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
The year is yet again coming to a close, and watch-wise it’s undoubtedly been a fruitful one. We’ve seen a literal treasure trove of exciting vintage pieces come to market, and command equally exciting numbers, helping paint a broader picture of just exactly what was available back in the golden era of watchmaking, and what now remains. What’s in store for 2019 is anyone’s guess, but if the last few days of 2018 are any indication of what’s ahead, we’re in for a good one.

In the automotive world, you’ll often hear the term “sleeper” used to describe a wolf in sheep’s clothing of sorts on the road. LS swaps, debadging, tuning a station wagon — you know how it goes. You’ve probably heard it used to describe watches, as well, and this is arguably the one piece most deserving of such a title. Without further ado, the ultimate sleeper watch – Patek’s awe-inspiring Ref. 3939H.

First introduced by the legendary manufacturer in 1993, this discrete tourbillon and minute repeater equipped timepiece (yes, both) was produced in all three variants of gold, and one year later in platinum. Having said that, 3939H production is estimated to have been under 300 total pieces, making rarity a major factor in its already considerable desirability. (As a side note, this particular example was sold recently via Sotheby's – you can see the listing here .)

It only measures 34mm across, which is admittedly small for modern tastes, but that’s part of the appeal, in my opinion. The smaller footprint effectively makes this immensely complicated work of art go unnoticed on the wrist, which can’t be said of many minute repeating tourbillons from a top tier manufacture. If you’ve got nothing to prove to the masses, don’t necessarily need to show all your cards, and have the capital to back it up, I’d indulge in what’s perhaps one of the ultimate references to leave the Patek Philippe factory.

Stephen Hallock of TickTocking has this piece currently listed for $305,000. Find more photos and insight here.

Here’s a watch I frankly don’t understand. Don’t misunderstand me: I’m a fan, and a big one at that, with a few of these having gone through my own collection over the years. So what is it I don’t get, you ask? It's this: how haven't these become far more expensive? Allow me to explain.

Certain pieces from Breitling have grown to become awfully collectible in the past couple of years, though curiously, the more subdued, classic chronograph designs are still accessible. They’ve got the same never-going-out-of-style aesthetics we’ve come to adore in watches like Heuer’s Carrera and earlier examples of the famed Rolex Daytona, and no slouch of a caliber on the inside, but still the market seemingly isn’t hooked.

What’s more, is it’s technically even a Bond watch, in that Sean Connery himself wore a version featuring a modified stainless steel case while filming Thunderball . With all this in mind, I’d advise getting in on the ground floor while you still can. If there’s a watch due for more focused scholarship and interest, I’d argue the Top Time is more than deserving.

Miami’s Menta Watches has this example of the Ref. 2002 currently listed for $3,500. Find more photos on their site.

The sports offerings from Rolex are at this point beyond ubiquitous in both the world of watches and pop culture as a whole. Taking a stroll through Instagram will confirm this,; you'll bombarded by the seemingly endless stream of Subs and Daytonas posted on the regular. While I myself love such watches, and am wearing one at this very moment, I thought we’d end the year on a more unique note, with something you truly don’t see everyday.

At first glance, it’s nothing more than your run of the mill Oysterdate, but break out the loupe and you’ll open up a world of awesome. This is what’s known as a “Rite Time,” which was produced with the coveted honeycomb dial in small numbers during the 1950s, and given the resurgence of interest in 34 mm Oysters, we thought we’d shed some light on this obscure model from the Wilsdorf back catalogue.

I’ve always been particularly attracted to the unique case architecture this reference has, with its straighter lugs and more defined edges, and the unconventionally shaped indices only intensity the attraction. For the collector that doesn’t want what’s on the next guy’s wrist, this is your watch.

Matt Forehand of Rusty Crowns is selling this example of this rare Rolex model for $14,500. Click here for more details.

For those deeply immersed in the world of vintage watch collecting, the name Mido should bring to mind a range of exciting and perhaps slightly quirky timepieces to mind. I’ve always been somewhat obsessed with the bright and colorful decompression scale dial that was briefly fitted onto the Powerwind Diver 1000, though it’s the chronographs from Mido that really hit the spot. My introduction to their chronograph production came by way of the Multi-Center Chronographs that were rather popular just a couple of years ago, but lately my center of focus has been the more conventional, two-register Multichrono, after spotting a rather nice example on Ebay this past week.

While the dial is admittedly a tad rough around the edges, all the original scales and details are more than visible, and certainly more than compelling. Its Borgel case — complete with those ornate pushers we oh so love — is beyond sharp (literally), and looks almost as if you could cut yourself on its edges. If you’re still not convinced of its beauty, pop the caseback and feast your eyes on the Minerva cal. 13-20. It’s certainly easy on the eyes, and also stands in horological history as one of the earliest wristwatch chronograph calibers produced.

An Ebay seller based out of Sydney, Australia has this Mido up for auction, with the current bid at Approximately $1,230 at the time of publishing. Find the full listing here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Patek Philippe Ref. 3939H Minute Repeater Tourbillon, A Breitling Top Time Ref. 2002, And A Rolex Ref. 6518 Rite Time

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Some might use today's Bring A Loupe to find a holiday gift at the last minute, so we made sure to feature a bit of everything, from the great classics to more under-the-radar picks. Among the obvious candidates, you will find two very coveted vintage wristwatches: the Rolex Explorer 1016 and the Heuer Carrera 3147N, with its peculiar date at 9 o'clock. We will also cover an interesting iteration of the Olimpico line from Girard-Perregaux, and a sexy blue-dialed Eterna. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 23, 2016.

The reference 1016 is one that vintage Rolex collectors are always looking for, as it combines sporty look and dressy proportions. It was even the number one of John Mayer's Five Best Buys In Vintage Rolex , back in 2012. Since then, prices have increased above the $8,000 threshold, especially for early examples with gilt glossy dials, so turning to the latter iteration with a matte dial can be a smart move. This often allows you to get a stronger case, and less pronounced patina, for much less money, while enjoying the very same case: a perfect 36mm Oyster case with slim profile. The example here is an R-serial, meaning that its R840634 serial number indicates a 1988 production (while the F code engraved on the clasp of the Oyster bracelet stands for 1981; this part is therefore not original to the watch). More importantly, the case does not show signs of over-polishing, and the lugs have kept most of their original shape, both in thickness and curvature. The chronometer-rated automatic caliber 1570 guarantees worry-free functioning, true to Rolex's reputation for accuracy and reliability. Lastly, the dial presents the painted numerals characteristic of the late Rolex 1016, just before this model was replaced by the reference 14270 in late 1989, marking the end of plexiglass crystals for the Explorer line – often seen as the true separation between vintage and modern Rolex.

Eric Ku, the dealer behind 10PastTen, and a previous Talking Watches guest, is selling this late Rolex Explorer 1016 for $8,800.

The Olimpico chronographs have a cult following among dedicated collectors, and not only for their connections to the Olympic Games. A year ago, we covered the Olimpico reference 9075 , believed to follow the reference 8846 offered here. Stylistically, this makes sense as the round case of the reference 8846 appears earlier than the thicker case of the 9075 . The one thing they share is the great chronograph caliber Excelsior Park P40, a manually-wound workhorse with column wheel construction. The merits of the reference 8846 are not limited to its 38mm case; the dial is extremely appealing and balanced, with the external scales in blue and red for the tachymeter and pulsations. The round pushers and lume dots also contribute to its sporty look, as opposed to its no-lume brother, the reference 8862 with less pronounced square pushers. The watch is mint, and is said to have come from the first owner – the type of condition and story we love. It might not be the most well-known vintage chronograph, but it is the type of piece well worth a trip off the well-traveled Daytona or Speedmaster paths.

The dealer RareBirds just listed this sweet Girard-Perregaux Olimpico for €5,800 or around $6,050.

2016 was in many ways the year of vintage Heuer, which soared at auctions, or wherever they were sold. The Autavia was definitely a solid contender, but the Carrera had its fair share of attention, especially the early models boasting the case with faceted lugs, and manually-wound chronograph movements from Valjoux. While the Carrera reference 3147 matches the chromatic effects of the family, it comes with a Landeron 189 caliber which offers a date display. In the first generation, it was placed at 12 o'clock, where it was unfortunately masked by the second hand of the chronograph. This explains why the date window was subsequently moved to 9 o'clock, explaining the "Dato 45" nickname of the latter Carrera 3147. Throughout its entire production, the reference 3147 only came with two dials: silver with matching sub-registers, and black with dramatic white registers (what is also called a reverse panda dial). The black dial here looks great, without issues except for the missing lume dot at 3 o'clock; it is often the first one to fall, sadly, but the others are all whole. The strap is said to be original to the watch, and it comes with the Heuer signed buckle. Lastly, the movement is described as recently serviced, and previously purchased at an established Parisian vintage dealer.

The listing for this Heuer Carrera reference 3147N expires in one day, and bidding is presently at €6,500 or around $6,800.

Eterna is often overlooked when considering vintage watches, despite the iconic KonTiki toolwatches. The model here has a more civilian look, but the blue dial really took me away. The deep color and sun-ray finish reminds me of great 1970s Pateks, while the 35mm case size is absolutely perfect, in my books at least. The case even seems unpolished, judging from the sharp lugs, and deep engravings on the caseback. The fact that this Eterna comes with the original crown, strap and buckle doesn't hurt either; note that all are signed with the characteristic 5-dots Eterna logo. While the hand-wound ETA caliber 2804 might not be the most elaborate movement, it more than does the job, and offers a quick-set date.

The dealer Watchsteez prices this Eterna Date at $635; to me that it is a no-brainer for a good-looking piece at an attractive price point.

To round-up this selection we needed a dive watch, so here is the aptly named Depthmaster. While we would not recommend testing its water resistance (this advice extends to almost all vintage watches, even former divers), there is lot to like about its tool-watch look. The bezel insert has faded nicely, (something you would pay serious cash for in any vintage Sub) while the patina on the indexes has taken on a warm orange note. The hands show some traces of deterioration at the base of their tritium lume, but that's preferable to a relume. Though the dial says Croton, it's a Nivada – Croton was the US-specific branding in the wake of a lawsuit by Movado, claiming that Nivada sounded too much like Movado and could confuse consumers. Like the previous Eterna, it is powered by an Eterna movement – the automatic caliber 2472 with date, which here would benefit from a cleaning.

The Davidoff Brothers listed this Croton Depthmaster for CHF 2,500 or around $2,430, which seems nonetheless a bit high for this watch based on available comparisons. If you are into divers, the sellers also have a great Aquastar Deepstar (Lorenz-signed, but still with the stunning oversized sub-register at 3 o'clock).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Late Rolex Explorer 1016, A Rare Girard-Perregaux Olimpico, A Black Heuer Carrera Dato 45, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This Bring A Loupe covers a few brands that rarely make it into BaL: a rare platinum Panerai or a crazy Movado Super Sub Sea, it really feels they deserved a spot this week. You will also find a stunning Zodiac, a great IWC Ingenieur, and an intriguing watch from Ochs and Junior. This is your last Bring A Loupe for December 26, 2015.

Broadly speaking, Panerais can be divided into three periods: vintage with most of the production around World War II, then pre-Vendome between 1993 and 1997, and finally Vendome (Richemont). Their relative desirability also follows this simple pattern, if we want to simplify things. The Panerai PAM21 is one clear exception to that simplistic rule: while it was only launched in 1997, Paneristis consider it as one of the most covetable references. This Radiomir has a 47 mm platinum case housing a manual-wound movement from Rolex, matching the earliest production from Panerai, the mythical reference 3646. The PAM21 also displays the characteristic dial layout that made Panerai famous, and offers fixed wire lugs reminiscent of its military past. The lucky number 8 – out of a total production of 60 watches – is listed for $150,000 on Puristpro here . This asking price is above the recent winning bid of another PAM 21 sold at Phillips Hong Kong for 812,500 HKD, or around $105,000. Also just consider how crazy this watch is...imagine Panerai asking Rolex to make a watch like this today . It would never happen, and that's even more reason why we love the PAM21 so much.

You might also know this chronograph as the Zenith reference A3736; this alternative branding is actually consistent for Zenith watches sold in the United States through the 1970s. Zenith and Movado had indeed merged in 1969, and the Zenith trademark for the U.S. belonged to another corporation. Therefore, after the merger, Zenith watches were then either branded Zenith-Movado, or simply Movado. The Super Sub Sea chronograph owes its name to its 200 meters water resistance, functioning like a Compressor model, with the left crown rotating the inner bezel measuring the diving time. The 41 mm square case houses the famous Zenith 146HP, the last manual-wound chronograph movement from Zenith, which was eventually swept away by the automatic caliber El Primero. The watch here comes with the original Gay Freres ladder bracelet that can also be found on the early El Primero chronographs reference A386. Last but not least, the combination of the grey dial and the orange hands is better than lovely, although the lume on the hour hand appears cracked. You can find this striking chronograph on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding had just reached $3,000.

"For the man who expects the impossible from his watch" – this was the way the ads described the first IWC Ingenieur. This IWC is indeed a serious contender among the first proper anti-magnetic watches, the obvious competitors being the Rolex Milgauss and the Omega Railmaster — and there is also the lesser known Eberhard Scientigraf that we covered earlier . The IWC Ingenieur reference 666-AD could actually be considered technically superior to those three since it is the only one to have a date display, an additional opening increasing the complexity of fighting magnetism. In the end, the Ingenieur achieves to remain amagnetic until 80,000 A/m or roughly 1,000 gauss, like the Rolex. The Ingenieur is clearly functional, but it boasts a fairly dressy case, with a great 36.5 mm diameter. You might also notice this tension on the dial, with the straight font of the Ingenieur line clashing with the IWC font, something that can also be said about the amagnetic Patek Philippe reference 3417 . A French collector is selling his well preserved Ingenieur — with the original box — for 6,500 Euros or around $7,100 on the Chronotrader forum here .

Nivada chronographs are not shy: they claim to reign everywhere, on the ground, in the air and under water, hence the the Aviator and Sea Diver lines on the dial. You will found those under several names between Nivada, Nivada-Grenchen and Croton; once again legal battles in the U.S. are responsible. Movado had indeed sued Nivada in the early 1950s on the basis of a phonetically similar name. Nivada then decided to locally add Grenchen — its production center in Switzerland — on the dial, or replace it with the name of its U.S. importer, Croton. Under any branding, the Chronoking stands out with an unusual date placement at 12 o'clock; this quirk actually gives a very interesting balance to the dial that can be also found in some early Heuer Carrera reference 3147 . To achieve that, the Nivada uses the caliber Valjoux 234, also found on the later Tudor Monte Carlo reference 7159, although the date is at 6 o'clock on the Tudor. The Chronoking comes in a well proportioned 38 mm case that wears nicely with its large bezel; note that the minute hands is unfortunately a bit dirty here. The Italian dealer Casowatches is selling this ChronoKing here .

You might be more familiar with the French company Zodiac for its inflatable boats, the eponymous Swiss manufacturer has a strong track record of producing rugged watches, the Sea Wolf among other. This reference was one of the very early dive watches, it was launched in 1953, the same year as the Blancpain Fifty Fathoms and one year before the first Rolex Submariner. The Zodia-Chron is another highly considered element of their production, actually stared in the reference book Chronograph Wristwatches . Zodiac's fixed tachymeter bezel reminds of the Omega Speedmaster or of many vintage Rolex Daytona; the Zodia-Chron also shares the famous Valjoux 72 with the latter. In addition, the Zodiac is quite dashing with its brushed silvery step dial, visually enlarging the 37 mm case. The U.S. dealer Mentawatches just listed this uncommon chronograph for $2,750 here .

The dial of this watch offers a layout that you are probably not used to: the 31 holes there represent each a day of the month. They also increase the legibility of the watch, holes being even minutes, and the gap between two holes standing for odd minutes. Beyond this fascinating design, Ochs and Junior swears by principles that we hold dearly at HODINKEE: a high attention to details and a lot of pain staking manual finishing. This creativity is mirrored by the many opportunities of personalization that we had described here and a limited production, around 35 Date watches per year. You can find more about this unique concept here , including an online customizer. A U.S.-based collector is selling his example for $4,000 here , if you like the grey color this one is for you.

2015 was very much a strong year for vintage Heuer, so it was fitting to conclude with a rare reference presently available: the Carrera 7753 NST. The reference 7753 is the quintessential Carrera, featuring the celebrated down-turned lugs around the 36mm case. It came with a variety of dials, including the much coveted black dial with white sub-registers — technically a reverse panda dial — with an added tachymeter scale printed on the periphery of the dial. This technical description does not do justice to a crazily attractive combination. The example here kept a great case – even better it comes full set, with original box and papers. It seems that the original retailer actually made a mistake on the papers, incorrectly quoting the reference 3647, which displays a 45-minute sub-register instead of the 30-minute from the reference 7753. The case number mentioned matches the one engraved on the case so it looks more like an honest mistake than recovered papers; in addition, the French writing makes sense in regard to the "Fab. Suisse" mention on the dial. Finally, the pushers are incorrect for this reference, they should smooth and non fluted, although those parts would be fairly easy to replace.

The seller is considering offer above $9,000 here , he also listed it for $12,000 on eBay here , which gives you an expected valuation range. Finally, what appears to be a mint three register 2447 NST Carrera ended on eBay from Poland this morning at just over $14,000. The watch looks completely honest and unmolested, a rarity among Carreras these days.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Very Rare Panerai, A Great IWC Ingenieur, A Stylish Movado, And Much More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
For the last Bring A Loupe of 2016, we focused on the most unusual vintage watches we could find over the past week. Those include a forgotten Breitling Compass from the 1980s, and a practical Girard-Perregaux Gyrodate that boast a high-frequency chronometer movement. You will also see a military Rolex Daytona reference 6265, and a vintage-inspired and fairly unknown Grand Seiko in yellow gold, the reference SBGW004. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 30, 2016.

While we tend to shy away from gold plated cases for Bring A Loupe, we had to make an exception for this Girard-Perregaux. Launched in the 1960s, the Gyrodate builds on its rotating bezel to display the days for an entire month, as long as you know how to line up the rotating bezel with the inner scale (and its catchy Sundays in red). It is a pretty unique display, and the fact that it comes with a high-frequency chronometer-certified automatic movement makes it all the more interesting. As indicated in the original booklet below ("Meet the Gyrodate") the reference 9080 either came with a black or silver dial, with a stainless steel or gold-plated 35mm case . All versions run with the automatic caliber 42.1, a chronometer grade movement that offered a frequency of 36,000 beats per hour. The seller indicates that the watch comes with the original strap and buckle, both signed with the GP initials. He also describes this Gyrodate as in new old stock condition, although it seems on the picture provided that there is a small impact to the bezel, just above the digit 1.

The dealer MM Antiques listed this unusual Girard-Perregaux Gyrodate for £1,400 or around $1,715.

At first glance, this Daytona looks like a regular reference 6265 with its screw-down pushers and stainless steel bezel. Obviously, it does not offer a "Paul Newman" dial (read Ben's Reference Points to catch up on that coveted configuration) but this watch needs to be turned around to be fully appreciated. The engravings on the caseback reveal its military origins, more specifically for the Peruvian Army. The Peruvian Airforce (Fuerza Aerea Del Peru) had indeed issued some Rolexes for its pilots, starting in the early-1960s (after previously entrusting Omega). The caseback is definitely the key part to check on any Rolex from the FAP, since the serial number is also engraved on the inner side with FAP watches, while it is only engraved in between the lugs of civilian models. The Peruvian Army ordered diverse models from Rolex such as Submariner or GMT-Masters, but most of the chronographs that it got did not bear the "Daytona" mention on the dial – although exceptions exist. Additionally, this example features the "Sigma" dial (from the symbols at either side of the "T-Swiss-T" line); they indicate that the indexes were made of solid gold. The case is described as lightly polished and the handset as original, which is great news for a military-issued watch that probably underwent many services. The dial is in great condition, but I disagree with the seller regarding the lume plots: based on the picture it seems those at 3 and 6 o'clock are mostly gone, which often happens but still ought to be noted.

This military Rolex Daytona reference 6265 is priced at $89,700 by the US-based dealer IconicWatchCompany.

You might often hear about vintage Breitling around here (especially from Mr. Toledano ), but 1980s pieces do not get much recognition. Many would say that they are more of an acquired taste, but there is no denying that the reference 80940 deserves a closer look. With its black PVD case in aluminum it already looks and feels tool-ish, but it's when it's opened that it reveals the full extent of its skills. Inside is a compass that can be removed, or read through the back, while the strap features several scales for reading a map. And the Morse code , just to be on the safe side after an emergency landing... This watch was engineered to work in extreme conditions, to meet "military and scientific requirements" according to the original ad. So you won't be surprised that the caseback shows a checklist of all necessary items for an expedition, from food & water to fire making equipment and ammunition. And the watch is obviously waterproof to 300ft while its quartz caliber ( ETA 955.112 ) is said to run under the most extreme temperatures. This peculiar reference was only produced in 1985 and 1986 (the highest serial number I could find indicated a production just above 650 examples, while the 800-number is often brought forward), so it is something of a rarity, especially in the new old stock condition described. One caveat though; the provided strap looks different than the original one.

A French collector offers this rare Breitling Compass for €4,000 or around $4,195.

Despite its vintage looks, this Grand Seiko was only released in 2001, to celebrate Seiko's 120th Anniversary. As such it is virtually identical in looks to the very first Grand Seiko ever produced, with the caliber 3180, whose ambition was to beat the very best Swiss dress watches. And just looking at the sharp hands, the wonderfully balanced dial and the elegant case, one could definitely say: "Mission accomplished". However, the reference SBGW004 is virtually unknown, as the 300 pieces were only sold in Japan at the time, and since then, it has remained in the shadow of the reference SBGW040 (also a Limited Edition of 120 examples) that was available 10 years later for $16,500. Both watches honor the original reference 3180, but I would go for the SBGW004 given its more accurately reproduced dial, which bears the original "star". Interestingly, most consider that this symbol indicates the presence of an applied logo, while it more likely represents the material of the applied indexes , since Mk1 and Mk2 dials for the 3180 did not have the applied logo but still featured this distinctive "star". In all fairness, the manual-wound caliber 9s64 of the later SBGW040 comes with a longer power-reserve than the 9s54 on the SBGW004 you see here (72 hours vs. 50+ hours, and 2011 vs. 1998 for the conception) but I could easily live with that. This example comes full set, with the original box and papers, and it's said to be in great condition.

A collector just listed this rare Grand Seiko SBGW004 on TimeZone for $8,350.

Heuer had a longstanding history with regatta timing , introducing dedicated stopwatches in 1959, and the first Skipper wristwatch nine years later. Very quickly, those chronographs moved from the original Carrera case to the Autavia one, which benefitted from a higher water resistance – not a small detail when you are on a boat. Launched in 1983, the Heuer Regatta reference 134.603 follows the same logic, but offers a revolutionary display allowed by the automatic caliber Lemania 1345 , also used for the same purpose by Omega, Aquastar and Tissot. The apertures allow you to track the countdown to the start of a regatta, each of the 5 minutes changing colors for every elapsed minute as described on a detailed review of the Regatta , or on the original documentation below. The reference 134.603 offered a grey-PVD case, while its sister references came with the olive- and black PVD finish that can also be found on the Autavia of the 1980s. The present watch also kept its original Jubilee bracelet with the Heuer-signed clasp. It is indicated as in good condition, with the chronograph function working properly, which seems backed up by the pictures of the listing, showing the countdown in action.

You can find this Heuer Regatta on Ebay at a Buy-It-Now Price of $3,659; the seller indicates he'll consider lower offers.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Selection Of Unusual Watches, Including A Military Rolex Daytona Reference 6265, A Girard-Perregaux Gyrodate Reference 9080, A Breitling Compass Reference 80940, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week's Bring A Loupe is mostly about chronographs, one of the most useful (if not the most useful) complications out there. I could not resist featuring a couple of Heuer and Universal Geneve; they simply look too good! You will also find a rare Eberhard Contograf with an interesting date display, and a smaller Submariner from Tudor. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 4, 2015.

A Heuer Camaro had already made its way into our favorite watches under $5,000 . While this example might set you above this budget, it really demonstrates the appeal of the Camaro, especially this reference 73443 with the date at 6 o'clock. Frankly, this is one of the best looking and best preserved Camaros I have seen in a long time, with a stunningly sharp case, and a warm patina that nicely complements the two orange hands. In addition, it comes with the original Gay Freres bracelet, sporting the correct HLA endlinks. This watch is a testimony to how beautiful a watch with a characteristically 1970s design can really look. This outstanding Camaro can be found here , and the seller is accepting offers until December 6th.

The Contograf is highly sought after by collectors for at least two reasons: its in-house movement, that allows an unusual date placement at 6 o'clock, and the well proportioned 39 mm case. This chronograph from Eberhard was launched in the 1960s, with either a silver dial or a black dial. Only the black dial features an alternate roulette date disc, with black numbers for the odd days, and red ones for the even days. The watch here belongs to the first generation as evidenced by the sword hands, which were replaced by straight hands for the second execution. Unfortunately, the hands on this example are replacements, as disclosed in the listing; this explains the lack of patina on the central luminous lines. The bezel might have a few rough scratches, but the sheer rarity of this chronograph more than makes up for this flaw; additionally, the original bracelet is included with the watch. AnalogShift is offering this Contograf here .

This Tri-Compax underlines Universal Geneve's ability to balance functionality with good legibility, and that is not a small challenge, especially in a 35 mm case. Think about it: the dial manages to feature a triple calendar, a chronograph and a moon phase indication without appearing cluttered for even one second. The dial deserves a good, long look thanks to the handsome brushed finish characteristic of the reference 222100-1; furthermore, the lack of any external scale improves the overall layout between the four sub registers and the two date registers. Finally, the dauphine hands bring a nice crispiness that I would have liked to see on the twisted lugs too, unfortunately they have been somewhat over polished although overall, this is still a very appealing watch. This Tri-Compax is listed for 8,400 Euros or around $8,950 on a French forum here .

The French-spelled day disc gives away the provenance of this yellow gold dress watch, which is about to be auctioned in Paris. Funnily, the month sub-register is still in English, otherwise May would be spelled Mai. But enough with the French lesson, let's focus on those outstanding thick lugs: they really make up for the restrained dimensions of the case, which is a very vintage 33 mm diameter. In a way, those lugs match well the massive winding crown, and really anchor the watch on the wrist. And, again, the dial layout is admirably balanced in order to display a triple calendar and the phases of the moon. I have a fondness for the typeface of the 12 o'clock index, which brings an unexpected flamboyance to an otherwise classical and elegant timepiece. The French auction house Pestel-Debord will offer this Universal Geneve on December 11th; estimates range between 2,000 and 3,000 Euros or around $2,300 to 3,200 here .

From our visit into Tudor's archives , you can make out the similarities in design between some of Tudor's diving watches and those of its sister brand, Rolex. Of course, there are minute differences (the movement for one thing) but also, very often, the indexes and the hands differ. However, the reference 76000 brings something unique: a 36mm case you would never find in any Rolex Submariner. Produced between 1987 and 1990 – according to the reference book Tudor Anthology – this smaller Submariner existed either with black bezel and dial, or with those parts in blue. If you were looking for a sturdy diving watch with a restrained diameter, this reference, or the later 75090, was made for you. Note that the watch comes with its original box but the Oyster bracelet included is a generic one, you would otherwise see the Tudor logo embossed on the clasp – the seller correctly pointed that out in his description. This smaller Submariner is listed on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding was just above $1,000.

The round case and the domed crystal give a vintage feel to this Grand Seiko; the reference SGBM031 was actually released in 2012 as a limited edition for the 10th Anniversary of the classical Grand Seiko GMT line. It offers the same 39.5 mm case, but boasts a very seductive blue lacquer dial, with an interesting orange color (almost a gold) for the GMT hand and Arabic numerals. The true specificity of this model can be found on its back: its rotor is made from an mix of titanium and tungsten, emblazoned with a rearing lion, the Grand Seiko emblem. The blue color of the rotor is the same color as the dial, and the power-reserve is upgraded to 72 hours, which allows you to put the watch away for a full week-end with any need for winding and be confident it'll be ready to put on Monday morning. A Japanese seller is selling one for $3,800 here , in line with the recent auction price from Sotheby's .

I was very excited to see this rare version of the Heuer 7753 offered for sale this past week, but this military chronograph was swiftly picked up. Heuer manufactured a very limited number of this reference back in the 1960s to answer a 250-unit order from the Belgian Army. Interestingly, it seems that the total production ended up above the number specified in the order – probably around 300 pieces, underlining the rarity of this special Carrera. This offshoot of the regular reference 7753 boasts a very appealing dial with big luminous numerals balancing the short Heuer label. It is also a unique Heuer with this early case shape, known to feature the movement Valjoux 7733, while the Valjoux 7730 powers the remaining two-registers Carreras with the same case. The story ends beautifully as this watch was somewhat reunited with its past: it actually flew back to Belgium to its new owner, an active duty officer in the Belgian air force.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Knockout Selection Of Vintage Chronographs From Eberhard, Heuer, And Universal Geneve

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe and to New York Winter Auction Weekend 2024! Same as we did for Geneva's auctions last month , this expanded edition of Bring A Loupe will serve as your guide, wrapping up lots on offer between the three major auction houses and hitting on as many brands, price points, and trends as possible. My favorite part about New York auctions? No travel is required; the lots come to me! The preview slate was less of a mad dash to see everything in one day and more of a steady chipping away. Plus, I had the luxury of Mark Kauzlarich's assistance on some of the photography.

My thoughts per lot will lean towards economical, mindful of word count with a ton to cover, but we did go in-depth on a few auction topics already. At Phillips, Mark has you covered on the top lot, a unique Philippe Dufour Grande & Petite Sonnerie , Malaika detailed the TimeForArt benefit portion of the sale , and Ben highlighted a group of lots from an important US-based collector (himself!) . Finally, at Sotheby's, I dug deep into the vintage Heuer selection – an area of collecting near and dear to my heart .

We're looking at four sales across three houses, here is the schedule:

Sotheby's – Important Watches: Friday, December 6th at 10 AM EST

Phillips – The New York Watch Auction XI: Saturday and Sunday, December 7th and 8th at 10 AM EST

Christie's – Important Watches: Monday, December 9th at 2 PM EST

Sotheby's – The GOAT Collection: Watches & Treasures from Tom Brady: Tuesday, December 10th at 7:30 EST

Compared to Geneva's 1,161 lots on offer, New York is a breeze at 557. I asked you to grab a cup of coffee before previewing the Swiss sales; maybe a double shot of espresso will get you through this one? Let's get into it.

The season will kick off with the Sotheby's main sale, dubbed "Important Watches," on Friday morning. Geoff Hess and team are offering a broad catalog of 252 lots, in contrast to the "curated" theme of Sotheby's most recent sale in Geneva. During a quick chat at the preview, Hess was abundantly clear that he has a total sale number to hit, passed down the Sotheby's org chart. Less multi-million dollar lots mean more quantity and, for the bidder, a decent chance to nab an overlooked watch (like this , this , or this ).

While high in quantity, the clear theme is storytelling and provenance. You can't go 15 lots without running into something with a story behind it. Lot 13 is former President Gamal Nasser of Egypt's Rolex Day-Date, on offer fresh to market via his family. Even a 20mm in diameter platinum Patek Philippe ref. 1289, lot 33 , has a backstory. It was awarded to recipients of the 1958 Knudsen Award for outstanding scientific research.

Lot 37 is a Cartier New York Tank dating to 1929, originally owned by Clarence Douglas Dillon, a member of a prominent New York family who served as Ambassador to France under President Eisenhower and Secretary of the Treasury for Presidents Kennedy and Johnson. That provenance is great but its current owner is no slouch either – TikTok's Mike Nouveau. Unearthing the watch and story, check out Nouveau's video here .

As a baseball guy, lots 34 and 35 jump out to me – both formerly owned by Joe DiMaggio (where have you gone). The Jaeger-LeCoultre, retailed by Cartier (lot 34), will draw the majority of attention here; it's on trend, has a great caseback engraving, and is accompanied by an engraved 14k gold ID bracelet. Hidden on the inside of the nameplate is a number that looks a lot like his social security number, which is funny. Though, I am particularly drawn to the ultra-thin white gold Audemars Piguet (lot 35). This watch has not been seen since a sale of DiMaggio's personal effects in 2006 at Hunt Auctions , selling for $1,900 (!). Also, in this 2006 Hunt sale, a similar AP made $1,200, selling again for $31,250 at Christie's in 2017, and his famous Patek Philippe ref. 130, which did $85,000, last selling for $281,250, also at Christie's in 2017. All this to say, there are collectors out there for DiMaggio's many watches, and whoever bought them at that initial Hunt auction presumably did very well for themselves.

Not a fan of history? There are plenty of flat-out exceptional, dare I say Important, watches as well. I'll be watching lot 21 , a Patek ref. 5208P to gauge the market for modern grand comps and, on the other end of the spectrum, lot 43 , a solid condition ref. 1518 in yellow gold that is offered with an attractive estimate. Fancy an ultra-rare modern Nautilus? Lot 77 , a ref. 5723/112R-001, is about as rare as they come, one of reportedly four examples stamped by Tiffany & Co. And on the heels of the record-breaking Journe result in Geneva , lot 88 , a brass movement Résonance in platinum, will be a tell as to any ripple effect.

Finally, lot 225 , a 1865(!) Patek Philippe bird box, which blew me away at the preview. Between 1860 and 1870, Patek produced a run of 12 bird boxes with watches, this being one of them. Right, 12 were made 150 years ago(!). The silver box is adorned with this intricate turquoise and black opaque enamel, the bird is so cool to watch (check it out here ), and the enamel scene on the top depicts, of course, Lake Geneva with Mont Blanc in the background. This is museum level.

I'll reiterate here that there are many stories already posted to Hodinkee regarding lots for sale at Phillips this weekend. I'll use this space to call out my picks but you can get your Philippe Dufour Grande & Petite Sonnerie, lot 12 , coverage from Mark here , your TimeForArt charity auction, lots 81 to 105 , coverage right here from Malaika , and, of course, a detailed description of each of his own watches, lots 30 to 34 , from Ben here .

What's that? You demand to know which one of The Ben Clymer Collection(™) watches would be my pick? Fine. It's the Laurent Ferrier Hodinkee LE, lot 32 . My favorite Hodinkee LE ever made, and serial number 01? That's enough to make me forgive its deeply troublesome provenance.

I'll be watching lot 10 , the first Rolex Daytona "Le Mans" to come up for public sale. After a few (dozen?) have traded hands privately, it will be interesting to see what the auction market thinks of Rolex's hottest release in years (decades?). I can imagine the room will crescendo early with this Le Mans and the Dufour two lots later, but a sneaky pick in the aftermath is lot 22 , a double reference 6536/6538 Rolex Submariner. Reluming hands is becoming more and more accepted for vintage Rolex, and this watch is in such exceptional condition elsewhere. That being said, in an auction catalog, project watches don't tend to do all that well. It's in with an attractive low estimate of $50,000.

Lot 24 , a Patek ref. 530 Calatrava, should tell us just how real the current elegant, time-only trend is. The 530 is the ultimate vintage Calatrava. Not only are these 1.5mm larger in diameter than the ref. 570, but they're rare. So rare that collectors barely knew they were real until a letter from Patek surfaced in 2004 confirming that some time-only 530s were made (typically the reference number is used for chronographs). Anyway, Breguet-numeral 530s, in particular, go crazy at auction. Other examples have sold for 1.4m CHF in 2016 (steel case, black dial) and $403k in 2014 (a yellow gold case, black dial). This one is in yellow gold with a champagne dial, all confirmed by the extract – I would hope it surpasses the high estimate of $200k.

Phillips New York always has a certain vintage lean to it. There is no better place if you're after a dead-mint yellow-gold ref. 6263 Daytona, for example – lot 27 is exactly that. You might have heard that London-made Cartier watches are in vogue? Lot 36 is a one-of-two-known London Tank Divan that might just start a bidding war. If this is a must-have for a collector, this may be their only chance – the other example sits in the Cartier Collection, likely never to leave, and is in far lesser condition.

Among the most stunning vintage Pateks I've seen this year is lot 54 , which is probably a unique reference 1436 split-second chronograph with Breguet numerals. Opposite Patek's signature on the dial is "Leading Apprentice Hialeah Park 1941," leading us to believe it was first owned by a jockey by the name of Wendall Eads, who won this award. In 1941, Eads was a big deal, taking home $7.3m in 2024 money by winning 150 races and placing in 110. It's an already rare watch (one of about 25) made unique by a "custom" dial. This watch last sold as a part of the Gordon Bethune collection at Christie's in 2012 for $182,500 .

Lot 120 is a previously unknown, fresh-to-market Rolex ref. 6100 with a Stern Frères-made cloisonné enamel dial dubbed "La Caravelle." Another example with this same dial in a ref. 6102 sold for 1.27m CHF this past May. These two dials are so identical that they both feature the same design code on the rear, hand-engraved by master enameller Mrs. Marguerite Koch. I fell in love with the 6102 in May, was surprised by the strong result, and am now picking this 6100 as my favorite lot of the auction season.

Before we move on, here are two sleeper picks near the end of the catalog. Lot 141 is a Vacheron Constantin "shutter" watch that is well-sized and gorgeous in the metal. And lot 150 is a Vacheron ref. 4072 chronograph. Condition-wise, this is far and away the best vintage chronograph of the season. Collectors should start to respect vintage Vacheron a lot more and should do so relatively soon before prices start to creep up.

Moving to Monday and to 20 Rockefeller Plaza, Christie's is continually investing in its New York-based Watches team, and the catalogs are getting stronger and stronger each season. Lot 20 , a Richard Mille UP-01, will draw attention early, thanks to the presumed price tag. This is the second example to hit the auction market after Antiquorum's 1.73m CHF result last month. Lot 29 , an Eska with another Stern Frères and Marguerite Koch-made cloisonné enamel dial dubbed "Cirque," should speak to anyone who loved our most recent Talking Watches with Adam Victor .

Lot 45 is a Rolex Milgauss ref. 6541 with some lovely patina, but that's not the full story here. The watch is on offer via a middle-man consignor who recently acquired it via the family of the original owner, a former CIA operator by the name of Roberto Pichardo. As a part of the CIA's Brigade 2506, Pichardo was a part of the infamous Bay of Pigs Invasion, in which he was ultimately captured and held in Cuban prison for 20 months. A few years after he returned to the U.S., he treated himself to this Milgauss. The watch is accompanied by its original papers, sales receipt, and a provenance letter from Pichardo's family.

A couple of unique shapes carry the middle portion of the catalog. Lot 47 is a probably unique Patek Philippe ref. 1252 "Chameleon." When Antiquorum sold the last example of this reference for 387,500 CHF in 2023, there were two known, the other being in the Patek Museum. Now, there are three known, with this being the only example in two-tone pink and yellow gold with a pink dial. The other shape is none other than the Cartier Crash. A 1986 Cartier London Crash to be specific, lot 49 . In really excellent condition, this example comes from a known batch of three Crashes made in 1986, all with the same serial number, 24175, and A, B, or C following. This one is the middle child, "B."

Lot 52 is a Vacheron Constantin 222 in yellow gold. That description might not make you drool alone, but the condition should – this is one of the nicest examples I've ever handled. It is accompanied by its original hang tag and a matching 222 money clip. Because, why not? Speaking of condition, if you were to have picked up that dead-mint yellow gold 6263 Daytona at Phillips, lot 66 would complete the pair. Equally as mint, this ref. 6265 would be great for nights when you want something a bit more flashy than the plastic bezel of the 6263 (how uncouth!).

A Rolex with star indices is always going to grab my attention. But lot 67 , a "Galaxy" ref. 6098 is made all the more special as it is fresh-to-market from the family of the original owner and has a unique provenance. The consignor's uncle, Julius "Julie" Holpit, received this Rolex as a 25-year appreciation gift from Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation. Holpit was Grumman's fifth employee and served as the leader of the Experimental Manufacturing division, building the first prototypes of Grumman's airplanes, testing, and, generally, figuring shit out as they went. The watch is rare, too, one of about ten examples with this dial. That's a recipe for auction success.

Looking for a vintage Patek Calatrava that may fly under the radar? Sorry, I'm stuck on aviation all of a sudden. Well, lot 71 , an early ref. 570 with a two-tone dial and long signature, could be the one to watch. Or if niche early Lange is your thing, check out lot 93 , an 1815 Up/Down in platinum. This is a reference I have been lusting over for years.

Back over to York Ave for Tuesday evening's sale of The GOAT Collection at Sotheby's. Twenty watches procured by Tom Brady, who I gather was a quarterback in the National Football League, are on offer as he publicly retools his watch box. Alongside memorabilia like lot 6 , Brady's wristband from Super Bowl LI (the one where he and the Patriots came back from a 28-3 deficit in the second half), you can bid on TB12's Tudor Black Bay 58 "Friends of Hodinkee" Edition, lot 5 . I may have to register for a paddle on this one, which is likely my best shot to add the legendary Hodinkee-stamped Tudor to my collection.

While many IWCs and a few Richard Milles are on offer, I am particularly interested in following Brady's vintage pieces. Lot 34 is a 1917 Patek Philippe grand complication pocket watch retailed by Tiffany & Co. for which Brady had a custom "TBXII" box made. The estimate may seem punchy at $200k to $400k, but a non-Tiffany example sold for €216,480 at Artcurial in January. Lot 36 is a clean Rolex Daytona ref. 6241 "John Player Special" that was made somewhat Brady-famous when he wore it to a special ceremony at Gillette Stadium in 2023 , his big reunion with the Patriots fans. I'm most curious about lot 39 , the second Patek ref. 3970 ever produced. Will the historical significance of the watch be lost amidst all of this Brady hoopla? I'm clutching my pearls!

Speaking of pearl-clutching, lot 41 , an Audemars Piguet Royal Oak Tourbillon piece unique made especially for Tom Brady, has induced more of it than just about any other watch I can remember. When we first saw it, at Netflix's roast of Tom Brady, a portion of the watch world saw it as an attack on everything they hold dear. Then, when Sotheby released the catalog for this sale, that same portion of the watch world was outraged that Brady would flip such an important watch so quickly. Tough to please! The estimate is $400,000 to $800,000. The watch is almost infamous at this point, and there are a ton of Brady obsessives who would do insane things to own something like this. Let's see what happens on Tuesday.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: The Great Big 2024 New York Auctions Preview Edition

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
After a two-week hiatus Bring A Loupe is back, with a killer selection of vintage watches. The Rolex GMT-Master reference 6542 is logically the star of the week, as it comes in great condition and retains its original bakelite bezel. On the dressy side, the Patek Philippe "Disco Volante" reference 2552 scores high as well, hiding a sumptuous automatic movement. You can also discover the Mimo Loga, a forgotten slide-rule watch that competed with the Breitling Chronomat and the Juvenia Arithmo in the 1940s. Lastly, two great chronographs from less obvious brands step in – a mint Minerva (with a gorgeous in-house Minerva chronograph caliber) and a beauty of a Nivada Chronoking Aviator Diver, at a no-brainer price. This is your Bring A Loupe for December 9, 2016.

The specific case shape of the Patek reference 2552 explains its "Disco Volante" ("flying saucer") nickname, yet there is another reason to look at this watch beyond mere aesthetics. It boils down to its movement, the caliber 12'600. It was indeed the very first automatic caliber ever made by Patek, first released in 1953 and produced until 1960. Its exceptional construction and lavish finishing are detailed in Ben's love letter to the reference 2526 , but a quick look at its sumptuous rotor suffices to make a lasting impression. The reference 2552 offered here is said to have remained in its original owner's family, and the seller accurately discloses that the second hand is a replacement part. The gold hallmarks on the side of the lugs show some previous polishing, but they are still present and legible, which is always a good sign.

This Patek 2552 is offered for $13,750 on the forum Breitling-source .

The 6542 is the Rolex GMT Master that started it all, back in 1954. Its ingenuity is striking; the combination of a second hour hand and a 24-hour rotating bezel still remains one of the best ways to display a secondary time zone. It exhibits a lot of the traits from early sports Rolex, from a smaller 38mm diameter to the lack of crown guards and the use of radium as lume material on the chapter ring dial. And, of course, it made a noticeable appearance in a James Bond movie, when it was worn by the pilot Pussy Galore in Goldfinger . One of the best feature of this 6542 is actually a seemingly simple part: its bakelite bezel. (If you're wondering, bakelite was an early industrial plastic, invented in 1907.) These are fairly fragile and were often swapped for more durable but less charming metal replacements. The case shows great lugs, while the gilt dial is remarkably preserved; this is a really rare combination to observe in any tool watch produced more than 50 years ago. The watch also comes with a box and the booklets documenting a sale in 1960. It is the same date that can be found on the "Big Logo" clasp, so the riveted bracelet is fairly close to the production year of this GMT (circa 1958, according to its serial number).

The dealer Matt Bain has this amazing GMT-Master 6542 for $135,000 on its website .

The Breitling Chronomat is often thought of as the first wristwatch with slide rule, but that honor actually goes to the Mimo Loga, which was the first in July of 1940 to apply for a patent regarding the use of logarithmic tables on a wristwatch, one month before Breitling (it seems that a similar patent was made for a pocketwatch in 1936). Juvenia followed in 1945 with the Arithmo model, still using the principle of scales to help perform calculations. (Circular slide rules are older than you might think – the first was from around 1630!)

As such, they were truly the smartwatches of their time, when electronic calculators took up the space of an entire room – not so bad for a 37mm wristwatch! The condition of this example is described by the seller as mint; the uncommon black dial and the different calculating scales seem indeed unmolested, but you would need additional pictures to evaluate the case.

Cestino offers this rare Mimo watch for €7,500 (or around $7,960) on Chrono24 , while this watch can also be seen on the Instagram page of this French dealer.

Minerva is often overlooked when it comes to vintage chronographs, and this reference 1335 from the 1940s shows how unfair that is. The dial is spectacular, in its two-tone silvery finish, and with applied numerals. And the 34mm case is impressive in its own right, with well defined lugs (fixed lugs as you should know), a waterproof case back, and large pushers. Its chronograph movement is the respected 13-20CH caliber. It was made in-house by Minerva, something worth mentioning since the company was eventually purchased by Richemont in 2006 for its proven skills at making chronograph calibers, which now equip Montblanc and Panerai.

You can find this Minerva Chronograph on Carsandwatches , there it is priced at €5,499 (or around $5,920).

While all other picks this week come from the 1940s and '50s this Nivada very obviously belongs to a later decade, as evidenced by its dial and the use of tritium (the luminous material was only used in watches from the early 1960s until the late 1990s). The Chronoking's legibility is impressive, partly due to its 38mm case size, which allows for a tachymeter track on the edge of its dial. The mirror finish of its sub-registers and the large font on its hour-minute rotating bezel definitely play a role there too. And the unusual date placement at 12 o'clock adds some character, courtesy of the chronograph caliber Valjoux 234, which is a higher beat version of the classical Valjoux 23 movement.

You can get this Nivada Chronoking for a Buy-It-Now at $2,499 on Ebay , and the seller will also consider lower offers.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Stunning Rolex GMT-Master 6542, A 'Disco Volante' Patek Philippe Reference 2552, A Technical Mimo Loga, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Weston Cutter
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
By way of introduction, here's my favorite watch story. In early 2017, my dad texted a picture and asked, " What do you think of my new watch?" I stared at the picture for a bit as my heart sped up, then I stood and started pacing to try to calm myself down.

He owned his own watchmaking company and occasionally helped sort through watch collections. I'd soon learn the watch he'd texted about had come from one of dozens of boxes he'd been helping dispose of, containing the leftover contents of a deceased parent's watch and jewelry shop that had closed in the 80s. Finally tired of paying for storage space, the adult children had enlisted my dad to process the watches and sell them on eBay for them.

By 2017 I'd been buying and selling vintage watches for several years, though I had, as the song says, no key, no plan: I'd gotten into buying and selling watches because a) Hodinkee made vintage watches really fun and interesting (especially Bring A Loupe , of which I've been a dedicated Friday reader since it was called What's Selling Where ) and b) my dad could repair anything I got that needed help. For years, my dad and I talked daily about watches: I'd huff excitedly about something I'd found, or he'd answer my endless questions about movements, brands, and complications.

One day, I called, and he was just delighted, talking about how he'd always loved LeCoultre Memovoxes, and how he hadn't seen a black-dialed one like this before, especially one with that big of a case, and in stainless steel, too. He asked if I'd ever seen one like it, and I explained that while I hadn't seen one in person, I knew what the watch was, which gave him pause.

Wes : But it's just an old Memovox. Right? Me: It is an old Memovox. (long pause) Wes: Is it not just an old Memovox? Me: Not quite. (another long pause) Wes: Well then what is it?

I told my dad to enjoy his watch and to call when he wanted to know more about it. A week later, when he called, I explained what he had, but for that week, he got to experience a vintage LeCoultre Polaris as just a watch . He enjoyed it in complete innocence, thinking not once of its price, or that it was one of only 1714 made, or who he could impress with it. He just got to like it, which he did.

All of which is a slightly long way of saying: Hi, I'm Weston Cutter. I'm thrilled to be bringing Bring A Loupe back in 2026, and the story of my dad's Polaris is my north star. There are plenty of reasons to dig watches, but a key one has to be pleasure, an enjoyment of these complex mechanical wonders simply for what they are. Thanks in advance for reading and for leaving comments telling me what I've missed or how I'm wrong—I promise I won't mind. And while I've seen a lot of watches, I'm sure I'll miss something—as Coleman Hawkins said, "If you aren't making mistakes, you aren't really trying." With that said, let's get into this week's picks.

Vintage dog-leg Constellations with pie-pan dials are notorious minefields (it's probably safer to say vintage Constellations in general are minefields). This watch— almost certainly a reference 168.005 —isn't some esoteric or deep-cut example: it's just a great, clean watch for anyone who can appreciate some of the best watchmaking Omega ever did.

The case seems to retain its original lines. On dog-leg Connies, given the tiny bevel on the outside edge of the lugs and the softness of gold, too, this example is in excellent shape. Beyond the case, this watch checks so many boxes. There's the decagonal crown, a caseback sporting no egregious scars and no evidence of heavy polishing to clean them up, and the dial's an absolute thing of beauty. If you know your vintage cross-hair Connies, you know the MOY test is critical, and this one passes swimmingly.

You'll note, too, that the dial's got no lume, with the hour markers having onyx laid atop the gold hardware, and the hands with black fill where the lume would otherwise be. And of course, the watch itself is (likely) powered by the legendary Omega cal. 561, a justly celebrated movement that punched way above its weight class. It must be said, you'll have to either polish or replace the crystal. As you'll doubtless get tired of hearing me hector: make friends with your local watchmaker if you haven't already (in this case, preferably someone with an Omega account).

Currently bid up to $1800 at the time of writing, this is one of the nicer all-gold Constellations I've seen in a while. The estimate is $3000-$4000, though I'd note the fundamental question around the value of dog-leg Connies is currently bubbling up in the community . Again, this isn't an uncommon watch in and of itself. However, if you're in the market for one, it's not often you see one in this condition. FWIW, in the same auction, there's also this excellent second-series Patek Ref. 96, which is worth closer attention if you're into that sort of thing (i.e., masterpieces).

My first horological love was two-register late-60s and early-70s chronographs, the more oddball or outré the design, the better, an admission I make simply to acknowledge that I continue to feel a tremendous nostalgic fondness for them. It's senseless, of course. I could've just as easily been sucker-punched by a Swatch or a dive watch, but you don't ever really choose what you love.

Which is to say: old Seaboard Yacht chronographs are, to me, total delights . There are three iterations of this particular watch, with the current example being a second execution—straight lugs, stick hands, and a Landeron 248 beating inside. From the sort of golden era of anyone-can-make-a-watch (i.e., you could buy cases and movements, get someone to print a name of your choosing on the dial, and presto), Seaboards were made for the Seaboard Marine Supply Co, in Islip, NY, your one-stop shop not only for yacht-timers but Wiggins and Sea-Lok cleats.

Everything in this example looks fairly excellent—a clean dial, a case that shows use but not abuse, and the original handset and crown. The crystal will need to be polished or replaced, and, depending on your style, you may want to have the (presumably) social security number etched into the caseback polished out. But those are fairly small prices to pay for such horological glory, are they not? At only $500 at the time of writing, this watch still has eight days left of bidding. The estimate is $1000-$1500; given the market, it'd be hard to make any strong argument about where this will or even should end up.

If the Sinn EZM1 were a potential partner, it'd be the dark/brooding/mysterious type with a sketchy past who equally thrills and discomfits you. I've personally owned an EZM1, and of the few watches I've owned and moved on from, it's the one I return to with the least clear thoughts. I know, intellectually, that it wasn't quite right for me, but every time I see one, I sort of stupidly hope maybe I've changed and I'm finally ready to be the sort of guy who wears an EZM1.

Originally released in 1997 (with a 500-piece limited edition model, the EZM1.1 , released in 2017 to celebrate the 20th anniversary), the EZM1 is a destro chronograph—crown and pushers on the left. Powered by a Lemania 5100, the watch has a central minute chrono counter, which, to my knowledge, isn't, like, statistically proven to be more legible or anything, but it's just cool. It's got the date in red at 3:30, and the 40mm titanium case feels not entirely dissimilar to the classic Autavia C-case from the late 60s (the lugs also look recognizably similar). As if all that weren't enough, the EZM1 was an issued watch to, among others, a unit of Germany's ZUZ (if militaria connections move you), and tested to 30 bar.

The example on offer looks to be in excellent shape, as shouldn't be all that surprising for a watch roughly 20 years old (and Sinn's watches generally are as tough as they come anyway). This is from late in the EZM1's run, so the lume plots have not aged as some of the earlier iterations have, and, if I were chasing this one, I'd pony up for a bead-blasted titanium bracelet to go along with it. FWIW, I don't even really remember what didn't work for me about the EZM1 I had: maybe it was the red date blending in with the black dial? Which somehow bugged me so much that I sold mine. What a fool I was. Please, I beg of you: learn from my mistakes. Bonham's will be selling this on the 25th of February. Begin psyching yourself up now. If you're not into the Sinn, Bonhams is also offering this great-looking Nivada Grenchen Depthmaster in the same auction .

I'll admit (again), I have a weakness for chronographs. Please note that I would absolutely be in the running for being the watch-wearer least in need of a chronograph, and I make zero pretense of even harboring any macho tendencies. I drive and enjoy driving my Mazda5 (it's a minivan; mine's gray). The most hardcore thing I do (other than the messiest janitorial work of parenting) is probably going for bike rides or, occasionally, disposing of the dead bodies of squirrels that've foolishly made their way first into the backyard and, eventually, between the jaws of my dog.

So no, I have no functional need for a chronograph, especially not something as elegantly sporting as this Universal Genève from Matthew Bain , featuring not only a tachymeter ring but a rotating divers bezel as well (I could, I suppose, use it to time how long a squirrel has before the dog chases it down to murder it, but that'd feel somehow unsporting). And yet, good lord, just look at the thing.

There's the 40mm case. There's the Gay Frères bracelet. There's the dauphine hands and that heartbreakingly beautiful black dial with white subdials and blue tachy ring. There is—yes, this is picky and minor, but so are most things about watches—UG's excellent typefaces for their subdial numerals (again: you don't choose what you love). The legendary Valjoux 72 beats inside this one, a movement I cannot help but have an overwhelming fondness for, given that it's the engine ticking inside many of the watches I've ended up falling hardest for.

Matthew Bain has this uncommon beauty for $18,500, and if I weren't driving a car worth less than a quarter of that price, I'd be tempted.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: We Throw It Back To The Classics With An Omega, A Universal, And An Off-Beat Chronograph In the Return of 'Bring a Loupe'

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Weston Cutter
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's Friday yet again, which means it's time to dust off your trusty loupe and mentally spend imaginary money on drool-worthy watches (or, you know, actually buy them—your mileage may vary). Thanks, all, for the kind words of welcome last week for my first outing running the column . The only watch from last week's Bring a Loupe to sell so far is the gold Connie, which sold for $3,400 (with a 25% buyer's premium). Rather than resting on the options we already know are still out there, let's go find some new things, shall we? This week's watches include a diver, a Datejust, a 1940s Tudor, and a fun (depending on your point of view, admittedly) example of one of the watches that defined the "Jewel Wars" in watchmaking in the 1960s. Onward to the good stuff!

I'm sure we've all got our own... well, I'm not even sure what. They're not secret watches per se , but they're so generally unfamiliar to even most other enthusiasts that they feel private—a bit like loving a band you only half hope blows up. For a long time, my ultimate private watch was the Favre Leuba Deep Blue, which has since been reissued as the Deep Raider . One of my others has always been the Zenith S58. Plenty about the watch is still unknown; here's a great thread with some early information, and H's own Rich Fordon covered the S58 for Rescapement here . As fun as it'd be for someone at Zenith to clarify everything about the model's history, it's almost immaterial: the mystery neither adds to nor detracts from the allure of the watch itself.

The specific watch on offer— from a private seller at Omega Forums —is as nice an example as you're likely to find, and priced better than any other S58 I can find. A black-dialed Mark IV, the dial looks extraordinarily clean, the hands and lume are excellent, and the case has the light wear that's always surprising on a 60+ year-old sports watch. I'm a bit amazed this watch is still available, to be honest; I hope, for my own sake, it sells soon so I can stop mentally selling things to purchase it.
