Therefore, this Universal Genève exhibits all the traits of the Spillmann design for waterproof chronographs, from the large bezel to the angular lugs and the screw-down caseback. As a true Universal Genève, this Compax nicely combines its practical abilities with some dressy attributes, such as the rose gold handset and indexes. And as always, the legibility is exemplary, with large sub-registers and blued hands to read the chronograph, powered by the caliber 285. Both the dial and case are incredibly crisp for a watch manufactured in 1941, and the deep engravings on the caseback confirm that the case remains unpolished. The seller indicates that the watch comes with an Extract from Universal Genève Archives.

The dealer Iconeek is asking CHF 8,800 (or around $8,825) for this mint Universal Genève Compax .

Just three weeks after the record-breaking Autavia 2446 that sold for $200,000 , a new example is now hitting the market. It shares the same dial configuration, the first execution called the "Big Subs" (big sub-dials) for its oversized chronograph counters. Yet, we know it was made later, as it has a later handset, the early full lume hands being replaced by the second execution dauphine hands . Like any 2446, the chronograph is powered by the trusty Valjoux 72, and has a screw-down caseback. The example here belongs to an experienced collector who was one of the first to contribute with pictures to OnTheDash back in 2002, which eventually led to an impressive registry of known early Autavias . The case of this 2446 is very sharp – very likely unpolished, judging by the large bevels on the lugs. The aged dial almost appears to have a fumé finish on the large sub-registers, while the lume on the hands has a very seductive patina also found on the hands, which are described as original. The strap is very likely not the original (one can always dream...) but the buckle is absolutely period correct.

The highest bid for this early Heuer Autavia 2446 was announced at $55,000 with offers accepted until this afternoon, 5 PM Eastern Time.

This is the second reference 22410 featured today, and has one of the most striking dials you will ever see. This futuristic dial has also appeared on references 12251 and 12392. There is some debate as to whether the dial can be found on more tool-watch cases, like the 38mm waterproof 22410, but it is now accepted that two versions of this dial exist. Nonetheless, it was promptly snapped up when the dealer MentaWatches offered it for sale at around $50,000.

The whole Compax thing got really interesting when members of the Omega Forum , a few hours later, spotted another of those dials for sale at "just" $2,500 . Needless to say, it was gone in seconds, and one can only wonder when and where it will resurface.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Two Universal Genève Compax Reference 22410, A Rolex 'Double Red' Sea-Dweller Reference 1665, A Heuer Autavia 'Big Subs' Reference 2446, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This Bring A Loupe will be dedicated entirelely to everyone's favorite complication, the chronograph. You will find an extremely rare (and very exotic looking) Zenith El Primero, the reference G383, produced in only 200 examples in 1970. We also scoured eBay in order to find some relatively affordable chronographs, most of them under $1,000. As you might expect, no Heuer or Universal Geneve made it this week given our budget constraint, but you will discover some other really exciting watches from Wittnauer, Minerva, and Mathey-Tissot. This is your Bring A Loupe for February 19, 2016.

This Minerva is a good example of a nice looking three-register chronograph that does not break the bank. The Minerva VD712 is powered by the famous Valjoux 72 (also found in vintage Rolex Daytonas) and apparently this chrono was even used by the Swedish army – you can find some great shots of the military engravings here . The dial here notably features a base 100 scale, mostly used by engineers to calculate, for instance, the output of a production chain within a certain timeframe (this is one of the less well known and less often seen chronograph scales). The condition of the 36 mm case is quite good, and the dial much better than it looks in the pictures – often, scratches on the plexiglass can give the illusion that the dial has faded in some parts; other images from the listing confirm that the Minerva logo and the red outside track are both fine. This promising Minerva just broke the $1,500 mark on eBay here .

This is one of the rarest, and craziest, El Primeros that Zenith ever produced. Only 200 examples were made, but this fact pales in comparison to the exuberant "Poker Chip" dial. Its design is absolutely unique, and it manages to be over-the-top and weirdly seductive at the same time. The indexes here might raise some questions, as they do not feature the surrounding metal markers seen on most of the other G383 – this subject was actually covered on some forums here and there . That said, other similar examples can be found: one other is currently listed, with papers, on Chrono24 , and some documentation also refers to this dial configuration here . The jury is still out regarding this dial – Zenith might simply have ordered two runs of dials for the G383, for the versions with and without the integrated bracelet.

Matthew Bain has this dashingly unconventional El Primero listed here .

Wittnauer is also one of those brands too often overlooked – and this chronograph is a good example of that. The black dial version of the Professional Chronograph is way more prevalent, but the brushed silver dial creates a dramatic effect, especially in contrast to the red 24-hour external track complementary to the world-time bezel. Its dimensions are pretty contemporary: 40 mm case with 20 mm lugs, like the modern Rolex Submariner. And everything on our checklist looks pretty good: great case, original signed crown, and serviced movement. Overall, the lume is attractively patinated – although it is a bit crackled on the hour hand. Yes, all this proves that Wittnauer was not only the U.S. importer of Longines, but they also made some very decent watches as well.

The dealer Watchsteez is offering this stunning Wittnauer for $2,650 here .

A quick run through eBay history reveals that this chronograph has been repeatedly offered on eBay over the past month, with the asking price decreasing from $3,300 to $1,750 today. Why hasn't it been picked yet? Well, the condition of the 44 mm (!) case could be better, and it has been polished pretty heavily – uou can see that from the dulled edges of the barrel case and the half-erased markings on the case back. The two missing lume dots on top of the dial are also arguments for a discount. That said, the grey dial with the red tracks is eye-catching, and inside you will find a Valjoux 726, an improved version of the same Valjoux 72 mentioned above. Note that this chronograph model is showcased on Mathey-Tissot's website here . Mathey-Tissot is not related to the Tissot brand in any way, but you might have heard of them as a favorite of Elvis Presley, or as the manufacturer of many vintage Breguet Type 20s. You can find this Mathey-Tissot for $1,750 on eBay here .

This is not a watch for the faint of heart, with its 47 mm case and 14 mm thickness. But if you are open to its atypical case shape, the Hamilton Fontainebleau is actually a great pick – a smart way to get a coveted panda dial for a reasonable amount. Here, I really like that the seller mentions the chronograph hands reset correctly, as finding out this isn't the case can be a costly discovery with a chronograph movement. This Hamilton Chrono-Matic uses the famous caliber 11, which is automatic thanks to its embedded micro-rotor from Buren and often identifiable by the setting crown at 9 o'clock. You can find the caliber 11 on many other chronographs from Hamilton, Heuer, and Breitling; in the mid-1960s those brands united in the race for the first automatic chronograph movement against the two other players, Seiko and Zenith. All came up with a different solution, back in 1969. This bold Hamilton is offered for $1,099 on eBay here .

Last but not least, Wakmann – who you might know as Breitling's local agent in the U.S. They also offered some pretty sweet watches, but let's get the facts straight – those were never manufactured by Breitling, unless the watch is double-signed Breitling/ Wakmann on the dial, as Breitling was doing in France with Lip or UTI. Nonetheless, this Wakmann is very reminiscent of the Top Time line from Breitling, with the same attractive reverse panda dial, but tops it with some really cool orange hands – although I am not sure that the hour and minutes hands are actually original. I would have expected some straighter hands than this syringe shape, more in line with a similar example offered here . You can find the interesting Wakmann on eBay here , at the time of publishing bidding was below $400.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Crazy Rare Zenith El Primero And Five Nicely Priced Chronographs From Wittnauer, Minerva, Mathey-Tissot, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and happy Friday! This week's selection starts with a watch I have never seen before, and I can imagine you haven't either. Then, it moves on to some old classics from our friends at Cartier London and, of course, Movado. Buckle in.

As for results from last week, I am happy to report that one of the nicest Tudor Submariners to ever come to market has found a new home for $80,000 . The 1930s Geneva Sport Chronograph sold relatively quickly at the asking price of $6,300 – congrats to PeteAron on the pickup! On eBay, our Old England "Jumbo Baignoire" sold in a flash at $199, and the Bulova Accutron for Hershey Chocolate Company was marked as sold at $749 . All in all, the column was four for five last week. Great work, everyone.

Let's get into this week's picks!

In 2003, Karl-Friedrich Scheufele, co-president of Chopard, told Europa Star, "Either you are a manufacturer, or you're not. There's nothing in between." When we look at Chopard watches from the 1990s, we can see and feel the spirit of that quote. Now, Scheufele was mainly speaking about Chopard's development of its first in-house movement, the caliber 1.96, but the watch we're looking at today shows the brand's rounded approach to what being a "true" manufacturer in the 1990s meant. Yes, you must make a great time-only movement, and it should be perfectly finished, but also, if a client comes to you with an insane request, you better be able to take an ébauche and execute that request.

Now, let's get the obvious out of the way—this piece unique is, well, unique, to say the least. It has a very, very distinct look. The finishing, open-work nature, and rarity drew my attention. Then, the case, pushers, and crown nearly completely turned me off the watch. But, that being said, the more I dug in, the more I fell in love with this very expensive, one-of-a-kind Chopard.

The bi-retrograde perpetual calendar chronograph complication is fairly novel. To my knowledge, Roger Dubuis is the only other brand in this era to produce something similar. Both brands did so on the same ébauche , which is notable because it is the legendary Lemania 2310. Simply put, your favorite chronographs are probably based on this same Lemania caliber. Patek (5004, 3970, 5970, 5070), Omega Speedmaster, Vacheron Cornes de Vache, the list goes on. And I don't mind drawing the throughline to Roger Dubuis because, in the 1990s, very few brands were making watches on the level of Roger Dubui s . If you have ever handled one, you'll know what I mean. To put this Chopard on that level is really a compliment.

Beyond technical specs and finishing, the more I observed and researched this watch, the more I fell in love with its very 90s aesthetic. I love the rounded edges of the "dial" and the screws holding it to the outer chapter ring. It's quite charming!

The seller, Kirill, over at Lunar Oyster, is asking $75,000. Find it right here .

When I saw a gaggle of Cartier London pieces pop up at a small auction in the UK, my interest was firmly piqued. Upon closer inspection, I have some gripes or concerns with a few. These are normal concerns with vintage Cartier watches, such as possibly replacing hands, dials, etc. That said, this Tank Allongée checks out to my eye and could prove to be the rarest of the bunch anyway.

When Jean-Jacques Cartier took the helm of Cartier London after WWII, he took some time to get comfortable, then began pursuing his passion for wristwatches. Whereas most of the Cartier family focused more on jewelry, it is relatively easy to identify Louis Cartier (designer of the Tank, Tank Louis Cartier, Santos, etc.) and Jean-Jacques as the genuine "watch guys." When we think of Cartier today, we're thinking of designs and models introduced by one of these two men. The Tank Allongée is seen in a few Cartier London advertisements in the 1960s and is said to be one of Jean-Jacques' first riffs on the classic brand style. It's no secret that I am a fan of the London Workshop's creations. I am always following the market and looking for new listings. I have only ever seen this model a couple of times, one in white gold in a collection, another in yellow gold owned by Cartier, and I believe I know of one other.

Some listings will quote this model as the Tank "Mini" Allongée, but I'm not so sure I would classify it as such. The auction house only gives us 17mm as the width, but these measure about 30mm(-ish) tall, which is actually quite nice on the wrist. This model is perfect if you can stomach a skinny, slightly undersized Tank. It certainly wasn't made as a mini or a ladies' wristwatch then.

This Cartier London Tank Allongée is lot 427 of Dreweatts' Fine Watches, Pens, and Luxury Accessories sale on March 20, 2025 (set your calendar reminders, I'm early in highlighting this one!). Check it out in full right here .

Switching up the pace here, this Porsche Design Ultra Sportivo is a great way to get in on the PD look for less than $1,000. The partnership between Porsche Design and IWC ran from 1978 to 1997. While the PD Chronograph 1 and Compass Watch from earlier in this run catch most of the attention, I have always been intrigued by these later models from the 1990s. Here, we see the partnership grow into an entire collection. PD offered a "normal" watch, the Ultra Sportivo, a dive watch, the Ocean, the world's first titanium chronograph, the Titan, and still, the Compass. Of the four, the various Ultra Sportivo iterations, found on both Hublot-esque straps and full titanium bracelets as we have here, are the most accessible way to get into the PD design language.

Towards the end of the partnership, we can observe a branding shift in these watches, focusing more on the Porsche Design language than the IWC. Rather than a full cursive "International Watch Co Schaffhausen" on the dial at 12 o'clock, here we have "PORSCHE DESIGN" loud and proud with a smaller "by IWC" at six. The reason for this shift is fascinating. By the early 90s, the Porsche Design lineup was said to have been 20% of IWC's sales, and these watches were doing quite well. As a result, PD looked to invest more heavily in watchmaking. The Porsche family poked around purchasing IWC at this time, unable to jar it from JLC's, or Mannesmann's, grasp. The family also reportedly made an offer on Vacheron. In the end, Porsche Design acquired Eterna in 1995.

As Porsche Design was getting Eterna up to speed, the brand was less interested in giving IWC the more prominent branding on PD dials. Given the non-Tritium lume on this watch, I assume it was made in one of the last few years of the IWC partnership. As you might expect, the Ultra Sportivo is quartz-powered and ultra slim, wearing very comfortably and retro-feeling at 32mm in diameter. It is a great daily wearer and an interesting piece of Porsche Design history.

An eBay seller in Madison, Wisconsin has listed Ultra Sportivo for a Buy It Now price of $900. Get all of the details right here .

As the interest in early Patek Philippe Calatravas starts to increase, it only seems inevitable that similarly elegant time-only dress watches of the same era will also rise in interest. As regular readers will know quite well, I am a big fan of both Movado and the casemaker François Borgel, the latter being Patek's supplier for many steel ref. 96 cases, and all ref. 565 cases. This Movado dates to the late 1940s or early 1950s and is sometimes branded "sport" on the dial, thanks to the Borgel screwback case. We see a lot of Movado Borgel cases, but this one is a bit earlier than most, featuring a more rounded midcase and lugs rather than the typically found more angular design of the 1950s and 60s.

There is a lot of radium lume here, so keep that in mind, but the condition of this watch looks great. The patina on the lume is attractive, and the case looks quite full. Plus, you even get a cool Movado box of the period! At 34mm in diameter and 10mm thick, this is very suitable for wear in 2025, not appearing too undersized on most wrists.

This Movado is lot 1033 of Bamfords Auctioneers & Valuers's The Bakewell Watch Auction on February 27, 2025. Click here for the full listing.

To wrap things up, we have a fun eBay pick. This very Art Deco purse watch is a fun departure from our standard wristwatch fare. No one tell Mark, but I still find pocket watches a bit repulsive. And I don't mean that in the full meaning of the word repulsive but more that when I see a standard round pocket watch, my eyes sort of glaze over. Purse watches, or even shaped pocket watches, do a bit more for me from a design perspective. This is a classic purse watch case design where the "watch" portion slides out from the top and hinges to mimic what we might recognize more as a travel watch. It is in solid sterling silver and dates to 1930, if you can tell that already by the case finish and dial design.

Don't let the word purse scare you either. Plenty of gentlemen of the time preferred a watch like this stuck in their pocket rather than a wristwatch or a standard pocket watch. Purse is simply a naming convention that has stuck!

An eBay seller in Belgium has listed Art Deco Omega for a Buy It Now price of $999. Check it out in full right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Piece Unique Chopard, A London Tank Allongée, And An Art Deco Purse Watch

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Interesting patina and objective rarity are the name of the game this week. In this installment of Bring A Loupe, you'll find picks like a seldom-seen Movado chronograph featuring Breguet numerals, in addition to a rare and early Rolex Explorer with potentially notable provenance. In an effort to shed more light on square-cased watches, there's another rather stunning Movado, again with Breguet numerals on its patina-heavy dial, plus a Universal Genève that enjoys an affectionate nickname. Here we go!

"Watch collectorese" is one of the most fascinating and funny dialects, and I happen to be fluent in it. This internationally understood tongue is made up of a handful of languages, a seemingly endless number of esoteric abbreviations, and seeing as it's 2019, hashtags have now entered the mix. Given the taste making reputation Italian collectors enjoy , it makes perfect sense that some of the best watch collecting terms hail from that boot-shaped nation. In a past installment of Bring A Loupe, we discussed the Ovettone, and with that out of the way, it’s time we move on to another linguistic curiosity that’s equally as fun to say. Enter the Cioccolatone! You might've heard of this one before, but for the uninitiated, allow me to play translator. Cioccolatone are square-shaped timepieces, notably produced by Vacheron Constantin, Rolex, and Omega, among others. With wide bezels and lightly rounded corners, they bear an unmistakable resemblance to the little chocolates you might have been lucky enough to receive from a special someone last Thursday. In a world that's undeniably dominated by round wristwatches, I thought we’d take a look at one of my favorite takes on the Cioccolatone, produced by Universal Genève.

What you're looking at is the ref. 10239/1, and a particularly sharp looking example at that. I've always loved the integration of a circular dial and crystal into a square case, especially when it's executed as tastefully as such. With an engine-turned bezel, chunky lugs, and an applied Universal Genève logo, this reference is bound to hold your interest for years to come, just as it captivated me at first sight. I'm guessing this example has possibly been polished at some point, though the case still remains thick, and has what appears to be an import hallmark. The dial also looks to be pretty clean, and with a quick polishing of the crystal, the full extent of its beauty will be unleashed. This is definitely a case of "bad pictures, nice watch." An eBay seller based out of Cold Spring, New York, has this piece listed with a starting bid of $950. Follow this link to take a stab at making it your own.

Watches are often described as under appreciated in an attempt to stimulate the market and generate interest, but know that when I use the term, it's simply because I think something is downright cool and far too many are sleeping on it. There's no angle being worked here – just a watch obsessed nerd who wants to share some insights and geek out a bit over wrist-mounted trinkets. For a long time, Movado could've been described as under appreciated, but it would seem that the masses are growing increasingly more hip to the genius that lies in the company's early watchmaking efforts.

The brand did produce a wide range of Calatrava style pieces and more complicated calendars, though the chronographs have always been the main attraction in my book. Both the caliber M90 and M95 were trailblazers in the world of chronograph production, as they featured a slightly modular construction which hadn’t been widely attempted at the time of their release. They also happen to be some of the smartest looking in-house chronograph calibers, with architecture like no other, making them truly deserving of your interest and attention.

These chronographs are quickly becoming staples of the vintage watch collecting mainstream, with notable auction results and all to back them up, and as a result, it’s become tougher to find desirable variants which fly under the radar. I do realize that by including this Breguet numeral fitted, M90-equipped chronograph in this week's roundup, I'm practically making it an alien mothership sized blip on the radar, but alas, it was too cool to not share with you. Though its dial might be less than perfect, the multiple scales, blued steel hands, and subtle Movado branding are really special. M90 chronographs of this size are also quite rare, so it's got that going for it as well. This Movado is being offered by an eBay seller in Miami, Florida. At the time of publishing the bidding stands at $719. Find the full listing here.

I strongly believe that the 36mm Rolex Explorer is the greatest sports watch ever made. Even if you set aside their celebrated history and involvement in countless feats of human achievement, the watch itself stands on its own as a titan of design. The case proportions are simply perfect and the dials are nothing if not legible, making for an altogether versatile and to-the-point timepiece that's wildly versatile. Bravely traversing a mountain range? You're set. Black tie affair later that night? Still good to go. Though the long running ref. 1016 is probably what most vintage watch collectors think of when 36mm Explorers are brought up, I tend to prefer the earlier ref. 6610, which you'd be forgiven for mistaking for a 1016. This reference replaced the ref. 6150 – another nearly identical looking Explorer – and incorporated the newer caliber 1030 movement, which had a slimmer caseback than its predecessor. Certain examples were even fitted with dials sporting red depth ratings, along with white seconds hands, creating an altogether more compelling aesthetic. The one in question today features such a seconds hand, and looks to be a top-notch specimen if I do say so myself. While the case has almost certainly been polished, the dial is pretty clean, and all the applications of luminous radium have aged evenly. This is what an honest watch looks like.

Furthermore, you'll notice that the caseback has been engraved with the name A.D. Henderson. Being the insatiable research junkie that I am, I naturally consulted the interwebs to find out exactly who this character was, and think I might have just figured it out. A quick search of the name pointed me in the direction of the Henderson family tree, in which there are many A.D. Hendersons. Given the age of the watch, I'd hazard a guess that this piece could've belonged to Alexander Dawson Henderson Jr., who was one of the driving forces behind the direct-selling beauty company Avon. While it can't be confirmed with absolute certainty that this is the same A.D. Henderson, I think it’s likely, and will post further findings down in the comments should I come across anything of note.

This example of the ref. 6610 will be offered next week by Sterling Vault Auctioneers in the English town of Farnham. The estimate has been set at £16,000–18,000, though bids starting at £12,000 are already being accepted. Check out the listing here.

The 1960s were a great time for chronograph design. While cases grew a bit bigger and many dials got a bit more outlandish, chronographs were still purpose-built mechanisms for demanding professionals whose livelihoods depended on the accuracy of their instruments. Breitling is well known for their history of supplying pilots with chronographs – most notably, the legendary Navitimer, with its several scales and special bezel that allowed for precise calculations while up in the air. Though I might have shared ref. 806s I've come across in the past, I don’t think I’ve shared one as unique and original as the one we’re about to take a look at. Buckle up and prepare for liftoff. As previously mentioned, this is a ref. 806 Navitimer, which is more or less the archetypal Navitimer that'll come to the minds of most upon mention of the model's name. Where it strays from the norm is in the way that its dial has aged, to a chocolate tropical tone. Examples of the 806 have been known to achieve tropical status over years of wear and exposure to the elements, though this is undoubtedly one of the more tropical examples I’ve seen. If the dial wasn't enough to convince you here, direct your focus toward the stainless steel case, which remains unpolished and crisp to this very day.

Its seller has hit the nail right on the head when he states that it's a "time capsule" watch, so much so that it even includes the original Breitling strap that the watch was presumably sold on (not the strap you see in the photo above, by the way). Should you be in the market for an 806, I recommend you both start and end your search with this watch. The bold presence of the reference is only intensified by its tropical dial, making this example a real stand-out piece. This piece is currently listed for sale on Instagram, with an asking price of $12,000. Find it here.

After coming across that first Movado up above, I was lucky enough to stumble upon another example that also features Breguet numerals. Unlike the previously mentioned chronograph, what we've got here tracks only the hours, minutes, and seconds, but all within an uncommon square case. Plus the watch just happens to be powered by another gorgeous caliber. If my knowledge of Movado calibers serves me correctly, this is the cal. 150MN, which, like the cal. M90, is quite the stunner. I've always been fond of the bulbous shape of the bridges in particular. With that said, the main attraction here is without question the dial. Though it might have left the factory looking white or perhaps silver, it has aged over time to effectively match the custard tone of the luminous applications, which you'll find in both the blued steel hands and on the dial's Breguet numerals. The center of the dial, however, looks have to have been produced with a different finish, given how it hasn't aged as dramatically, making for a pleasing contrast.

This piece also has one of the more interesting case designs I've seen in a while. On either side of the square case, you'll find sliding steel components, which run along a track, securing the caseback. Early alternatives to conventional case designs never fail to amaze me, as they reflect the inspired eagerness to try out new ideas that arguably defined watchmaking in the 1940s. All in all, this is a wildly interesting watch, that with the right strap could be an outstanding sight to see on your wrist. Should you be interested, try your hand at bidding on eBay, where this piece is being offered by a seller out of Billings, Montana. The opening bid is set at $300. Click here for the full scoop.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rolex Explorer Ref. 6610, A Movado M90 Chronograph With Breguet Numerals, And A Tropical Breitling Navitimer AOPA Ref. 806

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week we're making a special place for some special chronographs. Some are military issue, like the Breguet Type 20, and others have unusual features, like the full set Heuer Carrera 3647S, or the "Fab. Suisse" dial of the Longines 30CH. We have also included an early Patek Philippe Nautilus, the Jumbo reference 3700, as well as a colorful Zenith Super Sub-Sea reference A3736. This is your Bring A Loupe for February 24, 2017.

This Breguet Type 20 presents all the characteristics of the first configurations, from the anonymous dial to the pear-shaped crown. The Type 20 name comes from the specifications of the French military, which was looking for a chronograph for its pilots. Rumor has it that Breitling was very hopeful for its AVI model, but the French company prevailed and was awarded the contract in 1954, hence this watch getting the reference 5101/54 (note that the modern version, reference 3800 which was re-issued in 1994, was then called the Type XX). The current watch dated to 1954, and uses the caliber Valjoux 222 that allows for its flyback complication. Yet, its most important feature is definitely the engravings on the case back, which confirm its military past. The multiple "FG" lines stand for "Fin de Garantie" (end of warranty in French) which indicates the date of the next planned maintenance for the watch, engraved at each service by the dedicated watchmakers of the French military. So this 38m chronograph shows a fairly long military service, from 1954 to 1981, at which time it was probably replaced by a quartz-powered chronograph. The seller also indicates that the watch comes with a Breguet-signed box (not necessarily original to the watch), and an Extract of Archives from Breguet.

An Italian dealer offers this early Breguet Type 20 for $31,500.

The Nautilus was Patek Philippe's answer to the Audemars Piguet Royal Oak, the two watches being designed by the very same Gerald Genta. The Nautilus was released later than the Royal Oak, in 1976, and offered a different take on the idea of a sports-luxury watch, though like the Royal Oak, it was made in steel for the standard version. Its design stems from a ship porthole, offering a nicely crafted integrated bracelet, which testifies to its luxury positioning (the original price was $3,100, a considerable price tag for the time, and indeed comparable to the yellow gold Patek). The Nautilus was not only about design – its water resistance justified its name as well, while its slim automatic caliber still allowed for an elegant profile. While the exact year of production of this reference 3700 is not mentioned in the listing, we can assume it is an early one, since the minute track of its Sigma dial is made with lines rather than the dots we find later . You should also note that there is a small break in the tritium lume on the hour hand.

The US-based The Keystone just listed this Patek Philippe Nautilus 3700 for $49,500.

Launched in 1947, the 30CH caliber was the successor to the famous 13ZN caliber , while continuing to offer the flyback complication. This movement can be found in many chronographs from the 1950s, and was used for the next two decades, sometimes called the L530 when used in sportier cases. The current watch belongs very much to the 1950s, as its faceted lugs (with an almost spider-like appearance, like the Patek Philippe chronograph reference 1579) can testify. The dial bears the "Fab. Suisse" mention, which indicates a production for the French market ("Fabrication Suisse" or Swiss Made). Indeed, the Longines Archives confirms the delivery of this very watch to its French subsidiary in late 1956. The watch also comes with its original box, and a period-correct Longines-signed buckle.

You can find this Longines 30CH listed on the marketplace of a French forum for €4,500 (approximately $4,750 at time of publishing).

The Carrera chronograph introduced by Heuer in 1963 needs no introduction, but this one caught our eye for one reason: it comes with the original box and papers, something we virtually never see! The warranty card and purchase receipt confirm a production in 1965, eventually bought by a US-soldier stationed in Europe for a very competitive $37.50. The original box is quite a sight in itself, with the Heuer emblem placed inside and outside. The seller mentions that the chronograph movement Valjoux 92 works well, with the original crown (unsigned, as for the first Carreras) and pushers. The handset has taken on a very nice patina that can also be found on the lume plots. The silver dial (hence the 3647 S reference) is extremely legible, following Jack Heuer's wish to create a very practical chronograph. If you add the amazing condition, and all the other goodies together, you can guess that this watch won't stay for sale for very long.

The dealer Watches With Patina priced this full set Heuer Carrera 3647S for $9,000.

The orange inner rotating bezel of the Zenith reference A3736 might tip you off to the intended purpose of the watch: this 41mm chronograph was meant for underwater activity, and therefore was called the Super Sub-Sea, with a guaranteed 200-meter water resistance (although as always, we would strongly recommend not immersing a watch of this age at all, at least not without a gasket change and pressure check). Both the crowns are correctly signed, with the same star logo that can be found on the caseback. The crown at 10 o'clock controls the rotating bezel, that can be used to measure the dive time. The chronograph could therefore be used for other measurements. This Zenith uses a Martel-based manual wound caliber 146 HP that we find in many other contemporary Zenith watches. The condition of this Zenith looks really great: the case is untouched and shows the expected scratches but it has not been polished to death.. The lume on the hands and applied indexes has a light patina, matching in coloration, while the dial remains fairly clean especially in the sub-registers area, frequently a weakness of this reference. The original Gay Frères ladder bracelet is a further bonus; it comes with the correct ZG endlinks and the correct diver extension.

Abel Court offers this sweet Zenith Super Sub-Sea A3736 for €8,500 (approximate $9,000 at time of publishing).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Patek Philippe Nautilus 3700, An Early Breguet Type 20, A Zenith Super Sub-Sea Chronograph, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This Bring A Loupe looks at unusual watches, all with a twist that makes them collectible in their own right. We start strong with a rare military Omega, and a minty Rolex GMT-Master retailed by Tiffany. Chronograph love is there this week as well, with an undervalued Minerva and an unexpected Universal Geneve Aero-Compax. And you'll discover a couple of defunct brands, including the ephemeral Takano and the budget-friendly Clebar. This is your Bring A Loupe for February 26, 2016.

You might remember this super rare military Omega from our visit to Omega's museum collection, here . The Reference CK 2777 represents a crucial step for Omega: a 37mm antimagnetic watch to answer a special order from the British Minister of Defense for the Air Force, in 1952. This reference fights magnetism with a thicker dial and inner soft iron cage, exactly like the first Railmaster CK2914 launched in 1957. This watch bears all the signs of its military purpose: regulation thin arrow on the dial, fixed metal strap bars, and military engravings on the case back. It is incredibly rare to find the CK2777 in this condition, most of the 5,900 watches ordered were called back to have the radium dial switched to tritium. This change is not only noticeable through the circled T applied on the dial, but the repainting of the dial and indexes was hastily done, which explains the Tipp-Ex nicknname of those unfortunate examples. You can find this beautifully preserved, all original military Omega here .

The single word "Tiffany" bumps the value of any vintage Rolex or Patek, this prestigious retailer being a long standing account for both manufactures. The double naming with Rolex stopped in the 1990s, well after this GMT-Master was retailed and sold in 1977. It is actually a late reference 1675, a couple of years before the model was replaced with the reference 16750, which added a quickset date but retained the sweet proportions of the original. Beyond the Tiffany connection, this GMT's condition makes it very special: you get a fat case, all original parts, a tight bracelet, and a great patina, on the lighter side. Could we ask for more? Certainly, Tiffany stamped papers would have crushed it, but alas those have not made it through the past 40 years.

This rare GMT is offered for $25,000 here , and you should have a look at the fat lugs and sharp chamfers.

This was probably not the chronograph from Universal Geneve that you were expecting as the Aero-compax is not currently among the most coveted models of this brand. Hell, this one does not even look like an Aero-compax, neither featuring the 4 sub-registers of the first references, nor the external bezel from the later ones in the 1960s. However, there is nothing fishy here, you are just looking at the reference 22704/3, also classified as an Aero Compax Type B . However you call it, this chrono has a lot to root for: a great 38mm case, a beautiful blued handset, and unusual numerals on the dial. Granted, the movement is not in-house, it is nonetheless the famous Valjoux 72 that you can also find in the vintage Rolex Daytona. This watch even comes with a Universal Geneve bracelet, and a blue Nato for a stealthier look. You can find this early Aero-Compax offered for 7,500 Euros or around $8,300 here . The dealer MentaWatches is also offering a later version – the reference 890101/01 with 41mm case and the 24-hour bezel – for $9,500 here .

The title of this eBay listing is somewhat misleading; this Minerva is not a split second (rattrapante) but "simply" a very nice chronograph. It is indeed the same reference 1335 that we had covered a couple of months ago . So its case does not boast a 40mm diameter as indicated; 35mm is the right figure if you exclude the crown, yet it looks very much in proportion with the 18mm lug width. I have actually never seen a comparable dial before; it is puzzling to me but does not look re-dialed or tampered with. I am of course talking about the large blue base 100 scale, but also about the single Minerva line, I was expecting at least a "Shock-Absorber" mention. Is a the sign of a very early production? Frankly I don't have the answer here. Besides this point, you will get an excellent chronograph movement, the renowned 13-20CH column-wheel movement, which was recently serviced – for an increased peace of mind on your side. You can find this Minerva on eBay here , at the time of publishing bidding had just reached $560.

With the surge in prices for vintage Heuer, the so-called Poor-Man's Heuer has become increasingly coveted, and this Clebar is no exception. Here, the reverse panda dial does the talking: you can get a look similar to the Carrera for a fraction of the price, without the characteristic down-turned lugs of course. On the wrist, the 36mm case would look great, and the cheaper Landeron 248 movement is likely to give you full satisfaction anyway; note that its bridge bears the US import engravings that it shared with Zodiac. In short, you have here a great looking watch, especially if you are not a French speaking collector - Clebar meaning bad dog in French slang, an interesting choice for a brand's name. At the time of publishing, bidding was below $350 on eBay here .

It would not be surprising that you never heard of Takano, a defunct Japanese watch brand that produced wristwatch during 5 short years, until going out of business in 1962. They first tried to compete with the giants Seiko and Citizen with an in-house movement, but soon had to turn to Lanco ebauches, you can find more info about their adventures here . I chose this watch for a single reason: its dial! The mirror finish looks outstanding around the minute track, and I can guarantee that you will never see this fine dress watch anywhere else. A Japanese dealer listed this gorgeous Takano for 58,000 Yen or around $520 here .

Don't have the HODINKEE App yet? Get years of amazing watch content plus new stories, breaking news, and access to great new features like HODINKEE Live, free on iOS .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Diverse Selection Ranging From A $500 Takano To A $25,000 Rolex GMT Retailed By Tiffany

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! Sport watches are so, how do I say this, 2021, right? I kid, mostly, but the collector watch world has certainly shifted toward dress pieces over the last handful of years. And that is music to my ears! There will always be a place for sport watches vintage, pre-owned, or modern, and, ultimately, that is what most people wear on a daily basis. But oogling over amazing dress watches on the internet is a bit more fun. Feel free to tell me I'm wrong, of course. Wrong or not, this week is all about dress so put on a button down shirt or at least something with a sleeve, and settle in.

We have a few quick results from last week's column to cover first. The IWC Ultra Sportivo (said best in an over-the-top Italian accent) has been marked as sold over on eBay, the Buy It Now price was $900 . Across the pond in His Majesty the King's United Kingdom, our downright regal 1950s Movado hammered at auction for £300 plus fees .

Onward to this week's picks!

If you invested five hours of your time into Acquired's most recent podcast, diving deep into the history of Rolex , you would have heard the story of a brand shifting over decades from utility to aspirational luxury. When viewing the entire timeline of Rolex reference production, that trend is fairly obvious. The brand of the "bubbleback" Oyster case for the everyman, incrementally became the Crown we know and love, in all of its Rainbow Daytona and polished centerlink glory. Certain models in that lineage buck the larger trend. I'm sure you can assume where I'm going here, the reference 8171 "Padellone" is an outlier in the Rolex brand journey.

Produced from 1949 to 1953, we are firmly in the pre-Professional catalog, "bubbleback" Rolex era. The majority of the catalog featured stainless steel cases under 36mm in diameter with a heavy emphasis on robust wearability. Radium luminous material use was rampant. Yet, the Padellone is 38mm in diameter, wearing notably flat hence the "frying pan" nickname, and has a very elegant, dressy aura. This anti-Rolex, almost Patek Philippe wrist presence is especially so when an 8171 is found in yellow gold, like the one we have here. Of the estimated 1,200 examples produced, fewer than 350 are thought to be in yellow gold.

A rare bird, this particular Padellone is made remarkable by its condition. As a non-Oyster, snap back case, unfortunately, a ton of 8171s have aged poorly. Discolored and spotted dials are commonplace, and I don't mean the kind of aging that can be passed off as an attractive "tropical" patina. We could use that word with this one though as the light discoloration is even throughout. If you take a look at other examples of this reference, you will only love this dial condition more and more. Furthermore, the case condition here is jaw-dropping from the sharp lugs to the brushing on the case band and the crisp coronet on the caseback. This coronet and serial number engraving was notoriously shallow, most examples show their wear here and some have lost the caseback detailing completely.

The 8171 is close to our hearts here at Hodinkee. Ben (Clymer) went very in-depth on the reference back in 2019 for Vol. 4 of the Hodinkee Magazine , waxing poetic on the importance of this watch and the "elite club" that is owning a Padellone. Speaking for myself, I keep a keen eye on the reference both at auction and via dealers. In my memory, this particular is among the best condition examples to have surfaced for sale in quite some time.

The seller, Erik of Hairspring in Denver, has listed this Rolex 8171 just this morning and is asking $290,000. Check it out in full right here .

Founded in 1900, Tourneau grew throughout the 20th century to become one of the largest retailers in the United States. By 1940, there were two locations in New York City and by 1997, The Tourneau TimeMachine on 57th Street became the largest watch store in the world. Manufactured and sold long before The TimeMachine, this "fancy" lugged calatrava was produced in Switzerland exclusively for Tourneau and sold to a lucky customer in New York City. While the Auguste Reymond SA, or ARSA, ébauche inside isn't exactly something to write home about, these private label retailer watches always offer a solid value proposition.

Without the cache of a brand name, retailer signed watches like this and those from Gubelin or Bucherer typically trade hands below what you might expect. For less than €2,000, I can't think of a more interesting vintage watch to buy from a design perspective. The exaggerated lugs feature a nice large step and help this 34mm watch wear a bit larger, I can imagine. The two-tone salmon dial and feuille hands really do a lot for the overall look as well. As you may be able to tell, I don't have a ton of historical information or interesting tidbits to add to this one, it is a great watch for a great price.

The seller, Arthur at Shuck The Oyster in Germany, is asking €1,900 for this unique Tourneau. For all of the photos and inquire further, click here .

Here's another watch that I can't add much to other than what we can see in the photos. Because, well, there isn't much to be dug into. Unsigned by the maker, beyond the movement, this 1970s Tank Cintrée-esque design obviously was inspired by the designs of Cartier, but the proportions are a bit different. At 18mm wide by 43mm tall, the look is quite dramatic and would wear very long and skinny while not extending beyond most wrists. The classic dimensions of Cartier's Tank Cintrée "Jumbo" are 23mm by 46mm. While that might not seem like a big difference, I can imagine on the wrist you can really tell.

The case is marked with Swiss hallmarks denoting silver, which is interesting, you don't see too many silver cases, especially in the 1970s. An ETA caliber 2412 is found inside the case, which is interestingly the same caliber used in many of Cartier's pieces of the 1970s. All of this comparison to Cartier is a bit of a stretch but, nevertheless, this is a really cool and unique watch. If anything, the comparison to Cartier in this era shows just how razor thin the difference is between what we perceive as being special and important versus what we consider to be just another nice watch.

The seller, Alan of Foundwell in New York City, is asking $3,495 for this anonymous silver Tank. Get all of the details right here .

Coming to us from our good friends at Goodwill, there is a bit more to dig into here with this JLC. The Étrier design dates back to the 1930s and there is a common misconception that this watch was designed for, exclusively sold to, or named for Hermès. While that is not the case, in my opinion, the Étrier is a unique wristwatch design that doesn't get nearly enough attention today. These were made for quite a long time, as we can see with this example, ultimately being discontinued or, at least, stopping production in the 1970s. JLC did a good number of interesting wristwatch designs in the first half of the 20th century– it wasn't just the Reverso! The brand was really thinking outside the box.

Examples of the Étrier that were retailed by Hermès do exist and that is somewhat the ultimate level here, but I accept them in all forms, really! The "stirrup" design is elegantly undersized on the wrist with the dial being quite small but the footprint of the sweeping lugs lend themselves to a wrist presence that exceeds expectations. An oval-shaped JLC caliber 840 powers this Étrier. The movement was introduced in 1969, which helps date the watch and shows JLC's commitment to small-scale watchmaking at the highest level into the 1970s– very few brands were creating movements like this.

Listed on ShopGoodwill.com, this JLC is up for auction, ending Monday, March 3, at 11:21 PM ET. When this article was posted, the bidding had reached $601. Check out the full listing right here .

More of a dress watch in 2025, this Doxa might have been considered a sport watch back in the 1940s with its "waterproof shock resist anti-magnetic" billing. Before the tool watch era, this was the do-anything, take-anywhere, and it will still run option for the rambunctious man. With a case measuring 29mm or, if you take the seller's word for it, a generous 30mm, the look on the wrist is more "dress" today. The waterproof and dust resistant case is thanks to the innovation of the case maker and supplier, François Borgel, who supplied this very same case design to Patek Philippe for the ref. 565. So, if you have ever looked at a 565 and thought, "boy do I love that but I wish it was 29mm!" this is the one for you. In all seriousness, these Doxas and Movados that share cases with Patek are a great value play. When you consider that a 565 with a black gilt dial is about as rare as they get and will run you north of $350,000 , the Doxa get all the more attractive!

Full disclosure, this eBay find is a project! The seller notes that the movement requires a full service and his watchmaker has informed him that a new hairspring is needed. I don't know how easy that part will be to source, but if you have a great (and patient) watchmaker, I am sure it can be done. The project is worth it in my eyes! The gilt dial looks to be in great condition and the case is nice and sharp. Dare I say, unpolished?

An eBay seller in Craigavon, United Kingdom has listed Doxa for auction ending Sunday, March 2, at 8:02 AM ET. At the time of publication, bidding had reached $54. Get all of the details right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Minty Rolex Padellone, A Fancy Tourneau, and A Stirrup-Shaped LeCoultre

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week is dedicated to oddities, to the watches you rarely hear about. The ones we've found for this week range from a rare Rolex with a quartz caliber Beta 21, to a Movado chronograph, with an unusual method of operation. You will also find a great Breitling Co-Pilot with a colorful yachting bezel, as well as a stunning Longines Conquest with an unusual power reserve complication. This is your Bring A Loupe for February 3, 2017.

Movado was at one time renowned for its in-house calibers, chronograph movements included. In that regard, its calibers M90 (two-register) and M95 (three-register) offered an interesting peculiarity : the bottom pusher starting and stopping the complication, while the reset pusher is placed up top. This is the exact opposite of how a chronograph wristwatch usually works, so it takes some time to get used to it, but it does not feel less efficient in the long run. The highlights don't stop with the movement, since the waterproof cases of those chronographs were manufactured by the famous supplier Francois Borgel (later Taubert Frères), also known for its contribution to the first water-resistant Patek Philippe chronograph, the reference 1463. The chronograph here does not feature the characteristic " snake hands " usually found on the M95 chronograph, but the red handset is also correct for this reference. The Borgel case is astoundingly sharp, the lugs have kept their original shape, and the 5866 serial number is deeply engraved (and matches the numbers on the caseback, as it should). The dial shows some light aging towards its center, but the painted numerals remain intact and are very pleasantly balanced with the 3 sub-registers, which are well sized for the 35mm diameter of the case. Lastly, the large winding crown is original to the watch, and completes the look of what was engineered as a true tool watch.

You can find this gorgeous Movado M95 offered for €5,750, or around $6,196.

This reference 5100 does not look like your usual Rolex, either inside or out. Its chunky case with integrated bracelet is definitely reminiscent of its introduction in 1970 for a short two-year production. The thick and oddly shaped 39mm case can be explained by another factor: the Beta-21 caliber that it houses, the very first Swiss quartz movement. Beta 21 was developed by a conglomerate of manufactures including Rolex (obviously), but also Omega, IWC and Patek Philippe. It also showcases how modern the reference 5100 was, being the very first Rolex to offer a sapphire crystal and a quickset date. This Rolex came with another unusual feature: it was made in a limited run of 1,000 pieces, an almost unique practice in Rolex history. You can imagine that the price tag for the most modern Rolex of the time was astronomical, and it included a free trip to the headquarters of Rolex in Geneva, and inclusion in the Golden Register (no kidding, this was the name of the booklet compiling the name of the 1,000 lucky owners). This yellow gold 5100 comes with an even rarer feature: a dial bearing the arms of the Sultan of Oman. It is estimated that between 5 and 10 reference 5100 were specially ordered (in either 18k white and yellow gold), and the seller indicates that they all fall in the range 390 to 400 (I wonder if the present watch is the number 396 that was seen at a previous auction ). The gold hallmarks and the engravings are sharp on the watch, and the bracelet does not seems excessively stretched.

The dealer Watch Collector just listed this Rolex reference 5100 for $49,000.

The nautical purpose of this Breitling is instantly clear, with the regatta countdown placed on the bezel, and in the minutes sub-register. Reference 7650 was not the first yachting watch made by Breitling, which had already made the previous iteration, the AVI reference 765 , in such a configuration, although the Co-Pilot comes with a bigger 43mm case (vs. 41mm for the AVI), and a redesigned black bezel for the 15-minute countdown. Both chronographs took advantage of the modified Venus 178, with the chronograph minute counter offering a 15-minute layout, instead of the traditional 30-minute duration (note that the associated minute hand moves by increments of 30 seconds, so it still does 30 "jumps"). The present example here offers a stunning dial, with the white sub-registers well preserved. The colorful handset is correct for this reference (and it is also not unexpected for a watch born at the end of the 1960s). The lume has taken on a greenish tint but again this is not unusual with vintage Breitlings from that era (notably the 2nd generation of the chronograph Breitling Superocean). The case itself is described as in outstanding condition ("nearly unworn" are the exact words), which also matches the near mint condition of the bezel. This Breitling Co-Pilot Reference 7650 was just shown on Instagram, and is priced at $8,500.

The date placement at 12 is unusual but works extremely well on the Longines references 9035 ( a later execution of the reference 9032 , with a different caseback). The caliber 294 that can be found on both references comes with an interesting power reserve indicator, shown on the central disc which rotates to indicate how many hours are left for the full rotor automatic movement. It's not just practical; this disc looks stunning, offering an interesting visual counterpoint to the inner track and the applied indexes. The watch here showcases a perfect dial, which really underlines how beautiful this watch was when produced in the early 1960s (the serial number on the included original papers and caseback would help provide a better idea of the actual date). The condition of the case is equally impressive, with sharp lugs and intact brushing on the caseback (while the earlier reference 9032 came with a green enamel painting of a fish). The crown looks signed, but its shape is different than what's usually seen on the reference 9035, so it might not be original to the watch.

The Italian dealer Andrea Foffi offers this mint Longines Conquest Power Reserve for $5,800. UPDATE: Longines collectors just brought to my attention that the central disc was actually not rotating and therefore this Longines did not benefit from the power reserve that the dealer advertises but rather a "simple" date complication, from the caliber 291 instead of the caliber 294. Logically, its reference number is not 9035 either, more likely the reference 9024 , although the inside of the caseback would give more certainty.

This is a lot to like in this 35mm time-only watch, starting with its sharp case, with the characteristic screwback water-resistant caseback. The two tone dial seems free of imperfection, and the radium lume took a nice patina on both indexes and handset. The applied logo above the Alpina moniker offers a nice callback to the gold indexes, while the thick lugs and sharp engravings on the back really give credibility to the NOS condition advertised by the seller. As mentioned on the dial, this watch is powered by an automatic movement; the Alpina caliber 584 has a bumper mechanism which explains the spring you can see in the picture below. Lastly, the signed crown might seem a minor detail, but it shows this part is original, and further underlines how the Alpina branding was nicely scattered through the design (it might even originally have come with a signed tang buckle, but not included here).

The dealer Cars And Watches lists this Alpina Automatic for €1,480 or around $1,600.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rolex Reference 5100 With Omani Dial, A Movado Chronograph M95, A Breitling Co-Pilot Yachting Reference 7650, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, Bring A Loupe covers some iconic vintage pieces, but also some others flying more under the radar. Among the obvious choices, you can find an early Speedmaster, a great Heuer Carrera, and a promising Universal Geneve Polerouter Sub. There are also some watches that you probably expected less, including a quartz-powered Rolex and a Movado Datron relying on the mythical El Primero movement. This is your Bring A Loupe for February 5, 2016.

Let's start with the reason behind this Speedy's nickname: the astronaut Ed White actually wore a reference 105.003 during the Gemini IV mission when he became the first American to space-walk, in June 1965. Then, you should definitely look at our Reference Points on the Omega Speedmaster for more info on why this early Speedy is so special. The reference 105.003 was indeed the last of the non "Professional" dials, the last of the 38 mm cases, without crown guards, and it still housed the mythical caliber 321 movement. Frankly, all these remarkable facts still fail to capture how nice this watch looks, with its larger unprotected pushers, and the thin handset that remains the standard for Speedmasters to this day. Note that here the "DON" – Dot Over Ninety – bezel was not originally blue, it faded to this color , and the second hand for the chronograph is a replacement part. At the time of publishing, bidding for this early Speedy was already close to $7,000 on eBay here .

Okay, so I did not choose this Carrera because it was fitted on a HODINKEE strap . Trust me, there are great reasons for a closer look at this one. This Heuer reference 3647N solely owes its presence in Bring A Loupe to its outstanding condition, fully illustrating why Carreras are so hot right now. Its nicely preserved case underlines how inspired the design of the case and lugs really was – just look at the inner facets! The dial matches the condition of the case, deep black against the white inner bezel and the white hands of the chronograph. Furthermore, all the lume dots are whole, with a patina matching the hands. The German dealer Rare Birds is offering this great Carrera here .

"If a Rolex ticks, then it is a fake." This common belief probably explains the enduring unpopularity of the Oysterquartz reference, the first in-house quartz model from Rolex. The previous model, the reference 5100, used the Beta 21 movement; its development was shared among 20 manufacturers – read more here , from our Quartz Week – and importantly this caliber was equipped with a sweep second. The movement of the Oysterquartz is highly sophisticated as it was designed to be serviced and adjusted if needed but, yes, the second hand ticks. Launched in 1977, the Oysterquartz remained in the catalog until 2001, but it was never a best-seller – far from it. A testimony to this fact, the reference 17000 here was produced in 1991 but only purchased in 1994, so it likely waited a couple of years on the shelf of a retailer. Some might also blame the funky 1970's design, but to me this is a huge part of this model's attraction. In the end, you get a unique looking Datejust that you won't find on many other wrists. The integrated bracelet is of course reminiscent of the famous Royal Oak from Audemars Piguet, although I would argue that the beefy and angular case design of the Oysterquartz is closer to some vintage Omegas, if some inspiration had to be looked for. The Oysterquartz here comes full set and retains a sharp case; the latter is something you should absolutely aim for when hunting for those quirky Rolexes that bear the serial and reference numbers on the back side of their case. At the time of publishing, bidding was below $2,000 on eBay here , but the seller put a higher reserve for this full-set Oysterquartz.

Speaking of integrated bracelets, this Precision reference 9083 proves that Rolex had already tested the waters in the 1950s. And the combination of a rivet bracelet and a large and thin 36 mm case – also nicknamed "UFO" – works very well. This Precision's vintage appearance is even reinforced by the characteristic alpha hands, and the charming honeycomb dial. Here, the dial is patinated, but in a coherent way that does not decrease the appeal of the watch, nor its value. Note that on this unusual model you should always find an elongated crown on the case back, pictured below. You can find this stunning Precision "UFO" here .

One year ago, to this day, we published a love letter to the Movado Datron . Our feelings have not changed since then; this watch remains an underestimated chronograph on many counts. Movado was indeed a great manufacture that produced stunning vintage pieces, which are often overlooked. Here, it teamed with Zenith and benefited from the most advanced chronograph movement of the time: the El Primero. While we would not want to judge again who came up with the first automatic chronograph movement between Seiko, Zenith, or the Project 99 involving Heuer and Breitling, Zenith was obviously the very first one to offer a high beat automatic movement; the HS 360 denomination here stands for the legendary 36,000 beats per hour of the El Primero caliber. The Boston dealer European Watch Co. is offering this Movado for $2,250, or your best offer, on eBay here .

This might sound counterintuitive, but sometimes a listing makes the watch much worse than it really is. Here, the scratched plexi and the bad pictures play this role. You should rather focus on the intact reverse bezel, the honest case and the great dial. The intense crazing can't fully hide the attraction of the Polerouter's singular design combining bright red touches, a fairly unique handset, and some oversized indexes to balance the date window at 3 o'clock. Add to that a micro-rotor movement and an oversized signed crown, and you get a very attractive dive watch in a promising condition Unfortunately, this UG Polerouter Sub did not remain unnoticed, it just broke the $2,500 mark on eBay here .

Two Speedmaster "Ultraman" watches were sold this week; one on eBay here , and the other via Instagram here . This number should not hide the strong controversy over the actual existence of the "Ultraman" stirs among the Omega Collectors. The last time one was featured on Bring A Loupe , the comments echoed the debate, and since then no further proof has been brought forward. The romantic side in me still believes this configuration is correct, and would like to trust the range of "correct" reference 145.012-67 , despite a few outliers. Of course, as it often happen with contentious vintage watches, there are stories of one-owner watches claiming to bear the orange second hand since birth. And let's not forget the Japanese show Ultraman , which featured such a watch in the mid-1970s. In the end, what we really need here is some definitive documentation from 1967 regarding this colorful variant, and, to my knowledge, this has never been found – yet.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rare Speedmaster 'Ed White,' A Gorgeous Heuer Carrera, An Underestimated Rolex Oysterquartz, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and happy Friday! This column is typically offered with a subhead of "the best watches available for sale on the web." The problem is, I already covered the "best" vintage watch for sale right now on Tuesday. So, I must direct you back to a story on the Patek Philippe ref. 1518 in steel on offer for "in excess of $20,000,000"– if that is your kind of thing. If your kind of thing is an odd-ball Doxa with a Sun and Moon display rather than an hour hand and a dead-beat seconds complication, you can stay right here.

Although I remain confident in the picks, last week was a bit of a strikeout in terms of results! Our highlights, the early Franck Muller and the Boucheron "Gondole" remain available at their respective asking prices. That said, our eBay PolArouter did end up selling for a respectable $1,826.98 , and the LeGant chronograph has been marked sold out. Reminder: the Rolex ref. 6424 will finish up this afternoon, the bid is at $2,024.

Onward to this week's picks!

Of the thousands of watches I saw at the Original Miami Beach Antique Show last month, this simple, seemingly plain, and almost uninteresting Audemars Piguet is the one I still think about weeks later. Those descriptors might have you scrolling to the next watch, but please give me a moment to tell you why this AP is so special. While this has been stated elsewhere for years, I do not think collectors today appreciate just how incredible real-deal vintage Audemars Piguet watches are. Prior to 1951, every watch the brand produced was essentially a piece unique; no two were the same, and when references were made in series, each had slight differences. And these watches are rare. Consider all of the Patek chronograph references we find produced in the 1940s and that AP produced only 307 before 1951. Or think of Patek perpetual calendars (1518, 1526, 2497), of which 688 watches were produced– Audemars Piguet made nine perpetual calendars in the same period.

While that comparison may come off as more of a testament to Patek, the simple fact that AP was producing complicated wristwatches at this time is incredible. Comparing any brand to mid-century Patek is a tall task. Vacheron, for example, produced zero perpetual calendar wristwatches in the same span. Obviously, the watch we are looking at today is time-only, but all of this adds context to what you are looking at. This isn't just a cool vintage watch from a Holy Trinity brand; this is an incredibly rare, incredibly well-made watch from the only brand of the three that was both pushing itself horologically and not producing hundreds of every reference it created.

Nearly all of the above Audemars Piguet complications were produced using the same movement blank– the Valjoux-supplied 13VZ. The same is true for Patek's 1518s, 2499s, and the legendary J.B. Champion observatory wristwatch . The Precision 13VZAS we have here today is an Audemars competitor to something like the J.B. Champion. While not exactly the same, as there is no known record of this particular movement being entered into observatory chronometer trials (as is the case with the Patek), the ethos of both is identical. Observatory wristwatches were essentially the ultimate time-only movements created by a brand, produced to the highest standards of timekeeping and finishing. And that is all on full display in this AP. Without going too deep, admire the wide-set Geneva stripes, similar possibly only to Philippe Dufour's Simplicity (because Dufour cites the 13VZ as a finishing reference).

This example, housed in a 32mm stainless steel case, sold at Christie's in 2021 for CHF 106,250 , smashing its estimate of CHF 12,000 to CHF 28,000. The seller, Adam Golden of Menta Watches in Miami, has listed this incredible Audemars Piguet on his website for $135,000. You can view the listing in full right here .

Back in October, I featured a vintage Patek here on Bring A Loupe that I described as "cube-esque," the ref. 1485. This, one number higher, is the sister reference. Equally as cube-esque, the ref. 1486 features hooded lugs and was produced in gold, whereas the 1485 has intriguingly round lugs and is only found in stainless steel. While a bit confusing, it's hard not to describe these two references as a pair. Both feature the same case construction, one of my favorite quirky technologies ever produced in the watch world.

So long as this isn't your first time reading one of my weekly columns, you may have noticed my love of mid-century water-resistant cases, particularly those produced by the casemaker François Borgel. The case here is, of course, made by Borgel, utilizing a design that was patented in 1939. Basically, the caseback and bezel form the two main sections of the case, while three sliding and locking strips on the sides squeeze everything together. Thanks to the outstanding condition of this ref. 1486, we can see the clearly defined notches on the case sides used for sliding these strips.

With the rising interest in pre-1960 Patek, quirky references like this have seen a bit of a price increase over the past couple of years. That said, a great ref. 1486 has not come up for sale in a long, long time, by my count. In fact, the world record price for this reference was set at auction in 1999. Due to rarity and a lack of great examples, this particular Patek has not ticked up along with the market. At 27mm wide by 37mm tall with the lugs, wrist presence is quite nice with the ref. 1486, I find it extremely wearable.

This particular ref. 1486 is likely the nicest example ever to come to market. It is "pink on pink" with a rose gold case and a rose gold-tone "silvered" dial, confirmed by the Extract. The cherry on top of it all is the presence of the original box and papers. Check out the crispness of the hallmarks on the case. It doesn't get much better than this for a vintage Patek.

The seller, Ignacio, and the team over at Ancienne in Barcelona have listed this great Patek on their website for €12,000. Get all of the details right here .

As LVMH and Fabrique du Temps Louis Vuitton slowly work their way through the creations of Daniel Roth in the latest iteration of the brand, it's only a matter of time before a perpetual calendar is introduced. After stints at Audemars Piguet and Breguet, Roth launched his independent brand in 1989. Three years later, at Baselworld in 1991, he unveiled his perpetual calendar– the world's first with instantaneous functionality where all indications flick into place instead of a gradual move around midnight. Roth's creations have come to represent the epitome of early independent makers, and this perpetual is a great example. His early watches were tied to traditional watchmaking, yet an entirely new aesthetic with functionality that pushed the industry forward.

But Roth did not do all of this alone. In fact, in the development of this perpetual calendar, he asked a friendly neighbor by the name of Philippe Dufour for an assist. Dufour contributed heavily to the perpetual calendar module that would eventually sit on top of a Lemania cal. 8810 ébauche . Even with Roth and Dufour at the helm, two of the greatest watchmakers of the twentieth century, the project proved to be very difficult. Creating the world's first anything is no joke. "I remember it being hard work. It took me about six or seven months to finish the movement," Dufour has said.

On a random Sunday afternoon, walking on Manhattan's Upper East Side, my curiosity got the best of me, and I walked into The RealReal's Madison Avenue boutique. In the watch case was this very watch. While an early Daniel Roth is cool to see in this context, a perpetual calendar connected to Dufour really got me going. I quickly realized that no one around me would understand any of the words I wanted to say, so I put the watch down, said "thank you," and calmly walked out, hurrying home to write up this BAL entry.

This example is not perfect; it likely will require a service and does not have its box or papers, which would, of course, be nice to have for a watch from the 1990s. But it's worth noting that examples of this same reference have sold at Phillips for $48,946 and $70,674 in 2024 and 2023, respectively. Mind you, both of those were full sets.

The seller, The RealReal, has listed this Daniel Roth perpetual for $36,000, but it is currently on sale for $34,200! Check it out right here .

A fun eBay pick to round out our selection this week, this Doxa is full of vintage charm. A few volumes back in the Hodinkee Magazine, it must have been Vol. 11, I was asked to respond to "Hey Hodinkee" questions from the community. Probably the most interesting of which revolved around how to find a deadbeat seconds movement for relatively cheap. Of course, I mentioned Habring² as it is one of the only brands doing a deadbeat under $10k today, but the rest of my time was fairly focused on a supplier of the vintage period called Chézard.

Chézard SA was an ébauche manufacturer in the first half of the 20th century and eventually became a part of what would later be named ETA. Because that's easy to understand, right? Honestly, this is a tough Swiss manufacturer to find information about. Chézard did patent a family of deadbeat seconds movements in 1952, which is what the name is known for by collectors today. Realizing I haven't stated explicitly yet, this is where the seconds hand ticks, jumping from one second right to the next. Most achieve this via a spring with just enough power to hold the fourth wheel until a perfect amount of tension is built in the gear train to advance the wheel a single second.

This Chézard-powered Doxa certainly has a unique look. Without an hour hand, the sun and moon display depicts the hours, starting with the sun appearing at 6 a.m. (roughly around sunrise) and the moon appearing as the hour indicator starting at 6 p.m.

An eBay seller in "USA, United States," has put this Doxa up for auction, ending on Wednesday, February 12th at 11:21 pm EST. At the time of publication, the bid was at $280. Get all of the details and photos right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Observatory Dial Audemars Piguet, A Square Patek, And Not A Single Rolex

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It’s been a great week in the world of vintage watches, with all sorts of exciting mechanical curiosities having surfaced over the last seven days. This week, we’re taking an in-depth look at every piece and then some, with picks like an untouched Zodiac Sea Wolf, an Enicar Sherpa in new-old-stock condition, and a Breitling Premier believed to be coming from the collection of its original owner. All collecting tastes have been taken into consideration this week, with the inclusion of fun and mildly gimmicky pieces like the Seiko worn in Ghostbusters , and at the more serious end of the spectrum, an insanely rare Patek Philippe ref. 2526 in white gold with diamonds on the dial. We’re also spreading the word about a couple of less-than-perfect watches to steer clear of, including a faker-than-fake Patek Philippe along with a potentially deceiving Longines. Here we go.

Enicar sports watches of the 1960s are a world class case study in avant-garde and borderline futuristic design. On paper, they make little sense if you think about it. Loud bezels, dials featuring as many as five different typefaces, a logo inspired by the planet Saturn, and an entire line inexplicably named for Nepalese mountain guides — it’s a whole lot to process, with little adding up. But in the metal, it all works, and quite well if I do say so myself. My focus within Enicar collecting is largely rooted in the Sherpa line, as there are several exciting models to be discovered, from chronographs to GMT watches, divers, and more. I’m also particularly fond of their signature lug architecture, which affords these watches a terrific presence on the wrist. The one gripe I often have upon coming across a largely nice example, is that most of the metal rotating bezels fitted to certain models have worn down at their edges over the years, making their age no secret.
