If you too are peeved by this reality of vintage watch collecting, I think you’ll get a kick out of this one.

This is a Sherpa World Time Ref. 100/216, which is believed to have been produced during July of 1959. As the perfect world time bezel and intact caseback sticker would suggest, this piece remains in true new old stock condition, with not a single flaw. It even has its original Swiss Tropic strap still fitted, with an Enicar tang buckle tying it all together. Despite measuring 36 mm across, it’s quite a modern looking watch, which likely would’ve intrigued most who came across it back in 1959. Though the Space Age would’ve just begun, sports watches of this era were still decidedly unostentatious, making this Enicar an innovative piece for its time. The groundbreaking nature of the design, combined with the present condition, really is a collector’s dream. Justin Vrakas of Watch Steez has this stunning Enicar listed for $3,295. Click here for the full scoop.

Despite having grown up after the turn of the millennium, I like to joke that I was raised on a steady stream of 1980s comedies and synth pop so strong it could flood a village. This means I can quote Back To School and reference Frankie Goes to Hollywood songs like I have no business doing. Naturally, this upbringing resulted in a later obsession with finding out what was on Clark Griswold’s wrist, what timepiece John Candy sprung for after getting a piece of Monty Brewster’s new-found millions, and other watch spotting opportunities of this ilk. Since so many seemed to get a kick out of the inclusion of a bit of a left field Tissot last week, I thought I’d throw you another curveball that I’ve long been fascinated by. What you’re looking at is one of the many wrist-mounted computers of sorts developed by Seiko in the early 1980s, with the unique ability to record and play back short snippets of audio. This was made possible thanks to dual 16k solid state RAM modules, which at the time of this piece’s release would’ve been something to write home about. It was also the Ghostbusters’ watch of choice, presumably for the opportunities to make note of paranormal activity in the field it afforded.

After having once handled and operated an example in the metal, I’ve maybe seen one of these pop up for sale every year or so, and when they do, they’re often no longer functional, or offered on a replacement rubber strap. When I came across one in perfect working order on its original blacked-out bracelet, I couldn’t help but include it in this week’s round up. Yes, you can do the same and more with the pocketable device you’re likely reading this article on right now, but is it Ghostbusters approved? That’s what I thought. Should you wish to emulate the undeniable coolness of doctors Raymond Stantz, Peter Venkman, or even Egon Spengler, then this is the watch for you. It’s being offered by an individual out of Louisville with an asking price of $999.99, though you do have the option to make an offer on it. See the listing here.

Should you be a regular HODINKEE reader, chances are you’re already aware of the importance of the ref. 2526 within both the history of Patek Philippe and the watch collecting world as a whole. There was a point in time not too long ago when you could’ve described this reference as being overlooked, as our own Benjamin Clymer did , but those days are certainly past us. The reference is now regarded as a true grail among grails by countless collectors across the globe, and for good reason. Since the market has developed, and interest has increased, more dealers have made a point of regularly stocking examples of the reference. While it is indeed a rare watch produced in limited numbers, examples in yellow and rose gold are no longer as difficult to come across as they once were, with seemingly every name in the dealer sphere who’s who stocking one at any given time. In white metals like platinum and white gold, it’s an entirely different story, with the number of examples having come to market over the last few years still just in the double digits.

The example I’m sharing with you today is somewhat of a rarity within a rarity, seeing as not only is it cased in white gold, but it also features a diamond set dial. These executions of the 2526 were not produced with enamel dials, which some may see as a downside, but at the end of the day makes the hunt somewhere simpler in a sense. This is because such metal dials aren’t prone to the same spiderweb-like cracks to which many 2526 dials have fall victim over the years. All in all, this one checks a lot of boxes. It’s untouched, with both an unpolished case and a nicely aged dial that hasn’t seen any attempts at restoration or washing. What’s more, is it includes the box which it was sold with, from the Spanish retailer Soto Largo in Madrid. It can be stated with confidence that the watch was originally sold by this retailer, as the custom ordered white gold bracelet it’s fitted on is also signed Soto Largo. While many might not have the finances to pull off such a piece, myself included, I think this is a truly special watch by all definitions of the term, that is certainly deserving of your attention. Eric Wind of Wind Vintage is offering this hen’s teeth Patek with an asking price of $190,000. Contact him for more information.

When approached by friends with little knowledge of vintage watches who are looking to get in on the action, I’ll often point them in the direction of Zodiac’s Sea Wolf. In my opinion, it’s one of the single best looking dive watches of the 1960s, more than fit for everyday wear, and best of all, they’re still relatively affordable. Though they are slightly more expensive than they once were, noteworthy examples can still be had around the $1,000 mark and under. As with any other vintage watch, it ultimately comes down to the condition the piece is offered in, and just how complete it may be. Luckily for you, I’ve found one this week that really is about as good as it gets. This is the original Sea Wolf in its most desirable, no-date variant. These early examples were all fitted with metal bezels, many of which have gone missing or tarnished over the years, though in the case of the example in question, it’s both present and clean, with all numerals and hash marks clearly defined. Its luminous indicator at the 12 o’clock position is still present as well, and has even aged to a pleasing orange tone. All this would suggest that the watch has never been polished, which the sharp case would confirm.

If you weren’t already interested, then allow me to direct your attention towards the stainless steel, stretch riveted Oyster style bracelet. This is the same bracelet the watch would have originally been sold with, produced in-house by Zodiac. Later the watchmaker would outsource bracelet production to JB Champion who manufactured a Jubilee-style offering. Lastly, there’s the crown, which unfortunately isn’t pictured, though I’d be willing to bet it’s original as well, as I can make out a protruding emblem, and know its profile to be consistent with other examples I’ve seen. This really is a top quality vintage diver for the budding collector and dive watch aficionados alike. An eBay seller based out of Coos Bay, Oregon, has this complete example listed with a starting bid of $700. You also have the option to make an offer and beat the bidding frenzy. See the full listing here.

Long before oversized Breitlings were being fitted with lab-made diamond bezels by strip mall jewelers, the company was making serious waves in the world of chronograph development. In addition to birthing countless ultra legible chronographs and incorporating slide rules alongside for increased functionality, Breitling introduced the two-button chronograph in 1934, which now epitomizes the complication. Though the Navitimer is undoubtedly the crown jewel of the watchmakers’ back catalog of chronographs, I’ve always been particularly enamored with the more restrained Premier. This is an example of the ref. 788, which is my second favorite Premier reference, after the earlier and slightly more oversized, 38mm ref. 777. While no movement shot has been provided, I know this variant of the reference to be powered by the manually wound Venus 178, which was also used in many executions of the ref. 806 Navitimer. Best of all, this is likely a one owner piece, as the seller has it listed on behalf of their father, who I’m guessing purchased this new back in the 1950s. This certainly shows, as the piece looks to have all its original components intact, along with an unpolished case.

Here’s a little lesson in eBay watch hunting. When the photos are less than perfect, which is admittedly 99.99% of the time, you have to do your best to discern between flaws in the dial, flaws in the crystal, and flaws in the seller’s camera lens. When I first found this piece at the beginning of a long night, I initially thought the dial had spotting throughout and a scratched Breitling signature, though after analyzing every photo showing it from different angles, I was able to see that these were merely shadows cast by an old, dinged up crystal, and that the dial underneath is seemingly flawless. Moral of the story: Always take the time to look as closely as you possibly can. This applies to when handling watches in person, as well. In the long run, it’s always worth it to break out a loupe and spend the time to diligently inspect every detail found on a dial, as its all too easy to get wrapped up in the idea of a great vintage watch, only to get it home and take note of its flaws. This piece comes all the way from Witham, Essex, in the United Kingdom, where its owner has listed it on eBay. Bidding stands at $914.60 at the time of publishing. Click here to potentially claim it as your own.

Before we get into the funny stuff, I wanted to highlight a watch that while I don’t advise you purchase, I would suggest taking a closer look at. To put it lightly, this watch is a bit of a disaster, and looks like it might’ve spent an extended period of time sitting at the bottom of a body of water. It’s missing parts, is far from functional, and is most certainly not to be mentioned alongside the word patina, but from a scholarly perspective it’s significant.

This is a Uni-Compax with a dial I’ve never seen before, but had I not come across this far from perfect example, I would’ve been unaware of its existence altogether. Knowing something is out there to begin with is half the battle when looking for great vintage watches, which is why I’m glad to have encountered this piece. The Art Deco typeface used in both the six and 12 o’clock markers is interestingly circled and just too cool, which I why I’m now in search of a better example to hopefully share with you soon. The watch is being sold as a "project" and that's putting it lightly. Bidding on this piece starts at $999, but like I said, I wouldn’t touch it. You do you, though. See the listing here.

While you might not need me to point out that this watch is a blatant fake, and a bad one at that, I still felt I ought to make mention of this piece in the article this week. This is about as fake as it gets, from the questionably functional complications to the poorly laser etched reference number on the back of its case, which corresponds with no watch ever produced by Patek Philippe. The real problem here is that it’s being offered in an estate sale out of New York alongside other legitimate valuables, and could command far more than it’s worth even if only given the opening bid by an unsuspecting patron. Spread the word, as no one wants to see someone get duped. See the listing here.

Sometimes on the hunt you’ll come across something so good you think you’ve practically won the lottery. You see, many will list watches on eBay without knowing what they've got, so to speak, providing an attractive opportunity for collectors with keen eyes and years of research under their belts. With that said, these lottery winning finds are more than often too good to be true. I had one of those finds earlier this week, and luckily I held off after seeking out the advice of someone who knew more than me. This is a 35mm Calatrava-style piece produced by Longines, which I immediately recognized as an exceedingly uncommon and desirable timepiece. The only other times I’ve seen one like it were whilst flipping through the pages of John Goldberger’s Longines: Legendary Watches , and later when the very same watch featured in the book sold in Philipps Geneva Watch Auction: Seven for a staggering CHF40,000. I was more than ready to get in touch with the seller and make an offer, but after showing the watch to a knowledgable friend, I was made aware of its shortcomings. Take a closer look at the minute track tracing the dial and the Longines signature itself. Neither is exactly perfect, with inconsistencies in the weight of the typefaces and their kerning, which would both suggest that it’s been refinished. At the end of the day, I think I just got excited by the familiar indices found on the dial, and convinced myself I had struck gold, but this was not exactly the case. I hope from this story you can see the value in asking around for the opinions of others. I’ve long hated (yes, I know it’s a strong word) the term “expert,” as its self-referential use ultimately leaves you closed off to the acceptance of new ideas and truths. No one will ever know the full story, or every last detail, and that’s why it always pays to deem yourself a lifelong student. Check out the listing here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Diamond-Dial Patek Philippe Ref. 2526, A Breitling Premier Ref. 788, And A Zodiac Sea Wolf

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's been a few weeks, but fear not, I didn’t forget about you guys! Feeling recharged as ever, the column returns for another year of tempting picks galore, hailing from all corners of the globe. In this week's roundup we've got a couple uncommon Rolex references for both him and her, along with a military-issued Lemania. There's also a stainless steel Audemars Piguet, and a wildly special piece by Van Cleef & Arpels, which I got to get up close and personal with at the Miami Beach Antique Show. Speaking of which, it was great to meet so many readers while browsing the show — shout out to Charlie and Rich! Without further ado, let's kick off the breakdown of the fruits of the year's first hunt.

Like other symmetry obsessed collectors, I'm usually one to opt for a no date variant over a slightly more complicated timepiece in favor of cleaner aesthetics. For me, a date window at four thirty will always be a deal breaker, whereas one at six o'clock can be easily gotten over. It's those three o'clock situated apertures that really are a tossup, with some inducing audible confusion, and others surprisingly fitting right into the fold. More often than not, this is the case with sophisticated, well-finished dress pieces of the 1960s, with painstakingly considered details. Patek's Ref. 3445 is a fine example, with its gold surrounded window that elevates the aperture to something more than a hole in the dial.

Our next pick of the week is not the aforementioned Patek Philippe, but instead a considerably rarer timepiece of roughly the same vintage. This is the Ref. 5281 from Audemars Piguet, which is powered by the watchmaker’s Cal. 2072 movement. This caliber is based off a Jaeger LeCoultre ebauche, which was also employed by Vacheron Constantin as the Cal. 1072. Make no mistake, this mechanism is no instance of an off-the-shelf afterthought, as its widely regarded as one of the finest automatic calibers ever produced. With friction-reducing ruby rollers and an advanced shock absorption system, it’s got quite a lot going for it.

I had the pleasure of handling this very example in the metal this past weekend, and what initially drew me to it were its black enamelled indices, which Audemars Piguet also incorporated into the Ref. 5273. These indices modernize the reference the degree, and separate it from the bulk of dress watches introduced in this period. The fact that all this magic is housed within a 36 mm waterproof stainless steel case only amplifies its cool factor, making it a dress piece you could easily wear on the daily. Thanks for showing me this one, Ali!

The Miami-based dealer Matthew Bain has this Audemars Piguet listed for $17,000. Check out his instagram for more information.

Also at the antique show, I also got to catch up with my good friend Eric Wind of Wind Vintage, and former Bring A Loupe fame. Naturally, he came ready with a varied selection of stellar pieces for purchase, including a number of top tier vintage pieces from the usual suspects, but it was the more unique offerings of his which caught my eye. After checking out a one-off Patek I’d featured not too long ago, Eric handed me what was without question the coolest watch I saw all weekend, which I’d now like to share with you. For context, there were multiple Explorer dial Submariners and comparable heavy hitters at the show, but this piece easily takes the cake.

Though Van Cleef & Arpels is most commonly known as a jeweller, they do have a rich history of both watch and clock production that dates back to the early 20th century. As one would expect, their watches were made to the same demanding and detail-oriented standards at the rest of their offerings, and this one is no exception. While other pieces which emerged out of the 1930s visibly show their age, this bracelet watch is harder to place in time than its contemporaries, as a result of its many subtle details and finishing touches. All of this has been preserved astonishingly well for its age, making it all the more fascinating.

My favorite details on this piece include the dial, which has been carved to mimic the appearance of the bracelet, making it blend in with the rest of the watch, and the bracelet itself, with its large and bold links. This is the one and only time I’ve ever encountered a dial finished like so, and to the best of my knowledge, it's never been done again in the history of watchmaking. Also of note is the clasp, which you'd be forgiven for not spotting. That's because like the dial, it was designed to blend into the bracelet, making the watch double as not only a timepiece, but a true piece of haute joaillerie. This is the perfect watch for someone who appreciates great design, demands the very best, and wants something they're guaranteed to not see on another wrist.

Peter Planes and Eric Wind are offering this piece together for $35,000. Check out the listing on the Wind Vintage site to claim it as your own.

Contrarian naysayers are quick to write off Rolex for the brand's choosing to not stray far from designs established long ago. On one hand, I totally get it, and hear such folk loud and clear. On the other, to dismiss the brand like that is to disregard the lasting power of truly great design. Sometimes you get it right the first time, and in my opinion, there's nothing wrong with being well aware of it. Having said that, it isn't as if the offerings of today are carbon copies of those found in the 1965 catalogue, as they've always introduced tasteful updates which modernize the designs, all while remaining recognizable as the icons they are. This highlights one of the brand's many strengths — aesthetic and technical innovation within a framework — as demonstrated best by the current Submariner, GMT Master, and Daytona.

Modern Day Dates and Datejusts are a bit of a different story, with their continually strict adherence to the designs that came before them. However, they've managed to keep the line fresh and exciting through the fitting of unconventional dials, many of which they’ve produced in stone. One of the most compelling and demanding from a manufacturing standpoint are the lapis lazuli and onyx "pyramid" stone dials, which feature a dizzying array of protruding pyramids on the dial's surface, and bezel set diamond hour indices. Given the fragility of carving out pyramids in such thin pieces of stone, there's a not so insignificant failure rate when producing these dials, hence their rarity.

With this in mind, you’ve gotta wonder what the failure rate might be of producing a markedly smaller version of the same dial variant, as seen on the 26 mm Lady Datejust. Regular sized stone pyramid dial pieces don't surface often, and their smaller sisters even less so, which is why I was delighted to learn of an onyx example coming up at auction. Though it does seem to have been worn a bit over the years, the watch looks to be in excellent shape, and its dial as radiant as ever. If even just slightly interested, I'd recommend jumping on this one, as it’s going to be a good long while until you come across another.

Catch this ladies piece going up for sale on the 18th of January at Auktionhaus Bossard in Chemnitz, Germany, where it's being offered with a starting bid of €3,000. Further details and the rest of the catalogue can be found here .

I indulge the whole "new year new me" thing as much as the next guy, but one thing about me that's not bound change is my love for military issued watches. As I've said before, there's something about a purpose-built timepiece which saw action that no store-bought luxury can compete with. This is most definitely true of this week's next pick, a Ref. 818 from Lemania, which is powered by the manufacturer’s Cal. 1872, which is a two-register interpretation of the same base caliber that Omega modified into the Cal. 861.

This piece is interesting one in that although technically a Royal Air Force watch, it's also a Royal Navy issue. Allow to me explain. Its multiple branch associations come as a result of it being issued to members of the Royal Navy Fleet Air Arm (FAA), which was an organizational unit of the RAF which flew aircraft embarking from Royal Navy ships. As you'd expect of a military issued piece like this, its caseback is engraved with all the pertinent details that indicate its origins, plus it has fixed bars through which you’d most commonly find a NATO strap woven.

Collectors have come to appreciate these military chronographs for a number of reasons, especially for their size and the infrequency with which examples surface. At 40 mm across, the watch has decent presence on the wrist, and a unique one at that, thanks to its asymmetrical proportions. As for how often examples go up for grabs, that's not an everyday occurrence, largely due to how just 500 pieces of the reference were produced in total. Lemania issued the watch to the MoD for only two years - 1975 and 1976 - supplying just 250 pieces per year. The design's hard-wearing nature might've helped more examples remain in circulation today, though 500 is still 500, and that number won't be increasing.

This Lemania is being offered by a collector from Washington D.C. on the Omega Forum, with an asking price of $6,500. Get in touch with him by visiting the post.

Those who've seen me browse eBay lately know that I scroll through pages at lightning fast speeds. That's because the quality of watches regularly listed on the platform just isn't what it once was, making the hunt more of a needle in a haystack scenario than a goldmine ransacking. While I don't believe I've missed a piece by moving at the pace I do, I have mistakenly thought something looked good and opened it up in another tab, only to be let down by a poorly refinished dial. On more occasions than one, this is exactly what’s happened with the reference we're about to discuss, but fear not, today's example in question checks out.

You’re looking at a Ref. 4218 Precision from Rolex, which isn't the usual coronet-signed fare most are used to. Instead, it's an elegantly oversized non Oyster, powered by a high grade manually wound Cal. 700 movement. As the dealer notes, this piece's sister reference is the 4222, distinguished by the presence of center seconds and a smaller movement. While both impressive watches, the 35 mm Ref. 4218's superior movement affords it a slight edge over the 4222, which is also why examples of the former reference is notably more scarce.

Even more scarce, is an example of the reference without a refinished dial. Given the age of these watches, many have fallen victim to the effects of time, scratches, and water damage, resulting in the bulk of remaining 4218's now bearing poorly redone dials. This example however, has not met such an unfortunate fate, as evidenced by its visibly original dial. What’s more, is its "disco volante" style stainless steel case remains unpolished, checking off another box on the proverbial desirability-intensifying grading scale. Though I might've already used the "it’ll be a long time til you find another one of these" line, it's even more so true of this piece. Should you dig it, jump on it.

Golden Hour Time is offering this example of the rare and early Rolex on their website for a reasonable $7,500.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Military-Issued Lemania, A Ladies Rolex, And A '60s Audemars Piguet Dress Watch

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Let's be honest, not all weeks are created equally, with certain roundups undeniably duller than others. This is not one of those weeks, and this is not a drill! It’s an honor and a pleasure to share with you what might be one of the best collection of finds to date, including two Rolex watches with NFL provenance, a Cartier-signed Omega, and a rare IWC that pioneered the Portugieser collection. For the chronograph heads out there, we've also got a gilt dial fitted piece from Tissot, and a simply elegant Ref. 765 from Breitling. Hopefully this is an indication of what else will surface in the year ahead.

Bespoke watches have risen in popularity over the last few decades, as new customization-dedicated outfits have emerged to serve the more particular watch buyer. If you ask me, there's nothing wrong with tricking out a timepiece to your liking, but going the custom route straight from the manufacture will always be the bigger flex, and a flex likely to better hold value, at that. In modern times, doing so is indeed possible but not exactly as easy as it once was, with certain brands requiring that you order a number of pieces in different metals to realize the request. Now that we're on the topic, why don't we briefly explore a noteworthy story of ye olde big flex.

Back in the late 1930s, two businessmen hailing from Portugal weren’t satisfied with the sizing of watches offered during the era. As a result, they decided to approach IWC with their custom request for a larger watch, which is and always will be a decidedly baller move. After a bit of back and forth, the example you see before you was realized in its 42 mm form, and dubbed the Portugeiser for the two individuals who gave rise to the large timepiece. As the seller notes, a 42 mm watch is considered rather large even by today's standards, making one wonder what sort of reactions it would've yielded from the masses way back when.

While it would’ve definitely started a few conversations, it wouldn't have started many, seeing as the Ref. 325 was produced in very limited numbers. Over the course of four decades, IWC produced just 690 examples of the reference, using three different pocket watch movements (Cal. 74, 98. and 982). This example is attractive in that it's powered by the Cal. 98, unpolished, and still has its original "Louis XV" style hands fitted, which are often missing on the infrequent occasion that a Ref. 325 surfaces. As one might expect, this is a rare opportunity to own a massively important watch. I can only imagine the sort of collection this will end up in.

Menta Watches of Miami has this IWC listed for $30K The full listing and additional photos can be found on their site.

Some watches you’ve really gotta handle in person to fully appreciate in all their glory. This is especially true of any piece with a gilt dial, as the way in which light reflects off their glossy black surfaces and highlights contrasting details is quite the sight to see. Just under a couple weeks back, I met up with Roy Davidoff of Davidoff Brothers, who was quick to put a Tissot that met this very description in my hands. Employing exaggeration to describe a fancy trinket for your wrist is never a good look, but believe me when I say this piece floored me. Let’s see if it has the same effect on you.

Tissot manufactured this chronograph back in the 1940s, and pulled out all the stops in the design process. This is clearly evidenced by the both oversized and waterproof case, which houses the multi-coloured gilt dial, complete with both rose gold and yellow applications. Having two different tones really makes a difference here, as it adds an extra degree of warmth to the dial. I’ve personally found this makes it easier for such pieces to be dressed down and worn in more casual situations, which the stainless steel step case certainly helps with, as well. Speaking of the case, the pushers which boldly protrude from it are also interesting, and further intensify the oversized feel of the piece.

Before you jump to conclusions, no, this isn't one of those watches that’s just a pretty face, as it's got the mechanical brains to back up the aesthetic brawn, too. Beneath the screw down caseback of the watch, you’ll find a Lemania 15TL, which was also fitted inside Omegas as the Cal. 33.3. This is by all means a top tier column wheel caliber, and one that I'd be proud to wear on my wrist. Between the condition of the watch, the rarity of the configuration, and the high grade of all its components, there's a bunch to get excited about.

Davidoff Brothers has this outstanding Tissot listed with an asking price of $48K Get in touch here.

Since last week's feature of an Audemars Piguet with enameled indexes, I’ve gone on a bit of a kick, looking into other watches with that distinguishing detail. It might not be a bold application of color or a wild typeface, but it adds quite a bit of character to otherwise discreet designs, which I'm all about and then some. After AP, the next stop on the hunt was Omega, who notably incorporated such indices into a handful of models in the 1970s, including the automatic Seamaster Deville. While we could break down your run-of-the-mill example, I thought a little extra something something was in order, which I'd now like to share with you.

As you might’ve already noticed, this is no ordinary Seamaster Deville that your eyes would normally scan right past. Thanks to a little extra line of printing below the text which reads "AUTOMATIC," and an additional set of numbers on the caseback, we can tell that this example was originally retailed by Cartier. Naturally, this elevates the watch to that next level of cool. While I’ve always been able to respect them, double signed watches have never really been of extreme interest to me, but those signed by Cartier are a different story. A retailer is a retailer, but a retailer that’s also a watch manufacturer putting their name on another watchmaker’s product has this formidable air of borderline hubris that I can’t help but respecting while also cracking up at. It's definitely a power move if there ever was one.

Moreover, this piece also has another ace up its sleeve, or more accurately on the surface of its caseback. After reading the hand-engraved text, you'll learn that this piece was originally awarded to a man by the name of Winfield J. Wagner back in 1971 upon his 25th anniversary at the accounting firm Arthur Young & Company. Just under two decades later, the firm would merge with Ernst & Whinney to form Ernst & Young. Though accounting provenance might not exactly be riveting stuff, it's always interesting to know who wore a watch before you, and dive down a research rabbit hole to learn more of their life.

Rob Cooper is offering this Cartier-signed Omega for $6,750. Find the full listing on Instagram, and slide into his DMs if interested.

Keeping things moving, we've now got not one, but two pieces that follow the aforementioned Omega's provenance-driven lead. Interestingly enough, I came across the pair of watches while taking what I thought was a bit of down time from the hunt while researching for another project with no relation to watches in any way, but much to my delight, this epic duo reared its head. This just goes to show that it's not all searching for hen's teeth needles in haystacks, and sometimes you get lucky with a quick and easy find. All you NFL fans ought to get a kick out of this one.

In addition to both having been manufactured by Rolex, these two watches were both owned by football players of the mid 20th century. The first in question is a Ref. 6593 Bombe, which was originally awarded to the wide receiver Raymond Berry of the Baltimore Colts upon winning the 1959 NFL Championship game in the pre-Super Bowl era. The second is a Ref. 1500 Oyster Perpetual Date, which was purchased by the ferocious Philadelphia Eagles linebacker and centre Chuck Bednarik, presumably after his days in the game. Today, Berry and Bednarik are both held in extremely high regard by historians of the sport, making ownership of their timepieces an attractive proposition.

The watches themselves are in terrific shape, and have been preserved with care over the years. Concrete Charlie's Ref. 1500 is unpolished with a flawless dial, and the same seemingly goes for Berry’s Bombe. Of the two, the Ref. 6593 is arguably the more special watch, with a custom Baltimore Colts dial and personalized engraving on its caseback, though Chuck Bednarik’s reputation ought to attract some serious interest. Comparisons aside, we're talking about the personal watches of some of the NFL’s greatest players going up for grabs, which is far from an everyday occurrence.

You’ll find the pair of Rolex watches here and here being offered at auction on February 1st, when Hunt Auctions will hold their Super Bowl LIV sale in Miami. Their estimates have been set at $5,000 — $7,500 and $2,000 — $3,000.

Collecting is fun and all, but I genuinely get more enjoyment out of helping someone finding a great watch than adding one to my own stable. That's because when it's literally your job to hunt down watches, you tend to come across the next best thing on a daily basis, which often equates to a slippery slope into continually trading up. With that said, I'm truly wowed by a watch every now and then, which induces a hefty helping of head scratching and consideration. That's exactly what happened the other day upon discovering this piece. Given its timeless and versatile nature, it's the sort of watch I could see myself enjoying for years to come.

What attracted me to this piece was its dial, which is unlike any other I've seen on a Ref. 765. It's got a silver sunburst surface that's finished with applied indices, and no signs of contrast aside from thin black printing, affording the watch a subtle and sophisticated aesthetic. While exciting color schemes can be fun, there's no denying that a piece configured like this is guaranteed to age more gracefully. There's just no competing with a white metal watch with a monochrome dial.

Condition wise, there's a lot to love about this one, though most notably, its flawless dial. Don't be fooled by what would appear to be a scratch on the register at nine o'clock — that’s only a mark on crystal. Like I've said before, always make sure to inspect whether a mark is in the same place in photographs shot at different angles, as dial scuffs don’t travel, but the shadows cast by marks in the crystal most definitely do. The only major downside to note is a curious bit of wear surrounding the reset pusher, which I'm guessing was caused by a watchmaker's haphazard attempt at removal during service. Despite these marks, the case hasn’t been polished, and maintains its original lines.

A seller based out of Madison, Connecticut has this piece listed in an auction on eBay that’ll come to a close on Sunday morning. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at $410.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe:  A Pair Of Rolex Watches From NFL Players, A Mint Tissot, And An Unusual IWC

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and happy New Year! After a two-week break of rest and relaxation for your dutiful columnist (and I hope for all of you as well), we're back with our first edition of 2025. As I write to you, I am down in Miami attending one of the largest vintage watch events in the United States, the Original Miami Beach Antique Show. Expect a full photo report of all the outstanding and outrageous watches and objects right here on Hodinkee next week. But for now, we'll stick to a "standard" format, rounding up the best for sale across the web.

To recap the results from our last edition of 2024, the 1960s Heuer Carrera "Dato 45" saw some fervent bidding but was mysteriously delisted by the seller towards the end of the auction "because the item was lost or broken." I don't buy it. Let's assume a deal was brokered through private messages, and the watch found a new home in the range of $6,000 to $8,000 – about the market value at the moment. Lastly, another reminder: the Boucheron travel alarm clock will be up for sale at Tennants Auctioneers this Saturday, January 11th.

Let's get into this week's picks!

I am happy to report that, at long last, the Rolex Bubbleback market is showing signs of life. These watches were very popular and heavily collected decades ago but have since settled into the background of the vintage world. For a multitude of factors, including case size, difficulty in finding solid examples, and many, many reprinted dials, even the most passionate vintage collectors of the past 10 or 20 years have steered clear.

Well, on the heels of smaller case sizes being "cool," Cartier trending and, with it, interest in early 20th-century wristwatch designs spiking, Bubblebacks are starting to simmer to the surface. I saw a great rose gold example while visiting with TikTok's favorite dealer Mike Nouveau during November auction season in Geneva (you can check it out in the hero image of my Year In Review essay ). Nouveau owned that one for all of an afternoon, buying it in the morning from another dealer and moving it to a new home in just a few hours.

Trends don't start with one watch selling quickly through a dealer. A couple of weeks ago, Loupe This offered another excellent Bubbleback, a ref. 3372 in solid yellow gold with a two-tone salmon dial co-signed by Giudici Milano . While it is a mouthful to list out, this was a truly amazing watch. Even as a quiet Bubbleback supporter, the $33,000 final price surprised me. My trend-spotting senses are tingling.

Loupe This is keeping the train rolling with another ref. 3372, this time in two-tone pink gold and steel and a sportier dial variant with luminous markers and a 24-hour inner track. Yes, two have come up in quick succession on the same auction site, but I want to stress the difficulty of finding one of these with original dials like these – it's next to impossible at this point. Especially when the dial is luminous as the radium material usually degrades, damaging the dial, and someone along the line of ownership decides to "take care of it" with a fresh paint job. There is no funny business on this one, though; it is in excellent condition. If you trust me and want to get in on a Bubbleback before the trend really "hits," this is a great opportunity.

The auctioneer, Loupe This, is located in Los Angeles (I hope everyone stays safe out there), and the current bid price at publication is $10,001. The auction for this Rolex Bubbleback is set to end on Wednesday, January 1,5 at 12:00 PM ET. Get all the details right here .

Technically, this, too, is a Bubbleback reference. But before you accuse me of trying to make this "trend" more of a thing than it is, I will have you know that this being a Bubbleback is extremely secondary to my main reason for including it in Bring A Loupe. Full transparency: I had dinner with Tariq Malik of Momentum Dubai on Tuesday night in Miami (it was a group dinner of six, and the check was split evenly!). Toni's Sushi Bar was just okay, but Tariq's choice of watch for the evening proved far more memorable. Of course, it was this 1954 Oyster Perpetual with a blue enamel track on the dial.

Enamel dialed Rolex will always hold a special place in my heart, but the full cloisonné situations like the ref. 6100 from Phillips New York last month will set you back nearly $1m . For a fraction of a fraction of the price, this ref. 6332 captures much of the same heart-fluttering feelings for me. I especially love the subtle touch of red text in the "OFFICIALLY" line at six o'clock. The overall look of the dial is a bit like the faded red, white, and blue of a flag, be it American, French, British, etc. The selling party does not specify, but given the distinctive font of the 12, 3, 6, and 9, I can safely assume this dial is the work of Stern Frères – the same dial maker as the full cloisonné Rolex dials. This numeral font is seen on the ref. 8171 Padellone , for example, and we know Stern manufactured those dials.

And yes, by the letter of the law, this 34mm Oyster case is a Bubbleback, but without the iconic and distinctive semi-hooded lugs of the earlier, true Bubblebacks, this 1950s design could easily be mistaken for a standard "screwback" while on the wrist. This is a mistake I clearly made on Tuesday, not realizing I was looking at a Bubbleback until I looked up the listing today.

The seller, Momentum Dubai, is located in, you guessed it, Dubai but I know the watch is in Miami currently! This Rolex has an asking price of $38,116 and you can check it out right here .

While on the surface, the Patek ref. 3445 is one of the more "boring" Calatravas to consider; if you have the chance to see one in the metal or even own one, you will come to find that this is among the best everyday vintage iterations of the world's foremost watch. In a "soldier" photograph online, you cannot see the crisp edges and subtle shaping on those seemingly uninteresting lugs nor can you appreciate the simple elegance that is a well-designed Calatrava dial. I guess I shouldn't call it simple, the 3445 features a date complication which is quite complicated for a Calatrava! The date function is remarkable as this reference is the first automatic, water-resistant wristwatch with date produced in series by Patek – the more you know.

Speaking to this particular example, while buying a vintage Patek on eBay is not for the faint of heart, this one looks to be clean. From the photos, I can't quite tell if this is a "first series" with a raised hard enamel signature or a "second series" with a flat dial. Either way, the price is right for the reference, especially in white gold with a Gübelin retailer signature. All considered, there is some risk here; the seller only has a feedback score of 8, but you're paying what I believe to be under market value.

An eBay seller in Les Contamines Montjoie, France, has put this Patek up for a buy-it-now price just north of $12,061. Get all of the details and photos right here .

Sticking with the Gübelin theme, this private label triple calendar offers quite the value play as well. If you've been reading this column for long enough you should be aware of my personal love for all watches with Gübelin branding. In general, I find retailer-signed watches to be endlessly intriguing. With these vintage, retailer-signed-only private label pieces, the market doesn't quite know what to do with them. It could be due to the difficulty in understanding which brand, if any notable, produced the watch. Therefore, comps are hard to find as you can't price it against other vintage watches from said brand.

Regardless of who exactly produced the watch for Gübelin (even I am unclear on these triple calendars), we can easily assess other aspects that point to this watch being theoretically worth more than the seller's asking price. The watch was made in Switzerland in the 1940s and features a caliber manufactured by Valjoux. Very few complicated watches from this era with Valjoux movements are available for less than $5,000. I am quite fond of the Valjoux 90, not only because I am a sucker for triple date calendars but also because this caliber comes from the legendary Valjoux 72 family. The architecture is rooted in the 72, but when I view the caliber, I see hints of the earlier 13 ligne Valjoux "VZ" movements famously used by Audemars Piguet.

Condition here is solid with a very strong, dare I say unpolished, and oversized 38mm case. The radium dial shows its age, but if that's a look you are into, the discoloration is even.

An eBay seller in Lewes, United Kingdom, has put this Gübelin up for a buy it now price of £2,475. Check it out right here .

As many know, I am a lover of vintage Movado. Less known is my appreciation of college football. In fact, last night, I snuck away from the watch festivities here in Miami to see my Notre Dame Fighting Irish beat Penn State in the Orange Bowl to punch their ticket to the National Championship Game.

Given that bowl season will officially come to an end this evening at the Cotton Bowl, this Movado made for the 1966 Sun Bowl caught my attention. It's a fairly standard Movado wristwatch with a water-resistant case, but the huge Sun Bowl logo, right in the center of the dial, is quite striking. If I hadn't found a watch to buy at the Antique Show yesterday, I would probably have jumped on this one myself. My gain is now your gain.

An eBay seller in Halethorpe, Maryland, has put this Movado up for a buy it now price of $350. View the listing right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Pair Of Bubblebacks, A Vintage Calatrava, And A Gübelin Triple Date

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It’s that time of the week again, where we get into the nitty-gritty with a selection of noteworthy vintage watches from around the web. This week, nearly all the picks are crafted in stainless steel – save for a lone bezel and crown – though their similarities end there. Whether it’s a sporting chronograph from Heuer, a smaller Tudor Oyster now best suited for a lady’s wrist, a left-handed Ovettone, or a crispy Breitling Premier, we’ve once again got you covered. As a bonus, we’ve included a Beyer-signed Calatrava (once again in stainless steel), because who doesn’t like a quality example of stealth grandeur? Time to get the ball rolling.

The Heuer craze has shed light on an extensive catalog of top-tier chronographs, and rather unfortunately, it would seem as if the way the watch world powerfully promoted the development of this market resulted in a bit of a dip. While I personally don’t believe this to be justified in an age when watches powered by identical calibers, featuring dials produced by the same manufacturers, are going crazy, I think it presents an attractive opportunity to claim a piece of history at a now-reduced rate. The Heuer in question this week is a Camaro – one of the many Heuer chronographs named with the automotive inclined buyer in mind – and it’s a pretty remarkable one, with a razor sharp case, a spotless dial, and intact luminous plots. What’s more, the watch includes original HLC end links, which are becoming increasingly difficult to find, and has been recently given a full service to ensure reliability for years to come.

While the dial is described as having a “chocolate” tone about it, I’d advise that prospective buyers do not expect a rich brown dial. Having owned similarly aged watches myself, I can say that you will note a upon catching it in the right lighting conditions every now and again, though be prepared for a watch that looks black on most days. Having said that, we’re still talking about a genuinely incredible example of a legendary chronograph, and you should be able to come to your own conclusions upon reviewing the photos. A collector based out of Jakarta has this Heuer listed on the Omega Forum with an asking price of $7,400. Find more details here.

Though both Rolex and Tudor did produce an innumerable quantity of truly iconic watches, perhaps the most aesthetically iconic creation of the Wilsdorf empire was the underlying foundation of such timepieces – the Oyster case. In every size and metal, the unmistakable architecture is instantly recognizable as an Oyster, with its subtle curves and quiet elegance. Today, let’s start things off with one from the smaller end of the spectrum, which I think would be perfectly suited for a lady’s wrist in 2019. This is a roulette date wheel–fitted Prince Oysterdate 31, with 31 referring to the 31mm stainless steel case size. Condition wise, there’s a lot to love here. The case remains extremely thick and has perhaps only been polished sparingly, if at all. This is certainly accentuated by the straight end link, riveted Oyster-style bracelet signed “DAS” that is currently fitted to the watch. In addition to being a simply stunning watch at a reasonable price, this piece also presents an opportunity to highlight what true patina looks like.

Patina and damage are often confused, or perhaps used in place of one another for marketing purposes, when a less-than-satisfactory dial can’t sell itself. In the case of this particular Tudor, what you’re looking at is the naturally achieved result of consistent wear and use throughout the years. The aging is both even and not overpowering, which is exactly what I like to see when helping friends in search of watches with a dash of character and individuality. This piece is currently being offered by an eBay seller in Nottingham for just under $900. Claim it before someone else does! (Note: We color-corrected the photos from the eBay listing to better show what the watch probably looks like.)

In an attempt to highlight some of the more notable works of Breitling, I’d like to introduce you to my personal favorite of their early chronographs, the ref. 760 Premier. Like many of the offerings of this era, this example is a relatively humble looking piece, with only basic Arabic numerals, two registers, and a tachymeter scale to speak of, though this is one of those cases where less is most definitely more. At a conservative 35mm across, this piece will sit comfortably on a wide variety of wrists without attracting too much attention, except when in the presence of a Breitling aficionado. There’s admittedly a bit of aging on the dial, but after reviewing the multiple angles of the watch that the seller has generously provided, it doesn’t appear to overpower the overall aesthetic so much as to make it not worth considering.

Simply put, this is a really well-executed chronograph, with restrained styling that we could use in more of the watches of today. It’s also advisable that one factors in the cost of an overhaul, seeing as the movement could visibly use a good cleaning. This Boston-based eBay find’s opening bid stands at $1,600 at the time of publishing – where it ends is anyone’s guess. Click here to see the full listing.

Upon first diving into watch collecting, one thing that immediately captivated me was the varying tastes and terms used to describe notable watches, depending on where one called home. The Italians have played a more than significant role in developing the watch-collecting world we now enjoy, and their influence can be seen not only in the watches that are now considered desirable, but how we describe such watches. “Ovettone” translates roughly to “big egg” and is used to describe the oversized bubble back Oysters that are now awfully sought after by collectors across the globe. The playful nature of the term and lighthearted approach to collecting that it embodies attracted me to these watches initially, but it’s their beauty and substantial feeling on the wrist that cemented my obsession. What you’re looking at is no ordinary Ovettone, but an example produced for left-handed individuals, making it all the more compelling.

While some have speculated that such pieces were made upon special order, it’s more likely that these watches are the result of Rolex testing the waters of the market for left-handed watches, so to speak, given the infrequency upon which Rolex accepted bespoke orders. The present example has seen a bit of exposure to the polishing wheel in its day, though it’s still being offered in quite pleasing shape, as evidenced by the largely clean dial and patina developing on its gold bezel. Rolex collectors who appreciate eccentricity will surely get a kick out of this one – I know I did. The Italian dealer Stefano Mazzariol has this listed on his site for €5,500. Click here for additional photos and details.

I’ve always been one to prefer a watch that goes unnoticed by the masses. If you’ve got nothing to prove, and are truly collecting for yourself, vintage examples of Patek Philippe’s Calatrava offer a lot in the sphere of stealthy wrist-mounted goodness. To end off this week’s roundup, we’ve got a real heavy hitter with a few tasteful twists, which only the most scholarly of passerby’s will be able to identify. Though examples with central seconds will always have my heart, exceptions can be made, especially when the exception was produced in steel, and just so happens to be fitted with a Beyer-signed dial, linking it to the “oldest authorized watch and jewelry retailer in the world.” Those familiar with the reference will be aware of the cal. 27-460 that beats beneath the caseback – the second automatic movement produced by Patek Philippe, which improved upon the cal. 12-600 AT’s at times unreliable rotor ball bearing system.

As one would expect with a piece like this, the watch is being offered with a Patek Philippe Extract from the Archives, confirming its original date of sale, pertinent serials, and more. It has also just been given a full service, so no need to worry about factoring that in. Boston’s long-established European Watch Company is asking $41,900 for this clean example of the sought-after Calatrava reference. Find the full scoop here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Heuer Camaro Ref. 7220N, A Breitling Premier Ref. 760, And A Beyer-Signed Patek Philippe Calatrava Ref. 3466

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
At first glance, the five watches we found this week have little in common, representing an interesting mix of a diver, an ultra-thin, a 1970s luxury sports watch, and a pair of chronographs. Yet, looking closer they all united by each offering a little something special and desirable, be that a tropical dial for the Movado with an M95 chronograph movement or a complete set of box and papers for the Vacheron Constantin reference 222. Expect also to find a full set Rolex Submariner 5513 and an interesting Longines chronograph with flyback complication. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 13, 2017.

There is a lot to love in the Longines reference 6474, starting with the balanced dial, here only showing some light marks of aging. The lume has taken on a really nice patina that matches with the handset and painted numerals – it's exactly the type of combo we love. Note that the luminous material is radium, since the watch dates from the early 1960s, before the less radioactive tritium made its way into watches. The 38mm case wears large, and it comes with a nicely signed crown and a screwdown caseback, which offered a higher water resistance at the time than a snap back did (however we definitely do not advise you test it). However, there is a last surprise if you dig past its good looks: the chronograph movement 30CH comes with a flyback complication, the lower pusher allowing for an instantaneous reset of the timing. And the construction of the caliber 30CH is stunning, which explains why we dedicated an in-depth article to the Longines 13-ZN and 30CH calibers , arguably some of the best chronograph movements ever made. Lastly, the watch comes with a Longines-signed box (not guaranteed to be original to this chrono, however) and the Longines' Archive documenting a sale to its U.S. distributor in April 1960.

The dealer Cosimo offers this this Longines 30CH for €15,800 (approximately $16,800 at time of publishing).

You might remember the Vacheron Constantin 222 from the watches that the Hodinkee team wore the most in 2013 . The reference 222 was released in 1977 to celebrate the 222th Anniversary of the manufacture and its design is often falsely attributed to Gérald Genta. Its integrated bracelet does indeed look very much in line with the original Audemars Piguet Royal Oak and Patek Philippe Nautilus, Genta's most famous creations. But the father of the 222 is actually Jorg Hysek, who eventually started and then sold an eponymous brand. Interestingly, this watch relies on the very same ultra-thin movement that can be found on both the Royal Oak and the Nautilus; all three automatic calibers come from the same Jaeger-LeCoultre ébauche , the 2.45mm-thick caliber 920 that JLC never used for itself. While the Vacheron Constantin 222 came in three different versions ( stainless steel, yellow gold, and two-tone ), and in both 34m, or 37mm sizes, the 37mm stainless steel 222 with a black dial is clearly the most coveted. That's what we have here. This example shows a nice patina, with the tritium having turned orange on the indexes and handset, although some lume is missing on the minute hand. And, very importantly, the watch still comes as a full set, with the original box and papers, something we very rarely come across.

Matthew Bain just listed this this full set Vacheron Constantin 222 for $34,000.

In the collecting world, there is often a focus on in-house movements, and this Movado chronograph offers you exactly that. The M95 caliber also come with a twist – the lower pusher starts and stops the chronograph while the upper pusher resets, the exact opposite of most chronograph movements. The Sub Sea engraving on the caseback also indicates the waterproofness of the 35mm case, manufactured by the iconic casemaker Francois Borgel (later Taubert Frères), which notoriously made the case for the equally water-resistant Patek Philippe reference 1463. The dial here could definitely be described as "tropical"; it has taken on a very strong but homogenous patina, in a sort of light caramel tint (if not slightly lemony). The handset is original to the watch, with the characteristic "snake" hands in the two chronograph sub-registers. The 35mm case looks well-preserved with sharp casebands and lugs and the crown is original too, keeping the same profile it had when it was manufactured some 70 years ago. And, as a final good sign, the chronograph caliber looks very clean, always an important thing to check with vintage watches to make sure you won't have to finance extensive repairs on top of your purchase.

The U.S. dealer Vesper & Co has this this tropical Movado M95 for $5,950.

There is almost no need to say anything about the Rolex Submariner ref. 5513. This reference was produced for more than 25 consecutive years, starting in 1962, and is for many the iconic vintage Sub. The 40mm case houses a simple dial, without the chronometer certification of its more expensive brother the ref. 5512. In its first five years, it came with a gilt dial, after which the present matte dial was substituted. And James Bond enthusiasts might remember this watch from Live And Let Die , where its additional buzz-saw bezel and incorporated magnet saved Roger Moore. The watch here is a more civilian version for sure, but it comes full set, with the original box and papers from its sale in 1976. The case is in good condition; it was clearly polished before, but retains thick lugs. There is some nice patina on its bezel and lume, with the little aging marks that you would expect. The provided bracelet is later than the watch, but it seems to show little stretch. The seller also indicates that the 1520 caliber performs within the COSC specifications that this 5513 never officially got. The 5512 and 5513 sometimes received the same movements, so that's not all that surprising.

The Rolex dealer HQ Milton has this full set Rolex Submariner 5513 listed for $14,550.

Vintage ultra-thin watches tend to be overlooked, very likely because of their diminutive diameters. It is indeed extremely rare to find old ultra-thins larger than about 34mm, but the overall proportions need to be considered: on the wrist, the presence of a slim 33-34mm watch should not be underestimated. This 33mm IWC reference 1221 is no exception, with a 5mm height thanks to its tiny caliber 171, only offered between 1967 and 1973. This movement was manufactured by F. Piguet and, based on its caliber 21, it boasts a 1.71mm thickness . The seller describes this watch as a rare version with a 18k white gold case, as it seems that most of the other ref. 1221 you can find were cased in yellow gold with an integrated bracelet. The blued handset and indexes add a dash of elegance, and again the movement seems in impeccable condition. The crown might be a replacement part, as it seems bulkier than on the other examples found .

This ultra-thin IWC ref. 1221 is available for $2,500, at a lower price than its introductory price a couple of months ago.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Full Set Vacheron 222, A Tropical Movado M95, A Longines Chronograph With 30CH Caliber, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, Bring A Loupe covers some of the most emblematic wristwatches ever made – watches that have become symbols of the companies that made them. We're starting with a couple of vintage Speedmasters, and you will also see a Futurematic from Jaeger-LeCoultre as well as a Cricket from Vulcain, both technically advanced watches for their time. Finally, Bidder Beware returns with two watches that you should absolutely not buy on eBay today (or ever). This is your Bring A Loupe for January 15, 2016.

The Reference 2915 was the first Speedmaster ever produced, over a three-year span from 1957 onwards. As it often happens with vintage watches, it is also the most coveted and valuable of the Speedmasters; think for instance of the first GMT from Rolex or the first waterproof chronograph from Patek – both enjoy greater success with collectors than later models. The Speedmaster Reference 2915 actually saw three separate iterations: if you remember Ben's dedicated Reference Points , each display some small differences that makes it either a "Dash One," "Dash Two," or "Dash Three" for the Omega fans. The 2915 - 3 is interesting in that it may or may not show the two most identifiable characteristics of the reference 2915: a stainless-steel bezel and broad arrows. It is therefore called the Transitional, as it can also be closer in looks to the next Speedmaster model, the reference 2998. That's a lot of numbers; it is easier to simply look at the picture of this Speedmaster 2915-3, indeed featuring a black bezel and alpha hands that would be expected in a later 2998. Note on the black bezel the "DON" (dot over ninety) that you should find in early Speedmasters. Or course, this 2915-3 comes in a 38 mm case, housing the manual-winding caliber 321, as you would expect from a Speedmaster with a pre-Professional dial. The Italian dealer Andrea Foffi is offering this rare Speedmaster here , with an extract from Omega's archives documenting that this chronograph was initially sold in the U.S.

It is rare to see those two Speedmasters side by side. On the right, the original Speedmaster with the exotic racing dial; this special version of the 145.022 goes for around $30,000 nowadays. And, on the left, a later limited edition reference 3570.40, made in 2,004 examples for Japan back in 2004. You probably guessed the one I would like to own the most – the original, of course. That said, it is fun to play the "spot the difference" game with those two. Obviously, you would notice a different bracelet, a different bezel, different indexes, and much more. So now the choice is yours between a very unusual Speedy from the 1970s or its faithful reproduction more than 30 years later. I am only worried by the following sentence in the description of the original 145.022: "The hands appeared to have been painted in orange to match the dial" – hopefully not a sign that there is something amiss. Davidoff Brothers listed these two watches – you can find the original here and the re-edition here ; the latter is priced at 7,800 CHF (or around $7,700), reflecting the attention that this modern Speedmaster is actually getting.

This Futurematic Porthole might very well be my favorite vintage watch from Jaeger-LeCoultre. That's quite a big statement, but I have many reasons. First, its functionality is highly innovative: the watch does not feature any visible crown, as it can only be set from a recessed crown on the back. Winding is actually not an option, hence the power-reserve indicator on the left counter and the small arrow on the right indicating the running seconds. The watch was designed to keep at least six hours of power at all times so it could start at the first motion that released energy to its bumper movement. This quirky feature fully underlines the talent of Jaeger-LeCoultre for developing amazing movements – remember that they had none other than Audemars Piguet, Cartier, and Vacheron Constantin as contractors. The aesthetic of this version is quite striking, the best of any Futurematic in my book. Those two round counters explain the "Porthole" nickname of this Futurematic reference E502, and can't be found on any other watch. Lastly, it comes in a 37 mm stainless-steel case, while most of the Futurematic you would find are in yellow gold or gold plated, and solely signed LeCoultre, those being actually smaller at only 35 mm. A Dutch dealer is offering this glorious Futurematic for sale here .

Calatrava became a common name to describe a dress watch, as Patek Philippe really nailed down the quintessential design of such a watch when launching the reference 96 in the early 1930s. Ironically, the reference 96 was not even called "Calatrava" at the time; this marketing nod to Patek's emblem came much later. The original reference 96 was only 31 mm in diameter, so you should think of the later reference 570 as its larger carbon copy with a 35.5 mm diameter, which explains its current success. The watch here displays the rarer central second, the long blued hands nicely contrasting with the yellow-gold case. In addition, engravings on the case back and the signed bracelet seem to indicate that it was originally sold by Tiffany & Co, although no attached papers could substantiate that; let's call the Tiffany provenance a very strong – and attractive – possibility. The Keystone is offering this dashing Calatrava for $28,500 here .

While Vulcain was not the first to create a wristwatch with an alarm complication – Eterna seems to have been the first in the early 1900s – the Cricket model was the first really successful one from the early 1940s, later followed by the legendary Memovox from Jaeger-LeCoultre. The history is interesting but the actual watch we've found is just stunning, with an incredibly well preserved dial and a rare rose-gold case. The in-house Caliber 120 offers two independent barrels for the time and the alarm; you wind the first by turning the crown clockwise and the alarm by turning it counterclockwise. Setting the alarm almost requires an engineering degree but the owner will provide detailed instructions. The "Fab. Suisse" mention on the dial indicates that this watch was originally intended for the French market after WWII, when gold imports were strictly restricted. A collector is selling his beautiful Cricket – and the original box – for $2,750, you can find the detailed listing here .

"Ideal for all professional men who require a watch which is fully reliable, accurate and trouble free under all conditions" – this was the promise of the IWC Ingenieur when it was launched in the 1950s. The Ingenieur offers protection against one of the greatest enemy of watches: magnetic fields. It does so by encasing its automatic movement under a soft iron inner case and dial, which provides amagnetic protection. This watch here belongs to the first generation of Ingenieurs, as evidenced by its devilish reference 666A – A for automatic, while the AD denomination describes the addition of a date function. I especially like that this Ingenieur features a black dial, more rarely seen than the silver one, while the 36.5 mm case remains relevant some 60 years after the launch. This black dial Ingenieur is offered for $6,900 here .

There are so many things wrong with this Bulova that it would be faster to start with the correct parts: the case and bezel. Additionally, the minute and hour hands are okay for this model, but the chronograph second hand has the wrong shape and color. As for the rest, the dial is a tragedy. The sub-registers are horrific: their unit track doesn't match the size of the correct Bulova. It also lacks the mention of tritium and the "666" seems too close to "FEET." Lastly, the crown should be signed, and in a different style. You can find this mixed-up Bulova on eBay here .

Welcome back to the "Australian" Polerouter Sub with its bogus military markings. You can read all about those fakes on this fantastic thread , and all the signs are there: a second generation dial with a space in "Gene ve" typical of fakes, crude crowns, and the wrong engravings on the case back. As for the military markings, they are widely known to be fanciful, but you have to applaud that the faker went on creating consecutive numbers; the one we discovered last summer had a serial number two digits after the 20463 number here. This problematic Polerouter Sub is listed on eBay here but I would not recommend bidding.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Two Vintage Omega Speedmasters, A Larger Patek Calatrava, A Great Futurematic From Jaeger-LeCoultre, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! It's already been a huge week for vintage watch-focused content here on Hodinkee, and I'm not letting my foot off the gas. If you're a weekly reader of this here column, check out my photo report from The Original Miami Beach Antique Show, as it's full of vintage gems as well! Shameless plug, I know. I appreciate the click, regardless. One of this week's picks is a Rolex Datejust that I saw in the metal at the Miami show and has been stuck in my brain ever since, but the rest are good old-fashioned internet sleuthing finds!

Lots of results to recap from last week! Our two-tone Rolex Bubbleback ref. 3372 ended up at $19,801 on Loupe. This is another strong result for the simmering (wink) Bubbleback market. The other Bubbleback, a ref. 6332, with a blue enamel dial, has been marked as sold by Momentum Dubai; the asking price was $38,116 . Similarly marked as sold were our two eBay picks, a Patek ref. 3445G with Gübelin retailer stamp and a Movado made for the 1966 Sun Bowl. The asking prices were $11,997 and $350 , respectively.

Let's get into this week's picks!

Longines chronographs from the first half of the 20th century are considered by many to be technically excellent to an extent that cannot be overstated. The caliber 13ZN is the king of this era of Longines chrono movements. Officially the first flyback mechanism designed for and made in "mass" quantities, the 13ZN was produced for 11 years from 1936 to 1947, and the watches it is found within are far and away the most collectible Longines ever made. Steel examples with waterproof cases, like the one we have here, boast an extremely dedicated following and regularly sell for more than most vintage enthusiasts would assume.

This is one of those markets where a lot of vintage collectors might not totally understand what is going on, but for the few dozen Longines obsessives, a giant flashing red light appears in their vision immediately when seeing a seemingly honest example surface at a small auction house in Falls Church, Virginia.

As a reference 5415, this particular example features a waterproof case with round pushers and a high-pressure snap-back case. While not photographed by the auction house, this caseback is known to snap quite tightly and has an interesting design feature to combat the resulting removal process. Two "tongues" extend from one side of the caseback to assist and allow for more grip. As with any good vintage Longines chronograph, this reference has a charming Italian nickname, "Doppio Linguetta" or "Double Tongue."

Condition and originality can mean tens of thousands of dollars for a watch like this. Looking at other examples of this reference, I consider this dial to be at least correct, if not original to the watch. We see the same dial in another seemingly original watch with this same batch number , 23020. Also, notice how close the movement serial numbers are between the two: 6929186 for ours and 6929135 for the previously sold watch. The only difference between these two is the hand style, ours having a thicker hour hand. Don't worry; another example from this batch can be seen here , sold with an extract from the Longines archives as well. This third watch, movement serial 6929157, has that thicker hour hand. This is the kind of forensic accounting that must be done in evaluating the originality of a 79-year-old watch.

Condition-wise, this Longines is very, very nice. I like the look of the subtly aged and creamy dial. The case lines appear to be somewhat strong, but I can't make out much of the original brushed finish. So, it's probably not "unpolished," or maybe it was just worn a good amount over the years, but regardless, this is not an overpolished smooth case, the original architecture is there. One last tip: do you see that dirt/rust on the edge of the crystal? That's a great sign, as anyone trying to prep this watch for a shady sale would likely clean that off the dial.

This Longines lot 122 of the January Fine and Decorative Arts Auction by Quinn's Auctions Galleries on Friday, January 24th, 2025 at 10 am ET. The high bid at the time of publication is $900. Click here to view the lot.

Nestled in a case full of yellow gold Day-Dates and various stone dial Rolex at last week's Miami Beach Antique Show, this humble white gold Datejust caught my eye and has been on my mind ever since.

Given the trend towards "classy" watches as opposed to "sport" watches, most collectors I encountered last week wanted to talk about Day-Dates and Cartiers more than Submariners and Daytonas. That's great; I've always been a fan of the less-sporty vintage options. The watch you'll find me wearing most is a gilt-dialed Explorer, probably the "dressiest" "sport" Rolex. It's smaller and delicate looking and humble. This Datejust is similar to the "sportiest" "dress" watch from the same era. It's solid gold and very clearly a Datejust while featuring the same gilt dial finish as my Explorer and easily mistakable for stainless steel at a glance.

Tip: if you want to spot a vintage Datejust in white gold, look for the little infill on the inside corners of the lugs, mimicking the midcase construction of the Day-Date. Anyway, Tariq probably doesn't want to trade my 1016 for this 1601 straight up so someone else should go and grab this one while it's still available. You'll be the owner of the classiest, most under-the-radar vintage Rolex on the planet.

The seller, Momentum Dubai, is located in Dubai and this Datejust has an asking price of $31,300. Check it out right here .

Nicknamed the "Pre-Explorer," the Rolex ref. 6098 is a 36mm Oyster case that sits in a transition period for the brand. In 1931, Rolex introduced its Perpetual full-rotor automatic movement, thus creating the Bubbleback era of the 1930s and 1940s. By the end of the 1940s, trends moved towards larger cases, and Rolex updated its catalog with watches like the 6098 and the ref. 5030 "Pre-Datejust." Regularly referred to as the Big Bubbleback generation, these references were a big step toward a more familiar Rolex catalog, which by 1956 would officially offer the Submariner, GMT-Master, Explorer, Datejust, and Day-Date.

In this pre-model-name period of Rolex production, a single reference could be used for a variety of different watches. Let me explain with the 6098. This same reference encompasses solid yellow gold cases with "Galaxy" star dials as well as the "Pre-Explorer" stainless steel 6098s worn by mountaineers like Jürg Marmet and Norman Günther Dyhrenfurth on early Everest expeditions. See, very different watches.

The example we have here is more like the early Everest watches and looks to be in strong, original condition. It is on offer at a smaller auction house in the UK and, in my opinion, is one of the best ways to have a true piece of Rolex history for relatively very little money.

This Rolex lot 193 of Tayler & Fletcher's Fine Art, Vintage & Interiors sale starting today, Friday, January 16th, 2025. The high bid at the time of publication is £1,100. Get all the details right here .

After being a bit worried when I sat down to compile the picks this week, it turned out to be a shockingly good week to search the small auction houses of the world. This ref. 7753 NT hails from one of the final years of manually wound, classic case-shaped Carrera generation of the 1960s and very early 1970s. As I have spent time explaining many times before, any Carrera, in this case with a panda or reverse panda dial motif, is considered to be extremely collectible and desirable. For one of the most beautifully designed and legible chronographs ever made, contrasting black and white tones do a ton to send these watches over the top.

This example appears to have a fairly clean dial with little to no spotting or discoloration to note. The lume plots are mostly intact although with some discoloration that may not be the most attractive tone of creamy patina. I can look past some poorly aged lume plots when a watch is seemingly fresh to market, un-messed with, and original. FYI, this Carrera clearly needs a service and some cleaning, but with a Valjoux 7730 inside, that should be easy work for your local watchmaker.

This Heuer lot 1719 of Rendell's Auctioneers & Estate Agents's Antiques and Collectables, inc. silver and jewellery sale starting Thursday, January 23rd, 2025. The estimate is £4,000 to £5,000, and there were no bids at the time of publication. Check it out here .

As is tradition, let's end with a "cheap" eBay find. This one is up for auction and probably won't end above a couple of hundred dollars, but if you're a real vintage head like me, you'll love it.
