Mido was a great brand back in the 1940s and also a client of my favorite case-maker, François Borgel. Other Borgel clients include Patek Philippe (ref. 1463, 565, 2508, etc.), Movado, and Vacheron Constantin. The magic of vintage Mido in 2025 is that you can find stainless steel, water-resistant Borgel cases for what is, relative to the above brands, almost no money. For example, last year, I picked up a Mido-manufactured Abercrombie & Fitch Shipmate with this same rectangular case design for less than $200. At that price, why not?

Borgel's rectangular cases are extremely hard to find. Patented in 1939 under Swiss patent number CH 207378, the caseback and bezel form the two main parts of the case, and three locking strips slide onto the case sides to squeeze everything together. The tolerances are super tight, tight enough to ensure a water-resistant seal with the help of a thick gasket. This same case design is used in Patek's ref. 1485, one of my favorite niche vintage Pateks.

It is not noted on the eBay listing, but I know this Mido case, and it should measure as an undersized-for-2025-but-still-respectable 23mm across by 32mm tall. For reference, Cartier's largest Tank Normal in the 1940s measures the exact same dimensions.

An eBay seller in West Hartford, Connecticut, has listed this Mido for auction ending this Sunday, January 19th at 9:06 pm ET. The current bid price at the time of publication is $24.50. Get all of the details and photos right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Longines 13ZN, A White Gold Datejust, And A Rolex Pre-Explorer

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
After a week of announcements regarding the latest and greatest in the world of modern haute horology from SIHH 2019 , it’s time to flip through the back catalog once again and take solace in some classics. We’re covering all ends of the spectrum this week, with accessible, versatile picks like a timeless ref. 1601 Datejust from Rolex, a spartan Zenith chronograph, and the nicest early Heuer chronograph on the web for my money. For those in search of what they haven’t seen before, fear not, there’s an early Rolex GMT-Master II on the coolest bracelet you’ll see all week, and an alarm-equipped piece connected to a great American luxury retailer. Let's do this.

I’ve long considered the ref. 1601 and ref. 1603 from Rolex to be two of the best buys in watch collecting. With that said, I personally sway towards the 1601, as the fluted bezel is an objectively cleaner design and a more iconic component within the history of the Rolex brand. As other references of the same vintage have steadily climbed upwards in price, such Datejusts have remained relatively reasonable, with minimal market development over the last five or so years. During a late night eBay hunt, I came across this example of the aforementioned reference that stood out, hence its inclusion in this week’s roundup. Despite the fact that countless examples of this reference were indeed produced (along with most other standard fare from Rolex), this is a rather uncommon one, with a seldom-seen matte grey dial, complete with all its custard colored luminous plots still intact and matching the rest of the luminous compound seen on the watch.

At the end of the day, there’s very little to not like in a quality example of the ref. 1601. It’s an accessibly priced piece that can be worn with ease in just about every setting, cased within what many consider to be the ultimate form of the Oyster at 36mm across. While this piece might shortly go out of stock, I can guarantee with absolute certainty that it won’t be going out of style any time soon. An eBay seller based out of Atlanta has this example of the r ef. 1601 listed for $4,150. Click here for the full scoop.

Zenith’s early chronographs are a study in perfect proportions and aesthetic restraint. This watch wholly embodies the notion that less is more, and it shows. Just look at it – not a single element of its design seems unnecessary or out of place, and branding has been kept to a minimum, with just the word "Zenith" and a small applied star falling below the 12 o’clock position. This is what’s known as the ref. A273, and if you weren’t already familiar with this reference, allow me to acquaint you. Beneath its caseback, you’ll find the caliber 146HP column-wheel chronograph, which is based upon the Universal Genève cal. 285 produced by Martel. Visually it’s not dissimilar to most other column-wheel chronographs of this era, but it's still a sight to behold in the eyes of any true watch collector.

This particular piece is being offered in top shape, with a case that looks to have been minimally polished, and a near flawless dial. It also has the original crown which you’ll often find to be replaced on other examples, and it has been serviced within the last year, ensuring easy ownership from the get go. An Italian collector has this piece listed for sale on Chronotrader, and is asking €4,500. Check out more photos and details here.

Five-digit GMT-Master references have shot up in value over the last 12 months, though it's largely those produced in steel that have skyrocketed, and for obvious reasons. Need me to spell it out? P-E-P-S-I. While it may be an unquestionably genius aesthetic – a notion which many are more than aware of – I’ve always had a bit of a thing for full gold GMT-Masters and Subs alike, as the contrast of the rich gold and deep black accents affords these watches a bit more edge in comparison to the borderline preppy Pepsi. Upon seeing this watch pop up for sale, it practically demanded to be included. It’s a standard Ref. 16718, but fitted on an anything but standard bracelet, produced in vibrant 22K yellow gold by the jewelry brand Chrome Hearts. Known best for their oversized accessories emblazoned with gothic text and ornate detailing, the brand has a particularly loyal following in both Los Angeles (its place of origin) and overseas in Japan, where American styles of eras past have long been embraced and celebrated.

While no Chrome Hearts produced watch bracelet is a common sight, they’ve historically been produced in larger numbers in sterling silver, making examples in gold far more sought after. It’s not a look that’s for everyone, and does require a bit of pizzazz to pull off, but if a Rolex with some unique flair is what you’re after, this might just be the watch for you. The Los Angeles dealer Wanna Buy A Watch has this boldly equipped GMT listed on their site for $40,000. Should your wrist be capable of handling it, here’s a link to learn more .

As mentioned in previous installments of this column, the recent dip in Heuer pricing has made for a solid opportunity to acquire what are nothing short of incredible chronographs at a now-reduced rate. Though many would be inclined to sprint straight towards the sports offerings of the '60s and '70s, I’ve always found the earlier pieces to be more interesting, simply because they’re often more elaborately decorated. This example of a so-called “Pre-Carrera” likely dating back to 1940s is chock-full of appealing details, including not only luminous Roman numeral indices, but matching syringe style hands and a stunning salmon dial with sunburst finished sub-dials too. Though the vintage style bracelet that’s currently fitted is not original, I think it looks quite sharp, making this ready to wear by all means.

Though early Heuer chronographs are around, it's genuinely rare to find one in the same condition as the one in question. Its case is unpolished, the radium luminous applications are original, and the dial is largely blemish free. Can’t ask for much more in a watch that’s pushing seven decades. As the old ancient proverb goes, get it while the getting's good. Miami’s Menta Watches has this early Heuer chronograph listed reasonably at $7,000. Find the full listing here.

Once in a while you can see the the light in the strangest of places if you look just right. As a bit of a photography nut, vintage optics often factor into my daily check-ins on eBay, and while hunting down a certain lens from the 50s earlier this week, I chanced upon something truly unusual being offered by an individual specializing in old photo gear. In an age where information is so easily accessible, it's becoming increasingly tougher to find a watch you've never seen before, especially if you’re constantly searching, but I found just that this week, and am now excited to share it with you. Alarm-equipped mechanical watches really don’t get enough love, and at the prices they typically trade at, it’s hard to not see a considerable amount of value for your dollar. They’re functional, amusing to see in action, and offer an accessible entry point into the world of chiming and sound generating mechanical timepieces. This piece is no exception, but is perhaps more interesting than most alarm watches I’ve seen in a while, with Tiffany & Co. branding and an idiosyncratic dial orientation, similar to that of many watches designed with professional drivers in mind. At 33mm across, it is indeed on the smaller side, but this is one of those instances where I’d still advise pulling the trigger. It's just such a compelling watch, with all sorts of curious details, like the squiggly alarm hand, luminous indices, and 24-hour track that traces the outermost edge of the dial. I'd be lying if I said I fully understood who or what this watch was originally intended for, but I can say with full confidence that it’s unbelievably cool, and would make a killer addition any collection of horological oddities or pieces linked to Tiffany. An individual in Ardmore, PA, has this piece listed on eBay with both a starting bid of $995, and a Buy It Now option at $1,395. Find the full listing here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A 1969 Zenith Ref. A273, A Heuer 'Pre-Carrera' Ref. 2443 With Salmon Dial, And A Classic Rolex Datejust

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
After a full week dedicated to the SIHH novelties, vintage watches are finally back today. We start with a great Heuer Autavia "Rindt" coming from a very reputable source, and then look at an intriguing Wingo, which shares many features with the Yema Yachtingraf. You will also see an uncommon Rolex Date reference 1530 and a nice Zenith El Primero with fumé dial. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 20, 2017.

Not much information can be found on Wingo, except for a handful of different watches from the 1960s. For this chronograph, it seems the French company Yema supplied it, given the similarity with its Yachtingraf model, which was also signed LeJour for the U.S. market. They very obviously share the specific looking regatta counter on the right side of the dial, but the 9312 engraved on the inner caseback is a good clue as well, since it was a reference number for the Yema chronographs equipped with the caliber Valjoux 7730, back in 1967. Furthermore, the digits on the outer side of the caseback (without embossed logo) match the numbering system in use for the serial numbers of that period . The watch is described in excellent condition, with an unpolished case and a great bezel, although the listing does not mention the partially missing lume on the minute hand. The dial is in great condition, with a very legible 5-minute countdown on the oversized counter, a crucial indication before the start of a boating race. The winding crown has a similar shape to those observed on the Yema Yachtingraf and Rallygraf, so it is likely original to the watch, while the chronograph movement Valjoux 7730 is said to work properly.

This Wingo Yachtingraf is currently offered on Ebay for $3,500, an ask that seems a tad excessive (even to the seller himself, who prices it at $3,000 on Chrono24 ).

The Autavia (a contraction of Aut omobile and Avia tion) started as a rally dashboard in 1933 , before the catchy name made it to the dials of sporty chronographs in 1962. Those either came in a three-register configuration (reference 2446) or with two registers (reference 3646). Collectors being collectors, a specific classification now allows to sort out the cosmetic changes, with distinctive executions identifying a specific style for the dial and handset. Here, this reference 2446 belongs to the third execution, as it comes with a straight handset (as opposed to the dauphine hands of the first two executions) and lumed indexes on the dial. This configuration is often nicknamed "Rindt" after its famous owner, the Formula 1 driver Jochen Rindt, although he did not wear it with the tachymeter bezel that is mounted on the current watch. Besides the nickname, the present watch has almost everything you could wish for: the 38.5mm case is unpolished, and the crown, pushers, dial and hands are original. It even comes with the original Heuer-stamped buckle, and the chronograph movement Valjoux 72 has been recently serviced.

Abel Court, widely known for his skills at restoring vintage Heuer, just listed this stunning Heuer Autavia 2446 for €35,000 (or around $37,300), and he also considers offers.

The Rolex reference 1530 could hardly hide being born in the 1970s, its angular case and integrated bracelet a dead giveaway. It looks very different than most of Rolex product line, except for the Rolex Oysterquartz, and there is a good reason: these watches are actually related. The Oysterquartz was indeed a very ambitious project from Rolex to break away from the collective Beta 21 that it used in the bulky reference 5100, and come up with its own version of the ultimate quartz movement. Development took longer than expected and did not happen before 1977, so Rolex put the existing automatic caliber 1570 in the 36mm case that was planned for its revolutionary movement – and thus the reference 1530 was born (it was produced between 1974 and 1977). The example here comes with its original papers documenting a production in 1976, as well as some service papers. I would be curious to see whether the replacement of the inner ring is mentioned here, as the lume dots applied there do not match the patina of the handset and are larger than the initial ones. As expected, the case has been polished before, but the watch still comes with the Oyster bracelet reference 96660, which was solely dedicated to the reference 1530.

The Keystone offers this unusual Rolex Date reference 1530 with papers for $8,500.

The reference A385 was among the first Zenith to utilize the groundbreaking El Primero caliber released in 1969. It offered the same edgy case as its fellow reference A384, as opposed to the rounder and more traditional-looking case of the reference A386; its dial was really making it stand out in Zenith catalogs . Indeed, it pushed the concept of contrasting sub-registers further thanks to its fumé dial (the French work for smoky), a finish rarely seen on watches, save the recent production of H. Moser & Cie . The idea behind such a pattern relies on a darker shade around the edges, lightening as we get closer to the center of the dial. The reference A385 was only produced over two years, from 1969 to 1971 – 2,400 examples in total, so it is not a common chronograph. Here, the 37.5mm case is really sharp, with its brushings and angles well preserved. Note however some small imperfections on the minute counter on the left side of the dial; otherwise it is in excellent condition. The crown is original to the watch, matching the Zenith emblem of the caseback, and a Zenith-signed buckle is provided.

You can find this Zenith El Primero A385 priced at €5,499 (or around $5,850).

This final find of the week is definitely not the least interesting one, as the reference 5093 is a fascinating piece produced by Audemars Piguet in the 1950s. The AP museum actually owns one example and its historian, Michael Friedman, gave us a detailed overview of this piece , often nicknamed the “Disco Volante” after its characteristic case shape. This slim 35mm watch with oversized bezel does offer stunning looks, matched by its three-tone dial. Its 2003 caliber is also one of the thinnest ever made, at just 1.64mm thick. The example here comes in yellow gold with an integrated bracelet, different from the one pictured in the Archives from AP but very likely original to the watch nonetheless. The 20251 case number indicates a production in the early 1960s. The dial does not look as pristine as the previous white gold example from Phillips (eventually hammering for over $13,000), but the aging marks are not so bad and the golden ring still looks stunning.

This Audemars Piguet reference 5093 appeared on Instagram, with the seller expecting around $5,000.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Heuer Autavia 'Rindt' Reference 2446, An Angular Rolex Date Reference 1530, A Mysterious Wingo Yachtingraf, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, you followed all the exciting SIHH novelties live from Geneva; today, Bring A Loupe returns with some really gorgeous vintage watches – or vintage-inspired, in the case of my favorite Grand Seiko. You will notably discover how a Speedmaster looked when it was sold in 1971, and admire a nicely preserved Movado. I have also found an intriguing bullhead watch from Breitling, and a not-so-great medical chronograph from Universal Geneve. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 22, 2016.

Finding an untouched example is a recurrent fantasy for watch collectors – probably not as frequent as a time-travel machine to stock up on vintage references, but not far off. Here, this Omega Speedmaster reference 145.022 follows this time-capsule logic: it was purchased in the UK back in 1971 and left in the box since, to the point that the bracelet links are imprinted on the watch holder. As you would expect, the bracelet is tight, the case is intact and the engravings are deep. In short, this condition is exactly what the word "mint" was invented for. While the reference 145.022 is not among the holy grail references that Ben described here , finding a similar set remains pretty rarel, so this watch won’t probably stay around for long. This untouched Speedmaster can be found full set for 5,995 GPB (or around $8,500) here .

Here, I have something to confess: I own this reference and I love it. To me, this watch is the quintessential Grand Seiko, the link between its forgotten past and its current ambitions. At the first glance, this GS might look unassuming, but it traces its roots back to the first Grand Seiko, the mythical reference 3180 released in 1960. As an anniversary edition, the reference SBGW033 remained highly faithful to the original, with the same 35.8 mm case – true, the original version was not manufactured in stainless steel, but still. It made a couple of concessions to modernity between the domed sapphire crystal and the increased power reserve of the manual-wind movement. Grand Seiko notably reached a 72-hour power reserve on a single barrel thanks to a proprietary alloy for the mainspring. A glance at the case polishing, at the sharp katana hands, or at the blued second hand suffice: the attention to detail is obvious, everywhere up to the subtle off-white dial. Only a fraction of the 1,300 examples ever produced made it out of Japan, and this one is being offered for 3,900 GBP (or around $5,500) here .

There is so much to love about this Calendograph: the two-tone dial, the Breguet numerals, the sophisticated lugs, the sharp stainless-steel case – this watch is as good as a vintage triple calendar can get. The case might only be 34.5 mm, but on the wrist I can guarantee it would look bigger thanks to the generous lugs and the narrow bezel. To me, it really demonstrates that vintage Movado should not be overlooked. Some are absolutely stunning – especially when they come in this condition. This Calendograph is offered for 6,200 Euros (or around $6,800) here . While you are on the site you should also have a look at this amazing oversized Longines Calatrava pictured below, but unfortunately it is already spoken for .

William Massena's Breitling "Flying Pizza" is my most vivid memory of his Talking Watches ; its crazy 47 mm diameter might play a role. It also opened my eyes to the concept of a fun and bold weekend watch. This 42 mm Breitling would fit the bill nicely with its chocolate theme and the 1970s orange hands. It is extremely rare to find the chronograph pushers positioned on top of the case like the horns of a bull, hence the "bullhead" nickname that this Breitling shares with some other coveted Omega and Seiko. While this arrangement is visually striking, its true purpose was actually to facilitate usage while driving. You might remember the Chrono-Matic branding from very early automatic chronographs from Heuer; Breitling recovered the use of this term when Heuer decided that its marketing impact was not stellar. EDIT: Here this mention is actually an indication that a dial from a later Breitling was placed into this manual-wound chronograph, the crown of the Chrono-Matic model is indeed at the bottom of the case rather than on top between the pushers. Bachmann & Scher is selling this unconventional Breitling for 3,950 Euros (or around $4,300) here , and it was recently serviced.

This utilitarian chronograph from Universal Geneve is still under $500 on eBay here ; sadly, there is a very sound reason for that "bargain": a garbage dial! Straight away, the overall printing appears more than dubious: the color is too strong for a 65-year-old watch, and the thickness of the letters is too fat; in short, it looks too new to be true. As expected, this Medico-Compax displays the pulsation tachymeter relevant for the job, except this one was murdered! Very quickly you can see that the scales are too crude. And this leads us to the worst part of the dial: the sub-registers. The minute counter on the left one is a tragedy, the 3/6/9 minutes markers are not extended long enough. Those used to be crucial to time phone calls at a time where you would pay by increments of three minutes; therefore on vintage chronographs you often see them properly highlighted – a clear miss here. There are also numerous font issue, the most flagrant being the shape and spacing of the word Medico – but at this stage no doubt remains. If you want to look at a nicer one, the pink-gold piece below was recently sold by Menta Watches for $3,500 here .

This is the second time this Vulcain Cricket has made it to Bring A Loupe. It was indeed featured two years ago when it was ultimately passed on. It is offered again at the end of the month, and I feel the provenance is established a bit hastily. It has indeed been proven that this watch was NOT the famous Cricket gifted to Harry Truman by The White House Press Corps; the unmarked case back and the different dial established that easily. This watch came from the estate of Margaret Truman – Harry’s daughter – so it might have been owned by the President or some of his family members, maybe Margaret herself, which could explain the rather feminine look of the bracelet. This Cricket is offered here , while you can find pictures of the most famous Vulcain owned by Truman below.

Nowadays, finding a clean two-register chronograph from Heuer for less $2,500 seems like a great coup, doesn't it? I mean, good looking dial, the right logo – but if you start looking at the case, the opportunity suddenly becomes much less appealing. You can clearly see some darker spots where the chrome is flaking off. This is exactly why I would not recommend to ever go for chrome cases – or gold-plated ones for that matter – both are cheaper to get, but wear off very quickly. Note that here the case back is in stainless steel, and marked accordingly – this mix can sometimes be confusing. You can find more on those "economy models" from Heuer here . This marbled Heuer is listed for 2,250 Euros (or around $2,450) here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Untouched Omega Speedmaster, An Unusual Breitling, And Other Interesting Finds (Including A Vulcain Cricket That Might Have Belonged To President Truman)

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe, and Happy Friday! LVMH Watch Week 2025 is officially coming to a close and, with it, Hodinkee.com is littered with new releases from the group. Consider this your quick vintage reprieve. While you scroll back and catch up on Louis Vuitton's latest Tambours, TAG Heuer's new Formula 1 Chronograph, and so on, save Bring A Loupe for some time in the middle of your weekend reading session. Or stay right here and enjoy the best vintage watches available for sale on the World Wide Web immediately!

As for results from our last column, over in the UK, the Rolex Ref. 6098 Big Bubbleback "Pre-Explorer" hammered for 2,200 GB , and the 1970 Heuer Carrera Ref. 7753 NT ended up at 3,600 GBP . My favorite of the bunch, the Longines Ref. 5415 chronograph 13ZN, is heading up to the auction block this morning here , prebidding is at $4,250. Finally, on eBay, the Mido with a François Borgel waterproof case found a new home for $540 .

Let's get into this week's picks!

Ah yes, finally, a reference 6542 GMT-Master. This is where it all starts for the model that helped propel Rolex into sport watch stardom. First released in 1955, celebrating its 70th anniversary this very year, the design is universal, replicated by other brands for decades, and still very similar to the current model "Pepsi" GMT-Master II. The Bakelite bezel of this reference has come to be the defining feature but is also the reason that this specific watch was discontinued. The bezels are prone to cracking, much more fragile than their aluminum successors, and were often replaced back in the day. Beyond fragility, the radioactivity of the radium inside these bezels was the reason a US Naval officer and his family sued Rolex in 1961, claiming that his 6542 had caused cancer. Rolex then "recalled" the bezels. All that said, finding an example in original condition like the one we have here is next to impossible at this point.

Beyond sporting its original bezel, the condition of this watch is truly jaw-dropping. Knowing fully how annoying it is to read on a website where you only have the photos to go off of, 1950s Rolex in this condition has to be seen in the metal to be appreciated. The sharpness of a case like this is almost indescribable.

Continuing on the theme of outstanding condition, the dial here shows a bit of tropical hues and is overall even throughout. Full tropical dials are one thing, but when a gilt dial Rolex like this one sits somewhere between black and brown, the look is far less jarring while being just as charming.

This 1958 example has correct small lume plots, being later in the 6542 production run after Rolex experimented, creating a batch of dials with larger lume plots, and is offered on a period correct (at least) Rolex "big logo" Oyster bracelet. The seller notes that the watch is fresh to market after having been discovered in Southern California, sitting for a while, after a quick service and crystal change, it is now ready to find a new home.

The seller, Jon at Dial Back Vintage, has listed this outstanding GMT-Master as "price on request." I requested and found the ask to be a reasonable $160,000. Get all of the details right here .

The creations of Cartier London's workshop in the 1960s and 1970s are among my favorite watches ever made, flat out. The man behind all of this was Jean-Jacques Cartier. He assumed the position of his father, running Cartier London, after WWII, and by the early 1960s, decided to start producing wristwatches in London rather than continue to import Paris-made pieces. The watches were made by hand, one by one, and the designs are uniquely "Swinging Sixties" London – think Maxi Oval, Crash, Pebble, and so on.

Dialing into this particular Cartier London example, it is a Tank LC (or, if you want to be very specific, a Tank JJC for Jean-Jacques Cartier) in a "small" size that is the "smallest" offered by this branch of Cartier. Not quite as small as the 23mm by 15.5mm "mini" that came out of Paris in 1969, the London small measures 26mm by 19mm, fitting in the undersized bucket without being so much of "a look."

Coming up for auction in its birth city, this one caught my eye because of the bracelet. Any Cartier London watch is rare and interesting, but on a bracelet, that's really special. I am reminded of a Maxi Oval – on a bracelet – which sold at Phillips last year for $279,400 , and that was present a few weeks ago at the Miami Beach Antique Show in the booth of Davide Parmigiani. This watch also sparked a memory in my mind as I have seen a London LC on this bracelet before. In December of 2023, Christie's offered a "large" version of this same watch, which ended up selling for $47,880 .

This Cartier is lot 54 of the Watches & Luxury Items auction by Roseberys London on Wednesday, February 5th, 2025, at 5 am ET. The presale estimate is £10,000 to £15,000. Click here to view the lot.

P.S. If a Baignoire is more your speed, check out lot 55 , a London example in a great size with its original deployant buckle.

It's been a whole two weeks since I included a Movado in Bring A Loupe, and that should be considered commendable, really. My love for the vintage era of Movado is unavoidable at this point and if great examples keep popping up for great prices on eBay, I will be forced to continue to either buy them for myself or include them here.

Here we have a late 1950s time-only Movado with everything going for it, let me tell you why. The case is made by the great François Borgel, maker of the best waterproof cases of this time period and supplier to Patek Philippe, Vacheron, etc. It is 33mm in diameter and features interesting lugs that are actually quite similar to those found on Louis Vuitton's new Tambour Convergence . Now for the dial, it is certainly manufactured by Stern Frères as is evident by the numeral font. This is the same numeral font from the Rolex featured here a couple of weeks back and is seen on the ref. 8171 Padellone . Furthermore, the dial is black. Black dials are extremely hard to find on vintage Movados. Well, that's not entirely true because you can find a few for sale at any one time, but almost all of them have been repainted to be black. This one here is the real deal original.

Lastly, the movement. This is Movado's caliber 431 full-rotor automatic, produced from 1956 to 1960. During this era, Movado produced all of their movements in-house, and they were actually quite early to automatic winding calibers, most of those being "bumper" automatics. The 431 is the culmination of Movado's automatic winding development and, in my opinion, one of the best time-only movements they ever made.

An eBay seller in Tempe, Arizona, has put this Movado up for a buy-it-now price of $795. Get all of the details and photos right here .

While I might know more than any one person should know about that last Movado, here is one I know almost nothing about – probably because there isn't much to know about it! This is an unsigned or "anonymous" cabriolet-style wristwatch dating to the 1940s. The seller here is one I always check, as I think he has a great eye for vintage, and this watch is excellent proof of that. The condition is really solid overall, and if you're looking for something no one else is going to have, this might be the one for you.

The detailing on the case is really nice, with some engraved lines on the dial "cover," and the sizing is not bad at 25mm wide by 36mm tall. The movement doesn't look like anything to scoff at, either. It is unsigned but made in Switzerland with some surprisingly interesting finishing. I would expect to see a much more industrial-looking caliber in a watch like this. All in all, this is a funky, surprisingly well-made watch with a unique design – a perfect BAL watch!

The seller, Arthur at Shuck The Oyster in Germany, is offering this anonymous vintage watch for €1,500. Check it out right here .

One last eBay gem to finish off the week here: this is a Universal (no. l) Disco Volante with a sterling silver and wood case made in Germany in the 1960s or 1970s. I'm always looking for quirky vintage disco volante shapes, and this one fits the bill. I don't think I've ever encountered another watch with a sterling silver and wood case. The case is manufactured in Germany, but based on other Universal watches I can find on the web, it looks like the brand utilized Swiss movements, namely from ETA. So, while this seller does not state the maker or provide a photo of the manually wound caliber inside, I will go out on a limb to say that I would be willing to bet it is a Swiss-made movement.

An eBay seller in London has put this Disco Volante Universa for a buy-it-now price of £775.00. Click here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Stunning GMT-Master, A Cartier London Tank, And A Great Vintage Movado

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
For the second week in a row, a vintage Heuer Autavia makes the cover of Bring A Loupe, but there is a good reason for its return. The 1st execution reference 2446 offered today could indeed become the most expensive Autavia publicly sold, if its asking price is met. Our other picks for the week include a full set Rolex Daytona 6263, a stunning Omega Seamaster 300, and a funky Jaeger-LeCoultre Memovox nicknamed the "Snowdrop." But there is no doubt who the star of the show is – the Heuer 2446 is going to have collectors worldwide watching and waiting to see what happens. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 27, 2017.

The news shook the Heuer community this morning: a previously unknown first execution Autavia reference 2446 is being offered for sale. Without any doubt, the combination of full lume hands and big registers is the most coveted configuration for an Autavia (and we can say any Heuer) and the rarest. And when we say rare, we truly mean it, as less than 10 surviving examples are currently known . More importantly, all Heuer addicts seem to agree that this watch is the best looking first execution 2446 ever seen ; the words "finest vintage Heuer chronograph" were even used by none other than Jeff Stein, of OnTheDash fame (and the subject of a recent Talking Watches ) – strong words, coming from a guy who's handled literally hundreds of vintage Heuer watches.

Just a single close-up look at the dial shows how perfect this example is, with the type of flawless dial you almost never have a chance to see. The white oversized sub-registers contrast amazingly with the black dial, as they first did when the Autavia was released in 1962. The full lume handset, a key characteristic of the first elusive execution, is just as breathtaking; the radium lume has taken on an absolutely stunning patina that can be also observed on the indexes. And naturally, there is no question as to the originality of all parts. The serial and movement numbers of this example fall right within the correct production timeline (extremely close to those another Autavia 2446, well known in the Heuer community and seen in Jeff's Talking Watches).

A look at the case shows the same amazing state of preservation as the dial; the condition is again incredible, starting with the large, clean bevels on the lugs. Of course, not everything is perfect – there is some light chipping to the bezel but it's pretty insignificant in comparison to the impressive overall condition. The seller also adds that the caseback does not bear any watchmaker's engravings (it was common practice for the inside of the caseback to have the date of service scratched into it) which might indicate that its caliber Valjoux 72 was never serviced before. Indeed, the movement looks spotless, with none of the light traces that a servicing might produce. In short, this is probably the ultimate Heuer grail, and the type of watch I dream of being able to feature every week in this column.

As you can imagine this one won't come cheap; it is actually the most expensive Heuer Autavia 2446 ever offered publicly. If it sells it will become the new record for any Heuer without historical provenance, since a Heuer Monaco worn by Steve McQueen in the movie Le Mans was sold at auction for almost $800,000 in 2012 . So what is the listing price of this outstanding Autavia? The ask from the German dealer RareBirds is €200,000, or around $213,600. This is obviously a record for any Heuer Autavia but the rarity and condition of this example easily beats that of a later 2446 that fetched $125,000 at Christie's last year .

Now there is a pretty big gap between the $125,000 at Christie's, and over $200,000, but Jeff Stein thinks that given the rarity and condition of this 2446, it could happen. He says, "Someone will pay $200,000 for this Autavia if they want the very best of the vintage Heuer breed . . . no one could reasonably expect to ever find another of these, in this condition." You can find this exceedingly rare Heuer Autavia 2446 1st Execution on the seller's website.

A "full set" always brings a lot of extra value with vintage Rolex, much more than you would expect some old box and papers to be worth. Intrinsically, they should not be considered as an absolute guarantee of authenticity, since those elements can be easily sourced independently and doctored to make them look original. Yet, finding a genuine full set testifies to the meticulousness of the owner, which hopefully translates into outstanding condition for the watch. Here, this Daytona 6263 does come with the whole shebang, including the original receipt from the sale in 1988. And its condition is truly impressive, from the seemingly untouched case to the perfect dial with tropicalized sub-registers (that is, they've become slightly brown). The watch bears a serial number starting with the letter R, showing a really late production for this reference 6263, which would be replaced by the modern reference 16520 in 1988. The transition between vintage and modern is obvious in those 2 distinct references; the plexiglass crystal gives way to a sapphire crystal, the case diameter increases to 40mm, and the manual-wound Valjoux 72 disappears, and is replaced with an automatic chronograph movement supplied by Zenith.

The Italian dealer Tempus Orologi lists this full set Rolex Daytona 6263 for $85,000.

A double-signed dial is a coveted feature with vintage watches, as they make the piece even rarer than the "standard" iteration, and testify to a privileged relationship between a brand and a retailer (take Rolex or Patek Philippe and Tiffany & Co., for instance). In that regard, the Swiss-based Meister is well known for appearing on the dials of multiple examples of Omegas, notably on vintage Speedmaster and Seamaster. The early Seamaster 300 existed with or without date; the reference 165.024 here predates the date version reference 166.024 which was only unveiled in 1967. Here, the attraction of this Omega Seamaster 300 isn't just in the fact that it's got a double signed dial; its condition is truly outstanding. The 40mm case with characteristic lyre lugs looks unpolished, the lume is nicely preserved, and the plexiglass is the original part with the Omega logo. The watch also comes with its original bracelet (the reference 1939) just as another great-looking Meister-signed Seamaster 300 previously did.

The Italian dealer Alessio Zenga just showed this rare Omega Seamaster 300 with Meister dial on its Instagram page, and priced it at €15,000 (or around $16,000).

The panda/reverse panda configuration is always a big hit with vintage chronographs, whatever the signature on the dial might be. This explains the sustained popularity of these Hamiltons, often referred to as the "Poor Man's Carrera". Interestingly, they were actually manufactured by Heuer, which explains why the reference number engraved on the case matches Heuer's nomenclature, the 7723 reference being one of the economy chronographs that the brand offered. First thing to check, the caseback says "Stainless steel", and not "Stainless steel caseback", which would mean a steel caseback only, with a less desirable chrome plated case (among Heuer economy models, the reference 7723 did indeed come with a full stainless steel case, but many others did not ). The dial is nice, with only small traces of aging to the sub-registers. The crown is unsigned, so it is very likely a replacement part, and given the poor fit of the endlinks one must also assume that the Hamilton-signed bracelet is not original to the watch. Lastly, I would ask the dealer what he means when describing the original hands as bearing "color matched lume", which in the worst case scenario would imply a relume.

This Hamilton reference 7723 is offered for $2,100.

The vintage Memovox "Snowdrop" actually inspired the recent re-issue, the Master Memovox that Jack covered. Yet, the re-issue did not keep the very 1970s case design that the reference E877 originally came with. The 43mm case is very distinctive in not having any protruding lugs, and was even fitted with a funky two-tone bracelet – steel and blue as you would expect from the era. It is nonetheless a rare reference, only produced in around 2,000 pieces between the different dial configurations (silver, black and blue dials can be found in the stainless steel cases). The example here comes on a strap, with a radiating blue dial. The high-beat caliber 916 explains the "Speed Beat mention" on the case back and relies on a full size rotating rotor, as opposed to the bumper winding system of the early automatic Memovox. As with most Memovoxes, the alarm is set through the upper crown, which controls the rotating center disc. The handset here is correct for the reference E877, although some aging (dirt?) can be seen on the lume of the minute hand.

The Keystone listed this Jaeger-LeCoultre Memovox E877 "Snowdrop" for $6,500.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: The Most Expensive Example Of The Ultra-Rare Heuer Autavia 2446 Ever Offered (And More)

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! After bringing out a heavy hitter vintage Rolex to headline last week's edition, we'll stick to the niche this go around. One note about that great GMT-Master before we get to the picks. I mentioned that the asking price was a "reasonable $160,000," and I should have given more context, such as previous auction results of $301,297 and $318,507 for similar examples.

To recap the results from last week, the Movado that I fell in love with on eBay has been marked as sold; the asking price was $795 – someone picked up a great watch. I hope it was one of you! And a reminder, the two Cartier London pieces up for auction at Roseberys, a Tank LC on bracelet and a Baignoire , are set to hit the block next Wednesday, February 5th. Both could make a nice little Valentine's Day gift.

Let's get into this week's picks!

While an entirely different brand today, early Franck Muller was a powerhouse. You may have come across the "Master of Complications" billing of this brand, and in no way is that purely marketing speak. Muller started with a tourbillon wristwatch in 1984 and followed that up with a tourbillon wristwatch with jumping hours (1986), a minute repeater tourbillon (1987), a minute repeater with perpetual calendar and tourbillon (1989), a tourbillon split-seconds chronograph (1990), a minute repeater worldtime (1990), and, this watch, a double-faced worldtime with monopusher chronograph (1991).

All the above watches were signed "Franck Genève" on the dial and represent what collectors now call "pre-brand" as his eponymous brand did not officially launch until 1991, after the creation of these watches. Forgotten for years, these have become more and more sought after with the rise in interest for neo-vintage era watches in general. The connection to a modern brand that no longer produces watches like this only adds to the intrigue. Early Franck Genève-signed watches have done very well at auction as of late, with a minute repeater perpetual calendar selling for $251,978 last May at Sotheby's and a platinum Janus selling for $245,053 in November at Phillips.

This dual-sided world time monopusher chronograph was officially named the Janus after the two-faced Roman god of gates, doors, doorways, beginnings, and endings. In total, it is believed that Franck made about 15 examples of the Janus, with production split between platinum and yellow gold. The watch on offer here is made unique by the layout of the rear dial with pulsometer, telemeter, and tachymeter scales, whereas other examples display only a pulsometer. The caseback is engraved "piece unique."

Speaking of the case, it is hallmarked "JPH" denoting the casemaker – the legendary, GPHG Special Jury Prize-winning , and current in-house casemaker for Rexhep Rexhepi, Jean-Pierre Hagmann . Muller's choice of casemaker speaks to the overall quality of these pre-brand pieces. In my mind, I see these watches as a talented independent watchmaker in Geneva going around town and hiring the absolute best suppliers to produce the best watches possible. These have a real passion project vibe.

Check out the full listing over on A Collected Man. The asking price is £100,000.

I've been waiting for a great vintage Boucheron to pop up for sale ever since bringing back this column. Vintage Boucheron is so unlike almost any other watch brand you can find: modern, vintage, whatever. I'm a big fan. I've written about the brand in depth and can't seem to pass up the opportunity to buy a good example when it comes to me.

Produced in France and sold on Place Vendôme, the obvious comparison is Cartier. And that's not too far-fetched. Both brands created wristwatches mostly to serve jewelry client demand and stuck to a very clear aesthetic across models. Of course, for Cartier, that meant unique "dress" watch shapes with Roman Numerals and eggshell white dials. But for Boucheron, the look was more monochrome. Typically, you will find Boucherons with dial finishes that match the case finishing, and very rarely is the brand name displayed anywhere. You're lucky if you get hour markers.

The most common vintage Boucheron models we see on the market today are the Tank-esque Reflet and the square-shaped Carrée, but, like Cartier, many pieces were made in small numbers for clients upon request. I believe this "Gondole" shape is one of those upon-request situations. A new-to-me shape for Boucheron, the gently-rounded case clocks in at 30mm wide by 28mm tall, which is quite substantial for the era. The twelve o'clock lug features a design patented by Boucheron in 1958 and commonly found on the Carrée, where the "lug" portion slides out from the case, allowing for a custom-sized single-piece leather strap. While I have never seen another "Gondole," it's worth noting that this case is in white gold, and very few Boucherons are found in this metal. The seller notes that about 3% of Boucheron watches were made in white gold.

Gary at HighendTime has listed this Boucheron for $6,750. You can get all of the details via the Pushers.io app right here .

At this point, I can imagine that the UG Polerouter needs no introduction here on Hodinkee . But maybe the Polarouter does! When Gérald Genta's first design hit the market in 1954, it did so under this spelling rather than the one we know better today and the one that modern Universal Genève seems to prefer. I will let the newly relaunched UG off the hook here as it is quite confusing, and I don't expect them to "misspell" the name of their very first watch just to keep vintage nerds like me happy.

Anyway, yeah, the first examples of this now famous watch were signed and marketed as Polarouter – the more you know. Here, we have a non-luminous, silver-dialed example produced under the reference 20217/3. Any Polarouter is pretty hard to find at this point but I will say that if I had to own one (if you really twisted my arm) I would prefer one with a non-luminous dial and hands like this. In 1954, radium was the lume du jour, and, no, I'm not really afraid of radium hurting me, but it might hurt my watch over time! I have seen too many radium burned and pimpled dials to choose lume over non-lume if all else is equal.

The Polerouter was made in over 1,000 different variants, and most of them featured microtor automatic movements, but these early iterations predated microtor technology. So our watch correctly features the UG caliber 138SS "bumper" automatic, which is slightly annoying to hear and feel on your wrist, but if you want the rarity of a Polarouter, this is what you have to deal with!

An eBay seller in Billericay, United Kingdom, has put this Polarouter up for auction, ending on Wednesday, February 5th at 6 pm EST. Get all of the details and photos right here .

Immediately going back on my word about not preferring radium lume, this 1953 Oyster Precision features just that. The reference 6424 is not common enough for me to be picky about lume, I suppose. Among the various simple, non-Datejust, non-Explorer, non-Date vintage Rolex references, I am always on the lookout for this one. The 6424 has a 36mm case that is eerily similar to that of the early ref. 1016 Explorers, but because it is manually wound, it is actually quite a bit thinner on the wrist than that Explorer or even a Datejust of the period. I hesitated to put this watch in Bring A Loupe because I think it is one of the best-kept secrets in the world of vintage Rolex. If you want a watch that simply says, "This is a clean vintage Rolex," this is one to consider.

I call out the 36mm sizing because most of the OPs of this period measure 34mm, which is fine; of course, I will never scoff at a 34mm vintage Rolex. But I think we all can agree that 36mm is a more universally loved size, right? As much as we can all agree on anything here at Hodinkee, at least. Beyond the specs, it's important to wait for a 6424 that has a nice case like this one. Without the clearly defined lines of the lugs, an Oyster case of this period really loses a lot of its charm.

An eBay seller in Brazil has listed this Rolex up for auction ending on Friday, February 7th at 3:01pm EST. Check it out right here .

Lastly, a quick eBay find with a name on the dial that few will recognize. LeGant was created as an in-house, or in-catalog, brand for Montgomery Ward, the gigantic catalog and department store company based in my hometown of Chicago. The name was meant to be a play on the word "elegant" as an attempt to lure customers into thinking the watch was a bit higher quality than it really is. That's not to say this is a low-quality watch or anything. It was likely produced by Mondia, a part of the Mondia-Zenith-Movado triumvirate of the 1970s, and features a stainless steel case, Valjoux 7736 movement, and an absolutely wild dial design.

Mondia made an identical chronograph with this same dial design, you can find them in a bright blue , a deep red, and, like the one we have here, an almost forest green. I love the dials on these and actually prefer the LeGant branding, as I would rather have a watch with a connection to Montgomery Ward and Chicago. The look is very 1970s funk as is the case with most Mondia watches, it seems like the brand was used for creating very on-trend, almost fashion-watch-esque designs back when a "cheap" watch featured a Valjoux chronograph movement.

An eBay seller in Derry, New Hampshire has posted this Mondia for the buy-it-now price of $635. Check it out right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Pre-Brand Franck Muller, A Boucheron, And eBay Picks Galore

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
The first edition of Bring A Loupe for 2017 is solely focused on chronographs and features one of the most elegant of them all: a Patek Philippe Reference 1463 with a fine waterproof case, here in a dressy 18k rose gold. Other more tool watch propositions are also included, such as a military issued Breitling 817, which comes with its original box and expandable bracelet. We will also spend some time on a very dishonest Girard-Perregaux Olimpico currently offered on Ebay. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 6, 2017.

With any vintage Patek is often safer to assume that fewer stainless steel examples have been produced, than watches with rose gold cases. Except that it is not true for the Patek Philippe 1463 –quite the opposite, since the rose gold model is by far the rarest configuration among the 750 examples produced between 1940 and the late 1960s (the regular production ended in 1965 but Philipps recently auctioned one Cartier-signed 1463 produced in 1968). Interestingly, a 2013 lot description from Christie's notes that around 50 rose gold 1463s have surfaced, which still sounds like a reasonable number today. This chronograph definitely has a dressy side – especially in rose gold– but it was built as a true tool watch, with a distinctive waterproof case manufactured by the company François Borgel (later renamed Taubert Freres); the reference 1463 was actually the very first waterproof chronograph made by Patek. Yet, here this piece is not only worthy for its sheer rarity; its condition looks stellar as well. The dial is free of imperfection, with the accent on "Genève" remaining, always a sign that it has not been touched up severely. The case itself is impressive, and has kept its original lines, with thick lugs, crisp sunburst pushers, and sharp angles to the waterproof caseback. As we could have expected the gold hallmarks seem intact under the lugs, which explains why its case is described as "probably untouched." Lastly, it would be interesting to read on the provided Archives extract, whether this chronograph was originally sold in South America in the 1950s, as most of the other known rose gold reference 1463s were.

The dealer OnlyVintage offers this spectacular Patek Philippe reference 1463 for €310,000 (or around $325,000).

This West End Watch shares a common point with the previous Patek Philippe 1463. Its condition is obviously nowhere near as good, but its waterproof case came from the very same manufacturer: Taubert Frères . Furthermore, it is almost identical to the Mido Multicenter-chrono that we previously featured in Bring A Loupe . And there is a good reason: West End Watch was rebranding watches from several manufacturers, including Longines , Cyma and Mido. Multicenter-chronos from West End are hard to spot but they seem to have existed with the same dial configurations than Mido, including a version without scale , and the present one (formerly shown on Instagram ). Here, the radium on the hands and indexes shows the expected aging for a watch from the late 1930s and so does the dial. Compared with the more numerous Mido-signed versions, the handset looks correct, but the flat pushers are intriguing as we would have expected the same sunburst pattern as that found on the Patek Philippe 1463 – but they are not necessarily incorrect. Inside the case, the Mido caliber 1300 can be found; it allows for the center chronograph complication with the blued hand for the seconds, while the red one tracks the elapsed minutes.

This rare West End Watch Multicenter-Chrono was listed on Ebay €3,500 (or around $3,900), which is quite a price tag for such a watch, although the seller considers lower offers.

It seems that the Italian army had a real talent for getting amazing watches issued to itself, if we look at watches like the Zenith Cairelli, and the present Breitling reference 817. The Breitling actually fit the CP-1 specifications, hence its 39.5mm diameter; most of them were worn by helicopter pilots. Very few survived; only around 30 remaining watches are actually known today. It is even rarer to find one with the original yellow box AND the the original bracelet; the seller says this one was sourced directly from Italy. The overall condition is outstanding, described as NOS (New Old Stock) in the listing – something the pictures can vouch for. For instance, the caseback has kept its original brushing, with the military engravings clearly legible, E.I standing for Esercito Italiano , or Italian Army. The dial is near perfect, with the painted numerals taking a greenish tint, something common for the reference 817. It is also not problematic that the handset's patina is rather yellow/orange, this discrepancy in lume is often observed – although here, it must be noted that a small chip of tritium seems to have fallen off the chronograph seconds hand.

The UK dealer Sweeping Hand offers this very complete Breitling reference 817 for £14,000 (or around $17,250).

This Universal Genève Compur has a lot going for it, starting with a beautiful black dial with painted numerals. The case itself is very appealing (although slightly polished); its 38mm size would look even bigger on the wrist thanks to the flat bezel. Yet, it is not a perfect Ebay find, as the handset is incorrect for this reference. It is very obvious in the chronograph sub-registers where the hands are both too short and of an incorrect shape, but it is also true of the central handset, although those would be easier to source. In short, this watch is an attractive project, but a project nonetheless (by random luck, I spoke yesterday with a dealer who used to own this watch, and he had previously undertaken the search for correct sub-registers hands without any luck).

This Universal Genève Compur is listed on Ebay with current bidding above $4,150.

The Olimpico is a fascinating serie of chronographs released by Girard-Perregaux for the consecutive Olympic Games; this reference 8846 dating to 1968. It is however incorrect (unfortunately) in many points, the most obvious being again the hands – all of them, except the one placed in the sub-register at 6 o'clock. If you compare this one to a proper reference Olimpico 8846 with black dial, it also appears that the pushers and crown are incorrect here as well, all being too short and small. The low definition of the pictures doesn't allow proper evaluation of the bezel, but it still looks surprisingly un-smooth. Even worse, the caseback reads "Fond Acier Inoxydable," which could be translated into "Stainless Steel Caseback" which implies the case proper is not actually be made of stainless steel as described in the listing, although it is more likely coming from another reference, from comparing this watch with other known casebacks for the 8846. As for the stainless steel bracelet, it is undoubtedly signed Girard-Perregaux, but the gap between the lugs might indicate that it is another of the many parts not original to this watch.

You can find this avoidable Girard-Perregaux Olimpico offered on Ebay for a Buy-It-Now price of $2,499, although purchase is obviously not recommended.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Chronograph-Only Edition, Including A Splendid Patek Philippe Reference 1463 In Rose Gold, A Complete Breitling Reference 817, A Disappointing Girard-Perregaux Olimpico, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, Bring A Loupe focuses on a perennial favorite: vintage Omegas, from an intriguing Railmaster to an exotic Speedmaster. We will also examine one of the very first Rolex Submariners with crown guards, a reference 5513 with a flawless gilt dial. And to round things out, we have a couple of chronographs from Breitling and Universal Geneve, each with their own interesting quirks. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 8, 2016.

This dial instantly tells you one thing: you are not looking at your average Submariner. This vintage Sub exhibits exactly what we love: a deep glossy dial and a fat case. The dial is marvelous, with a lacquer finish characteristic of the early 5513s. Until 1966, the reference 5513 boasted a glossy dial with gilt lettering ("gilt" referring to the golden color). Only after 1966 would the Submariners have the matte dials we described here . Manufactured in 1963, this Submariner was made within the first year of production, and offers the early pointed crown guards. At the bottom of the dial you can observe the word "Swiss" only; accompanying the underline below the line Oyster Perpetual, it marks the transitional period from radium to the much less hazardous tritium as the lume material. The case of this 5513 has been polished, but the lugs and bevels testify that it was done sparingly, and by an experienced hand. The back sticker remains as well, a sign that this watch was not worn much over the past 50+ years. Though the riveted bracelet is a couple of years away from being period-correct, it still matches the style of a vintage Sub, so don't consider its presence a deal-breaker. You can find this lovely 5513 offered by a French dealer here .

When I first saw this Top Time, I instantly fell for the watch; but I was also worried by the lack of transaction history for the seller, with only one previous deal being listed on his profile. Since then, I've checked with the renowned Breitling collector who initially owned and sold this beautiful reference 1765 to ensure that everything was okay. Good news: this Top Time is indeed his former watch, and the buyer is now considering parting with this great chronograph on eBay. Breitling Top Times came in a variety of shape and sizes; the reference 1765, with a 42 mm diameter, is the biggest of them all. It is also the only one to feature a bezel, which you might have recognized from the coveted Co-Pilot reference 765. And this extra large chronograph houses the reliable Venus 178, a three-register chronograph from the late 1960s. Lastly, you should note the contrasting orange second hand, which adds a dash of color to an otherwise more conventional design. This awesome Top Time is offered for 6,300 Euros or around $6,900 on eBay here , it was apparently serviced a couple of weeks ago.

We spent one week with a similar Memovox , and we loved it! Finding a Memovox is not hard, as this iconic model from Jaeger-LeCoultre was widely produced starting in the 1950s. As it often happens with dressy vintage watches, black dials are much more rare. Add to that the original Gay Frères bracelet and the box, amd suddenly this combo appears much more exceptional. The dauphine hands as well as the engravings allow us to date this Memovox to the early 1960s, as such it still features a bumper-winding movement (that is, one in which the rotor swings in a semicircle rather than a full circle, striking against spring bumpers at either end of the arc) – the caliber 825, a reference for its alarm complication. I really like here that all the tritium points are intact, which helps one forgive the unfortunate deep marks on the case back. This Memovox is offered on eBay for $6,995 here , and the seller considers lower offers.

Vintage Rancheros are regular guests on "Buyer Beware" – most of those are obvious "Franken" watches, built out of spare parts from other Omega references. However, this Ranchero looks nothing like the original Ranchero reference 2990, and here this is not a problem. This is actually a much later iteration sold only in the Belgian market in the mid-1970s – Omega dates the production to 1976 here . This later Ranchero is the reference 166.0218, and offers a very interesting case shape. This case reminds me of vintage King Seiko and Grand Seiko, and I mean that as a compliment. You will notice the same alternation of sharp angles and polished surfaces that you see in the wonderful case work of the vintage Japanese models – nicely preserved on this Swiss counterpart. The crystal is not free of scratches, but that's an easy fix and the great case condition more than makes up for the inconvenience. In addition, the watch comes with an Omega box, and what might well be the original strap and buckle. The fact that it is offered from Belgium – its original intended market – is another encouraging sign. While those Rancheros are obviously less valuable than the original references from the late 1950s, this one seems like a smart pick as it is currently available for 500 Euros or around $550 on eBay here .

The Space-Compax is also widely faked. You probably know the main telltales by now, but if not this page offers a great recap. Good news this time, though; this example is all correct, right down to the rubber pushers and the plexi with the UG logo engraved in the center. The indexes on the dial reveal that it belongs to the second generation (also the most commonly faked). With its 38 mm asymmetrical case, this is an atypical, and very attractive, chronograph, with some great red accents, echoed by the red stitching on the included strap. The UG-signed buckle provided is magnificent and the only regret here is that this watch does not come on its original Gay Frères bracelet. The seller is accepting offers above 10,000 Euros or around $11,000 here .

When you love vintage watches, sometimes you have to face the harsh truth: you simply don't know or can't find anything about a particular combination. This is the case with this Omega Railmaster: it bears the correct reference number 2914-4, the case is the usual 38 mm, but it is troubling on many counts. The hands, for instance, exhibit a completely different shape than those found on "regular" Railmasters. Similarly, the dial displays Arabic numerals that I have never seen, instead of the more familiar 12-3-6-9 configuration. The minute track also differs widely from one version to the other. The Railmaster text here follows the the normal font, but it is placed slightly higher on the dial. At the same time, the Omega logo matches the design of the more common dial. In conclusion, this Railmaster does not look suspicious, just radically different – special even. Matthew Bain is offering this mysterious Railmaster here , and I would love to learn more about it.

Undoubtedly, vintage Omega Speedmasters sporting a racing dial were the rising stars of the December auctions in New York. T he one offered at Bonham's quadrupled its estimate, and ended finding a buyer at $20,000. One day later, in the Speedmaster-dedicated auction at Christie's, another example with a better case and dial reached $32,500. You can imagine the frenzy when a third one was offered on the Omega Forum this week; it was not only in excellent condition, it came with box and papers. The asking price was a hefty $35,000. In any case, it was quickly gone, confirming the attention around this very attractive and unusual Speedmaster. Note that for a fraction of the price, you could also get a re-edition created for the Japanese market in 2004 – the reference 3570-40, produced in 2004 examples – and nowadays it might very well be the smart buy in modern Speedmasters.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Spectacular Submariner, A Large Breitling Top Time, A Very Complete Memovox, An Unusual Omega Ranchero, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Bring A Loupe kicks off 2016 with an unexpected selection: the watches featured today are not the usual crowd-pleasing favorites but, for one reason or another, they deserve more attention than they generally get. We've found a fantastic vintage dress watch from Rolex, and a modern Seiko Credor with a moon phase complication. I will also highlight some of the best – and worst – listings from eBay this past week, from a very promising Gallet to a sadly fraudulent Breitling Top Time. This is your Bring A Loupe for January 1, 2016, and I wish you a Happy New Year!

I consider the 101X line as one of the greatest successes in Rolex vintage production. Think about it: you have the reference 1016, an amazing Explorer, and the reference 1019, a super attractive Milgauss. Besides those iconic tool watches, dress watches were represented with a lot of great style, between the reference 1012/1013 with gold cases – the only difference being the use of a fluted bezel for the later reference, – and the reference 1018, in stainless steel. The Oyster Perpetual here proves to be much more than a simple 'little gold watch' in many ways. First, the Oyster case boasts a 36mm diameter with 20mm lug-size – rare dimensions for a time-only watch in the 1960s, and still relevant on anyone's wrist today. Then this Rolex also comes with a rose gold case, for a more formal and dressy look, and with a pair of dauphine hands, also characteristic of Rolex in the mid-1960s. Last, the Oyster case does not feature the traditional lug holes – as you should expect from any dressy Rolex with a gold case, the Datejust and Day-Date in precious metals follow the same rule. Usually, Rolex 1013s hardly ever come for sale. It's rather unusual, therefore, that you can actually find 4 examples on the market today, with this one being the only one in a rose gold case. An Italian dealer is offering this rare Rolex for 7,000 Euros or around $7,600 here . The others available at the time of this writing, are a yellow gold example that was previously featured in this column , and the outstanding pair pictured below, offered by a US collector for $16,000 here .

You probably know Credor from the Eichi and the impeccable finishing of its manual-wound movement , one of Philippe Dufour's favorites (unsurprisingly, as he advised Seiko watchmakers on finishing techniques.) This high-end brand from Seiko offers more than just that single exceptional piece, however; there are also ultra-slim watches powered by the movement 6870, and a couple of less formal pieces in the Signo collection. This moon phase watch actually belongs to the Node line, a now defunct collection, and underlines the incredible quality offered by Seiko in its Credor watches. The automatic Spring Drive movement might have a rather industrial finish but it's still very cleanly done, and as is generally the rule for Seiko, the polishing on the case and bezel and the sophistication of the multi-step dial are simply outstanding for that price range. A French owner found many esoteric clues in this watch , I would at least point out that there's a possible nod to the Golden Ratio – could a 41.3mm diameter and a 13.4mm height be just a coincidence? – and the influence of Japanese traditional design in the shape of the hands and the circular pattern of the power reserve. This is simply a very appealing watch with two complications – the moon phase and the power reserve – nicely integrated into the dial; something I often struggle to find in modern production, at least under the $10,000 price point. This special Credor is offered for 5,000 SGD or around $3,500 on a forum here , and it comes with the papers from the last service in 2012.

This Movado offers so many things I want in a dress watch: a balanced dial, interesting indexes, and sophisticated lugs. Furthermore, it comes in a 35mm stainless steel case that would come with an astronomical asking price if there was another name starting with the letter P on the dial. For me, the dial is always the make or break for a vintage watch, and here it does not disappoint: the sub-second is properly sized and nicely placed, neither too high where it would be shadowed by the other hands, nor too low where it would disturb the minute rail track. Add to that the stunning Breguet numerals, and you pretty much have everything you could ask for in a vintage dress/daily wear wristwatch. The complex shape of the lugs is an extra bonus; the way they emerge from the case increases the perceived size of this watch on the wrist. The Dutch dealer Cosimo is selling this very elegant Movado for 2,200 Euros or around $2,400 here .

The first thing you might notice in this picture is the scratched replacement bezel, but I suggest instead that you focus on the full lugs. Here I'll quote Ben and his Reference Points on the Speedmaster : "Reference 105.012 was the first Speedmaster to have an asymmetrical case with crown guards." The well defined beveled lugs, and their integration into the overall case design,gives us a diameter of 42mm – a significant increase from the 38mm cases of the previous Speedmaster references. In short, produced between 1964 and 1969, the reference 105.012 is the quintessential "modern" Speedy: the first reference to feature "Professional" on the dial; the first reference with a 42mm case with crown guards; but also, the second to last iteration to feature the celebrated 321 column wheel chronograph caliber. A French collector is offering this great example for 12,500 Euros or around $13,600 here .

When you look at the color scheme of this chronograph there's no doubt: it comes straight to you from the groovy 1970s, with the characteristic yellow and orange subdials and the magenta hands. At no other time would a designer have dared such a bold combination! The Marinemaster is not only a visually striking watch – this Fortis chronograph was manufactured as a dive watch, boasting a 200m water resistance. The case back and its diver bezel are a testimony to this sporty vocation, and explain the Marinemaster name – shared with a Seiko dive watch, though there's of course no connection between the two companies other than the coincidence of the name. In addition, the 39mm case – and not 41mm as the listing described erroneously states – houses the famous Valjoux 72 movement that you can also find in vintage Rolex Daytona. The straight lugs are in a decent condition and the watch comes with the original bracelet; note the signed clasp pictured below, which is another Rolex echo although you certainly don't need that to enjoy this watch purely on its own terms. It is listed at a $4,500 Buy-It-Now price on eBay here, but the seller is also considering lower offers. UPDATE: The Swiss seller just decreased its asking price to $3,900.

One frustrating thing about putting together Bring A Loupe every week is that many watches disappear before my eyes, just before the article gets published. This was unfortunately the case with this very promising Gallet MultiChron 12H, which was quickly snapped up from eBay . The listing ended very quickly, the seller having probably accepted an outside offer, rather than waiting for the bidding process. This Gallet from the late 1950s was interesting because it underlined how much a beat-up plexi can hide great potential. The pictures taken from different angles showed indeed that the imperfections on the dial were just illusions, caused by the scratches on the plexi – it's always a good idea, if you see a promising watch but with a marginal-looking dial, to make sure that the problem is really a sub-par dial and not sub-par photography. In the end, this was a really sweet vintage chronograph, and I am pretty sure this Gallet will soon pop up again, looking all dashing with a new plexi (a $15 cosmetic intervention that will certainly prove well worth it to the re-seller.) An interesting detail, under one lug you can decipher a faint H engraving, a sign that this chronograph features an Excelsior Park movement, rather than a Valjoux 72.

A couple of hours ago, as I write this, someone just got this vintage Breitling Top Time for $750 on eBay. Was it really a good deal? I am not so sure, the listing here is worrying on many accounts. First, the case is absolutely not correct for a Top Time; those chronographs successively came in round cases – think Heuer Carrera without the down-turned lug – and square-shaped ones, the later versions being much less coveted nowadays. This case and the associated 2214 reference are correct for Breitling production from the 1970s, however they are simply not associated with the Top Time line. The hands in the sub registers are also incorrect, not with the right length and color. Lastly, the large indexes on the dial denote a later Top time version, which also came with a fatter handset for the hour and minute. In the end, someone paid $750 for a lot of Breitling spare parts, not for a full watch.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Dressy Rolex, A Complicated Credor, And A Great Speedmaster To Start Off 2016

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's Friday, which means we're back with another edition of the column, complete with a stand-out cast of exciting characters. In the mood for a heavy hitting, statement piece? We've got you covered, with the inclusion of the original "SARU" GMT-Master, which we're pretty sure is listed in the dictionary under the definition of "wow." On a similarly impressive though decidedly different note, there's the most incredible perpetual calendar pocket watch from Patek Philippe, with a retailer signature you ought to appreciate. If you're after something more accessible, though equally compelling, we've got a pulsations dial-fitted Top Time from Breitling, along with a watchmaking school project watch, produced back in the 1970s. Rather than spill all the beans here, let's dig into the roundup and see what's what.

If legibility, durability, and lasting style are important to you, then I'd recommend making a beeline straight for chronographs of the 1960s. Sure, other decades of chronograph production gave life to genius, as well, but it was arguably during this specific period that the sports chronograph reached its peak. While we could go on all day about a certain model that's already been discussed to seemingly no end, I thought we'd focus on another one of my favorite chronographs of the era, that's perhaps still a bit under-appreciated.

Nope, this isn't a Heuer, nor is it a Rolex – we're talking about a Breitling to kick things off today. You're looking at a Ref. 2002 Top Time, but no ordinary one as you'll quickly learn, unless your savant-like ways have already tipped you off to this one's special something. Though the dial is rather demure with a standard, sunburst silver finish, it has an ace up its sleeve, or rather a pulsations scale around its minute track, which adds a great deal of intrigue to an otherwise subtle watch. Considering other instances in which I've found similarly configured dials, I'd wager that this piece was originally ordered by a medical professional back in the day.

This medical professional in question might've had great taste in watches, but it seems as if they weren’t much for actually wearing them, as evidenced by the stunning condition. Though the photos aren’t exactly top notch, the dial appears to be free of any large scuffs or flaws, aside from a single marking in the seconds subdial. Its case also looks to be very sharp, as well, making this exactly the sort of piece I'd go after.
