Taking its rarity, appearance, and unconventional origins into account, it truly is one of those watches that screams “I’ve got taste, and I’m not messing around” when spotted in the wild.

You’ll find this surrealist Cartier in the catalog of Fortuna Auction’s upcoming New York sale, with an estimate of $30K — $50K. Get the full scoop here .

I can appreciate just about anyone, of any ilk, who is genuinely passionate about something. For this reason, I’ve always had a deep respect for collectors who focus on one specific model, making it their mission to learn all the ins, outs, and what-have-yous. Polerouter collectors are one such flock. Many seem on a never-ending quest to understand Gerald Genta’s original design in its entirety. Given that the model’s production spanned a sizeable number of references, there’s a lot to take in, but few are as captivating as the Ref. 20217-8 from the mid 1950s. Those that dig a hefty helping of lume will surely enjoy this one.

The case shape and crosshair detailed dial may be familiar. But, unlike the majority of other Polerouter references, this watch is distinguished by two defining traits. The first is nearly impossible to miss. Like the most desirable of early Omega sports pieces, this reference is fitted with luminous “Broad Arrow” hands that make the watch considerably more legible than its more common, dauphine-handed cousins. While on the topic of luminous and legible, let’s address the 12, gigantic applications of radium which surround the dial. This is what has earned the Ref. 20217-8 such a legendary status among Polerouter collectors.

Back in 1955, this would’ve been seen as a revolutionary design, as luminous compounds on wristwatches were quite sparingly applied, for the most part. Also worth noting is that this was likely an experimental timepiece of sorts. As a result of the engine-turned detailing which can be seen beneath the radium in certain areas, one can infer that Universal Genève manufactured these watches without a reference-specific dial, so as to test the market’s waters before going all in on a lume-heavy variant. If a real top of the heap Polerouter is what you’re after, look no further.

Berlin’s SHUCKTHEOYSTER is offering this rare Universal Genève with an asking price of €12,500. The full listing can be found on their site .

Following the sale of Tiffany to LVMH, there’s been a good bit of hubbub over what that means for the retailer’s relationship with Patek Philippe, and their double-signed dials. Personally, I’d be willing to bet this acquisition has no impact whatsoever on the coveted reference variants, but a parting of ways is indeed possible. Rather than speculate about a potential separation, let’s keep things moving with the story of a watch bearing two noteworthy names in horological history. While there’s no Calatrava cross on the dial of this next piece, it still ought to pique your interest and then some.

You’re looking at a Ref. 806 Navitimer by Breitling, but what sets it apart from the rest is the small LIP signature that’s wedged between the text reading “GENEVE” and the vintage chronograph’s hand stack. LIP was a French watchmaker, with a documented history of innovation, but in the case of this Navitimer, the brand served as a middleman of sorts. The partnership came as a result of Willy Breitling’s desire to increase Breitling’s presence in France, and what better way to do so than by joining forces with one of France’s top watchmakers. After receiving the now desirable extra dial signature, Breitling’s offerings were distributed through LIP’s French sales network.

If you’ve been after one of these for a while, this is where your hunt ends. With an unpolished case, spotless dial, and evenly aged luminous applications, it’ll be quite hard to track down one better than this example. Even the scalloped edge of its slide rule bezel is sharp and well defined, suggesting that it was likely worn with extreme care, or barely worn at all over the years. What’s more, is it’s being sold with the Navitimer brochure that accompanied the original purchase, making it a little more complete than your run-of-the-mill example.

The dealer and Navitimer authority Rob Cooper has this example listed for $4,950. More information can be found on his Instagram page. Shoot him an offer here.

In a recent conversation with a friend, who is one of the vintage watch scene’s foremost experts, we discussed how vintage marketing materials and brand paperwork might be cooler than the watches themselves. As I’ve noted before, it’s wildly fascinating to explore how our beloved watchmakers forged their legacy in print, and shaped the way they’re now seen by the masses. I thought we’d wrap things up with a booklet I found coming up at auction, which recounts the life and influence of the man who founded A. Lange & Söhne – Ferdinand Adolph Lange.

Simply put, the man is a legend, and is largely responsible for the rise of the watchmaking industry in Glashütte today. Back in 1845, Lange set up shop in the Saxon town with the intention of turning things around after the region had hit hard times. The quickly thriving craft of watchmaking brought new industry to the town. Lange also served as Glashütte’s mayor for nearly two decades, proving his commitment to the people.

Henry’s Auktionshaus of Mutterstadt will offer this piece of German watchmaking history in a sale on the 7th of December. More details, along with the rest of the catalog, can be found here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Wittnauer Ref. 242T, A '90s Cartier Crash, And A Rare Polerouter

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Auction season is upon us, which means some of the most exciting watches in existence are emerging from the woodwork. This year's spread is particularly epic, with a number of historically significant and important offerings, but instead of focusing on what's already been covered, why not look into some pieces that might have perhaps been overlooked? With this theme in mind, we've cherry-picked a selection of subtle but superb timepieces, including a Tudor Prince with aviation provenance, an impossibly sharp Movado chronograph, and a rare Ref. 2508 by Rolex. Those who appreciate a sharp watch will also enjoy Dennison-cased Omega coming from a descendant of the original owner, along with a Philippe Dufour Simplicity in all its awesome glory.

As a good friend and distinguished dealer once told me, these watches aren't getting any younger any time soon. With this in mind, it's important to buy the very best examples that your budget will allow, as condition is practically everything. Though there's something to be said for interesting patina, a clean watch is more liquid in the majority of cases, plus it's just plain old special to see something in near identical shape to how it left the factory. Having said all this, let's now take a look at a clean watch if there ever was one.

You're looking at one of my all time favorite chronographs, which just so happens to be powered by one of history’s finest calibers. The uniquely shaped bridges of Movado's Cal. 95M are instantly recognizable, possibly affording the caliber a more iconic status than the watch itself. On the flip side, its hour and minute tracking functions are displayed beautifully using a set of squiggly shaped hands, adding a dash of whimsy.

Not only is this example's dial spotless, but its case is also razor sharp. You'll often hear dealers use such terminology to describe their watches when it's perhaps not quite warranted, but that's just not the case with this one. Without even handling the watch, it's clear to see that the case's architecture remains unchanged, thanks to the lighting that highlights its many differently finished facets.

Phillips is selling this Movado in Geneva Watch Auction X with an estimate of CHF 4,000 to 8,000. Based upon how Movado chronographs have performed at Phillips over the years, I'd bet it'll achieve a pretty penny. See the watch here .

It's one thing to look down at your wrist and romantically wonder who’s worn your now vintage timepiece, and what it's seen under their care, but I'd argue it's far more interesting to confirm provenance for certain. Regardless of who wore it, knowing the full story is always worth something in my books. That's why I was so delighted to come across this piece, which took me down some unexpected avenues while researching its former owner, whose name you'll find engraved on its stainless steel caseback.

This a Tudor Prince, but no ordinary one. Just above the marker at the six o’clock position on the dial, the logo of Sikorsky Aircraft can be seen in bold, black print. Founded in 1923 by a Ukranian immigrant to the United States named Igor Sikorsky, the firm was a pioneering force American aviation history, as well as of the first firms to manufacture helicopters for both military and civilian use. With over 50 historic designs in their back catalog, the aircraft manufacturer is highly regarded to this very day under the ownership of Lockheed Martin.

Its original owner's name can be found on its caseback, where text reading "SIKORSKY AIRCRAFT - FRED J. BOLDUSOFF - 25 YEARS - 4-28-56" is engraved. This is where things get interesting, and curiously funny. In addition to having spent 25 years as an engineer at Sikorsky, Boldusoff was also a bit of an inventor in his own right, with a rather peculiar focus. You know those little dental floss picks you can buy at the drug store? They’re more or less an evolution of a Boldusoff dental floss holder design, which the Connecticut man patented back in June of 1939. Never in a million years did I think I'd be writing about a watch owned by both an aircraft engineer and visionary of the flossing field, but here we are.

Antiquorum will sell this watch in their sale taking place on Sunday in Geneva, with an estimate of CHF 1,000 to 2,000. More details along with the rest of the catalog (which is rather massive) can be found here .

Your next pick of the week is a bit of a departure from our theme, in that it's not being offered in the upcoming Geneva auctions, but it's an auction watch nonetheless. This eBay piece is a 34 mm Omega and like the previously mentioned Tudor, its past is known and confirmed. Omega collectors and completists alike surely ought to get a kick out of this one.

I was so inclined to share this find with you for a number of reasons. Firstly, the watch is coming direct from the grandson of the original owner, who was a man by the name of W.J. Empson. This resident of the English village of Gamlingay was an enlisted mechanic in the Royal Naval Air Service, who served in both the First and Second World War. I was also informed by the seller that his grandfather was a renowned breeder of pigeons, who is still well known amongst English pigeon connoisseurs to this day. Following his service, he purchased the watch one year before his death, after which the watch remained unworn. This has resulted in its absolutely pristine present state, with a truly flawless dial, and a thick, English-made Dennison case. Moreover, the watch remains ridiculously complete, unlike few others I’ve encountered. Allow me to break it down for you.

Not only is the aforementioned dial original, but so are the hands, the strap that the watch is mounted on, and the branded buckle that's fitted at the end of the strap. What's more, the folding leather box you'll find the timepiece resting in is also original, along with the protective outer box, which has the name of the original owner on it. All of these extras are incredibly rare, and exceedingly special to see included in one tidy package. This is yet another case of a watch with a confirmed provenance, which shouldn't be underestimated. This watch is listed on eBay in an auction that'll end on Tuesday afternoon. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at £1,475. Click here for more photos .

If rarity is the name of your game this auction season in Geneva, I'd advise taking a look in this direction. Everyone and their brother with the means has put a Daytona on their wrist over the past the few years, and with good reason. It's an outstanding design with a similarly epic history, though its predecessors are nothing short of magical. This notion has seemingly become more known as Pre-Daytonas have begun to climb, but those are still "common" watches in comparison to the references with which Rolex cut their chronograph producing teeth.

Production of the Ref. 2508 can be categorized into two separate generations. Many will mistakenly inform that these generations are best classified by case size, with earlier examples coming in at 37 mm and later pieces measuring 35 mm across. While it is true that second generation examples were not produced in the larger cases, smaller-cased examples did emerge from the first generation, as well. With this in mind, it's easiest to direct your attention towards the chronograph pushers when trying to determine the age of a particular watch, as a first generation will be fitted with oval-shaped pushers, as opposed to the square-shaped pushers found on later pieces.

Applying this rule to the watch in question, it can be identified as a small-cased example of the first generation. What separates it from the bulk of other examples offered at auction in recent years is its black lacquer dial. Few of these watches have emerged in recent years, with even fewer being in such presentable shape. While there's surely something to be said for an iconic design and pop culture significance, watches like such give comparably priced Daytonas a serious run for their money.

Sotheby's will offer this rare Rolex chronograph in their sale taking place on Tuesday in Geneva. Its estimate has been set at CHF 100,000 to 200,000 and where it ends up is anyone's guess. Take a look here .

Our humble column is typically reserved for the feature of vintage timepieces, but every now and then, something comes along that forces me to break from our regularly scheduled programming. While it might be a modern watch, our final piece for the week is crafted in the same fashion as the most celebrated of vintage pieces, with an obsessive commitment to handcrafting. Yes, we're wrapping things up with a Simplicity – the famed Philippe Dufour creation regarded by many to be the finest time-only watch in recent history.

I won't pretend like I was in the know and on the waiting list long before the masses. My introduction to the genius of Philippe Dufour came by way of a little site by the name of HODINKEE, and I've been captivated ever since. If you're not already familiar, Dufour is a true master of his craft, who personally manufactures his watch components by hand. This yields a singular expression of watchmaking, reflecting Dufour's mastery of both movement design and finishing techniques.

Given just how long people wait for the chance to purchase a Simplicity, you typically don't see them hitting the market. When they do, it's usually in a heavily publicized auction, so to see one hit the private sale market is a bit of a treat. Worth noting is the fact that another Simplicity is headed to auction at Phillips this weekend, with an estimate of CHF 150,000 to 300,000. Personally, I prefer this example's lacquer dial, and knowing you won't have to duke it out with the paddles is always a plus.

The Keystone of Beverly Hills has this highly sought after piece listed with an asking price of $250,000. Find the full listing here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rolex From The '30s, A Philippe Dufour Simplicity, And A 'Sikorsky' Tudor Prince

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, Bring A Loupe starts with a discontinued Seiko diver, the "Samurai" SBDA005. It might not be vintage already, but its stunning orange dial and brushed titanium case make it a coveted piece, especially in such mint condition. You'll also hear about an unusual Rolex Explorer with a gold-filled case – the Canadian-model 5506. Our favorite chronographs definitely made the cut as well; they are represented by the Zenith A271, the Universal Genève Compax "Nina Rindt," and the Breitling Sprint "Bow Tie" – this Friday, you'll hear some of the cool nicknames of some equally cool watches.

The grey dial of this Zenith reference A271 might seem familiar as it was one of the inspirations for our recent Limited Edition with Zenith . Obviously, the original chronograph from the 1960s housed a manual wound movement, the caliber 146D built from a Martel ebauche also used by Universal Genève. The sub-registers offered the 3/6/9 and crosshair divides that we loved, while the lack of lume increase the dressy feel of the all-grey dial. As good as the A271 looks, it must be noted that the example here has been polished quite intensively, erasing the angles of the lugs and the serial number on the caseback. Additionally, some flaking can be observed on the dial, around the subdials and the applied star logo.

The Japanese dealer Private Eyes listed this rare Zenith A271 for 498,000 Yen or around $4,600.

It is no secret that over the past 50 years, Seiko has managed to produce some of the most impressive diving watches in the world, from the 6217 to the SKX007 , one of Jack's darlings. Initially launched in 2005, the Prospex SBDA measured up to its deep-diving brethren easily, offering a 42mm titanium case, water-resistant to 200M. Three version were offered and they were quickly nicknamed "Samurai" after the specific shape of their hands. The orange reference SBDA005 has the strongest diving identity, as this color is prized for its underwater legibility, although the black SBDA001 and blue SBDA003 have their own merits, especially fitted on their original titanium bracelet that the orange version never got. The automatic caliber 7S25 is not known for its chronometer-grade accuracy, but it does the job well enough (7S25 can give very good results on the wrist, especially with some careful regulation) and offers a quickset date. The watch here is notable for its advertised unworn condition (up to its original rubber strap), and the accompanying box and papers – a set becoming increasingly rare to find given that all "Samurai" were discontinued in 2008.

You can find this mint Seiko Samurai SBDA005 on Ebay; at the time of publishing, bidding was just above $750.

In truth, the Rolex reference 5506 is much more an Air-King than an Explorer, despite the latter mention often being found on its dial. These 34mm watches were made for the Canadian market, where a lot of naming experiments were undertaken by Rolex in the 1950s. Furthermore, this reference is actually gold-filled (with a stainless steel caseback), the case enveloped in a fairly thick gold layer of 40 microns as described between the lugs, which is much more resilient than the usual plating on vintage watches. The serial number indicates a production in 1960, which is consistent with the leaf handset and the "Swiss" mention at the bottom of the dial, which indicates radium – in use by Rolex before it was replaced by tritium a couple of years later. The seller indicates a replaced plexiglass crystal, and adds that the automatic caliber 1530 works flawlessly.

With one more day to go, this Rolex Explorer 5506 has just reached $385 on Ebay.

The Sprint reference 2010 shows Breitling's skill with chronograph dials. This "bow tie" dial goes one step beyond the reverse panda dial that Breitling introduced in the late 1950s, many years before the Heuer Autavia and the Rolex Daytona. The rotating external bezel also means a bigger case size; the Sprint comes in at a rather contemporary 40mm. It is powered by the reliable, workhorse Valjoux 7733, and here the black-striped handset indicate a production in the late 1960s, although the serial number engraved on the caseback is not mentioned in the listing. Lastly, the minute track on the dial appears worn in some pictures, but this is just an optical illusion, due to some glare on the plexiglass crystal.

This Breitling Sprint Reference 2010 is offered on Ebay Germany for €3,950 or around $4,300, but the seller seems willing to consider lower offers.

The Formula 1 driver Jochen Rindt was known for wearing a Heuer Autavia 2446, but his wife, the model Nina Rindt, had a pretty serious wrist game too. She was photographed on several occasions sporting a Universal Genève Compax with contrasting sub-registers, very useful for reading the lap-times of her husband. She actually had it fitted on a large leather cuff, and it looked amazing . Yet, on a "simple" strap this UG is is still just as striking, offering a nice 37mm case, and an extremely interesting black and white handset. For its first watch-dedicated sale , the Swedish auction house Bukowskis presents a good example, with the correct Tachymeter bezel (the dot over ninety is there) and the correct crown adorning the large U logo. The panda dial looks nice and clean, and the hour, minute and chronograph seconds hands are characteristic of the reference 885103-03. The hand-wound chronograph movement is engraved 85, as this was Universal Genève denomination for the ubiquitous Valjoux 72.

The estimate for this great Universal Geneve Compax "Nina Rindt" is 110,000 SEK (approximately $12,000 at time of publishing).
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Orange Seiko 'Samurai' SBDA005, A Grey Zenith Chronograph A271, A Rolex Explorer Reference 5506, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week's Bring A Loupe will focus on quirky watches, and explain the special something that makes each stand out from the standard production of the brand. Among the beautiful oddities featured, you will discover an interesting Patek with removable lugs and an IWC that hides a compass. This is your Bring A Loupe for November 13, 2015.

The Compass model was launched in 1978 as a collaboration between Porsche Design and IWC; it offered a very astute way to combine a compass and an automatic movement. Detailed R&D – yielding six patents – was necessary to properly shield the movement from the compass' magnetism and allow a swift opening of the top part to reveal the compass underneath. As a result, the watch is obviously thick – 12 mm – but the 39 mm case size partly makes up for that. In addition, it is divinely light, having been manufactured in both aluminum and titanium. I disagree with the description of the watch: this case can't be in titanium, like the bracelet it is rather made of aluminum with black PVD, which is typical of production from the early 1980s. In any case, it is definitely light.

The Danish auction house Bruun Rasmussen is estimating this IWC Compass around $3,300 here , and its condition is spectacular.

The reference 3419 is a very interesting model from Patek, and not only for its stainless steel case, a rarity in Patek Philippe's production. The unique nature of this 1960's reference comes from its removable lugs, which allow you to alternate between a stainless steel bracelet or a leather strap. In short, it gives you two Pateks at once, although you need to take the case back off to switch between options. The watch offered here includes both, which is rarely seen nowadays. It comes with an extract from Patek's archives as well as a box; on the negative side its dial is not in the freshest condition, especially around the 3 o'clock area.

This steel Patek is currently listed on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding was just above $6,000.

The second generation of Solunar shares nothing with the original model that we had recently featured : the case increased substantially from 35 mm to 44 mm and took on a barrel shape characteristic of the 1970s. Its automatic movement – an ETA 2790 – offers a day-date display at 3 o'clock, with an attractive blue dial. Besides this bold evolution in style, one cannot overlook the striking inner and outer bezels: they allow you to track the tidal movement over a two-week period in one quick adjustment. You do need to set the external bezel at the time of a high tide on a given day and align the position with the day on the inner bezel; more mathematical explanations are provided here . This funky Solunar is listed for $2,190 here . Note that the case has been heavily polished, as the lack of bevels indicates.

Reference numbers from Grand Seiko might obey obscure laws, but they are nonetheless very descriptive. From the odd number in the SBGW047 reference you should expect a stainless-steel case and the SBGW indicates an in-house manual winding movement, here with a mighty 72-hour power reserve. What is does not reveal though is the absolute work of art that his case represents. This watch was launched in 2013 as a tribute to the 44GS line, which embodied the new aesthetic principles of Seiko, hence the abundance of sharp edges that magnificently play with light. Its angular case, notably, is perfect to exhibit the Japanese mastery of brushing and Zaratsu polishing – the shiny mirror finish – while the hands are katana sharp. The case diameter is "only" 38 mm but it sits prominently on the wrist thanks to the thick full lugs. One example was recently listed for $5,500 on Timezone here . At this price point very few watches can match its level of finishing.

Chronographs from Enicar have seen a definite rise in popularity lately, and this is well deserved: they often combine dashing looks, great dimensions and a respected movement such as the Valjoux 72, which can also be found in vintage Rolex Daytona. They tend to go quickly on forums and eBay, so this one might have been promising. Unfortunately, it lost its most appealing feature: the minute bezel with the pointing red arrow. Instead, it was fitted with the 24-hour bezel of another reference, manufactured at the exact same time: the Jet Graph chronograph that does have an additional GMT hand – hence the 24-hour bezel. You can find this flawed Enicar on eBay here ; unless you miraculously have a spare bezel in a drawer somewhere, I would not recommend bidding on it.

Aside from the Omega auction that we recently mentioned , the Swedish auction house Kaplans will also present interesting watches from other manufactures tomorrow – you can find the full catalog here . I had the chance to examine my favorites a couple of weeks ago, and here are my top three.

It made sense to include the Polerouter as this watch from Universal Geneve shares a very special link with Sweden: it was actually launched in 1954 to celebrate the first commercial flight to Los Anglees from the Swedish airline SAS, a trip over 27 hours long starting from Copenhagen, Denmark. This very Polerouter is also one of the nicest I have seen with an incredibly crisp case and a neat silver dial. It is an earlier version with a bumper movement rather than the famed micro-rotor one. You can find Lot 67 here ; at the time of publishing bidding was at 5,300 SEK (or around $600) – a great value in today's market for Universal Geneve.

The TG 195 chronograph is another watch with a strong Swedish connection: it was the equipment issued in the national army back in the 1950s. Two big batches were produced for the army, one in 1954 and the other in 1958, the example here belonging to that later generation. Their case backs differentiate them – the watches from 1954 feature three small crowns, while you can find three large crowns on ones from 1958. The TG 195 case is a modern 40 mm with an interesting mono-pusher for the second hand. Bidding on Lot 14 was around estimate at 14,200 SEK or around $1,650 here , there is a trade-off to consider between the attractive tropical dial and the deteriorated hands, missing lume on the minute hand, and oxidation on the second hand.

The Heuer Dato obviously got its name from the addition of a date window to the otherwise typical two-register layout. While the first generation displays the date on top of the dial just under 12 o'clock, the second generation went for a unique symmetry, with only one register to the right, and the date on the opposing side. On the black dial the result is even more attractive, which explains its stunning success with Heuer aficionados. Lot 96 is already well above the conservative estimates; bidding was at 41,000 SEK (or around $4,700) here when I typed those words. But I expect it to go much higher, despite the few dings on the case.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Heuer That Tracks The Tides, An IWC With A Compass, A Patek With Removable Lugs, And Many Others

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! This week's edition is more standard than last's. After taking a week to preview the wide breadth of watches available in Geneva at four major auction houses, we're back with more niche picks from eBay, smaller local auction houses, and all across the web. Good news for those who enjoy following the mostly spectator sport of major watch auctions, I'll be back soon to recap the results last weekend. And expect an equally enthralling BAL edition previewing the New York auctions in early December.

Before all of that, let's reverse course two weeks to recap some results from our last "standard' edition of BAL. The Rolex Explorer ref. 6610 with RAF provenance ended up hammering for £14,600 (before fees) – a fairly strong price for the reference. Our Rolex Zephyr over on LoupeThis sold for $5,225 . For dealer watches, the "geezer" Audemars Piguet has been marked as "reserved" by Mike Nouveau and the Cartier "Mid-Size" Cintrée from the 1970s has sold on MentaWatches.com. I actually ran into the Cartier while in Geneva and the buyer was very happy with his recent purchase.

Alright, let's get into this week's picks!

Considering the historical importance of these early Zenith El Primero references, I have found the pricing to be extremely attractive over the last few years. Among the movements developed in the 1960s and introduced in 1969, all via a race to create the first automatic chronograph – the El Primero caliber is the most aesthetically beautiful and technically advanced. Not only did Zenith create an automatic chronograph movement, but it also threw in a little high frequency, 36,000 vph sauce to top it all off. I've been enamored with the El Primero since I first saw it. When I think of vintage chronograph calibers, my mind goes to the Longines 13ZN for manual winding and to the El Primero for automatic – those are the icons.

Is a technically impressive and horologically important movement not really your thing? That's fine too, these early El Primeros are flat out gorgeous watches – even if you don't remove the caseback. When I glance at a great vintage watch, I want it to be abundantly clear which era it was designed and produced. This is why I am a lover of gilt-dialed Rolex sport models. Yes, the overall look hasn't change all too much in almost 100 years, but if it has a gilt dial, you can feel that it was made in the 1950s or 1960s. This first batch of El Primero references, starting with the A381 and including the A384, are so obviously from the era of the 1970s yet have a timeless appeal. That's the magic line to walk and these do it so, so well.

The A384 is typically a panda-dialed chronograph with a white base dial and black sub-registers. As you can see here, there is a batch of dials within the A384 that tend to turn a beautiful tropical brown. If you're curious how this watch started its life, take a look at the modern A384 Revival – stark black on white. Something within the paint mix leads to this color change, I have seen too many examples that are similar to believe they all baked in the sun the same way. But these watches are not common by any means. In total, Zenith produced only 2,600 of these back in the early 1970s.

The look is great and condition here is nearly bulletproof. The uniquely shaped case shows all the right finishing and sharp edges, something I always look for on these. Obviously, the dial is incredible as is the lume patina throughout. The cherry on top is the Zenith-signed Gay Frères laddar bracelet with correct ZKM endlinks – this accessory is very hard to go out and buy if you wanted to.

The seller, Arthur at Shuck The Oyster in Germany is offering this Zenith A384 for €17,990. Check it out right here .

Thanks to a friendly Bring A Loupe tipster, I was alerted to a strange eBay auction for a modern Omega photographed on the wrist of Hollywood A-lister George Clooney. While my yellow flags went up, by the time I clicked through and saw the listing, my concerns were quickly alleviated. The watch is up for auction to support Homes For Our Troops, Inc. – a charity geared towards building and donating specially adapted custom homes nationwide for severely injured post-9/11 Veterans, to enable them to rebuild their lives.

It's all for a good cause and, honestly, I found the lead photo quite entertaining. There aren't many opportunities to put a George Clooney selfie wristshot on Hodinkee and today, I am capturing my chance. The watch is a reference 231.50.42.21.06.002 Aqua Terra 150M 41.5mm in Red Gold on a Red Gold Omega bracelet – the original retail price would have been $39,000.

Home For Our Troops has put this Omega with movie star provenance up for auction on eBay ending Thursday, November 21, at 1:39 PM ET. At the time of publishing, bidding was at $18,200. Get all of the details right here .

I'll be the first to admit, I have an extreme soft spot for vintage Boucheron watches. I have previously gone deep here on Hodinkee but to recap in the context of this watch, Boucheron was producing wristwatches in Paris from the 1940s to the 1960s that seriously rivaled their neighbors at Cartier. The look is a bit different, sure. Boucheron tended to prefer an overall "metallic" and monotone look using precious metals whereas Cartier is typically associated with a stark white dial and bold black dial printing. In terms of build quality and handmade charm, Boucheron cases are on the same level as EJ-made Cartier of the period yet the brand is almost entirely disrespected by the market.

This one, for sale via a smaller auction house in London's Mayfair, caught my eye as it is not one of the more "popular" Boucheron models from this era, the Reflet and Carrée. In fact, I follow most Boucherons posted for sale on the Internet and have yet to see another example exactly like this one. So yeah, it may look quite standard in its rectangular, Tank-esque shape but the watch is actually quite rare. If a vintage Boucheron has an unpolished caseback, where I can clearly see the hand engravings, I immediately want to buy it. The Omega logo there indicates the use of an Omega movement inside the Boucheron-made case – this was typical of the higher-priced options of the time.

This Boucheron is lot 481 of Noonans Mayfair Jewellery, Watches, Silver and Objects of Vertu auction on Tuesday, November 26, at 7 AM EST. The pre-sale estimate is £600 to £800. Click here for the full listing .

Early this week, I attended TAG Heuer's 2024 Collector's Summit – expect a ton of vintage Heuer photos in an article soon. Is that affecting this pick for BAL? Yes. Is this also a great example of an under-the-radar vintage Heuer? Also, yes.

The ref. 73463 Autavia features the tonneau-shaped case of the first automatic caliber 11 Autavias but with a manually-wound movement. So, what you get is the exact same look as something like a ref. 1163 Autavia "Siffert" but for less than half the price. The true Siffert Autavia is legendary, gaining its nickname from Swiss driver Jo Siffert, who wore the model in 1969 – but there is a premium associated with that history. The ref. 73463 has the same dial motif and the same case plus, you get to wind the movement every morning, who doesn't love doing that?

For sale at an auction house in London, this example has an overall clean look with a full case and fairly even patina to the lume on the dial and hands. There is a slight dark spot in the minute hand but it shouldn't take away from the watch too much.

This Heuer Autavia is lot 79 of Lyon & Turnbull's London Watches auction on Thursday, November 21, at 9 AM EST. The pre-sale estimate is £4,000 to £6,000. Check it out right here .

I'll be very honest. I don't know much about this model and have never seen one before, let alone with a BMW logo on the dial. If you happen to be an expert on 1980s Favre-Leubas, feel free to jump in the comments and tell me why this one might not be good but, to my eye, I am liking what I am seeing. The dial printing looks right to me and the BMW logo is the version that brand would have been using in the 1980s.

The watch itself is quite interesting with a half-cushion half-ellipse shape, a wave brushed dial, and an ETA movement inside. The seller notes that this Favre-Leuba "was sourced from a lot of new old stock pieces from a collector's estate in Switzerland" and that claim somewhat checks out. There are a few scratches on the caseback which can happen in a drawer or something and the original strap does look unworn. This is a lot of fun for under $700.

The seller, Astiegan Watches in Switzerland, is asking $689 for this Favre-Leuba co-signed by BMW. Check it out right here .

This same watch came up for sale last year with a Movado signature. Now, it is back on the market with no Movado on the dial and a way-too-modern Tiffany & Co. in its place. Just don't buy it .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Early And Tropical El Primero, George Clooney's Seamaster Aqua Terra, And A Boucheron

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Auction week has come to an end, and as expected, the vintage Rolex market continues to amaze and advance with confidence. With highlights like the sale of the Pulsometer dial ref. 6239 Daytona for over $820,000 at Sotheby’s, the tropical dial ref. 6238 chronograph for nearly $500,000 at Phillips, and a host of both conventional and Paul Newman Daytona’s performing strongly at Christie’s, there’s no sign of vintage Rolex slowing down any time soon. With that said, my personal highlights of the weekend were the 2499 sales, including the Serpico y Laino retailed first series example that fetched north of $3.2 million at Christies and the unique Asprey-dialed example that set a new world record at Sotheby's . But with all of that in the rear view mirror, it's important to remember that there are still a ton of great watches out there, ripe for the picking. So without further ado, here's your post-auction edition of Bring A Loupe.

Despite the fact that the Heuer market arguably isn’t what it once, briefly was, there’s no disputing the fact that the legendary brand has been behind some of the most important sports chronograph designs of all time, including the Autavia, Monaco, and Carrera. Of the many variants of each model, I’ve always been particularly taken with the earliest references of the Carrera, namely the various ref. 2447 and two-register ref. 3647 models, as their styling was seemingly distilled down to the absolute essentials, so as to maximize legibility. This has also resulted in a truly lasting design that’s not about to go out of style any time soon. While browsing through the catalogs of a few lesser-known auction houses, this piece immediately caught my attention, given the rarity of the reference and the condition in which it’s being offered. As the date aperture at the nine o’clock (or 45 minute) position would indicate, this is the Dato 45 variant of the famed Carrera, also known as the ref. 3147N.

Based upon the fact that it’s currently mounted on a bit of an oddball, vintage-looking bracelet, and the location where it’s being offered (rural England), I’m going to guess that this piece is likely fresh to market. It also happens to be in quite nice shape, as well, with all lume plots present, a sharp and perhaps unpolished case, and a clean and unmarked minute track. This is a super cool watch and one that you won't want to sleep on if you're looking for a Heuer that you won't see on other wrists every day. Hannam’s Auctioneers in the English town of Alton will be offering this piece on November 21, though advance bids have already brought it up to £2,600 at the time of publishing. Click here for more details.

I’ve said it before and I’ll say it again, too many modern watches designed for ladies are rather uninspired. It’s as if most watchmakers have conspired to follow a simple formula of dainty profiles, added diamonds, and delicate looks, which has ultimately resulted in more women choosing men's designs instead. This is why I often recommend looking into back catalogs of ladies offerings, or even at older men's pieces, when female friends express their interest in a new watch.

Here we’ve got a piece that, although likely originally intended for men, would look terrific on a lady’s wrist. It’s an iconic Vacheron Constantin, featuring the watchmaker’s now celebrated “Cornes De Vache” style lugs, which the brand has payed homage to in recent collections. In essence, the piece is direct and to the point, with just three hands and a simple dial, but details like the aforementioned ornate lugs and tasteful applied markers and numerals make it really pop, and likely stand out despite only measuring 31mm across. Boston’s European Watch Company has this outstanding Vacheron listed at $4,250. Find the full scoop here.

We all know the score. Big watches are fading in popularity and in collector circles regarded as a trend that has now come to an end. Yes, there are still a number of watchmakers producing pieces that exceed the 44mm mark, but the mainstream is now seemingly aware that watches with desk clock–esque proportions are a tad less than functional in daily life. With all this in mind, our next watch is another that falls on the smaller end of the spectrum, at just 34mm across. Though many are comfortable with wearing vintage pieces at the 36mm mark, many are often hesitant to go smaller. With a thin bezel on a piece like the one here, you’ll find it to wear larger than expected, so I highly encourage you to give it a second look. It’s also a stunning Universal Genève, so there’s that working in its favor, too.

Although several of the luminous plots appear to have succumbed to the effects of several decades past, this can be somewhat overlooked given that the dial has clearly aged to a consistent caramel tone, most certainly worthy of the title tropical. Also worth noting is the resemblance this time-only piece has to the ref. 570 from Patek Philippe, making this a solid option for those who lust after the legendary Calatrava but lack the bankroll to make it happen in their collection. Those Watch Guys currently have this piece listed at $2,190. There’s a lot of value up for grabs here — as well as an awfully attractive timepiece. Click here for the full listing.

As previously mentioned, one of the main takeaway’s from the past week of auctions was that early Submariners are once again on the up-and-up, suggesting that there just might be more to vintage Rolex collecting than the Daytona! While early Subs haven’t exactly been accessible to most for a good while, they’re continuing to reach new heights that are arguably long overdue, given the performance of other Rolex models on the market. With this in mind, many are looking to jump on the bandwagon while the getting is good, and I don’t blame them. Despite their prevalence in all forms of watch related media, these early Submariners are rarer than one might expect, partially as a result of the way in which many examples were used at the time of the Submariner’s release. This makes for a lot of potential upside moving forward into the future of vintage watch collecting.

Though the bezel insert might be a replacement, this is nothing short of a stunning piece, as evidenced by the seemingly spotless gilt dial, that just so happens to feature four lines of text. This particular variant is referred to as "SCOC," meaning the text is in the order "Superlative / Chronometer / Officially / Certified” on its dial. What’s more, is it has the coveted exclamation point at the six o’clock position. Michael Morgan of Iconic Watch Company currently has this example of the small crown ref. 5508 listed for $79,200. Find the full listing here .

Now, to touch upon a matter that inspired a bit of a ruckus last week, let’s discuss the Skipperera that sold at Christie’s. The long and short of it is that the catalog photos didn’t accurately represent the watch, which is not at all what you’d expect from an auction house like Christie's. It’s undoubtedly a let down to see something misrepresented, especially at auction, however it’s worth noting that Christie’s did supply live photos reflecting the true condition to those interested. What’s more surprising is the fact that it realized a handsome sum – CHF 53,750 in the end – which in my opinion is rather strong, knowing what other, better examples have fetched privately in the past. Maybe the Heuer market isn’t as soft as we thought, after all? Here's an image of the actual watch, as seen in OnTheDash's report :

I think the lesson here is to do your homework as a buyer, especially when bidding at auction. This means physically going in to preview watches in the metal, and maybe even bringing a loupe (hey, where could I have gotten that idea!?), which is also a terrific way to learn more about the finer details of vintage watches and collecting.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Special Rolex Submariner Ref. 5508, A Heuer Carrera Dato 45, And A Vacheron Constantin Ref. 4308 'Cornes De Vache'

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, Bring A Loupe focuses on very different chronographs, from a 1940s Longines 13ZN to an Omega Flightmaster with a tropical Mk1 dial. Speaking of dials, you will also find here a yellow gold Datejust with a rare malachite dial, and an interesting Universal Genève Tri-Compax, although its bezel appears problematic, to say the least. And we've also found a full-set original Zenith El Primero reference G381, in yellow gold.

The 13ZN is considered one of the most beautiful chronograph movements ever made – we discussed its history and technical attributes in great detail last year , along with the entire lineage of this chronograph with flyback complication. Steel examples are obviously extremely coveted, and tend to go crazy on Ebay – one example rose from a $10 introductory price to a final bid of almost $42,000 . The one currently offered is very similar in style, as it was produced the previous year (in 1944) and its dial is spectacular, save for some traces on the mileage track.

This element matches the seller's claim that the watch was aimed at the U.S. market – a European version would use a kilometer-based scale – a fact also confirmed by the LXW import mark on the movement. Lastly, the movement number seems to match the provided extract certificate from Longines, dated from 1990.

This Longines 13ZN is listed on eBay with a Buy It Now at €42,000 (or around $44,800), and the seller will also consider lower offers.

The Flightmaster is something of a niche collectible model among Omega's chronographs, as its futuristic case is something of an acquired taste. It was specifically designed for pilots (hence the name with a legible chronograph and a very visible 12-hour GMT hand in blue. The Mk1 version presented here also offers a green 12/24 hour indicator, while the later reference 145.036 would replace it with a running second (the movement designation changing from caliber 910 to caliber 911). Interestingly, the pushers and crowns have applied enamel on their tips, with their color matching the hands of the complications they control – a smart take on making its operation easier. In addition to the obviously beautiful tropical dial, the seller mentions a recent service, which ensured the proper functioning of this chronograph. The case is in great condition, as its elliptical shape and brushing have been well preserved (vintage Flightmaster are often overpolished, but that's not the case here). With a 44mm diameter, it is not small by any means, but the lack of lugs make it feel less heavy on the wrist, while the caseback displays the proper engravings – from the stylized Douglas DC-8 to the "flightmaster" line in all lower case letters.

The UK dealer Sweeping Hand offers this tropical Omega Flightmaster Mk1 for £3,495 (or around $4,355).

The Zenith reference G381 shares the same case shape as the more commonly known stainless steel El Primero A386 , but it's much rarer, as only 700 examples were produced versus 2,500 for the A386. Rarity is not the only reason to look at this chronograph, as it offers the celebrated caliber 3019PHC, the high-beat automatic chronograph movement that Zenith proudly released in 1969. Here, the watch is said to come from the original owner, as a complete full set. The serial number in the papers matches the one engraved on the caseback; the original box, and the Zenith-signed strap and buckle are also included. Lastly, the handset and index lume match, but are lighter than expected; this might be due to the pictures' exposure, but this point would need to be checked.

You can find this full set Zenith El Primero G381 on Chronotrader with an ask of €9,000 (or around $9,540).

Rolex was not the only brand to offer malachite dials – some glamorous Piagets immediately come to mind – but the emerald green color could not be more aligned with the manufacturer's official color. The pattern on each malachite dial is unique, and in this Rolex Datejust 1601 the wavy theme looks amazing. As with all exotic Rolex dials, one must check that a given example was produced in the time frame that's correct for such a dial. Here, the 5.0M serial number disclosed by the seller places the production in 1976/1977 – slightly before what I was expecting time-wise but other 1970s examples also come up, although those dials appear more often in the early 1980s. The seller mentions a few hairlines, which are often found on those fragile dials, although they don't seem obvious to the naked eye. The condition of the 18k yellow gold case is also good news, as its hallmarks remained deep, and the engravings between the lugs easily legible. Both signs show that this Datejust was not over-polished, which translates into thick lugs and a good presence on the wrist.

The LA-based dealer Uberz has priced this rare Rolex Datejust 1601 with malachite dial at $6,950.

The tachymeter scale surrounding the brushed dial of the Universal Genève Tri-Compax reference 222100/2 always looks wonky but it is correct – as is the Swiss mention at the bottom of the dial, although "Swiss T" is more often found . So the seller's claim of an original dial checks out – but an issue arises when you look into its stainless steel case. This UG reference did offer a waterproof caseback, but it was never found with the classical tachymeter bezel from UG (exhibiting a Dot-Over-Ninety and an accent on Tachym è tre). Actually, such a build does not make any sense since the dial already bears a tachymeter scale, so this configuration is undoubtedly wrong. This surprising Universal Genève Tri-Compax can be found on Ebay but bidding is not advised.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Tropical Omega Flightmaster Ref. 145.013, A Great Longines 13ZN, A Yellow Gold Rolex Datejust 1601 With Malachite Dial, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
As the saying goes, you’ve gotta take the good with the bad, and this week we’re doing just that. In addition to a hefty helping of top-tier works of art for the wrist, with pieces like a Pre-Viceroy Heuer Autavia, a rare chronograph from Girard-Perregaux, obscure curiosities from Vacheron and LeCoultre, and a sharp, small crown Submariner, we’re shedding light on some less than savory pieces that you’ll want to avoid. You’re in for an educational one this week – enjoy.

While Heuer's connection with motorsport does date back to the brand’s early days, the golden age, so to speak, was of course the 1970s, when the watchmaker began to partner with racing teams, and some of the most celebrated names in the automotive world. As Heuer aficionados will surely already know, the watchmaker once partnered with Viceroy cigarettes to offer their famed Autavia at a discounted rate to American smokers, following a steep decline in sales. By mailing in the end flap of a Viceroy carton, individuals could purchase a black Autavia with red accents for just $88, which many certainly jumped at. It’s estimated that roughly 5,000 examples were sold by way of this promotion, and for good reason. Just look at it!

This particular example is what’s known as a "Pre-Viceroy" Ref. 1163MH, in that it was produced prior to the beginning of the Viceroy promotion. It has survived the decades with grace, and is now offered in excellent condition, with a perfect dial, handset, and blemish-free bezel. I happen to like the presence of the personal engraving on its caseback, which reads: "All Our Love, Laurie & Eddie." Here's to the awfully generous couple, and this piece's next owner.

Los Angeles' Craft & Tailored has this sharp example listed at $5,950, which is quite reasonable for an example of this quality. Get the full scoop here .

Over the years, the American market was delighted to a number of aesthetically pleasing and highly functional watches from LeCoultre, and this is automatic bumper-powered Master Mariner is no exception. Complete with a handy power reserve indicator at the 12 o'clock position, this 34 mm, 14K yellow gold piece would make quite a nice everyday piece with the ability to be dressed up and down with ease. This piece essentially represents LeCoultre's answer to the need for smart looking timepieces that were still able to withstand the elements, which is exactly what the Master Mariner did. The watch was marketed as being "self-winding, superbly accurate, with maximum protection against water, moisture, dust and shock," which we wouldn’t advising putting to the test in 2018, though LeCoultre did also add that it was “cased with distinction,” so who knows.

As mentioned above, this is a full set example, meaning that along with the watch, the seller has included the original box and accompanying documentation. It’s always nice to see such accessories included with the sale of any watch, but it’s particularly interesting in the case of a more obscure model like such. Those with a keen eye for detail might also be surprised to see that LeCoultre produced a specific box just for the Master Mariner model, which you’d be forgiven for not being aware of until now.

An individual based out of Lititz, PA – a town with some serious watchmaking credibility in its own right – has this LeCoultre listed on eBay for $1,700 . You have the option to make an offer, as well.

Recently, I found myself in the company of a group of esteemed collectors and dealers, discussing what we believed to be truly rare watches. While Daytona's will come and go, with examples always available to those willing to pay up, there are certain pieces that you just can't track down, no matter how deep your pockets may be. Early examples of Girard Perregaux's Olimpico demonstrate this notion effectively, as tracking one down can prove to be quite the challenge. This is what’s known as the Ref. 8846N, which was introduced to the Olimpico line in 1960 to commemorate the Summer Olympics in Rome. This brings us back to the ultimate point of the Olimpico line, which was to celebrate the occurrence of the Olympic games. GP would often release multiple variants of the chronograph upon the beginning of a new series of games.

With it's sleek case and tri-color dial, there's a lot to get lost in with this piece. I personally prefer it to all other variants of the Olimpico, as the later examples of the chronograph began to take on a more architectural look with thicker cases. It is also powered by the venerable Excelsior Park Cal. 40 movement, which is no slouch either. Horare in Paris has this Ref. 8846N currently up for sale, with an asking price of EUR 7,990. Click here for further details.

As someone who's constantly on the hunt for the next great watch, it always excites me to encounter something totally foreign to me. Not only is it refreshing to simply be looking at something that isn’t another similarly styled, three register sports chronograph of the 1970s, but it’s an opportunity to gain further insight into some of the more obscure creations of watchmaking history as a whole. This week, I came across just that, in the form of a time-only piece from Vacheron Constantin. According to its seller, this is what’s known as an Altesse, and it’s easily one of the wildest looking time-only pieces I've ever come across, largely thanks to the stepped bezel, and central dial component that reveals only the tips of the hour and minute hands. This Vacheron honestly looks like something that would be produced by an independent today, which is why it’s so fascinating.

It wears rather conservatively at just 32 mm across, though ownership of such a piece is less so about on the wrist appeal, and more so custodianship of an oddity from the workshop of none other than Vacheron Constantin. With that said, this could make an outstanding ladies piece, as well. Caso Watches of London has this rare Vacheron Constantin listed at EUR 12,500. Click here for the full listing.

In terms of the market, the Submariner is hot. With regards to aesthetics, they could be described using the same terms, as well. Having said all this, there are still some who see Rolex as being too immersed in the mainstream, and as a result refrain from collecting coronet clad watches. Should you happen to be such a collector, I'm about to make a believer out of you with one of the most stunning Submariners we’ve seen in a while. Looking back, the 6536/1 represents a transitional period in the history of the Submariner, as evidenced by present design elements that would later go into the now famed Big Crown. That aside, it's just a terrific early dive watch by all means, and it wears beautifully thanks to the thin case which was only produced for a short while.

Condition-wise, there's a lot to love here, including an unpolished case, a bezel/insert in absolute mint condition, and the presence of the original radium luminous compound on both the dial and hands. It's also being offered on its original rivet Oyster bracelet dated to the fourth quarter of 1957, so you'd pretty much be set upon taking delivery of such an example – no tracking down of parts is necessary. Miami’s Menta Watches is offering this example of the Ref. 6536/1 for $50,000. Find more details here on their site.

I love a complicated vintage Patek as much as the next guy, but if I was looking to throw down the big bucks on an example, I'd want it to be perfect. These fragile and beautiful mechanisms of watchmaking's golden age aren't getting any newer, and with an investment class as such, springing for the best is advisable. This sadly isn't that perfect watch many of us dream of, and is instead the stuff of nightmares. Allow me to explain. What you’re looking at is an example of the famed Ref. 2499, in the same sense that a Fiero with a fiberglass bodykit is a 288 GTO. This was likely pieced together by someone who knew what they were doing, but as with any pieced together watch, the devil is in the details.

Starting with the dial and case – they're just not right. After having handled and examined a healthy share of 2499s, the dial is instantly recognizable as no good. Furthermore, the moon phase wheel and date discs visible through their respective apertures all seem off, and by no means in line with what we’re used to seeing from Patek. The fun continues when you look beneath the caseback to find a modified Valjoux caliber finished in a most underwhelming manner, and at the counterfeit papers, which were strangely made to resemble something that would’ve accompanied a more modern offering from Patek. The nail in the coffin, so to speak, is the serial found on this piece has also been lifted from another pink gold, second series example of the Ref. 2499, which sold at Christie’s Geneva sale in November of 2013. All in all, I'd advise steering clear, but that's just me. See the watch here .

Starting off small, we’ve got a watch that never was, regardless of how attractive such a configuration may be. As many will know, Tudor notably manufactured the Oyster Prince Ranger as a more accessible alternative to the Rolex Explorer, producing in a number of variants and references throughout multi-decade its run. With that said, Tudor never produced examples with red "RANGER" text. As the pictures indicate, this is one such Red Ranger, and for the reason previously stated, it should be avoided. For scholarship's sake, I'll still point your attention towards the engraving found on the caseback, which is far too shallow, and likely done with a laser. Both this, and the reference number between the lugs raise eyebrows far higher than they have any business of being raised.

If in the market for a Ranger, it's highly advisable that you do your homework, and handle as many examples as possible, in that it's simply one of the most commonly faked watches from the Wilsdorf back catalogue. See the watch here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Vacheron Constantin Ref. 3681 'Altesse,' A 1972 Heuer Autavia Ref. 1163MH 'Pre-Viceroy,' And A Rolex Ref. 6536/1 'Small Crown' Submariner

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week's Bring A Loupe will cover rare and attractive watches, available right now on eBay and at upcoming European auctions. Among our picks, you should not miss one of the rarest Tudor Submariners: the reference 7923, an early diver with a manually-wound movement. You will also find a white dial chronograph from Enicar, and a highly controversial Heuer Autavia. Dive watches will be represented, with a rare Longines and a funky Universal Geneve. This is your Bring A Loupe for November 20, 2015.

The second execution of the Polerouter Sub makes the first execution that we covered here appear almost conventional in comparison. Design-wise the second execution announced the 1970's with its bright orange hands, its large asymmetrical case, and the innovative indexes. Like the first execution, it houses an automatic movement with micro-rotor, the caliber 69, a rare feature in a diver watch! Bonus point here, this diver comes with its original Gay Frères bracelet, and its bezel appears original – I am still puzzled by the triangle at 12 o'clock, whereas most bezels for this reference usually display a large circular dot. Note the style and quality of the pictures are really unusual for an eBay listing, it almost makes up for the over polished case, the replacement plexiglass crystal, and the deteriorated indexes. This bold and bright diver can be found on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding was around $700.

The military version of the Railmaster is well known to collectors; several even made it to Christie's auctions . The reference here was intended for the Pakistani Air Force – hence the P.A.F. engravings – in the late 1950s, shortly after the Railmaster launch in 1957. Strangely enough, the dial reads "Seamaster" but it is a Railmaster reference CK 2914 in full form with its specific hands and anti-magnetic properties. The ones intended for the Peruvian Air Force were engraved F.A.P. for "Fuerza Aérea del Perú" and often featured "Flightmaster" on the dial; one example was recently auctioned for close to $50,000 . The Pakistani example shown here has the smooth case-back characteristic of the early reference, and the correct military engravings. Unfortunately, the radium of the index has started burning the dial, though thankfully the latter was manufactured very thick to ensure outstanding anti-magnetic protection. It is offered for sale on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding had just reached $4,000.

All the first and second execution Autavia reference 2446 that recently appeared on eBay stirred a fair share of controversy; this one follows the trend, and, for me, it even constitutes a no-go. One of the issues is that the condition of the hands does not match the aging of the dial; from the shape of the lume they appear later replacement. In addition, the lume has been redone, probably a more recent paste, that was hastily applied. An additional troubling detail is that the crystal shape is different from that of correct Heuer parts; its curve is more flat. The really perplexing element is obviously the dial: Heuer experts' opinion about the peculiar lemon patina range from believing in a thorough natural aging – possibly due to smoke exposure at some point – to speculation that this might be a prototype for a never-produced yellow-gold Autavia. Unfortunately, the latter story sounds too good to be true when examined in the light of all the other suspect or incorrect components. On the plus side, the Valjoux 72 movement and the 39 mm screw-back case seem fine. This shady Autavia is offered for 23,500 CHF, or $23,000 on eBay here . The seller also considers lower offers; on the listing, he gives a very cheeky answer to a somewhat aggressive query about the asking price. It's entertaining to take a look at this one, but if I were you, I would avoid actually bidding on it and instead, go check out how beautiful a truly genuine 2446 2nd execution looks.

The UK-based auction house Watches Of Knightsbridge is rolling out a new auction this weekend and you can expect plenty of exciting lots. I focused on my three favorites below; you can find the full catalog here .

The Tudor reference 7923 is not merely difficult to find – it's a unicorn-rare reference. We only covered it once on HODINKEE, and we had to dig into Tudor's own archives to find it! In design, the 7923 is fairly similar to the earliest Submariner from Tudor, the reference 7922, itself close to the Rolex Submariner 6538, notably worn by James Bond as played by Sean Connery in the first movies. One shortcut to this complicated lineage: the reference 7923 offers something truly unique versus any other Submariner, a manually-wound movement. The caliber 1156-1188 was solely used in this very Tudor and allowed a thinner case, thanks to the absence of an automatic winding system and rotor. If you doubt how historically significant this watch is, you should know that the reference 7923 was the inspiration for the Tudor Heritage Black Bay ONE that sold for $368,000 in Geneva two weeks ago . I am really intrigued by the rose logo overlapping the 12 o'clock index; that said, every single dial of known Submariner 7923 presents minute difference so nothing can be inferred, though there is the interesting fact that we found the very watch presented on a dedicated Rolex forum six years ago. The dial is obviously not perfect, but the extreme rarity trumps that consideration, especially considering that the watch comes with a Tudor box and open papers – though as they are not filled out it doesn't quite qualify as a full set. The UK auction house Watches Of Knightsbridge is offering one example here ; estimates range between 35,000 and 45,000 GBP (or around $53,000 to $69,000).

The Reference 6921 is a totally underestimated watch, like so many vintage Longines. The Skin Diver was only produced for two years at the very end of the 1950's, for a total production estimated at around only 300 pieces. The dial indicates an automatic movement; interestingly, this is the first time I have seen the "automatic" line just below the Longines emblem, though it is nonetheless consistent with a picture found on an Italian forum . It can be fully considered as the mother of the later Longines Diver Compressor, the reference 7042, that Longines brought back to life with the Longines Legend Diver, a lovely modern watch, especially in its early version without the date. The estimates for this watch range between 2,500 and 3,500 GBP (or around $3,800 to $5,300); the full description can be found here with a picture of the sumptuous case back featuring the usual diver figure. I am unsure of the crown's originality, I was expecting a gridded pattern on it to underline its diving abilities; in addition, the lume on the hour hand has a small crack.

The Top Time from Breitling represents another line being overlooked. It is similar to the Heuer Carrera in many ways, displaying the same kind of legible and well balanced dial. Of course, its 38 mm case does not feature the fantastic lugs of the Carrera with their attractive inward angle but the stepped lugs of the Top Time second execution case are not bad either. And similar to the Carrera, the movement is a well-recognized chronograph caliber with proven resiliency and efficiency – here, the Valjoux 178 instead of the Valjoux 72 found in an early Carrera with three sub-dials. This Breitling does have something you would not find in any Carrera, a 24-hour dial, very probably inspired by the Breitling Navitimer Cosmonaute, which was worn by Scott Carpenter on Project Mercury back in 1962. Granted Scott never walked on the moon, but being the fourth American in space is a mighty achievement in itself. The 24-hour dial takes some time to get used to, but it is actually a simple and genius way to sort out day or night time. The estimates for this watch range between 2,200 and 2,600 GBP (or around $3,400 to $4,000); the full description can be found here .

This Saturday seems to be a great day for interesting auctions in Europe: the French auction house Pestel-Debord will also offer more than 200 watches. I have selected two of them that you should absolutely not miss, and the others can be found in the catalog here . The cover features an attractive Breitling Co-Pilot, famous after Raquel Welch worn it with dashing glamor in the movie Fathom , back in 1967.

If we were to use the Rolex vernacular, this chronograph could be nicknamed the Enicar Solo. The reference 2303 was indeed released by Enicar to take advantage of its involvement in exploration of the Himalayas in 1956, through the Sherpa mention on the dial. Therefore, this early reference exhibits a fairly uncluttered dial in comparison the following reference 2343, the Sherpa Graph. The latter is actually one of the most coveted vintage chronographs lately, as it benefits from a thriving racing aura, similar to the most distinguished Heuer. The Formula One champion Jim Clark famously worn a splendid grey-dial Sherpa Graph throughout its career, and notably during his first title in 1963. What makes this Pre-Sherpa Enicar special? Beyond any doubt the answer lies with its silvery dial; this is actually the first I ever saw on this reference, and it looks dashing with the red lollipop second hand, and the lume points characteristic of the pre-Sherpa production. Therefore, I would date this chronograph from the mid-1950s rather than the late 1960s announced on the listing. This Enicar Solo is estimated between 1,400 and 1,800 Euros (or around $1,500 to $1,900); you can find the full description here .

If you have been watching the latest auctions in Geneva, you must have noticed the strong return of elegant time-only watches, especially in their oversized format. Well, with this Cymaflex you get a deeply underestimated watch that offers everything that made those auctions go crazy. The 38 mm case is on point: made out of 18k pink gold it further demonstrates how classy this Cyma is. Add a very respected – and in-house – Tavannes 586K movement that is actually well dimensioned for the oversized case, and you can be sure that you just found a mighty vintage watch that ticks all the boxes you could want for a desirable vintage dress watch, at a fraction of what you might expect from other brands. The estimates for this oversized Cyma range between 500 and 800 Euros (or around $550 to $850), which sounds a steal for a watch of that caliber; more information can be found here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Submariner You Probably Haven't Seen Before, An Enicar You Likely Did Not Know, And A Heuer Autavia You Absolutely Should Not Buy

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Welcome back to Bring A Loupe and happy Friday! If last week's edition was a return to "regular" form in this column, with the general focus being vintage chronographs, this week we stretch our legs a bit as we dip into the strange and esoteric.

Let's recap some results from last week before we get started. The Favre-Leuba for BMW has been marked as sold out – that was a good deal. George Clooney's Omega sold for $34,100 on eBay and the Heuer Autavia ref. 73463 went for £5,040 all-in at Lyon & Turnbull. And lastly, the Boucheron is up for auction next Tuesday, the 26th.

Enough about the past, here are this week's picks!

Jean-Claude Biver and Jacques Piguet acquired Blancpain for CHF 21,500 in 1981. To kick things off, the two established a new brand slogan: "Since 1735 there has never been a quartz Blancpain watch. And there never will be." Consider the context of this move in 1981, when the traditional Swiss watch world had been decimated by quartz technology. Blancpain was the ultimate zag. The first collection of neo-vintage Blancpain watches was dubbed "the six masterpieces." With varying complications, all six were produced in simple 34mm platinum cases and powered by Frédéric Piguet ébauche calibers. Go figure, Jacques Piguet being Frédéric Piguet's son.

The Villeret Ultra Slim was the simple, time-only model of the six masterpieces. This is the "base" model that would come to shape the new Blancpain. Making this watch all the more interesting is the "No 001" on the caseback and "No 1" on the dial, both indicating that this was one of the first watches made by Biver and Piguet. Watches of Knightsbridge, the selling auction house, has marketed the lot as "believed to be the very first watch sold by Jean-Claude Biver and Jacques Piguet in early 1980." I would happen to agree with that claim as we know where the full set of "No 00" is .

This Blancpain is, fittingly, lot 1 of Watches of Knightsbridge's Modern, Vintage & Military Timepieces auction on Saturday, November 23, at 9 AM ET. The pre-sale estimate is £6,000 to 9,000. Click here for the full listing . And click here to check out the whole catalog, which has some real gems in it, including some newsworthy Cartier London pieces ( here and here ).

It's been a while since a vintage Daytona was featured here on Bring A Loupe. That's the result of the market not yearning for Daytonas quite as much as it once did, yes. But it is also due to low supply. Given the attention focused elsewhere, collectors with great vintage Daytonas are holding these watches close to the chest. Why sell a watch that is experiencing a market downturn when you know you have an excellent example and eventually attention will come back?

That all being said, this ref. 6262 is the exception – it is an excellent example of a somewhat rare reference. I say "somewhat rare" because ultimately this is a Rolex and Rolex made a ton of watches. However, the 6262 had a relatively short production run compared to other vintage Daytona references. A successor to the ref. 6239, the 6262 was introduced in 1970 and essentially the only change between the two was the upgraded caliber 727. Other than that and a slight bezel change, this is the same watch as the very first Daytona, the 6239.

This particular 6262 is being offered by a dealer in London with a keen eye for quality and niche dial variants. When Robin Mann stocks a Daytona, I pay attention. This one is a great example of what I am talking about, as it has a silver dial with subtle black Daytona branding wrapped around the six o'clock sub-register. Sure, "Big Red" and "Cherry Red" dials are great and really pop on the wrist, but this more subdued variant requires a higher taste level to appreciate. Condition-wise, this one is clean and well-represented in the description. One luminous plot shows degradation, but find me a Daytona with perfect lume plots and I'll show you the one that has been reapplied. At least we know this one isn't messed with.

The seller, Mann About Time in London, is asking £42,000 for this Rolex Daytona ref. 6262. Check it out right here .

While I'm not crazy about pocket watches, this one is undeniably interesting and worth a quick feature here on BAL. If you squint and look at the photos, you would think this is a Breguet, given the dial and hand style but, of course, it is not. Signed by Patek Philippe and complete with the original certificate, this is a pocket watch I can get down with! It's just weird enough.

I have seen Patek Philippe pocket watches with guilloché dials in the past but those tend to date more toward the 1910s and aren't so obviously inspired by Breguet. Check out a few examples of these earlier watches here , here , and here . The one we are focusing on here dates to the 1940s and has more overtly Breguet dial cues, like the outer minute track and the sub-seconds register styles. It's worth noting that the Patek signature is correct for the second half of the 1940s with "& Cie."

I like what I see but, to be sure, I reached out to Collectability's John Reardon, my favorite Patek expert, for a quick check. He said, "I would want to see an archive to confirm it's legit… gut reaction is that is it most likely okay."

An eBay seller out of St. Louis, Missouri, has put this Patek pocket watch up for auction, ending Sunday, November 24, at 8:15 PM ET. At the time of publishing, bidding has been very active and has reached $19,200. Get all of the details right here .

This Japan-only brand has been hard to miss on watch social media over the past year or so. Ōtsuka Lōtec has a unique aesthetic (and distribution strategy) that, it seems, works for the masses. And I mean really works. So much so that this exact model was awarded the "Challenge" award at the 2024 Grand Prix d'Horlogerie de Genève.

The brand is the brainchild of Jiro Katayama, a Tokyo-based industrial designer with a background in cars and appliances. He enjoys things with an analog, low-tech feel (hence the second part of the brand name). Katayama draws inspiration from old film cameras, industrial instruments, and gauges – a through-line that is very evident in the end product of the No. 6. The first time I heard about this brand it was because a U.S.-based friend was asking for help in acquiring one. After looking into it, I quickly found out that buying directly from Ōtsuka Lōtec is currently impossible for guys like myself and my friend.
