Currently, these watches can only be purchased in Japan. A lottery system is in place and, if you win, the watch must be delivered to a Japanese address and the payment method must be tied to a Japanese bank account. If that won't work for you, Loupe This has one for auction right now. It's just going to be a bit more expensive than the retail price of $2,725. If you need to have an Ōtsuka Lōtec, this is your only option right now.

The auctioneer, Loupe This, is located in Los Angeles and the current bid price at publication is $6,900. The auction for this Ōtsuka Lōtec is set to end on Tuesday, November 26 at 12:09 PM ET. Get all the details right here .

Dipping into the fun, quirky eBay portion of our weekly Bring A Loupe, this Dufonte caught my eye. This is one of those watches where I have never seen another and think it is really cool, but that doesn't mean it is valuable at all. Rarity does not automatically equal expensive. I can't even say I am an expert on this watch; frankly no one is. Everything on the dial seems to be period correct and I am assuming it is "real" because why would someone fake this? If you were going to fake a Dodgers World Series watch wouldn't you pick a brand that people know?

I have no idea what size the watch is as the seller has photographed it next to a worn ruler that I can't do much with but it looks like 32-34mm generally. If I bought something like this I would probably only wear it once, show one or two friends who don't really care, and move one. So why not let someone else buy it who will enjoy it much more?

Fun fact, in 1963 and in 2024, the Dodgers beat the Yankees to win the World Series. There has never been a better time to buy and wear this watch!

An eBay seller out of Raleigh, North Carolina has put this Dodgers World Series watch up for auction, ending Saturday, November 23, at 7:23 PM ET. At the time of publishing, bidding has reached $99. Get all of the details right here .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: The First Neo-Vintage Blancpain, A Breguet-esque Patek Pocket Watch, And A Japan-Market-Only GPHG Winner

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week's Bring A Loupe focuses on finding great vintage watches on a budget under $3,000. An impossible goal? Not at all – we found a very funky Hamilton, and a triple calendar from Gallet, and even a great looking vintage Rolex. However, that budget constraint will be put aside for two insanely rare Universal Geneve watches that definitely deserve a place in our Friday lineup. This is your Bring A Loupe for November 27, 2015 – and I wish you a nice Thanksgiving weekend.

The Super Compressor case revolutionized dive watches in the mid-1950s, when the case maker Ervin Piquerez SA, or EPSA, designed and patented an innovative case-sealing method. They had the inspiration to use the increasing pressure of water on the case back in order to actually tighten the sealing gasket – the deeper you went, the better the water resistance. Many brands required those cases to be employed for their diving offering; among the mythical Super Compressors, we could name the Jaeger-LeCoultre Polaris, the Longines Compressor, or the Universal Geneve Polerouter Sub. Wittnauer also offered such a watch in a 36 mm case with the typical oversized double crowns with cross-hatch markings. This diver can be found on eBay here ; at the time of publishing bidding was around $1,100.

You are looking at a true '70s watch, with the unique case shape of the Fontainebleau line. It houses a very famous movement, the caliber 11, one of the first automatic chronographs along with the Seiko 6139 and the Zenith El Primero. Interestingly, Heuer also used the Chronomatic designation – in one word – for the very same movement, at the very beginning of the production. They quickly concluded that its marketing impact was insignificant, so gave the rights to the word to Breitling. Going back to this Hamilton, its grey dial is one of its most striking features, very nicely contrasting with the two black sub-dials and the date window surroundings. This very cool piece of 1970s watch design from Hamilton is offered for $1,650 on Timezone here .

The Oyster Perpetual reference 1500 represents the essence of Rolex – it was actually one the brand's best sellers. It is at once a practical, reliable, and elegant watch, thanks to its automatic movement with a date function. The dauphine hands of this reference 1500 are a sign of early 1960s production, and make this 34 mm Oyster Perpetual much rarer than the usual version with stick hands. It comes with the original rivet bracelet, the correct endlinks, and, for anyone who wants to actually wear it, a recent service. This Oyster Perpetual Date is listed on eBay here ; bidding was still below $850 as I typed those words.

One weird thing drew me to this listing: the seller uploaded seven similarly looking pictures! However, if you look closely you see the second hand running, and the chronograph hands working – always a good sign with a vintage watch offering that many complications. Besides the low quality of the photos, you should anticipate this Gallet to look much better once all the scratches on the plexiglass are buffed out. For a very reasonable budget, you get a great chronograph from a renowned manufacturer, featuring a triple calendar. The listing is about to run out on eBay; at the time of publishing this watch was still under $600 here . Given the misalignment of the day-disc I would plan for a service.

Vintage Omega Seamasters are plentiful, but finding one in a truly good condition is a much harder task. Additionally, the Seamaster Calendar adds a bit of irreverence to an otherwise very classical design; the unusual date placement at 6 o'clock really brings harmony to the case, balancing the logo above. The example here, dating back to the 1950s, boasts a great overall condition, with a clean dial and a sharp stainless-steel case. It is listed on a French forum for 1,350 Euros or around $1,450 here .

Interestingly, this is not the first time we cover this beautiful Universal Geneve featured in Bring A Loupe: Eric highlighted it when it was offered at Antiquorum more than a year ago . The dial is the key here; it is not only in a great condition, but the letters "HPC" also make it a real rarity. HPC stands for "High Precision Chronometer," a proof of testing from Le Bureau de Contrôle des Chronomètres. The blued hands nicely complement the elegant Arabic numerals in rose gold on the patinated silver dial. Note that the 35 mm case remained in a crisp condition, a rare feat for a 70-year-old watch. The dealer Lunar Oyster is offering this rare chronograph for $6,750 here .

This is an insane Universal Geneve watch – just have a look at its specs: a split second chronograph in a stainless-steel case with a 44 mm diameter – and remember we are talking about the 1940s era here, where a dress watch could easily be 30 mm. The 24-hour dial with the Cairelli mention reminds us of its original military vocation with the Italian forces, while the single pusher further emphasizes the technical marvels behind that piece. We showed you a similar chronograph when we covered the stellar collection of John Goldberger . To further understand the extreme rarity of this split second chronograph, you should know that one example, with a better preserved dial, reached 120,000 CHF at Sotheby's Geneva back in 2012. The seller Matthew Bain just offered one here in crazy good condition; the price is not mentioned in the listing, but will obviously reach a high multiple of our initial $3,000 budget. If you were after an exceptional Thanksgiving purchase, you have just found it.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Selection Of Watches Under $3,000, With Two Incredible Exceptions

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Rich Fordon
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
Happy Friday, and welcome back to Bring A Loupe! I come bearing awful news: my personal eBay account was hacked this week. My watchlist full of gems is temporarily inaccessible, and the years of effort creating the perfect list of saved searches are currently for naught. How does that affect the column? Well, this week, we will be eBay-less. But I'll be back – eBay customer service is working diligently on the case. Have no fear; four picks round out today's eclectic collection nonetheless.

To recap last week, the first neo-vintage Blancpain sold hammered for 7,000 GBP over at Watches of Knightsbridge – possibly a steal? Our Breguet-esque Patek pocket watch ended up at $20,798 and our Lucien Piccard with an LA Dodgers logo sold for $305 , both on eBay. Finally, the Japanese-market-only Ōtsuka Lōtec No. 6 went for $10,340 all-in on Loupe This – a relative bargain considering the same watch just made $60,366 at Phillipe Hong Kong .

Alright, let's get into it… here are this week's picks!

If you read my momentous Boucheron article here on the pages of Hodinkee in October of 2023, you should be familiar with Alexandre Tritz, the strap maker and vintage watch scholar who goes by "the Boucheron guy" on Instagram under the handle @thewatcham. I first followed his account due to our mutual love of mid-century Boucheron, but slowly, I understood that Tritz has one of the best "eyes" for elegant vintage dress watches I have ever known. After buying and selling for his personal collection over the past few years, Tritz has decided to become a "dealer" in this niche space. His new account, @galbegallery, will offer pieces for sale with a focus on mid-century French-made watches like this JLC "Tank Galbée."

While I don't typically introduce you, dear readers, to the seller before speaking about the watch itself, I do so in this case because it is of paramount importance to understand who Tritz is. When I say his eye is good, I mean really good. And his scholarship on these French-made watches is among the best in the world. Even without purchasing a watch, his listings are an education in and of themselves.

As is evident by the "EJ" case hallmarks, this JLC was produced in Paris by the workshop of Edmond Jaeger – in the same period as EJ-made Cartier Paris watches. You can see this connection via the case construction, which features two screws on each side to hold the inner case and "caseback" inside the outer case or bezel and crystal. This mirrors many of the Cartier pieces of the era and is unique when compared to 1950s Swiss-made JLCs, which have a more standard snap back. So, why does this matter? Collectors go insane over pre-acquisition Cartier watches, and much of this insanity is driven by the handmade nature of the cases under Edmond Jaeger. What you are buying here is all of that same charm under a different brand name. Jaeger and the Swiss movement manufacturer LeCoultre joined forces in 193,7 making this watch an "early" JLC.

The seller, Alexandre Tritz of @galbegallery in Geneva, is asking €4900 for this French-made Jaeger-LeCoultre. Check it out and contract Tritz right here .

I will never shy away from my relative lack of understanding (and, quite frankly, love) of important pocket watches. Yet here I am featuring a pocket watch in back-to-back Bring A Loupe columns. Don't ever say I curate this list solely to my own taste, okay? Saying I don't love pocket watches is a bit tongue-in-cheek as I can still understand when one is special, and this particular Longines is exactly that.

At first glance, this looks like a perfectly nice 68mm diameter Longines pocket watch from the "interwar" period. The Longines extract confirms exactly that. In June of 1925, the watch was invoiced to the brand's agent in Budapest, Herpy Arnold (as is evident by the retailer's signature on the dial), and features a Longines caliber 24.99. I will stop here to call out the condition of this watch, which is remarkable. The white enamel dial appears free of any major cracks and really shines. As is very evident, whoever owned this watch appreciated it and kept it in top condition.

The condition and appreciation of this watch could be because it was owned by a master watchmaker for at least some time. As is described in the auction catalog and confirmed by the Longines heritage department, this pocket watch was modified, likely in the late 1920s or early 1930s, by a German watchmaking student to feature a flying tourbillon with spring detent escapement.

The detent escapement is also referred to as the chronometer escapement because it typically yields extreme accuracy. The technology dates back to the late 18th century when it became the standard "top-of-the-line" escapement for marine chronometers and chronometer-rated pocket watches of all kinds until the mid-20th century. So why don't we use detent escapements in all watches? Firstly, it is hard to execute, typically reserved for only the best watchmakers, and it is notoriously fragile when compared to the Swiss lever escapement.

Typically, a detent escapement tourbillon pocket watch (like our Longines) is the first project taken on by promising young watchmakers. For example, F.P. Journe's first ever watch is a detent flying toubillon – it took him five years to complete. Today's Longines is thought to be the first project of another young, promising watchmaker, only this time in 1920s Germany. We can come to this conclusion due to the style of the tourbillon works which mirror other known examples from the German school like this one that sold for 86,400 CHF at Sotheby's earlier this month.

This Longines "school" pocket watch is lot 31 of Iconeek's Timeless & Iconic Timepieces VII auction on Friday, December 6, at 9 AM ET. The pre-sale estimate is 20,000 to 40,000 CHF. Click here for the full listing.

In 1969, Movado and Zenith merged to become one company located in La Chaux-de-Fonds. A few months later, they acquired Mondia to become "MZM" or Movado-Zenith-Mondia. The watches made after this merger have always been interesting to me as a vintage Movado lover but are best described as a general sharing of movements and parts between the three companies. This is when we see Movado signed El Primeros, branded as "HS 360" on the dial but, for all intents and purposes, nearly identical to Zenith models of the same era. The watches are more Zenith than they are Movado but remain fascinating.

Especially fascinating are the dials co-signed by both Movado and Zenith. To get really into the weeds, the watch we are looking at here is a Movado-branded Zenith El Primero co-signed by Zenith – got it? Whatever we want to call it, it is a very 1970s-styled solid, 18k yellow gold, and El Primero-powered chronograph for less than $10,000 from a reputable seller. That's enough for me.

The seller, Arthur at Shuck The Oyster in Germany, is offering this Movado / Zenith chronograph for €6,800. Check it out right here .

I'd like to pre-emptively apologize to my friends in the "Heuer Heritage" WhatsApp chat for featuring this watch. I understand the want to keep things quiet when a great vintage Heuer comes up for sale in an unexpected place but I have a duty to my Bring A Loupe faithful who stick with me through ramblings about detent tourbillon pocket watches.

Among manually-wound Carreras, the ref. 2447 NST is the ultimate "standard" variant due to its reverse panda motif and relative rarity versus other 2447 dials. The relative rarity is likely due to a short production run. We see these Carreras with case numbers pointing to production in 1970, maybe 1969, just before the manually-wound Carrera ceded to "Chronomatic" Carreras. Plus, the white periphery tachymeter text and registers provide a nice contrast that really makes the dial pop. To Carrera collectors, this is the apex mountain.

Seemingly fresh to market via a small auction house in Cheshire, England, the condition looks to be… great. The case is sharp with clean lines visible on the edges of the lugs, and the dial is above average with some wear near the bottom but full lume plots at every marker other than three and nine o'clock. Without an hour marker to cling to, these are normally the first plots to go. The watch looks like it could use a service as well as a new crystal and white tension ring, but those are easy fixes in the grand scheme of things. It even comes with what looks to be box and papers!

This Heuer Carrera is lot 1810 of Adam Partridge Auctioneers & Valuers's Three Day Sale of Jewellery, Silver, Watches & Boutique (Macclesfield) happening Wednesday through Friday, December 4 to 6, at 5 AM ET. As lot 1810 of 2879, I assume this lot would be on Thursday or Friday. The pre-sale estimate is 5,000 to 8,000 GBP. Click here for the full listing.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A French-Made JLC, A Flying Tourbillon, And A Dual-Signed El Primero

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week, the hunt for vintage watches unveiled some highly unusual finds, the type you almost never see. In that vein, the 1980s Heuer Verona reference 371.213G might not be the hottest Heuer chronograph currently, but it's nonetheless a real rarity and an exceptional find for Heuer collectors. Here we also have a Zenith Extra that was made for the Canadian railroad in the middle of the 20th century. And, of course, classic chronographs are not forgotten here either – we've got a great Mathey-Tissot as well as an appealing Omega De Ville.

The 120T caliber is not especially rare; the total output is estimated at 18,000 movements, produced until 1963. However, finding the Extra R.R. line on the dial is definitely noteworthy, as it indicates a refined version made for the Canadian railways (R.R. standing for railroad), a client that demanded the highest precision possible. The dial configuration itself confirms the purpose of this watch, displaying the familiar red numerals for the afternoon hours, as seen on many other railroad watches made by Cyma, Longines , and Universal Genève. This 35mm wristwatch was undoubtedly made for a specific environment; however, for total peace of mind I would have preferred to see the Extra R.R also mentioned on the 120T caliber, as seen in this example . That said, the 766A966 serial on the caseback falls squarely in the range that Zenith provided for this production of the Extra R.R. wristwatch. Lastly, the embossed emblem on the crown indicates that it's a replacement, as it corresponds to the logo adopted when Zenith and Movado merged, after 1969.

The Italian dealer Lorologiese offers this rare this rare Railroad Zenith Extra for €2,900 (approximately $3,300 at time of publishing).

Most of what can be found about the Verona reference 371.213G comes from a 1981 Heuer catalog , which describes it as a twin chronograph; dfinding live pictures of this watch proves highly challenging. This late version of the Verona offers a double display, both analog and digital, much like the Heuer Manhattan (affectionately nicknamed "The Coffin" by Heuer aficionados after its peculiar case shape). The quartz movement will be polarizing, of course, but it provides the the date, a second timezone, and of course the chronograph function, as proudly mentioned in red letters on the dial. Obviously, it is a quirky piece: its weirdness is the sign of a bygone era, and will not leave anyone indifferent – you'll either hate it or love it.

This very rare Heuer Verona with a patinated dial is offered on a German forum for €2,300 (approximately $2,550 at time of publishing).

Among vintage enthusiasts, Mathey-Tissot is mostly remembered for producing the vintage Breguet Type 20, but their branded watches are interesting in their own right, as this 40mm chronograph easily proves. A similar example was featured in Bring A Loupe last summer, but its appearance on the last Phillips' auction catalog shows the increased recognition that this good-looking chronograph is getting. The reverse panda dial and touches of orange contribute to its winning appearance, and the compressor case and black rotating bezel complete a very pretty picture. Interestingly, the dial supplier here was Singer, famous for making dials for the vintage Rolex Daytona – while both chronographs are powered by the trusted Valjoux 72 caliber.

The dealer Watches With Patina priced this attractive Mathey-Tissot chronograph at $6,500 on his website.

Back in the 1950s, the Zodiac Autographic was described as the " World's Most Modern Watch ." The reason for that claim lay behind the power reserve indicator visible at the bottom of the dial: it was a world premiere on an automatic wristwatch. The function and calculation behind this innovative up-and-down indicator are thoroughly presented in a 1949 paper written by a watchmaker for the Journal Suisse d’Horlogerie et de Bijouterie , who emphasizes the necessity for having a design for a power reserve indication, that doesn't interfere with the action of the main gear train, as well as the technical challenges involved in implementing a power reserve in an automatic watch. Note that this Zodiac is clearly described as a project watch, which will need a full service before running properly. Similarly, the listing mention some damage to the numerals, although the photos are not quite clear enough to figure out how bad it is. And the hour hand is hidden behind the minute hand, but its lume material seems to have held out nicely, although this would need to be checked. You can find this Zodiac Autographic listed on Ebay ; at the time of publishing, bidding is still below $30.

The Omega De Ville reference 145.017 remains an undervalued chronograph – a great bi-compax design with a restrained 35mm case. Initially launched in 1968, this model was powered by the caliber 860, which afforded those two sub-registers, while the caliber 861 used in the contemporary Speedmaster permitted a three-counter configuration. The reference 145.017 can be found in silver or black dial, with or without lume and external scale. Here it should be noted that the lume dots are not perfect, and the movement's bridge has been replaced, which implies that its serial number has disappeared – all correctly described in the listing.

You can find this Omega De Ville chronograph offered for $2,100 on Omegaforums .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Extremely Rare Heuer Verona, A Zenith Extra For The Canadian Railroad, A Great Mathey-Tissot Chronograph, And More

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Louis Westphalen
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
This week's Bring A Loupe will feature an early Autavia chronograph from Heuer, and reveal another from Longines – a true eBay find in all its glory, horrendous pictures included. We will also look into watches with some very special dimensions, starting with the first ultra-thin Piaget with automatic movement, and then examining two incredible Vacheron Constantins, both with oversized cases in pink gold. This is your Bring A Loupe for November 6, 2015.

Two weeks ago we covered in depth an early Autavia that ultimately fetched $45,000 on eBay and today it kind of starts over. Well, this Autavia is not as rare, as it is the second execution of the reference 2446 with screw-back case. In comparison to the first execution that we had spotted earlier, the difference is minute, but for a Heuer aficionado it means a lot: the hands are not full lume anymore, you can see their frame here. Unfortunately, those hands were badly re-lumed as the weird yellowish color reveals. The dial on the other hand still belongs to the first execution with those big sub-dials and the long indexes integrated into the minute track, and it remains in good condition. At the time of publishing, bidding on this Autavia had already reached $26,850 on eBay here .

This Zenith reference A277 represents another very attractive chronograph dating from the late 1960s. This Zenith, or its alter ego the Movado Sub Sea, remains underestimated: both stand out as being much more affordable that many Heuers or Universal Geneves, while not compromising one bit on quality or look. The case has a significant 40 mm diameter, and here it still displays some well-defined bevels on its lugs. Furthermore, the watch comes with its original bracelet, manufactured by Gay Frères and similar to the ones fitted on the first El Primero model. The Italian dealer CasoWatches is offering this great example here .

This chronograph from Longines is a typical eBay find: a very bad pic of an interesting watch that did not get that much attention – yet. As far as I can tell from the dark and blurry pics the condition of this Longines is obviously not perfect but it remains pretty great for a 50-year-old watch. The crystal has seen some battles but, more importantly, the dial and hands look nice, only the crown is incorrect. And, of course, the Longines reference 6592 is powered by the famed 30-CH caliber, one of the most well respected vintage chronograph movement. You can find the listing on eBay here and with less than 2 days to run the bidding were still below $500. At this price it is the steal of the year. UPDATE: The seller just terminated the bidding on the Longines, which probably means that he decided to sell outside of eBay. While the amount can't be precisely known I have heard rumors about an offer around $4,500.

The Chronometre Royal might have a pretentious name, it does deliver on point. Obviously, the caliber P1007/1008 – small second as here or central second hand – is one of the best manual wind movement ever manufactured. And each case is a joy to look at, with a special focus on the lugs. Many models have integrated faceted lugs that can also be found on one reference from Patek Philippe (the reference 2568, second execution) but the reference 4838 here really stands out with its bold lugs that makes its 35 mm case in yellow gold much bigger on the wrist. A dealer based in Spain is offering this beautiful example for $8,750 here .

If this watch was coming from any other brand it would reach stratospheric prices; imagine a triple calendar from the 1950s with moonphase, and all that in a pink-gold case to add a dash of style! While the face might be super pretty, what really drew my attention was actually its case back. Its specific shape indicates that it was designed with waterproof consideration in mind, a pretty crazy endeavor for a triple calendar moonphase. It is actually very similar to the case back of the Patek chronograph reference 1463 that we absolutely love at HODINKEE ; for that reason I would take bets that the case of this Movado was manufactured by the exact same supplier – François Borgel, later renamed Taubert Freres. Matthew Bain listed this special and stunning Movado here .

In 1960 the Journal de Geneve described the birth of this Piaget with the following words: "The launch of the 12P movement an event destined to enter the annals of horological history." Why so much enthusiasm? In short, the 12P is a fantastic ultra thin movement! Let's talk numbers: it took two years to engineer it from the already slim 9P movement – 2 mm thick. In the end the integrated micro-rotor was only 1 mm thick but in 24k gold to add mass, allowing the caliber 12P to finish at a staggering height of 2.3 mm. Both 9P and 12P remain in use by Piaget to these days, with only minimum changes – this tells you how R&D was some 65 years ago. The reference 12103 here comes with the cross-hair dial that I have seen on the early production and, small detail, its box is pretty sweet to look at too. While the diameter might only be 34 mm wide, when a watch is that thin you don't need much more in order to avoid the "frying pan on the wrist" syndrome. As I typed those words, bidding had just been over $1,500 on eBay here , which in my opinion is a bargain for a watch this significant in the watchmaking history.

The upcoming Christie's Auction will feature many amazing lots in a couple of days in Geneva, and Ben shared his two favorites here . When I read the auction catalog two watches really captured my eye: none feature multiple complications but they both boast an extremely rare 38 mm pink-gold case knowing that the standard for a dress watch in the 1950s was around 34 mm, if not smaller. Both come from Vacheron Constantin: the references 4537 and 4539. And if you were wondering about references 4536 and 4538, they also come with a 38 mm case, the latter was actually included in the famous "The Art of Vacheron Constantin" auction, in 1994.

The reference 4537 captured the attention of Vacheron Constantin collectors thanks to its oversized case in 38mm and was consequently described as a Jumbo. From information in a very good thread on a dedicated forum , it seems to have remained in Vacheron's catalogs from 1950 to 1955 but production of this specific reference halted in 1953, resulting in a total production just over 500 pieces with 258 in pink gold. Besides its size and rarity, the elaborate shape of the lugs are obviously a very striking characteristic of this charming vintage watch. You can find the listing for lot 81 here and estimates are ranging between $10,000 and $20,000.

Much less is know about the reference 4539, although its 38 mm case adorning thin and straight lugs features in good place on the 1950 catalog from Vacheron Constantin. Interestingly, I found two other reference 4539s ever offered at auction, one being part of the aforementioned "Art of Vacheron Constantin" auction. Given the fact that those three examples were standing within a narrow range of 50 watches in terms of production number, you can easily imagine how limited the production of the reference 4539 was. And when you know that this one, lot 22, is the only one with a pink-gold case to have ever surfaced, well the notion of rarity gets even more concrete. You can find the listing here and its auction price is estimated at between $15,000 and $25,000.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: Two Oversized Vacherons, One Very Slim Piaget, And One Promising Chronograph From Longines

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
That time of the year is here again, when dealers, collectors, and journalists flock to Geneva to kick off auction season. With this in mind, we’ve highlighted a few pieces on offer at Christie’s, Phillips, and Antiquorum, including an ultra-desirable Heuer Skipper Carrera, a prototype Speedmaster originally delivered to NASA, and a range of Rolex ephemera that ought to impress the scholarly collector. For good measure, there's also a fun chronograph that competed with the Speedmaster for the moon watch title and an often overlooked variant of yet another stellar vintage chronograph. Let’s do just what the title of the column would suggest, and take a closer look at this week’s curiosities of note.

Eberhard has produced some of the greatest chronographs of all time, and the Contograf is no exception. While it may not be as mechanically sophisticated as some of the brand's other offerings of years past – though the in-house cal. 310.82 is no slouch – it is a legendary design, and one that you can’t help but love, regardless of your collecting background. The example in question today is no ordinary Contograf though, as it features the seldom-seen silver dial, that’s accented tastefully with red and black markings.

This watch is in incredible condition top to bottom, and even still has the original bracelet that so many are now missing. Its hands are also particularly appealing, as they’re the less common, thin stick variant from Eberhard, which followed the earlier dauphine-style hands. The aptly named, Netherlands-based dealer of both cars and watches, Cars & Watches, has this Contograf listed for €16,500. Visit their site for more details.

Aside from watches, music, and photography, I’m somewhat obsessed with all things related to NASA, for the simple reason that the administration represents one of the coolest, most daring efforts in the history of humankind, itself. How’s that for justification? With this in mind, I couldn’t help but include this historically important piece coming up for sale at Phillips in Geneva this weekend. This is a Mark II ref. ST 145.014 Speedmaster, but it’s not an ordinary one by any means. As the extract from the archives from Omega will confirm, this is a prototype example of the sought after Alaska Project Speedmaster that, interestingly enough, was delivered to the Apollo Program Office at NASA’s Johnson Space Center. It’s believed to be one of a handful of prototypes produced with a set of durability-increasing modifications, per NASA’s request.

To own a Speedmaster that’s confirmed to have been delivered to NASA is one thing, but to own an Alaska Project prototype delivered to NASA is on an entirely different level. We’ll certainly be watching to see what this rarity among rarities realizes in the sales room this weekend. Phillips has the estimate on this piece set at CHF 40,000-60,000. Click here for more details (and to see the rest of the catalog).

Speaking of space, it’s worth noting that the Speedmaster didn’t earn the Moonwatch monicker without going through rigorous testing – and during the process, a few other watches were put to the test as well. One such watch was the ref. 242T manufactured by Wittnauer, and it’s arguably one of the best looking sports chronographs ever made. Allow me to explain. On paper, the 242T has a lot to offer, with a Valjoux 72 at its core and a liberal 38mm case diameter, though as one would expect, it's on the wrist that it really shines, thanks to the truly unique dial design. In addition to the outer decimal scale that traces the dial, you’ll find puffy luminous plots marking every five minute increment, along with an inner ring of small white dots that cut through the all the sub registers. This all makes for one of the most daring chronograph dial designs of the 1960s, which I personally find to be reminiscent of solar system models.

242T prices have been climbing steadily over the last few years, but in comparison to some other Valjoux 72 powered chronographs that are perhaps more desirable, the Wittnauer offers a lot in the way of value. While it might not have necessarily been up to NASA’s standards, I’m sure you’ll find it to be more than satisfactory – just don’t go to the Moon with it. Justin Vrakas of WatchSteez is offering this outstanding example for $10,500. Click here for the full listing.

The Skipper is perhaps one of the rarest Carrera’s to have ever been produced, and also one of the most attractive. Its multicolored dial has long mesmerized the upper echelons of the vintage watch collecting world, and for good reason. The design was simply so radically different from both anything Heuer had ever produced, and anything available on the market at the time, which is largely why it’s so coveted in today’s collecting landscape. Heuer’s involvement in the timing of yachting dates back to the early 1960s, when the watchmaker began production of several stopwatches branded with the name Yacht Timer – many of which featured dials accented vibrantly with touches of blue, red, and green. These instruments were relied upon as true tools by sailors and skippers across the globe, to the extent that many teams competing in the America’s Cup had Heuer chronographs on board. Aspects of the existing yachting design language would later be translated into their wristwatch lineup with the release of the first Skipper, the Carrera-based ref. 7754, and later the ref. 7764 based on the ref. 2446 Autavia.

Following the weekend, Christie’s will offer an example of the famed yachting chronograph in their Geneva sale. The sale of a so-called “Skipperrera” is a rather big deal in the watch world, in that these are just unbelievably rare, and those that own them are understandably reluctant to part ways. Be sure to watch what this piece realizes on Monday, as the stunning condition will surely make it a benchmark for all future sales. Christie’s has the estimate on this rare Heuer set at CHF 25,000-45,000, though I wouldn’t be surprised to see it go far higher considering what examples have fetched in the past. Find more details here.

Editor's Note: The description for this watch has been updated on Christies.com: "Please note that the image of this watch in the catalogue does not accurately reflect the condition. Additional photos will be sent to you to show the dial and the case that reflects the description in our condition report of this important watch."

As much as I love watches, I’ll often joke that marketing materials and watchmaker associated ephemera are arguably cooler than the watches themselves. I say this because such materials frame and contextualize how brands wished to be, and ultimately were perceived in an era we now so fervently celebrate as watch collectors. Marketing and Rolex go together like peanut butter and jelly, and if you somehow weren’t already convinced, then maybe these several lots on offer at Antiquorum (via Invaluable.com ) this weekend will change your mind.

In addition to a directional sign from the Rolex offices , there are several unique looking brochures , watchmaking tools and materials , and a variety of hand painted sketches that were presumably used in Rolex window displays. There’s also what appears to be a Rolex branded pressure gauge, which is just unbelievably cool. Should you need a break from watches but still want a piece of the action, this might be a good direction to look in.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Omega Speedmaster Alaska I Prototype Delivered To NASA, A Silver Dial Eberhard Contograf, And A Super Rare Heuer 'Skipperera'

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
There's a bit of a theme to this week's roundup, and if you're fond of black-dial-fitted sports watches, you're sure to enjoy what's in store. From gilt dial Submariners to a clean complete example of Vacheron Constantin's 222, big brands are represented. At the slightly more obscure end of the spectrum, there's a Bond-associated Breitling that's deserving of more attention, along with an Enicar made famous by Formula 1 driver Jim Clark. For good measure, there's an horological objet d'art of sorts, which ought to impress those with an affinity for Patek Philippe. Without delay, let's dive in.

Kicking things off for the week, we’ve got a little something that might just make the perfect one-watch collection. I've often described the Rolex no date Submariner – both early and modern examples – as the white t-shirt of watches, as it's one of the few pieces that never looks out of place. Whether you’re replacing light bulbs around the house or attending some properly fancy affair, it just feels right. Having said that, there are levels to everything, and Submariners are no exception. With this in mind, let's take a look at a really nice white t-shirt.

You’re looking at the Submariner in one of its most celebrated forms, the Ref. 5513. Over the reference's nearly three-decade lifetime, the watch-buying public was treated to a variety of exciting iterations, but few as aesthetically compelling as those featuring gilt dials, dating back to the early days. Risking the chance of coming across as a total loon, I'd argue that such variants afford a figurative warmth that their matte dial descendants just can't compete with.

Typically, I look for pristine gloss perfection in a gilt dial sports Oyster, but every now and then I'm surprised by what does it for me. This was one of those instances, as the crazed surface of the once mirror-like dial really spoke to me. Pair that with the perfect tone of aged luminous compound, and you've got a seriously attractive package. Though examples free of any flaws are always sure to turn heads, never underestimate the value of a piece rich with character.

The dealer Rob Cooper has this piece listed on his Instagram feed with an asking price of $17,500. Find more details and make an offer here.

There's a certain beauty to things that make little to no sense on paper, but inspire awe upon execution. The oxymoron that is the luxury sports watch embodies this aforementioned beauty, and its origins are nothing short of inspiring. For those not familiar, allow me to fill you in. Such timepieces emerged in the wake of the quartz crisis, when radical reinvention was necessary for surviving the market's changing landscape. Through the development thin stainless steel cases housing top grade movements, secured to the wrist with intricately integrated bracelets, a new and now dominating segment of the market was born.

In this segment, there are two models that lead the class as the darlings of seasoned collectors across the globe. Each has reached a beyond iconic status, to the point that I don’t even think I need to spell out their names. With these two consuming the bulk of collector attention, many often overlook the 222 from Vacheron Constantin, which arguably brings just as much to the figurative horological table.

Released in 1977 in honor of the manufacture's 222th anniversary, this Jorg Hysek-designed offering featured an integrated bracelet and JLC Cal. 920 movement, just like its holy trinity brethren. Unlike the others, the 222 is differentiated by its ornate, scalloped bezel and lug-mounted Maltese cross, which I've always considered to be one of the single coolest watch design details of the 1970s. Apart from the likely unpolished case, this particular example stands out from the rest thanks to the presence of the original boxes and papers, which you just don't see all that often.

The Keystone of Beverly Hills has this outstanding Vacheron Constantin listed on their site with an asking price of $45,000. Check it out and make an offer here.

Like countless others, much of my awareness of history's noteworthy timepieces came by way of studying old photos. When flipping through shots of early Hollywood greats and mega-stars of the motoring circuit, it's far from unusual to find a sought after watch hiding under a cuff, or in plain sight if you're lucky. Care to skip straight to the goldmine of vintage watch spotting? Do a Google image search for the name "Jim Clark," take however long you need to admire the man's impeccable taste, and thank me later.

In addition to his Breitling Navitimer and Gallet Multichron, the Scottish-born driver is well known for having worn an Enicar Sherpa Graph, which many now reference using Clark's name. With four different typefaces found on its dial, along with an applied Saturn logo, this is another one of those watches that doesn't make the most sense on paper, but works awfully well in the metal.

Although new old stock is always ideal, the next best thing in my books is a watch that's been worn but remains untouched by meddling hands. Today’s example in question fits the latter description, with a case and crystal not free of scuffs, but certainly free of unfortunately polished-away lines. What appears to be slight corrosion on the surface of the hands would further confirm that this piece is truly untouched, giving prospective bidders additional peace of mind.

Hannam’s Auctioneers of Selborne will offer this Enicar in a sale taking place on the 16th. Online bidding has already opened , with the high bid standing at 2,200 GBP at the time of publishing.

While on the topic of timepieces with important affiliations, I thought I’d make mention of one of my all-time favorite chronographs. Believe it or not, this is a Bond watch, but you’d be forgiven for thinking that, given its slightly different form in the film Thunderball . The watch worn on the silver screen was a unique Geiger counter variant produced for the film, and the example in question today is a modest layman's chronograph, but what it lacks in radiation measuring abilities is made up for and then some with striking good looks.

Though some would describe this dial configuration as a reverse panda, its two silver sub-registers elevate it to that next level. Unlike other chronographs with silver accents, the paint used on the Top Time's dial has a quite a strong metallic flake effect to it that really shines under natural lighting. If you’ve never handled one in the metal, I'd recommend tracking one down and treating yourself.

You can't ask for much more in terms of condition with this piece, as evidenced by all of the present luminous plots, the razor sharp stainless steel case, and the absolutely spotless dial. In comparison to other vintage chronographs of similar quality, the Top Time is still substantially more affordable, which is something I've never been able to wrap my head around. I guess I'd advise getting one while the getting’s still good, as Breitling certainly isn't making any more of these any time soon.

Corr Vintage Watches of London is offering this example for 3,250 GBP. Find the full scoop and make an offer on their site.

To end things, I thought I'd share my favorite find of the week, which isn't a complete watch. Your last "pick" is four disassembled movements produced by none other than Patek Philippe. No, this isn't some loose arrangement of parts organized in cloudy plastic bags, but instead is an artful arrangement of components in a block of acrylic. I don't know about you, but I've never seen something quite like this, and want this in my possession for several reasons, some of which are beyond my knowledge.

In this display you'll find the wristwatch calibers 9’’'90, 12’’'120, and 7’’'70, along with a 17’'' pocket watch caliber. These deconstructed mechanisms span roughly four decades of Patek Philippe's history, making this a visually stimulating way of tracing the manufacture's past of legendary movement production. As someone who appreciates symmetry, I can't get enough of how everything has been arranged, with four partially coiled mainsprings leading towards a single balance wheel.

Though it's not explicitly stated, I'm guessing that this piece of horological art was produced by Patek themselves, seeing as each of the four movements is said to be unused and without serial numbers. Whether you're a diehard Patek Philippe collector or simply a budding enthusiast of watchmaking, you're sure to get a kick out of this.

Bonham's will offer this unique acrylic display in their New York sale of watches taking place on the 16th. Find more details along with the rest of the catalog here.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Enicar Sherpa Graph, A Breitling Top Time, And A Rolex Sub Ref. 5513

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
That time of the week has come once again, and the roundup is just as good as you'd expect. Kicking things off is a striking Pre-Moon Speedmaster Professional, followed by the single coolest Patek Philippe we've encountered in a long while. Scroll down, and you'll be pointed in the direction of an uncommon world time watch from Breitling. Those who enjoy the occasional "fun" piece will appreciate the rally-bezel-fitted Zodiac Sea Wolf, along with an automatic example of a Keith Haring-designed Swatch, in new-old-stock condition, complete with the original box. It's temptation time!

To get things started for the week, we've got an iconic chronograph if there ever was one, which predates its claim to fame ever so slightly. I'm talking about a Speedmaster, and no ordinary one, as the presence of its uniquely aged dial would suggest. Don't worry, there's no derivative recounting of the Moon mission ahead – we're getting right down to the nerdy, nitty gritty.

On most examples of the Ref. 145.022, you'll find that the fitted caseback boasts the timepiece's NASA flight qualifications and Moon mission involvement, but on this watch, such an engraving is nowhere to be found. This is explained by its date of production, which, despite having been manufactured in 1969, still predates Apollo 11. For this reason, collectors refer to such examples as "Pre-Moon" Speedmasters. Like other examples of this reference that were produced during that year, the dial on this one has achieved a tropical tone.

Slightly tropical dial aside, this is an attractive example for a number of reasons. First, it's fitted with the correct "dot over 90" bezel that's been replaced on all too many examples after years of wear and tear. On this piece, it remains nearly unscathed, save for a single scuff near the six o’clock position. Secondly, this example comes complete with the correct 1039 bracelet and 516 end links that many spend a great deal of time and money attempting to track down. If you've been after a Professional case Speedmaster for a while, might I suggest taking a stab at this one.

An eBay seller in Australia has this piece listed for AUD 14,500, which equates to just under $10,000. You've also got the option to make an offer, so have at it!

Check out the listing here.

When you think of pilot's watches, Patek Philippe is far from the first name that comes to mind. No, this isn't a case of unsung under-appreciation, but instead a reflection of the simple fact that Patek Philippe never really produced watches in series for aviators. With that said, a mere handful of oversized, hour angle style pieces from the manufacture do exist, the majority of which are featured in the brand's museum. Our next piece for the week isn’t one such 55 mm behemoth, but instead something that's perhaps even more rare – a conventional dress piece with piloting provenance. This rarity surfaced for sale on Tuesday, and not to make mention of it in this week's roundup would be a disservice to the vintage watch collecting community.

This rectangular piece from Patek Philippe corresponds with the reference number 404, and enjoys a particularly exciting provenance, as its caseback engraving would suggest. Flip the watch over, and you'll find the name of French aviator Lieutenant de Vaisseau Henri-Laurent Dallière, along with a commemoration of a historically important flight. From the 22nd to 23rd of June in 1935, Lieutenant Daillière flew a seaplane known as the Croix du Sud from Cherbourg, France, to Ziguinchor, Senegal. In doing so, Daillière set a world record for the longest distance traveled in a seaplane (2,711 miles, to be exact), which presumably resulted in being awarded this very watch.

Apart from its provenance, the watch is noteworthy in its own right thanks to a likely unique dial configuration, distinguished by its cat whisker-resembling enamel hour-markers at its center. As if that wasn't enough, it also has what might be the coolest applied seven on any Patek Philippe. The way in which the tail of the numeral reaches a curved point adds an element of symmetry that typography and watchmaking enthusiasts alike are sure to enjoy. Despite having handled some heavily complicated, grail-worthy pieces from the manufacture in question over the last little while, this is probably the single coolest Patek Philippe I've encountered all year.

Eric Wind has this piece of both aviation and Patek Philippe history listed for sale on his site for $17,000. Find more photos and information here.

This next piece comes from a brand that's always been a favorite of mine, largely because they're responsible for manufacturing my first decent watch. Since that piece entered my collection, I've always been a fan of Zodiac dive watch designs. When approached by budding collectors looking to pick up a diver, I'll often point them in the direction of the Sea Wolf.

In its more conventional forms, the Sea Wolf is far from a rare watch, with examples regularly available on the market. In this form, with the coveted rally style bezel, they're seldom seen. What’s more, this might be the least common of all the rally bezel pieces from the Sea Wolf collection. For every five pieces I've encountered with the blue and white variation, I've likely seen just one with this red and black curiosity.

I don't know about you, but the relatively ornate nature of the bracelets fitted on this era of Sea Wolf have always surprised me, given the rugged watches they're fitted to. I especially like the thin stripe that runs through the center links of the bracelet, adding an additional element of dimension to the busy-to-begin-with dive watch. If you've got an horological itch that needs scratching, and your collection is missing a little fun, this might be a good piece to consider.

This Zodiac is being offered on eBay by a seller in Chino, California, for $3,000. The option to make an offer is there, as well. Find the full scoop here.

Seeing as many seemed to enjoy the inclusion of an often overlooked vintage Breitling in our last installment, I thought we'd keep things going with another that unjustly gets even less widespread praise. Unlike last week’s Top Time, this piece dates back roughly one decade prior to the 1950s, when Breitling’s offerings were arguably of a higher grade, and produced with a closer attention to detail. Your next pick of the week is known as the Unitime, and it’s quite the looker, if I do say so myself.

The Unitime's name is somewhat ironic, in that it's characterized by its world time function that affords the ability to track different time zones. Like other world time watches, you'll find a small map printed on the dial’s surface, though unlike most others in its class, the graphic is extremely subtle. Paired with the coin-edge bezel, the watch has a decidedly sophisticated vibe about it, and at 37 mm across feels modern on the wrist.

Though the case might have been polished, its defining lines remain visible, along with one mark that adds a great deal of intrigue to an already exciting piece. If you direct your attention to the top left lug, you'll notice a small imprint that could be mistaken as a mere dent to the untrained eye. This is in fact an import hallmark, indicating that following the production of the watch, it was imported into Portugal and the taxes on it were paid. It's details like these that help tell a more complete story about where a watch has been, and what it might have seen, making it all the more compelling to the discerning collector.

The Munich-based dealer Watchpool24 has this watch listed with an asking price of €10,500 – though upon inquiring as to whether it was still available, I was informed that they'd like to offer a €600 discount to all HODINKEE readers. Find more information about the watch here.

If you're up to date on the latest and greatest in the auction world, you might've caught wind of how Philipps will be offering a curated selection of timepieces manufactured by Swatch for private sale. As a big fan of all things Swatch, and their borderline irreverence as a whole, I was tickled pink to hear this news. Since the watches don't go on sale until the 25th of October, I thought I'd point you in the direction of a particularly interesting piece to tide you over in the meantime.

This is a new old stock example of a limited edition piece, produced in collaboration with the artist Keith Haring. For those not familiar with Haring's work, my advice would be as follows – run, don’t walk. Though he's regarded as key figure of the pop art movement, I'd argue that the way in which he used his work to raise awareness of pressing social matters elevated it to a level above that of his contemporaries. If you're interested in checking out more his work, I'd recommend looking into the artist's New York City subway chalkboard drawings, which he was arrested for doing on multiple occasions.

Fear not die-hard mechanical watch folk: Unlike many offerings from the brand, this Swatch is powered by an automatic movement. This places it within history as a posthumously released collaboration, in that the earlier examples from 1985 were powered by quartz movements. Also, its Haring-designed dial depicts a human centipede on the dial, which is pretty cool if you're into that sort of thing.

You’ll find this piece listed here on eBay, where a seller in Palm Springs, California, is after $795.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: An Omega Speedmaster Ref. 145.022, A Patek Philippe Ref. 404, A Zodiac Sea Wolf Rally, And A Breitling Unitime Ref. 1-260

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
It's Friday, and that means we've got another handful of the latest and greatest listings from around the web to share with you. There's a bit of an aeronautical theme going on this week, with the inclusion of an AOPA branded Elgin that predates the Ref. 806 Navitimer, along with a Universal Geneve Polerouter De Luxe, and a Paul Newman Daytona with high flying provenance. If two-register chronographs are your thing, you're in luck, as we have a Heuer Carrera and the mintiest Ref. 7413 Longines that you ever did see. Time to get down to it.

The way I see it, half the fun of hunting down vintage watches is the demanding and unpolished way, so to speak, in which watches are presented and acquired. If you do your homework and dig deep you can strike gold in unlikely places, without the need for a third party's seal of approval. That's the difference between a mere purchase and a true find, and believe me, it pays off. A piece that fits this description popped up for sale earlier this week, and if you'll indulge me, I'd now like to share it with you.

Heuer’s Ref. 3647N Carrera emerged out of the late 1960s, and effectively took Jack Heuer's desire to produce an ultra-legible driver's chronograph to the next level. Though it wasn't capable of tracking elapsed hours, the freed up real estate (not to be confused with free real estate) on its dial made for easier reading at a glance while behind the wheel. Besides, there's an argument to be made for hours not being of extreme importance in a sport where fractions of a second make or break your success.

Call me crazy, but I kinda love a listing with bad photos. There's a sort of rush you get when you take an educated leap of faith on a desirable timepiece presented poorly, and it then pays off in spades. Although this seller's photos are less than ideal, you can still make out the fact that its case remains unpolished, and the dial is seemingly perfect, with all luminous applications matching and intact. Polish up that crystal, get it fitted on a decent strap, and you'll be off to the races.

An eBay seller based out of Bremerton, Washington, has this piece listed in an auction that'll end on Tuesday afternoon. At the time of publishing, the high bid stands at $4,650.

When you think of AOPA-associated pilot’s watches – should you happen to find yourself pondering such matters at random – there's one piece and one piece only that comes to mind. This is of course the Ref. 806 Navitimer, which launched the collection and put Breitling on the map as a premier manufacturer of pilot's watches. With that said, there's another piece worth noting that often gets overlooked. Though it might not bring the same sort of punch to the party that the Navitimer does, its historically important nonetheless, and perhaps a more logical choice for an AOPA watch.

You're looking at an Elgin produced for the AOPA, dating back to 1951. This is an important year to note, in that it predates the Navitimer's release in 1952. Therefore, this is the original timepiece put into production expressly for the American pilot's association, and a fitting one at that, seeing as the Elgin Watch Company was indeed American. This raises the question, "Why would the AOPA jump ship for Breitling?" Ultimately, I'd say it came down the fact that Breitling was offering greater value, along with a more compelling watch.

As original advertisements would indicate, the 24-hour dial fitted Elgin was available to AOPA members for $49.50, including taxes. Compare that to the marginally higher price that the Navitimer originally commanded, and you'd be forgiven for scratching your head while eyeing down the bigger, better deal. Despite its usurpation of sorts, I think the watch is still historically important, and an attractive proposition for anyone after an all American, aviation-associated watch.

A member of the Omega Forums going by the handle "primabaleron" has this piece listed with an asking price of €700. More photos and even a video can be accessed here .

While Rolex intended for many of their watches to serve in specialized professional applications, it's safe to say that a large portion of these purpose-built timepieces were worn by regular folk, who simply valued a well made watch. Knowing this makes the discovery of a watch that saw some action quite the cause for celebration. Recently, a piece that fits this description became available for purchase, and it just so happens to be a Paul Newman Daytona. Fascinated by its original owner's story, and the aeronautical feats it witnessed, I couldn't help but include it in this week's roundup.

This pump-pusher Ref. 6239 was produced in 1969, and later purchased as a wedding anniversary gift for a woman by the name of Barbara. By all definitions of the term, Barbara was a bona fide badass, who realized her dreams of becoming a pilot, and a seriously accomplished one at that. Not only did this original owner race planes, but she also provided air support during search and rescue missions. Pushing the envelope even further, Barbara – who I believe wished to remain known simply by her first name for the purposes of the listing – was an airplane mechanic credited with working on the Polaris A-2 missile's tactical thrust vector control system. Do I even know what a tactical thrust vector control system is? Nope. Am I properly impressed? You bet I am.

Considering the numbers that Newman Daytonas command, their collectors are understandably condition sticklers. With that said, there are exceptions to every rule, and I believe this to be one of them. Though the case on this example has been polished, and the luminous plot at three o'clock is partially missing, its provenance, supported by accompanying photographs and documentation, truly sets it apart from the rest. Provenance is the secret sauce that can elevate a great watch to that next level of desirability. This watch certainly has it, as opposed to being just another chronograph with the right dial.

San Francisco’s H.Q. Milton has this unbelievably cool piece listed with an asking price of $183,350. Find more details on their site.

It seems that a theme is becoming clear in today's Bring a Loupe, as here we have another aviation-related watch coming in hot. Contrasting the rest, this one does not feature a highly functional 24-hour dial, or a handy chronograph function, but instead proved itself in the cockpit by way of its antimagnetic shielding, which proved useful to Scandinavian Airlines Systems pilots who experienced these meddling fields when flying over the North Pole.

Today's example in question corresponds with the reference number 10357-1. Although the markings have worn off its caseback, this is indeed the variant we're dealing with. What makes this one special is its 18k gold case, which, unlike the majority of gold Polerouters, is rose gold. This not only affords it the ability to stand out from the pack a bit more, but it also makes the watch a little bit more wearable in casual settings. Worn-off reference aside, its condition is outstanding, with a thick case and flawless dial. This is just about as good as it gets.

In 2019, the Polerouter line offers a serious amount of bang for your buck. As the seller of this example has stated, it's a solid gold Universal Geneve, powered by a top tier micro-rotor caliber, thought up by none other than Gerald Genta himself. For under the $5,000 mark, it's quite tough to do much better in the vintage sphere, with few other horologically significant equals. If you're able to pull off an all gold watch – which is easier than you'd think considering that the case measures a conservative 34.5 mm across – I say by all means, go for it.

You'll find this piece being offered on the Omega Forums, where its owner has it listed for $3,500. Get in touch with the seller here.

Ending things off for the week, we've got another dual-register chronograph. Like the last, it's cased in stainless steel, and was produced roughly around the same period, though that's about all it has in common with the aforementioned Heuer. This is a totally different animal that's a great deal more refined, while still maintaining a dash of sporty versatility. Simply put, this Longines has got it going on, but we both know you didn't click on this week's column for things put simply, so let's get into the details.

Coming in at 38 mm across, this oversized Longines features contrasting telemeter and tachymeter scales executed in blue and red. Against the otherwise reserved silver dial, these scales really stand out, making it easy to put them to the test. With that said, the main attraction here is what's found beneath the caseback. For my money, Longines is one of the absolute best chronograph caliber manufacturers of all time, and its Cal. 30CH is among their greatest. Though admittedly less desirable than the gold standard 13ZN, its legendary construction and butter-smooth operation make it anything but a slouch.

In comparison to the gold case variants of this reference, those crafted out of stainless steel tend to have been worn a little harder. This example defies that notion, as it's been preserved beautifully over the course of nearly five decades. Not only is its case unpolished, but its dial is also perfect, and its caseback free of any tool marks. If a tasteful chronograph with a slightly sporty twist is what you're after, look no further.

Miami's Menta Watches has this Longines on offer for $8,750. Additional photos and contact information can be found on their site.
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Rolex 'Paul Newman' Daytona, A Universal Geneve Polerouter, And A Longines Ref. 7413

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
We’re back this week with yet another eclectic mix of vintage oddities and icons of watchmaking history. From an insect-inspired Omega to a platinum-cased Swatch, there's much in the way of the unorthodox, not to mention the 51 mm Eberhard chronograph that quite literally changes the historical production number notions surrounding the timepiece. For those that prefer a more conventional timepiece that still retains a twist of excitement, there's a Ref. 1158 Carrera in yellow gold, along with the difficult to track down Tudor Ranger. Onwards and upwards!

With a seemingly endless number of over-the-top watches on the market today, sometimes you just want a no nonsense piece, distilled to its necessities. Tudor's Oysters have always fit this description, with few getting the job done as well as the elusive Ranger. In contrast to the rest of the brand's offerings, the Ranger was produced in far fewer numbers, making it awfully desirable in today's market. As one might expect, this has lead to a great deal of fakes surfacing for sale on the market.

One of the easiest ways to tell if you're dealing with the real McCoy is to inspect the color of the text, which reads "RANGER." Red Ranger at night or in morning – watch collector’s warning! All of such dials are guaranteed fakes, and will likely be surrounded by other fakes parts, as well. Luckily for you, the piece in question today is genuine as the day is long, and offered in nice shape.

You're looking at a Ref. 7966, which you may know to be the date-equipped, shield dial variant of the mid-sized Oyster. The years have been particularly kind to this specific example, as indicated by the matching luminous compound found both on the dial and uniquely shaped hands, in addition to its stainless steel case which remains thick. As these watches don't surface all that often, I'd suggest acting accordingly should you be on the hunt for one.

A collector based out of Australia is offering this seldom seen Tudor on the Omega Forum with an asking price of $7,500. More photos can be seen here .

At the frenzied height of the Heuer market, I came to the realization that unlike most collectors who regard the earliest triple-register references as the model's zenith, I preferred the later references powered by the Chronomatic caliber. This wildly unpopular opinion is likely rooted in the fact that I've always had a thing for weird watches, so to speak, and with this era of Carrera packs a hefty punch of '70s strange. Plus, if it's a 36 mm, Singer-dial-fitted, Valjoux-72-powered chronograph that I’m after, I'm unapologetically opting for a Daytona all day, everyday.

Of the Chronomatic Carreras, few are as stunning as the Ref. 1158. Produced in brilliant 18k yellow gold, the watch has a distinct "expletive you" vibe that I've always dug, not to mention its Formula 1 connection. For those who never spent far too many nights of their formative years up late, voraciously reading Jeff Stein's On the Dash , let me fill you in. Following Heuer's partnership with Ferrari’s Formula 1 team in 1971, Jack Heuer made a habit of presenting all of the team's drivers with examples of this very reference. Must not have sucked.

Though this example isn't the first to hit the market in ages, I'd argue that it's easily one of the best I've seen all year. Not only does its gold case remain thick and oxidized, but the subdials found on its flawless dial have also achieved a pleasing tropical tone that's likely to intensify as the years continue to pass by. This is what a top-shelf Carrera looks like, folks.

Christie's is offering this example of the Ref. 1158 in their online sale, which runs until the 15th of October. Find more details along with the rest of the varied catalogue right here.

With watch design being an inherently limited task, it never fails to amaze how some brands stand out from the crowd. In some instances, intricate complications are employed to scratch the itch of horologically savvy collectors, but for the more design focused, it's often a uniquely shaped case that'll do the trick. That being said, I thought we'd take a moment to focus on one of the more interesting examples of aesthetic ingenuity hailing from Omega's back catalogue.

As a result of its angular and extended lugs that resemble the jointed legs of beetle, this Omega is affectionately referred to by collectors as the Scarab. Apart from looking rather excellent on the wrist, the Scarab's design is also very functional, in that its extended and articulating lugs allow the watch to conform to the curvature of your wrist. It is rare that form and function exist in harmony, and when they do, it shouldn't go unnoticed.

This is a special example for two reasons. First off, despite likely being somewhere in the ballpark of 80 years old, the original dial with white enamel numerals and hands remains in outstanding condition, as does the unpolished case. Secondly, the case measures a modern 36 mm across, making it the largest case variant of the Scarab ever produced by Omega. With that said, the watch wears slightly larger than your average 36 mm piece, given the way in which its ornate lugs extend past the case.

The aptly named dealer of cars and watches Cars and Watches has this Scarab listed with an asking price of €2,999 .

Here we have an important and oversized piece from Eberhard to share with you. Not only is it somewhat of a military watch, given that its pocket watch-cased counterparts were once used by the Italian Royal Air Force, but it's also a watch that's rewriting history with its offering. Allow me to explain.

Up until the publishing of the catalogue that contains this 51 mm piece, scholars thought it to be a unique piece in wristwatch form. As the story goes, 10 examples of the caliber found within its case were produced, the majority of which were used in pocket watch applications, save for one sole example. With the discovery of this piece, the days of that tale are now officially gone. This development ought to excite collectors of both Eberhard and Italian military timepieces.

It's likely that Italian officials outlined specific design requirements for their military timepieces, as this piece bears a striking resemblance to the similarly complicated split seconds chronograph produced for the Italian navy by Universal Genève. Though not quite as large, and lacking both subdials and a date aperture towards the top of the dial, the A. Cairelli-retailed watch was clearly guided by the same design principles. With all this said, the Eberhard undoubtedly has the upper hand in the rarity department.

This Eberhard will be offered at auction in just over two week’s time at Aste Bolaffi in Milan. Its starting bid has been set at €24,000.

To cap off the roundup, I thought we'd end on a slightly more lighthearted note with a piece from Swatch. The brand’s various limited editions have always spoke to me, but to keep things in line with the rest of the week's picks, we've got what might be one of the most decidedly luxe pieces ever produced by the brand. While a quick glance might suggest you're looking at a more traditionally styled piece inspired by the moon and the stars, this is truly anything but your run-of-the-mill Swatch.

Unlike its plastic-cased, quartz-powered cousins, this piece is differentiated thanks to the presence of a solid platinum case and an automatic caliber. Yes, you read that right, a platinum-cased Swatch. As one might expect, this precious metal entered the equation in honor of a special occasion – the watchmaker’s tenth anniversary after first revolutionizing watchmaking back in 1983. It also features an astral influenced dial, along with a double-tiered, coin-edge detailing on the crown.

Worth noting is the fact that the Tresor Magique was produced in an arguably not-so-limited, limited edition of 12,999 pieces – though it's a special piece nonetheless, and surely one of the more unconventional offerings in Swatch's history. Should its limited edition nature still resonate with you, you'll surely appreciate the individual numbering found on the rotor of each example, in addition to the presence of the original display box and additional strap.

An eBay seller based out of New York has this limited edition set listed for sale with an asking price of $3,500. You've also got the option to make an offer .
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.title: Bring a Loupe: A Platinum Swatch, An Oversized Eberhard, And A Gold Heuer Carrera

https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.author: Isaac Wingold
https://www.hodinkee.com/articles/bring-.body_text:
We're back at it this week with another stacked selection, including several rare and important pioneering dive watches. From Tudor we've got one of the nicest Big Crowns to have surfaced in recent memory, and from Breitling, the dive watch that started it all for them. For those that choose to stay on land, we've also included a few dressier pieces as well, with an Omega originally sold in Portugal, an uncommon Universal Genève, and a simple Movado in remarkable condition. Buyer Beware is back too, with a shady-at-best chronograph. Here's the lowdown.

You can tell a lot about a manufacture by the looks of their time-only calibers, and from your first glance at the hand-wound caliber 75, you can instantly tell that Movado wasn’t messing around in the 1940s. The movement stuns from architectural and aesthetic standpoints, and it is quite an efficient mechanism, too. While on the hunt for watches this week, I came across a time only Calatrava-style piece from Movado that just so happened to be powered by the aforementioned caliber. Oh, and it’s actually new old stock, with the box and hangtags all included. In the past we’ve discussed how the term "New Old Stock" gets thrown around all too often, but yet again, we’ve chanced upon a watch worthy of the title. This was a watch most likely intended to be sold via a Madrid-based watch retailer called Calvo, as one of the included original hang tags bears that name, but for some reason the watch looks to have never been worn. In addition to the case's sharply defined lines, the brushed finished on the caseback doesn't show a single mark or scuff on it.

Whether you’re a scholarly collector looking to admire a never-worn watch, or someone who simply wants to wear a great looking, 35mm time-only piece with a legendary Movado caliber inside, there’s a lot of watch here for the money. A seller on eBay based in Boston, Massachusetts, is selling this Movado for $2,499 (a notable number in the watch world). You can also make an offer. See the full listing here.

Our next watch the week is something of an unusual abstract design. If you’re not a die-hard Universal Genève obsessive, this is going to be a bit of an acquired taste. As the saying goes, not everybody likes licorice, but the people who like licorice really like licorice. I think this watch is very much the same. During the 1950s, Universal Geneve produced what’s admittedly a bit of an oddball of a watch, but one worthy of your consideration, no less. This is the ref. 100110, which features a borderline medallion-esque bezel, which is much larger than the minuscule black and gold dial itself. Even more unconventional is the fact that the date window sits below the dial, and has its own aperture in the bezel.

Everything on this watch checks out, as you’d expect, given that Universal Genève didn’t produce all that many pieces. Given the rarity, and the fact that we can’t remember the last time one of these came up for sale, it’s advisable that you chase this one down while you’ve got a shot (if it's to your taste, that is). This watch is available from Heritage Auctions and bidding on this watch starts at $1,500, plus buyer’s premium. The live auction takes place on October 24. Find the full listing here.

As someone who appreciates vintage dive watches, both obscure and mainstream, I’ve always been fascinated with the numerous bezel designs that came out of the late 1950s and '60s. No vintage dive watch bezel discussion would be complete without mentioning Breitling’s famed SuperOcean either. The SuperOcean family might mean one thing in the world of modern Breitling, but this piece comes from the back catalo – in fact, it's the reference that launched the line back in 1957. This was essentially Breitling’s answer to the dive watch frenzy that the watch world was experiencing in the wake of the Fifty Fathoms, Submariner, Seamaster 300, and Deep Sea Alarm, among others. Fit with a concave bezel crafted out of Bakelite, this watch has a very bold look, with dagger-shaped luminous markers on the dial too. That slick bezel also serves to protect the domed acrylic crystal and all the radium found on the original black dial underneath.

The bezel on this example is perfectly intact, and the dial and hands look honest and original. But what’s really outstanding is the presence of the original mesh bracelet, which is now both extremely hard to find and a signature trait of the SuperOcean line at large.
